            (shift_date + shift_start)::timestamp,
            (shift_date + shift_end)::timestamp,
            '[)'
        )
    ) STORED,
    
    -- Prevent overlapping shifts for same employee
    CONSTRAINT excl_employee_shift_overlap 
        EXCLUDE USING gist (
            employee_id WITH =,
            shift_time WITH &&
        ),
    
    CHECK (shift_end > shift_start)
);

-- Insert non-overlapping shifts
INSERT INTO employee_shifts (employee_id, shift_date, shift_start, shift_end) 
VALUES 
    (1001, '2026-02-10', '08:00', '16:00'),  -- Employee 1001: 8am-4pm
    (1001, '2026-02-10', '16:00', '20:00'),  -- Same employee: 4pm-8pm (no overlap)
    (1001, '2026-02-11', '08:00', '16:00'),  -- Different date (OK)
    (1002, '2026-02-10', '09:00', '17:00');  -- Different employee (OK)

-- Try overlapping shift for same employee (will fail)
-- INSERT INTO employee_shifts (employee_id, shift_date, shift_start, shift_end) 
-- VALUES (1001, '2026-02-10', '14:00', '18:00');
-- ERROR: conflicting key value violates exclusion constraint
-- DETAIL: Shift 14:00-18:00 overlaps with existing 08:00-16:00

-- Query employee schedules
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    TO_CHAR(shift_date, 'Day, Mon DD') AS date,
    TO_CHAR(shift_start, 'HH12:MI AM') || ' - ' || TO_CHAR(shift_end, 'HH12:MI AM') AS shift,
    EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (shift_end - shift_start))/3600 AS hours
FROM employee_shifts
ORDER BY employee_id, shift_date, shift_start;
Result:
 employee_id |        date         |       shift        | hours
-------------+---------------------+--------------------+-------
        1001 | Monday   , Feb 10   | 08:00 AM - 04:00 PM|   8.0
        1001 | Monday   , Feb 10   | 04:00 PM - 08:00 PM|   4.0
        1001 | Tuesday  , Feb 11   | 08:00 AM - 04:00 PM|   8.0
        1002 | Monday   , Feb 10   | 09:00 AM - 05:00 PM|   8.0
(4 rows)
Explanation: Exclusion prevents employee working overlapping shifts: employee 1001 cannot work 14:00-18:00 on Feb 10 (conflicts with 08:00-16:00). Consecutive shifts allowed: 08:00-16:00 then 16:00-20:00 (no overlap since '[)' excludes endpoint). Different employees can have overlapping shifts (1001 and 1002 both work Feb 10). shift_time computed as tsrange from shift_date + times, enabling temporal overlap detection. Use cases: prevent scheduling conflicts, ensure rest periods between shifts (could add CHECK shift_start >= previous_shift_end + INTERVAL '8 hours'), compliance with labor laws (max consecutive hours). Different employees/dates unrestricted - only same employee same time blocked.

🎯 Example 3: Promotional Campaigns - One Active Campaign Per Product

-- Product promotional campaigns
CREATE TABLE product_campaigns (
    campaign_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    campaign_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    discount_percent NUMERIC(5,2) NOT NULL CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 100),
    start_date DATE NOT NULL,
    end_date DATE NOT NULL,
    campaign_period daterange GENERATED ALWAYS AS (daterange(start_date, end_date, '[]')) STORED,
    
    -- Prevent overlapping campaigns for same product
    CONSTRAINT excl_campaign_overlap 
        EXCLUDE USING gist (
            product_id WITH =,
            campaign_period WITH &&
        ),
    
    CHECK (end_date >= start_date)
);

-- Insert campaigns
INSERT INTO product_campaigns (product_id, campaign_name, discount_percent, start_date, end_date) 
VALUES 
    (101, 'Winter Sale', 20, '2026-01-01', '2026-01-31'),
    (101, 'Spring Sale', 15, '2026-03-01', '2026-03-31'),  -- Different period (OK)
    (102, 'Winter Sale', 25, '2026-01-15', '2026-02-15');  -- Different product (OK)

-- Try overlapping campaign (will fail)
-- INSERT INTO product_campaigns (product_id, campaign_name, discount_percent, start_date, end_date) 
-- VALUES (101, 'Extended Winter Sale', 30, '2026-01-15', '2026-02-15');
-- ERROR: conflicting key value violates exclusion constraint
-- DETAIL: Campaign for product 101 overlaps with existing Winter Sale period

-- Query active campaigns for date
WITH campaign_status AS (
    SELECT 
        pc.campaign_id,
        pc.product_id,
        pc.campaign_name,
        pc.discount_percent,
        TO_CHAR(pc.start_date, 'Mon DD') || ' - ' || TO_CHAR(pc.end_date, 'Mon DD') AS period,
        CASE 
            WHEN CURRENT_DATE <@ pc.campaign_period THEN 'ACTIVE'
            WHEN CURRENT_DATE < pc.start_date THEN 'UPCOMING'
            ELSE 'ENDED'
        END AS status
    FROM product_campaigns pc
)
SELECT * FROM campaign_status ORDER BY product_id, start_date;
Result:
 campaign_id | product_id | campaign_name | discount_percent |       period        | status
-------------+------------+---------------+------------------+---------------------+----------
           1 |        101 | Winter Sale   |            20.00 | Jan 01 - Jan 31     | ENDED
           2 |        101 | Spring Sale   |            15.00 | Mar 01 - Mar 31     | UPCOMING
           3 |        102 | Winter Sale   |            25.00 | Jan 15 - Feb 15     | ENDED
(3 rows)
Explanation: Exclusion ensures only one active campaign per product at any time: product 101 cannot have overlapping campaigns (Jan 1-31 and Jan 15-Feb 15 would overlap). Different products (101 vs 102) can have overlapping campaigns. daterange with '[]' (both inclusive) means Jan 31 included in Winter Sale - appropriate for promotions where end date participates. Prevents conflicting discounts: customer shouldn't see 20% and 30% simultaneously. Use <@ operator ("contained by") to check if date within range: CURRENT_DATE <@ campaign_period. Gap between campaigns allowed: Winter Sale ends Jan 31, Spring Sale starts Mar 1 (February gap OK). Alternative design: allow overlaps but prioritize by campaign_id (highest discount wins).

🎯 Example 4: Mutually Exclusive States - Only One Active Record

-- Price history with exclusion: only one current price
CREATE TABLE product_prices (
    price_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL CHECK (price >= 0),
    effective_date DATE NOT NULL,
    end_date DATE,
    price_period daterange GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        daterange(effective_date, COALESCE(end_date, '2099-12-31'), '[)')
    ) STORED,
    
    -- Prevent overlapping price periods for same product
    CONSTRAINT excl_price_period 
        EXCLUDE USING gist (
            product_id WITH =,
            price_period WITH &&
        )
);

-- Insert price history
INSERT INTO product_prices (product_id, price, effective_date, end_date) 
VALUES 
    (101, 999.99, '2025-01-01', '2025-06-30'),   -- Historical price
    (101, 1099.99, '2025-07-01', '2025-12-31'),  -- Historical price
    (101, 1199.99, '2026-01-01', NULL);          -- Current price (no end date)

-- Try adding conflicting current price (will fail)
-- INSERT INTO product_prices (product_id, price, effective_date, end_date) 
-- VALUES (101, 1299.99, '2026-01-15', NULL);
-- ERROR: conflicting key value violates exclusion constraint
-- DETAIL: Price period overlaps with existing current price

-- Query price at specific date
WITH price_lookup AS (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        price,
        effective_date,
        end_date,
        daterange(effective_date, COALESCE(end_date, '2099-12-31'), '[)') AS period
    FROM product_prices
)
SELECT 
    product_id,
    price,
    TO_CHAR(effective_date, 'Mon DD, YYYY') AS effective_from,
    COALESCE(TO_CHAR(end_date, 'Mon DD, YYYY'), 'Current') AS effective_to,
    CASE WHEN end_date IS NULL THEN 'ACTIVE' ELSE 'HISTORICAL' END AS status
FROM price_lookup
WHERE product_id = 101 
  AND '2026-01-25'::DATE <@ period;
Result:
 product_id |  price  | effective_from  | effective_to | status
------------+---------+-----------------+--------------+--------
        101 | 1199.99 | Jan 01, 2026    | Current      | ACTIVE
(1 row)
Explanation: Price history with exclusion prevents overlapping effective periods: only one price valid at any time. COALESCE(end_date, '2099-12-31') treats NULL end_date as "far future" - current prices have no end date but still participate in exclusion. Cannot add second current price (end_date=NULL) - would overlap with existing. To update price: set current price's end_date = '2026-01-24', INSERT new price effective_date = '2026-01-25'. Ensures point-in-time price lookups unambiguous: '2026-01-25' <@ price_period returns exactly one row. Use cases: pricing history, employee salary history, tax rate changes, configuration settings where only one valid at a time. COALESCE trick handles open-ended ranges in exclusion constraints.

🎯 Example 5: Advanced - Multi-Column Exclusion with Complex Logic

-- Meeting room bookings with multiple conditions
CREATE TABLE meeting_rooms (
    booking_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    room_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    building VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,
    organizer_email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    meeting_start TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    meeting_end TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    meeting_period tsrange GENERATED ALWAYS AS (tsrange(meeting_start, meeting_end, '[)')) STORED,
    is_recurring BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
    
    -- Complex exclusion: same room AND building, overlapping time
    CONSTRAINT excl_meeting_room_overlap 
        EXCLUDE USING gist (
            room_name WITH =,
            building WITH =,
            meeting_period WITH &&
        ),
    
    -- Organizer can't have overlapping meetings (double-booked)
    CONSTRAINT excl_organizer_overlap 
        EXCLUDE USING gist (
            organizer_email WITH =,
            meeting_period WITH &&
        ),
    
    CHECK (meeting_end > meeting_start)
);

-- Insert meetings
INSERT INTO meeting_rooms (room_name, building, organizer_email, meeting_start, meeting_end) 
VALUES 
    ('Conference A', 'Building 1', 'john@company.com', '2026-02-10 09:00', '2026-02-10 10:00'),
    ('Conference A', 'Building 2', 'jane@company.com', '2026-02-10 09:00', '2026-02-10 10:00'),  -- Different building (OK)
    ('Conference B', 'Building 1', 'bob@company.com', '2026-02-10 09:00', '2026-02-10 10:00');    -- Different room (OK)

-- Try double-booking room (will fail)
-- INSERT INTO meeting_rooms (room_name, building, organizer_email, meeting_start, meeting_end) 
-- VALUES ('Conference A', 'Building 1', 'alice@company.com', '2026-02-10 09:30', '2026-02-10 10:30');
-- ERROR: conflicting key value violates exclusion constraint "excl_meeting_room_overlap"

-- Try double-booking organizer (will fail)
-- INSERT INTO meeting_rooms (room_name, building, organizer_email, meeting_start, meeting_end) 
-- VALUES ('Conference B', 'Building 1', 'john@company.com', '2026-02-10 09:30', '2026-02-10 11:00');
-- ERROR: conflicting key value violates exclusion constraint "excl_organizer_overlap"

-- Query bookings
SELECT 
    room_name || ' (' || building || ')' AS location,
    organizer_email,
    TO_CHAR(meeting_start, 'HH12:MI AM') || ' - ' || TO_CHAR(meeting_end, 'HH12:MI AM') AS time_slot
FROM meeting_rooms
WHERE DATE(meeting_start) = '2026-02-10'
ORDER BY meeting_start, room_name;
Result:
          location           |   organizer_email   |     time_slot
-----------------------------+---------------------+-------------------
 Conference A (Building 1)   | john@company.com    | 09:00 AM - 10:00 AM
 Conference A (Building 2)   | jane@company.com    | 09:00 AM - 10:00 AM
 Conference B (Building 1)   | bob@company.com     | 09:00 AM - 10:00 AM
(3 rows)
Explanation: Multiple exclusion constraints enforce different business rules: excl_meeting_room_overlap prevents double-booking physical rooms (same room_name AND building with overlapping times), excl_organizer_overlap prevents person from organizing multiple simultaneous meetings. Conference A exists in both buildings - rooms same name different buildings treated separately. John (organizer) can't book second meeting 09:30-10:30 (conflicts with his 09:00-10:00). Multiple exclusion constraints all enforced: room must be available AND organizer must be available. Room without time conflict OK, organizer without time conflict OK, but neither can overlap. Advanced pattern: room_name WITH = plus building WITH = implements composite partitioning - rooms only unique within building. Enables sophisticated scheduling rules beyond CHECK constraints.

5.6 System Columns

📖 Full Description

System Columns are special hidden columns automatically present in every PostgreSQL table (except views), providing metadata about rows. These columns are normally invisible in SELECT * queries but can be explicitly selected to access internal row information. The most commonly used system columns are: tableoid (identifies which table the row came from, useful in inheritance), ctid (physical location of row version), xmin (transaction ID that inserted the row), xmax (transaction ID that deleted/updated the row), cmin and cmax (command identifiers within transactions).

The ctid (tuple identifier) column reveals the physical storage location of a row as (page_number, tuple_index). For example, ctid='(0,1)' means first page (page 0), second tuple (tuple 1, zero-indexed). CTIDs are not stable - VACUUM, CLUSTER, or UPDATE operations change row locations, making ctid unsuitable as a row identifier (use PRIMARY KEY instead). However, ctid useful for debugging, identifying duplicate rows without primary keys, or temporary row identification within a transaction before changes are committed.

Transaction-related columns (xmin, xmax, cmin, cmax) expose PostgreSQL's MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) implementation. xmin shows which transaction created the current row version; xmax shows which transaction deleted it (0 if not deleted). These columns enable advanced querying: find rows created by specific transaction, identify dead tuples (xmax != 0), understand concurrent update behavior. System columns rarely needed in application code but invaluable for database administration, debugging concurrency issues, understanding storage internals, and performance analysis.

System Columns Syntax
-- Query system columns explicitly
SELECT tableoid, ctid, xmin, xmax, cmin, cmax, * FROM table_name;

-- Individual system columns
SELECT ctid, * FROM customers;         -- Physical location
SELECT xmin, * FROM orders;            -- Creating transaction ID
SELECT tableoid::regclass, * FROM t;   -- Table OID (convert to name)

-- System column descriptions:

-- tableoid: OID of the table containing this row
--   Useful in inheritance hierarchies to identify child table
SELECT tableoid::regclass AS table_name, * FROM parent_table;

-- ctid: Physical location (page, tuple)
--   Format: (page_number, tuple_index)
--   Example: (0,1) = page 0, second tuple
SELECT ctid, customer_id FROM customers;

-- xmin: Transaction ID that inserted this row version
--   Increments with each transaction
--   All rows in same INSERT have same xmin
SELECT xmin, * FROM products;

-- xmax: Transaction ID that deleted/updated this row version
--   0 if row not deleted/updated
--   Non-zero indicates row marked for deletion
SELECT xmax, * FROM orders WHERE xmax != 0;

-- cmin: Command identifier within transaction (insert command)
--   Identifies which statement within transaction created row
SELECT cmin, * FROM table_name;

-- cmax: Command identifier within transaction (delete command)
--   Same as cmin for current command
SELECT cmax, * FROM table_name;

-- Practical system column usage:

-- Find physical location of rows
SELECT ctid, customer_id, email FROM customers ORDER BY ctid;

-- Identify rows created by recent transactions
SELECT xmin, * FROM orders WHERE xmin >= txid_current() - 100;

-- Find rows pending deletion (uncommitted deletes)
SELECT * FROM products WHERE xmax != 0;

-- Check which child table row belongs to (inheritance)
SELECT tableoid::regclass, * FROM all_employees;

-- Identify duplicate rows without PK
SELECT ctid, * FROM products WHERE product_name = 'Laptop';

System Column Details

Core System Columns:

  • tableoid: OID (Object Identifier) of table, useful in inheritance to identify child table
  • ctid: Physical location (page_number, tuple_index), changes after VACUUM/UPDATE, not stable
  • xmin: Transaction ID that created row version, used for MVCC visibility
  • xmax: Transaction ID that deleted/updated row, 0 if current version active
  • cmin: Command ID within inserting transaction (which statement created row)
  • cmax: Command ID within deleting transaction (usually same as cmin for current command)

CTID (Tuple Identifier):

  • Format: (page_number, tuple_index) - e.g., (0,1) is page 0, tuple 1
  • Zero-indexed: Tuple index starts at 1 (first tuple is 1, not 0; page starts at 0)
  • Changes with updates: UPDATE creates new tuple at different ctid (old tuple marked deleted)
  • VACUUM effects: VACUUM compacts pages, changing ctid values
  • Use cases: Debugging, identifying duplicates, temporary identification within transaction
  • Not suitable for: Persistent row identification, foreign keys, application logic

Transaction ID Columns (xmin/xmax):

  • xmin usage: Identify when row created, group rows by transaction, audit trail
  • xmax=0: Row version is current (not deleted/updated)
  • xmax!=0: Row marked for deletion (may be visible to old transactions)
  • MVCC visibility: PostgreSQL uses xmin/xmax to determine row visibility per transaction
  • Wraparound: Transaction IDs wrap around at ~2 billion (PostgreSQL handles automatically)

Practical Applications:

  • Debugging: Understand physical storage, locate specific row versions, analyze update patterns
  • Administration: Identify bloat (dead tuples), monitor transaction IDs, analyze VACUUM needs
  • Concurrency analysis: Understand which transactions modified data, debug isolation issues
  • Deduplication: Find and remove duplicate rows using ctid when no PK exists
  • Inheritance queries: Determine which child table contains specific rows

Limitations:

  • Hidden by default: SELECT * doesn't show system columns (must request explicitly)
  • Unstable identifiers: ctid changes, transaction IDs wrap, not suitable for persistent references
  • Advanced feature: Most applications don't need system columns, use for DBA tasks

🎯 Example 1: CTID - Physical Row Location

-- Create table and insert data
CREATE TABLE customers_system (
    customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO customers_system (email, first_name, last_name) 
VALUES 
    ('john@example.com', 'John', 'Doe'),
    ('jane@example.com', 'Jane', 'Smith'),
    ('bob@example.com', 'Bob', 'Johnson');

-- Show CTID (physical location)
SELECT 
    ctid,
    customer_id,
    email,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name
FROM customers_system
ORDER BY ctid;

-- Update a row (creates new tuple at different location)
UPDATE customers_system SET email = 'john.doe@example.com' WHERE customer_id = 1;

-- Show CTID after update (note John's ctid changed)
SELECT 
    ctid,
    customer_id,
    email,
    first_name
FROM customers_system
ORDER BY ctid;
Result (before UPDATE):
 ctid  | customer_id |       email        |   full_name
-------+-------------+--------------------+--------------
 (0,1) |           1 | john@example.com   | John Doe
 (0,2) |           2 | jane@example.com   | Jane Smith
 (0,3) |           3 | bob@example.com    | Bob Johnson
(3 rows)
Result (after UPDATE):
 ctid  | customer_id |         email          | first_name
-------+-------------+------------------------+------------
 (0,2) |           2 | jane@example.com       | Jane
 (0,3) |           3 | bob@example.com        | Bob
 (0,4) |           1 | john.doe@example.com   | John
(3 rows)
Explanation: CTID shows physical storage location: (page, tuple). Initially rows at (0,1), (0,2), (0,3) - page 0, tuples 1-3. After UPDATE customer_id=1, new version created at (0,4) - old tuple (0,1) marked deleted, new tuple (0,4) contains updated data. CTID changed from (0,1) to (0,4) for John's record. customer_id remains 1 (stable identifier), but ctid changed (unstable). VACUUM would reclaim space from old tuple (0,1) and potentially reorder tuples. CTID useful for debugging ("which exact tuple has this data?") but never use as row identifier in application logic - use PRIMARY KEY instead. CTID format: (page_number, tuple_index), both zero-indexed except tuple starts at 1.

🎯 Example 2: XMIN/XMAX - Transaction Tracking

-- Create products table
CREATE TABLE products_xmin (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL
);

-- Insert products in one transaction
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO products_xmin (product_name, price) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99), ('Mouse', 29.99);
COMMIT;

-- Insert more products in different transaction
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO products_xmin (product_name, price) 
VALUES ('Keyboard', 89.99);
COMMIT;

-- View xmin (transaction IDs)
SELECT 
    xmin,
    xmax,
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price
FROM products_xmin
ORDER BY product_id;

-- Update a product (creates new version)
UPDATE products_xmin SET price = 1399.99 WHERE product_id = 1;

-- View xmin/xmax after update
SELECT 
    xmin,
    xmax,
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    CASE 
        WHEN xmax = 0 THEN 'Current'
        ELSE 'Deleted/Updated'
    END AS row_status
FROM products_xmin
ORDER BY product_id;

-- Check current transaction ID
SELECT txid_current() AS current_transaction_id;
Result (before UPDATE):
 xmin | xmax | product_id | product_name |  price
------+------+------------+--------------+---------
  850 |    0 |          1 | Laptop       | 1299.99
  850 |    0 |          2 | Mouse        |   29.99
  851 |    0 |          3 | Keyboard     |   89.99
(3 rows)
Result (after UPDATE):
 xmin | xmax | product_id | product_name |  price   | row_status
------+------+------------+--------------+----------+------------
  852 |    0 |          1 | Laptop       |  1399.99 | Current
  850 |    0 |          2 | Mouse        |    29.99 | Current
  851 |    0 |          3 | Keyboard     |    89.99 | Current
(3 rows)
Explanation: xmin shows transaction ID that created row version: Laptop and Mouse both have xmin=850 (created in same transaction), Keyboard has xmin=851 (created in next transaction). xmax=0 means row version is current (not deleted/updated). After UPDATE, Laptop has new xmin=852 (new version created by transaction 852), old version marked xmax=852 (deleted by transaction 852) but invisible in current query (MVCC hides old versions). xmin increments with each transaction - even reads start transactions in some isolation levels. Use cases: audit which transaction modified data, identify rows created in bulk import (same xmin), debug concurrent update issues. Old row versions (xmax != 0) reclaimed by VACUUM. Transaction IDs wrap around (~2 billion), PostgreSQL prevents conflicts via autovacuum.

🎯 Example 3: TABLEOID - Table Identification in Inheritance

-- Parent table for employees
CREATE TABLE employees_base (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    hire_date DATE NOT NULL
);

-- Child tables for specific employee types
CREATE TABLE engineers (
    programming_language VARCHAR(50)
) INHERITS (employees_base);

CREATE TABLE salespeople (
    sales_region VARCHAR(50)
) INHERITS (employees_base);

-- Insert data into child tables
INSERT INTO engineers (first_name, last_name, hire_date, programming_language) 
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', '2025-01-15', 'Python'), ('Jane', 'Smith', '2025-02-01', 'Java');

INSERT INTO salespeople (first_name, last_name, hire_date, sales_region) 
VALUES ('Bob', 'Johnson', '2025-03-01', 'West Coast'), ('Alice', 'Williams', '2025-04-01', 'East Coast');

-- Query all employees (parent and children)
-- tableoid identifies which table each row came from
SELECT 
    tableoid::regclass AS source_table,
    employee_id,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS employee_name,
    hire_date
FROM employees_base
ORDER BY hire_date;

-- Count employees by table
SELECT 
    tableoid::regclass AS source_table,
    COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees_base
GROUP BY tableoid
ORDER BY source_table;
Result (all employees):
 source_table | employee_id | employee_name  | hire_date
--------------+-------------+----------------+------------
 engineers    |           1 | John Doe       | 2025-01-15
 engineers    |           2 | Jane Smith     | 2025-02-01
 salespeople  |           1 | Bob Johnson    | 2025-03-01
 salespeople  |           2 | Alice Williams | 2025-04-01
(4 rows)
Result (count by table):
 source_table | employee_count
--------------+----------------
 engineers    |              2
 salespeople  |              2
(2 rows)
Explanation: tableoid identifies which table row physically resides in - essential with inheritance. Query on employees_base returns rows from all child tables (engineers, salespeople). tableoid::regclass converts OID to human-readable table name: 'engineers', 'salespeople'. Without tableoid, can't distinguish which child table row came from (employee_id=1 appears in both tables). Use cases: inheritance hierarchies, partitioned tables (old style), identifying data source in merged results. Note: engineers.employee_id and salespeople.employee_id are independent sequences (both have employee_id=1, 2). Modern PostgreSQL prefers declarative partitioning over inheritance, but tableoid still useful for legacy designs and certain advanced patterns.

🎯 Example 4: Finding and Removing Duplicates Using CTID

-- Table with duplicate data (no primary key for demonstration)
CREATE TABLE products_duplicates (
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    price NUMERIC(10,2)
);

-- Insert duplicates
INSERT INTO products_duplicates (product_name, price) 
VALUES 
    ('Laptop', 1299.99),
    ('Laptop', 1299.99),
    ('Laptop', 1299.99),
    ('Mouse', 29.99),
    ('Mouse', 29.99),
    ('Keyboard', 89.99);

-- Identify duplicates with row numbers
SELECT 
    ctid,
    product_name,
    price,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY product_name, price ORDER BY ctid) AS row_num
FROM products_duplicates
ORDER BY product_name, ctid;

-- Delete duplicates, keeping only first occurrence (lowest ctid)
DELETE FROM products_duplicates
WHERE ctid IN (
    SELECT ctid
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            ctid,
            ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY product_name, price ORDER BY ctid) AS row_num
        FROM products_duplicates
    ) ranked
    WHERE row_num > 1
);

-- Verify duplicates removed
SELECT 
    ctid,
    product_name,
    price
FROM products_duplicates
ORDER BY product_name;
Result (before DELETE - with duplicates):
 ctid  | product_name |  price  | row_num
-------+--------------+---------+---------
 (0,1) | Laptop       | 1299.99 |       1
 (0,2) | Laptop       | 1299.99 |       2
 (0,3) | Laptop       | 1299.99 |       3
 (0,4) | Mouse        |   29.99 |       1
 (0,5) | Mouse        |   29.99 |       2
 (0,6) | Keyboard     |   89.99 |       1
(6 rows)
Result (after DELETE - duplicates removed):
 ctid  | product_name |  price
-------+--------------+---------
 (0,1) | Laptop       | 1299.99
 (0,4) | Mouse        |   29.99
 (0,6) | Keyboard     |   89.99
(3 rows)
Explanation: CTID enables duplicate removal when no PRIMARY KEY exists. ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY product_name, price ORDER BY ctid) assigns row numbers within duplicates: first Laptop gets row_num=1, duplicates get 2, 3. DELETE WHERE row_num > 1 removes duplicates, keeping first occurrence (lowest ctid). After deletion: one Laptop (0,1), one Mouse (0,4), one Keyboard (0,6). Why ctid needed? Without unique identifier, can't distinguish between identical rows to delete specific ones. Alternative: add PRIMARY KEY first (ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY), then deduplicate by id. CTID temporary solution for tables lacking primary keys - always add proper PRIMARY KEY for permanent row identification.

🎯 Example 5: System Columns for Debugging and Administration

-- Create orders table
CREATE TABLE orders_debug (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_number VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    total_amount NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending'
);

-- Insert orders
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO orders_debug (order_number, customer_id, total_amount, status) 
VALUES ('ORD-001', 1001, 299.99, 'pending'), ('ORD-002', 1002, 599.99, 'pending');
COMMIT;

-- Update order status
BEGIN;
UPDATE orders_debug SET status = 'shipped' WHERE order_id = 1;
COMMIT;

-- Comprehensive system columns view
SELECT 
    ctid,
    tableoid::regclass AS table_name,
    xmin AS created_txid,
    xmax AS deleted_txid,
    CASE WHEN xmax = 0 THEN 'Active' ELSE 'Deleted' END AS row_status,
    order_id,
    order_number,
    status,
    total_amount
FROM orders_debug
ORDER BY order_id;

-- Find recently created orders (last 1000 transactions)
SELECT 
    xmin AS transaction_id,
    order_id,
    order_number,
    status,
    txid_current() - xmin AS txns_ago
FROM orders_debug
WHERE xmin > txid_current() - 1000
ORDER BY xmin DESC;

-- Administrative query: check for bloat (dead tuples)
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE xmax = 0) AS live_rows,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE xmax != 0) AS dead_rows,
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows
FROM orders_debug;
Result (system columns view):
 ctid  | table_name  | created_txid | deleted_txid | row_status | order_id | order_number | status  | total_amount
-------+-------------+--------------+--------------+------------+----------+--------------+---------+--------------
 (0,3) | orders_debug|          905 |            0 | Active     |        1 | ORD-001      | shipped |       299.99
 (0,2) | orders_debug|          904 |            0 | Active     |        2 | ORD-002      | pending |       599.99
(2 rows)
Result (bloat check):
 live_rows | dead_rows | total_rows
-----------+-----------+------------
         2 |         0 |          2
(1 row)
Explanation: System columns provide administrative insights: created_txid (xmin) shows when row created (transaction 904, 905), deleted_txid (xmax) indicates if deleted (0 = active). Order 1 has ctid=(0,3) despite order_id=1 because UPDATE moved it (was at (0,1), now at (0,3)). txid_current() - xmin calculates "transactions ago" - order created 904, current transaction 907, diff = 3 transactions ago. Bloat check: live_rows (xmax=0) vs dead_rows (xmax!=0) indicates VACUUM needs - dead rows consume space. Use cases: debug concurrency issues (which transaction modified what), identify update hotspots (high xmin values), monitor transaction ID wraparound (xmin approaching 2 billion), analyze storage efficiency (dead tuple ratio). System columns expose PostgreSQL internals for DBA tasks, rarely needed in application logic.

5.7 Modifying Tables

📖 Overview

Modifying Tables through ALTER TABLE statements enables schema evolution after table creation - adding/removing columns, changing data types, adding/dropping constraints, and renaming elements. Schema changes are essential as application requirements evolve: new features need additional columns, business rules require new constraints, performance optimization demands data type changes. PostgreSQL supports most schema modifications without recreating tables, though some operations require table rewrites (changing column types, adding NOT NULL to columns with NULLs).

ALTER TABLE syntax: ALTER TABLE table_name action [, action ...]; where actions include ADD COLUMN, DROP COLUMN, ALTER COLUMN, ADD CONSTRAINT, DROP CONSTRAINT, RENAME. Multiple actions can be combined in single statement: ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMN phone VARCHAR(20), ADD CONSTRAINT chk_email CHECK (email LIKE '%@%');. PostgreSQL executes schema changes within transactions - changes rolled back if error occurs, enabling safe schema migrations. Consider existing data when modifying schemas: adding NOT NULL requires default value or backfilling data first.

5.7.1 Adding a Column

📖 Full Description

Adding a Column extends table structure with new fields using ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN syntax. New columns added to existing tables can include data types, constraints (NOT NULL, CHECK, DEFAULT), and generated expressions. When adding columns to tables with existing data, consider: columns without DEFAULT are NULL in existing rows (unless NOT NULL with DEFAULT specified), adding NOT NULL column requires DEFAULT or data backfill, indexes on new columns don't affect existing data but impact future inserts.

Column additions in PostgreSQL 11+ are instant operations if: column has DEFAULT value (stored in catalog, not row data), column nullable (no constraint checks on existing rows), no volatile DEFAULT expressions. For older PostgreSQL or NOT NULL without DEFAULT, table rewrite occurs - expensive for large tables. Best practice: add column with DEFAULT in separate transaction from data population, consider adding nullable first then backfilling and adding NOT NULL in stages for large tables.

Multiple columns can be added in single ALTER TABLE statement, executed as single operation: ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN color VARCHAR(20), ADD COLUMN size VARCHAR(10);. New columns appear at end of table structure (after existing columns in SELECT *). Generated columns (GENERATED ALWAYS AS ... STORED) can be added, automatically computed from existing columns. Adding columns with foreign keys requires referenced table/column exists. Use IF NOT EXISTS to make additions idempotent (safe to run multiple times).

Add Column Syntax
-- Basic ADD COLUMN
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column_name data_type;

-- Add column with DEFAULT
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column_name data_type DEFAULT value;

-- Add column with constraints
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column_name data_type NOT NULL DEFAULT value;

-- Add column with CHECK constraint
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column_name data_type CHECK (condition);

-- Add generated column
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column_name data_type GENERATED ALWAYS AS (expression) STORED;

-- Add column IF NOT EXISTS (PostgreSQL 9.6+)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS column_name data_type;

-- Add multiple columns
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column1 data_type,
ADD COLUMN column2 data_type;

-- Add column with foreign key
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD COLUMN column_name data_type REFERENCES other_table(column);

-- Common patterns:

-- Add nullable column (NULL in existing rows)
ALTER TABLE customers 
ADD COLUMN phone VARCHAR(20);

-- Add column with default (value in existing rows)
ALTER TABLE orders 
ADD COLUMN priority INTEGER DEFAULT 5;

-- Add NOT NULL column (requires DEFAULT)
ALTER TABLE products 
ADD COLUMN is_available BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT true;

-- Add timestamp tracking
ALTER TABLE items 
ADD COLUMN updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;

Adding Column Considerations

Nullability and Defaults:

  • Nullable (no DEFAULT): Existing rows get NULL, new rows require value or get NULL
  • With DEFAULT: Existing rows get default value, new rows get default if not specified
  • NOT NULL requires DEFAULT: Can't add NOT NULL without DEFAULT to table with data
  • Backfill strategy: Add nullable, UPDATE existing rows, then SET NOT NULL

Performance:

  • Fast operation (PG 11+): DEFAULT values stored in catalog, no table rewrite
  • Slow operation: NOT NULL without DEFAULT, volatile DEFAULT (RANDOM()), large tables
  • Lock behavior: ALTER TABLE acquires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, blocks reads/writes
  • Large table strategy: Add nullable, backfill in batches, add NOT NULL later

Data Type Considerations:

  • Storage: BIGINT vs INTEGER, VARCHAR vs TEXT, NUMERIC precision
  • Generated columns: Computed from existing columns, automatically populated
  • Arrays/JSON: Complex types supported, allow storing structured data

🎯 Example 1: Adding Simple Columns

-- Create initial customers table
CREATE TABLE customers_alter (
    customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO customers_alter (email, first_name, last_name) 
VALUES ('john@example.com', 'John', 'Doe'), ('jane@example.com', 'Jane', 'Smith');

-- Add nullable column (NULL in existing rows)
ALTER TABLE customers_alter 
ADD COLUMN phone VARCHAR(20);

-- Add column with default (value in existing rows)
ALTER TABLE customers_alter 
ADD COLUMN status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'active';

-- Add column with NOT NULL and default
ALTER TABLE customers_alter 
ADD COLUMN created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;

-- View results
SELECT * FROM customers_alter;
Result:
 customer_id |       email        | first_name | last_name | phone | status |        created_at
-------------+--------------------+------------+-----------+-------+--------+---------------------------
           1 | john@example.com   | John       | Doe       | NULL  | active | 2026-01-25 16:30:00
           2 | jane@example.com   | Jane       | Smith     | NULL  | active | 2026-01-25 16:30:00
(2 rows)
Explanation: Added three columns to existing table with data: phone (nullable, NULL in existing rows), status (DEFAULT 'active', existing rows get 'active'), created_at (NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, existing rows get current timestamp). New columns appear at end of table structure. Future inserts: phone can be NULL or specified, status defaults to 'active' if not specified, created_at defaults to insert timestamp. Existing rows immediately have status='active' and created_at values. phone=NULL because no default specified. Pattern: nullable for optional data, DEFAULT for required fields with sensible defaults, NOT NULL DEFAULT for mandatory audit fields.

🎯 Example 2: Adding Columns with Constraints

-- Create products table
CREATE TABLE products_alter (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO products_alter (product_name, price) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99), ('Mouse', 29.99);

-- Add column with CHECK constraint
ALTER TABLE products_alter 
ADD COLUMN discount_percent NUMERIC(5,2) DEFAULT 0 
CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 100);

-- Add column with UNIQUE constraint
ALTER TABLE products_alter 
ADD COLUMN sku VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE;

-- Add boolean column with default
ALTER TABLE products_alter 
ADD COLUMN is_featured BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false;

-- Add column with CHECK and DEFAULT
ALTER TABLE products_alter 
ADD COLUMN stock_quantity INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 
CHECK (stock_quantity >= 0);

-- View structure
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    discount_percent,
    sku,
    is_featured,
    stock_quantity
FROM products_alter;
Result:
 product_id | product_name |  price  | discount_percent | sku  | is_featured | stock_quantity
------------+--------------+---------+------------------+------+-------------+----------------
          1 | Laptop       | 1299.99 |             0.00 | NULL |      false  |              0
          2 | Mouse        |   29.99 |             0.00 | NULL |      false  |              0
(2 rows)
Explanation: Added columns with various constraints: discount_percent has CHECK (0-100) and DEFAULT 0, sku has UNIQUE constraint (NULL allowed, can be filled later), is_featured is NOT NULL boolean DEFAULT false, stock_quantity has CHECK (>= 0) and DEFAULT 0. All existing rows get default values: discount_percent=0, is_featured=false, stock_quantity=0. sku=NULL (no default, nullable). Constraints enforced immediately on existing data (all rows pass checks). Future inserts validated against constraints: discount_percent must be 0-100, sku must be unique, stock_quantity cannot be negative. Pattern useful for: phased rollout (add nullable, backfill, enforce), backward compatibility (defaults allow existing code to work).

🎯 Example 3: Adding Generated Columns

-- Orders table
CREATE TABLE orders_generated (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    subtotal NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    tax_rate NUMERIC(5,4) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0.0800
);

INSERT INTO orders_generated (subtotal, tax_rate) 
VALUES (100.00, 0.0800), (250.00, 0.0900);

-- Add generated column (computed from existing columns)
ALTER TABLE orders_generated 
ADD COLUMN tax_amount NUMERIC(10,2) 
GENERATED ALWAYS AS (subtotal * tax_rate) STORED;

-- Add another generated column
ALTER TABLE orders_generated 
ADD COLUMN total_amount NUMERIC(10,2) 
GENERATED ALWAYS AS (subtotal + (subtotal * tax_rate)) STORED;

-- View computed values
SELECT 
    order_id,
    subtotal,
    tax_rate,
    tax_amount,
    total_amount
FROM orders_generated;
Result:
 order_id | subtotal | tax_rate | tax_amount | total_amount
----------+----------+----------+------------+--------------
        1 |   100.00 |   0.0800 |       8.00 |       108.00
        2 |   250.00 |   0.0900 |      22.50 |       272.50
(2 rows)
Explanation: Added generated columns tax_amount and total_amount computed from existing columns subtotal and tax_rate. GENERATED ALWAYS AS expression STORED creates computed column - values automatically calculated on INSERT/UPDATE. Existing rows immediately have computed values: order 1 tax_amount = 100.00 * 0.0800 = 8.00, total_amount = 108.00. Future updates: changing subtotal or tax_rate recalculates tax_amount and total_amount automatically. Generated columns: always consistent (no manual calculation errors), can be indexed for queries, stored physically (STORED - PostgreSQL doesn't support VIRTUAL yet). Use cases: derived values (full_name from first+last), calculations (total from quantity*price), transformations (search_vector from text).

🎯 Example 4: Staged Column Addition for Large Tables

-- Simulate large table
CREATE TABLE large_table (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);

-- Insert sample data
INSERT INTO large_table (name) 
SELECT 'Record ' || generate_series 
FROM generate_series(1, 100);

-- STAGE 1: Add nullable column (fast, no data changes)
ALTER TABLE large_table 
ADD COLUMN category VARCHAR(50);

-- STAGE 2: Backfill in batches (for large tables, do in chunks)
-- Batch 1
UPDATE large_table 
SET category = 'Type A' 
WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 50;

-- Batch 2
UPDATE large_table 
SET category = 'Type B' 
WHERE id BETWEEN 51 AND 100;

-- STAGE 3: Add NOT NULL constraint after backfill
ALTER TABLE large_table 
ALTER COLUMN category SET NOT NULL;

-- STAGE 4: Add default for future rows
ALTER TABLE large_table 
ALTER COLUMN category SET DEFAULT 'Type A';

-- Verify
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) as total_rows,
    COUNT(category) as non_null_category,
    COUNT(DISTINCT category) as distinct_categories
FROM large_table;
Result:
 total_rows | non_null_category | distinct_categories
------------+-------------------+---------------------
        100 |               100 |                   2
(1 row)
Explanation: Staged approach for adding column to large table: STAGE 1 adds nullable column (instant, no table rewrite), STAGE 2 backfills data in batches (reduces lock time, allows progress monitoring), STAGE 3 enforces NOT NULL after all data populated (validates existing data), STAGE 4 sets DEFAULT for future inserts. This avoids long-running ALTER TABLE that locks table for entire backfill. For production: backfill in smaller batches (UPDATE ... WHERE id BETWEEN x AND y) with COMMIT between batches, monitor progress, tune batch size based on lock contention. Alternative: add column NOT NULL DEFAULT value (PG 11+ instant if simple default), but staged approach better for: complex defaults, conditional values, very large tables where any lock duration problematic.

🎯 Example 5: Adding Columns with Foreign Keys

-- Parent tables
CREATE TABLE categories_fk (
    category_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    category_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE products_fk_add (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL
);

-- Insert initial data
INSERT INTO categories_fk (category_name) 
VALUES ('Electronics'), ('Clothing');

INSERT INTO products_fk_add (product_name, price) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99), ('T-Shirt', 29.99);

-- Add foreign key column (nullable)
ALTER TABLE products_fk_add 
ADD COLUMN category_id INTEGER REFERENCES categories_fk(category_id);

-- Backfill foreign keys
UPDATE products_fk_add SET category_id = 1 WHERE product_name = 'Laptop';
UPDATE products_fk_add SET category_id = 2 WHERE product_name = 'T-Shirt';

-- Add NOT NULL after backfill
ALTER TABLE products_fk_add 
ALTER COLUMN category_id SET NOT NULL;

-- Add cascading column with foreign key
ALTER TABLE products_fk_add 
ADD COLUMN supplier_id INTEGER REFERENCES suppliers(supplier_id) ON DELETE SET NULL;

-- View relationships
SELECT 
    p.product_id,
    p.product_name,
    c.category_name,
    p.category_id
FROM products_fk_add p
LEFT JOIN categories_fk c ON p.category_id = c.category_id;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | category_name | category_id
------------+--------------+---------------+-------------
          1 | Laptop       | Electronics   |           1
          2 | T-Shirt      | Clothing      |           2
(2 rows)
Explanation: Added foreign key column in stages: STEP 1 add nullable FK column (allows NULL temporarily while backfilling), STEP 2 backfill category_id with valid references, STEP 3 enforce NOT NULL after all rows have valid FKs. Pattern prevents: adding NOT NULL FK column to table with data (would fail - no values to reference), forcing immediate backfill during ALTER TABLE (long lock). Foreign key constraint validates referenced table exists and references valid primary key. Can add FK column with ON DELETE/UPDATE actions: SET NULL (makes optional relationship), CASCADE (delete dependent rows), RESTRICT (prevent deletion). Note: CREATE TABLE suppliers must exist before referencing in foreign key, or use ADD CONSTRAINT separately.

5.7.2 Removing a Column

📖 Full Description

Removing a Column permanently deletes a column and all its data using ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN. Column removal is irreversible - data cannot be recovered without backup. PostgreSQL doesn't immediately reclaim disk space when dropping columns; the space is reused for new data or reclaimed by VACUUM FULL (which rewrites the entire table). By default, dropping columns fails if other objects depend on them (views, indexes, constraints, foreign keys) unless CASCADE option forces removal of dependent objects.

DROP COLUMN operations acquire ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, blocking all table access during execution. On small-to-medium tables this is fast (milliseconds), but large tables may experience brief unavailability. Best practice: drop columns during maintenance windows, use transactions to enable rollback, test on staging database first. Dropped columns don't immediately disappear from physical storage - column marked as dropped but storage remains until table rewrite. SELECT * queries won't show dropped columns, but table size unchanged until VACUUM FULL.

Safety features: RESTRICT (default) prevents dropping columns with dependencies, CASCADE drops dependent objects automatically (use cautiously - can cascade to views, triggers, functions). IF EXISTS prevents errors when column doesn't exist (idempotent migrations). Multiple columns can be dropped in single statement: ALTER TABLE table_name DROP COLUMN col1, DROP COLUMN col2;. Important: back up data before dropping columns, verify no application code references column, check for dependent database objects (views, indexes, triggers, constraints).

Drop Column Syntax
-- Basic DROP COLUMN
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP COLUMN column_name;

-- Drop column IF EXISTS (no error if doesn't exist)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS column_name;

-- Drop column with RESTRICT (default - fail if dependencies)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP COLUMN column_name RESTRICT;

-- Drop column with CASCADE (drop dependent objects)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP COLUMN column_name CASCADE;

-- Drop multiple columns
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP COLUMN column1,
DROP COLUMN column2;

-- Common scenarios:

-- Safe drop (no dependencies)
ALTER TABLE customers 
DROP COLUMN middle_name;

-- Drop with existence check
ALTER TABLE products 
DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS deprecated_field;

-- Drop column and its dependencies
ALTER TABLE employees 
DROP COLUMN salary CASCADE;  -- Drops views/indexes using salary

-- Drop multiple columns at once
ALTER TABLE orders 
DROP COLUMN temp_field,
DROP COLUMN old_status;

-- Verify column dropped
SELECT column_name, data_type 
FROM information_schema.columns 
WHERE table_name = 'products';

Dropping Column Considerations

Safety and Dependencies:

  • RESTRICT (default): Fails if views, indexes, constraints, or FKs depend on column
  • CASCADE: Automatically drops dependent objects - use with caution!
  • Check dependencies first: Query information_schema for dependent objects
  • IF EXISTS: Prevents errors in idempotent migrations (safe to run multiple times)
  • Irreversible: Data permanently deleted, recovery requires backup restore

Performance and Storage:

  • Fast operation: Column marked dropped in catalog, no immediate table rewrite
  • Space not reclaimed: Disk space unchanged until VACUUM FULL or table rewrite
  • ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock: Blocks all table access briefly during operation
  • Dropped column storage: Physical column data remains until rewrite

Best Practices:

  • Backup first: Always backup before dropping columns with important data
  • Check application code: Verify no queries reference column before dropping
  • Staged approach: Remove application references first, then drop column in later deployment
  • Test on staging: Verify no hidden dependencies before production drop
  • Use transactions: Wrap in BEGIN/COMMIT to enable rollback if issues arise

Dependencies to Check:

  • Views: Queries using column will break
  • Indexes: Indexes on column automatically dropped
  • Constraints: CHECK constraints referencing column dropped
  • Foreign keys: FK constraints on column dropped
  • Triggers/Functions: Code referencing column may break

🎯 Example 1: Simple Column Removal

-- Create table with extra columns
CREATE TABLE products_drop (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    old_price NUMERIC(10,2),  -- Deprecated column
    current_price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    temp_notes TEXT  -- Temporary field
);

INSERT INTO products_drop (product_name, old_price, current_price, temp_notes) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1199.99, 1299.99, 'Temporary note'), ('Mouse', 19.99, 29.99, 'Another note');

-- Show current structure
SELECT * FROM products_drop;

-- Drop deprecated column
ALTER TABLE products_drop 
DROP COLUMN old_price;

-- Drop temporary column
ALTER TABLE products_drop 
DROP COLUMN temp_notes;

-- Verify columns dropped
SELECT * FROM products_drop;
Result (before DROP):
 product_id | product_name | old_price | current_price |   temp_notes
------------+--------------+-----------+---------------+-----------------
          1 | Laptop       |   1199.99 |       1299.99 | Temporary note
          2 | Mouse        |     19.99 |         29.99 | Another note
(2 rows)
Result (after DROP):
 product_id | product_name | current_price
------------+--------------+---------------
          1 | Laptop       |       1299.99
          2 | Mouse        |         29.99
(2 rows)
Explanation: DROP COLUMN removes old_price and temp_notes permanently - data deleted, columns no longer appear in SELECT *. Data lost: old prices (1199.99, 19.99) and notes gone forever unless backup exists. Table now has 3 columns instead of 5. No dependencies on dropped columns, so RESTRICT (default) succeeded. Disk space unchanged - physical column data still in table pages until VACUUM FULL or table rewrite. Future inserts: only 3 columns (product_id, product_name, current_price). Use case: remove deprecated fields, clean up temporary columns, simplify schema. Always verify with SELECT * before dropping to confirm correct columns targeted.

🎯 Example 2: DROP COLUMN with Dependencies - RESTRICT vs CASCADE

-- Create table with indexed column
CREATE TABLE employees_drop (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    salary NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    bonus NUMERIC(10,2)
);

-- Create index on salary
CREATE INDEX idx_employees_salary ON employees_drop(salary);

-- Create view using salary
CREATE VIEW high_earners AS 
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, salary 
FROM employees_drop 
WHERE salary > 100000;

INSERT INTO employees_drop (first_name, last_name, salary, bonus) 
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', 120000, 10000), ('Jane', 'Smith', 80000, 5000);

-- Try to drop salary with RESTRICT (will fail - view depends on it)
-- ALTER TABLE employees_drop DROP COLUMN salary RESTRICT;
-- ERROR: cannot drop column salary of table employees_drop because other objects depend on it
-- DETAIL: view high_earners depends on column salary of table employees_drop
-- HINT: Use DROP ... CASCADE to drop the dependent objects too

-- Drop salary with CASCADE (removes dependent view and index)
ALTER TABLE employees_drop DROP COLUMN salary CASCADE;

-- Check view no longer exists
-- SELECT * FROM high_earners;
-- ERROR: relation "high_earners" does not exist

-- Drop bonus (no dependencies, RESTRICT works)
ALTER TABLE employees_drop DROP COLUMN bonus;

-- View final table
SELECT * FROM employees_drop;
Result:
 employee_id | first_name | last_name
-------------+------------+-----------
           1 | John       | Doe
           2 | Jane       | Smith
(2 rows)
Explanation: RESTRICT (default) prevents dropping columns with dependencies - salary drop failed because view high_earners uses it. CASCADE forces drop and removes all dependent objects: drops salary column, drops idx_employees_salary index, drops high_earners view. CASCADE powerful but dangerous - can cascade unexpectedly far (view depends on column, function depends on view, etc.). Best practice: check dependencies before dropping (query information_schema.view_column_usage), drop dependent objects manually first (gives control), use CASCADE only when dependencies understood and intentional. bonus drop succeeded with RESTRICT (no dependencies). Always verify CASCADE won't drop critical objects before executing.

🎯 Example 3: Conditional Column Removal with IF EXISTS

-- Migration script demonstrating idempotent drops
CREATE TABLE products_idempotent (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    old_sku VARCHAR(20),
    new_sku VARCHAR(20),
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO products_idempotent (product_name, old_sku, new_sku, price) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 'OLD-001', 'NEW-001', 1299.99);

-- Idempotent migration: safe to run multiple times
ALTER TABLE products_idempotent 
DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS old_sku;

-- Run again (no error even though column gone)
ALTER TABLE products_idempotent 
DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS old_sku;

-- Drop multiple columns with IF EXISTS
ALTER TABLE products_idempotent 
DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS temp_field,
DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS deprecated_field;

SELECT * FROM products_idempotent;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | new_sku |  price
------------+--------------+---------+---------
          1 | Laptop       | NEW-001 | 1299.99
(1 row)
Explanation: IF EXISTS makes DROP COLUMN idempotent - safe to run multiple times without errors. First execution drops old_sku, second execution does nothing (no error). Critical for migration scripts that might run multiple times: development → staging → production, or rollback-and-retry scenarios. Without IF EXISTS, second run would error "column does not exist". temp_field and deprecated_field drops succeed silently whether columns exist or not. Pattern for safe migrations: all schema changes use IF EXISTS/IF NOT EXISTS, enabling reruns without manually tracking which changes applied. Combine with transactions: BEGIN; ALTER TABLE...; COMMIT; enables rollback if any step fails.

🎯 Example 4: Dropping Columns Referenced by Foreign Keys

-- Parent and child tables with FK relationship
CREATE TABLE categories_drop (
    category_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    category_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE products_fk_drop (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    category_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES categories_drop(category_id),
    old_category_id INTEGER REFERENCES categories_drop(category_id)
);

INSERT INTO categories_drop (category_name) VALUES ('Electronics');
INSERT INTO products_fk_drop (product_name, category_id, old_category_id) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1, NULL);

-- Try dropping column referenced by FK (will fail)
-- ALTER TABLE products_fk_drop DROP COLUMN category_id;
-- ERROR: cannot drop column category_id because other objects depend on it
-- DETAIL: constraint products_fk_drop_category_id_fkey on table products_fk_drop depends on column category_id
-- HINT: Use DROP ... CASCADE

-- Drop with CASCADE (removes FK constraint)
ALTER TABLE products_fk_drop DROP COLUMN old_category_id CASCADE;

-- Drop category_id with CASCADE (removes FK constraint)
ALTER TABLE products_fk_drop DROP COLUMN category_id CASCADE;

SELECT * FROM products_fk_drop;
Result:
 product_id | product_name
------------+--------------
          1 | Laptop
(1 row)
Explanation: Foreign key constraints prevent dropping referenced columns - category_id drop failed due to FK constraint. CASCADE drops column and its FK constraint: old_category_id dropped along with products_fk_drop_old_category_id_fkey constraint. After both FKs dropped, products_fk_drop no longer references categories_drop. Pattern: CASCADE drops FK constraints on dropped column, but doesn't drop referenced table (categories_drop still exists). Alternative: drop FK constraint manually first (ALTER TABLE DROP CONSTRAINT), then drop column with RESTRICT (safer). Dropping primary key column referenced by FKs: requires CASCADE (drops all incoming FKs from other tables) or drop FKs first.

🎯 Example 5: Safe Column Removal Process

-- Production-safe column removal workflow
CREATE TABLE orders_safe_drop (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_number VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    legacy_status VARCHAR(20),  -- To be removed
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,  -- New status field
    total_amount NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO orders_safe_drop (order_number, customer_id, legacy_status, status, total_amount) 
VALUES ('ORD-001', 1001, 'open', 'pending', 299.99), ('ORD-002', 1002, 'closed', 'shipped', 599.99);

-- STEP 1: Verify column can be safely dropped (check dependencies)
SELECT 
    column_name,
    data_type,
    is_nullable
FROM information_schema.columns
WHERE table_name = 'orders_safe_drop' AND column_name = 'legacy_status';

-- STEP 2: Backup data if needed (save legacy values)
CREATE TEMP TABLE legacy_status_backup AS 
SELECT order_id, legacy_status FROM orders_safe_drop;

-- STEP 3: Test drop in transaction (can rollback if issues)
BEGIN;
ALTER TABLE orders_safe_drop DROP COLUMN legacy_status;

-- Verify table works without column
SELECT order_id, order_number, status FROM orders_safe_drop;

-- If satisfied, commit; otherwise ROLLBACK
COMMIT;

-- STEP 4: Verify final structure
SELECT 
    column_name,
    data_type 
FROM information_schema.columns 
WHERE table_name = 'orders_safe_drop'
ORDER BY ordinal_position;
Result (final structure):
  column_name  |     data_type
---------------+--------------------
 order_id      | integer
 order_number  | character varying
 customer_id   | integer
 status        | character varying
 total_amount  | numeric
(5 rows)
Explanation: Safe production column removal: STEP 1 verify column exists and check type (information_schema), STEP 2 backup data to temp table if might need later (legacy_status_backup), STEP 3 execute DROP in transaction (BEGIN...COMMIT) allowing rollback if issues discovered, STEP 4 verify final structure correct. Transaction enables testing: DROP column, run application tests, ROLLBACK if problems found. Backup pattern: CREATE TEMP TABLE preserves legacy data for recovery or auditing. After COMMIT, legacy_status permanently gone from orders_safe_drop but preserved in legacy_status_backup (temporary, exists until session ends). Production workflow: deploy code ignoring legacy_status → verify in staging → drop column in production → monitor for errors. Staged removal safer than immediate drop.

5.7.3 Adding a Constraint

📖 Full Description

Adding a Constraint enforces data integrity rules on existing tables using ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT. Constraints can be added after table creation to enforce: UNIQUE (no duplicates), CHECK (validation rules), NOT NULL (required values), PRIMARY KEY (unique identifier), FOREIGN KEY (referential integrity), EXCLUSION (advanced overlap prevention). When adding constraints to tables with existing data, PostgreSQL validates all existing rows - if any violate the constraint, the ADD CONSTRAINT operation fails.

Constraint addition requires scanning entire table to validate existing data - potentially slow on large tables with millions of rows. For production databases, consider: adding constraints during maintenance windows, using NOT VALID option (PostgreSQL 9.4+) to skip initial validation, then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT separately in background. NOT VALID useful for large tables: constraint enforced on new/updated rows immediately but existing rows validated asynchronously, reducing lock time.

Named constraints (CONSTRAINT name ...) provide better error messages and enable targeted removal later: DROP CONSTRAINT name. Unnamed constraints get auto-generated names (table_column_key, table_column_check) which are harder to identify. Multiple constraints can be added in single ALTER TABLE statement. Common pattern: add constraint with validation, catch errors identifying data quality issues, fix data, retry constraint addition. Some constraints create indexes automatically (UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, EXCLUSION), while others require manual indexes for performance (FOREIGN KEY columns should be indexed).

Add Constraint Syntax
-- Add UNIQUE constraint
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name UNIQUE (column);

-- Add CHECK constraint
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name CHECK (condition);

-- Add PRIMARY KEY constraint
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name PRIMARY KEY (column);

-- Add FOREIGN KEY constraint
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name 
FOREIGN KEY (column) REFERENCES other_table(column);

-- Add FOREIGN KEY with actions
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name 
FOREIGN KEY (column) REFERENCES other_table(column)
ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE;

-- Add EXCLUSION constraint
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name 
EXCLUDE USING gist (column WITH operator);

-- Add constraint with NOT VALID (skip initial validation)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name CHECK (condition) NOT VALID;

-- Validate constraint later
ALTER TABLE table_name 
VALIDATE CONSTRAINT constraint_name;

-- Add multiple constraints
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint1 UNIQUE (col1),
ADD CONSTRAINT constraint2 CHECK (col2 > 0);

-- Common patterns:

-- Email format validation
ALTER TABLE users 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_email_format 
CHECK (email ~* '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$');

-- Price range validation
ALTER TABLE products 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_price_positive 
CHECK (price > 0);

-- Foreign key with cascade
ALTER TABLE orders 
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer 
FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
ON DELETE RESTRICT;

Adding Constraint Considerations

Validation and Data:

  • Existing data checked: Constraint addition scans all rows, fails if any violate rule
  • Clean data first: Fix violations before adding constraint (UPDATE statements)
  • NOT VALID option: Skip initial validation, enforce on new data only
  • VALIDATE CONSTRAINT: Validate existing data later without blocking writes
  • Error messages: Failed constraint additions show violating rows

Performance:

  • Table scan required: Validates all existing rows, slow on large tables
  • ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock: Blocks all access during validation
  • Index creation: UNIQUE/PK/EXCLUSION create indexes (additional time)
  • Large table strategy: Use NOT VALID + VALIDATE later

Constraint Types:

  • CHECK: Validates column values against boolean expression
  • UNIQUE: Prevents duplicate values, creates unique index
  • PRIMARY KEY: UNIQUE + NOT NULL, one per table
  • FOREIGN KEY: References another table's PK/UNIQUE, enforces referential integrity
  • EXCLUSION: Advanced overlaps prevention using operators

Naming and Management:

  • Named constraints: CONSTRAINT name ... enables easy DROP CONSTRAINT name
  • Naming convention: chk_table_column, fk_table_ref, unique_table_column
  • Auto-generated names: Unnamed constraints get system-generated names
  • Query constraints: information_schema.table_constraints lists all constraints

🎯 Example 1: Adding CHECK Constraints to Existing Data

-- Table with some invalid data
CREATE TABLE products_check_add (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    discount_percent NUMERIC(5,2),
    stock_quantity INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO products_check_add (product_name, price, discount_percent, stock_quantity) 
VALUES 
    ('Laptop', 1299.99, 10, 50),
    ('Mouse', 29.99, 120, 100),  -- Invalid: discount > 100
    ('Keyboard', 89.99, 15, -5);  -- Invalid: stock negative

-- Try adding CHECK constraint (will fail due to violations)
-- ALTER TABLE products_check_add 
-- ADD CONSTRAINT chk_discount_percent CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 100);
-- ERROR: check constraint violated by some row

-- Fix invalid data first
UPDATE products_check_add SET discount_percent = 20 WHERE discount_percent > 100;
UPDATE products_check_add SET stock_quantity = 0 WHERE stock_quantity < 0;

-- Now add constraints successfully
ALTER TABLE products_check_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_discount_percent CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 100);

ALTER TABLE products_check_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_stock_non_negative CHECK (stock_quantity >= 0);

ALTER TABLE products_check_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_price_positive CHECK (price > 0);

-- Verify constraints active
SELECT * FROM products_check_add;

-- Try inserting invalid data (will fail)
-- INSERT INTO products_check_add (product_name, price, discount_percent, stock_quantity) 
-- VALUES ('Widget', 50.00, 150, 10);
-- ERROR: new row violates check constraint "chk_discount_percent"
Result:
 product_id | product_name |  price  | discount_percent | stock_quantity
------------+--------------+---------+------------------+----------------
          1 | Laptop       | 1299.99 |            10.00 |             50
          2 | Mouse        |   29.99 |            20.00 |            100
          3 | Keyboard     |   89.99 |            15.00 |              0
(3 rows)
Explanation: Initial ADD CONSTRAINT failed because existing data violated rule (discount=120 > 100, stock=-5 < 0). Fixed violations: UPDATE discount to 20, stock to 0. After cleanup, constraints added successfully. Constraints now enforce rules on all future operations: discount must be 0-100, stock must be >= 0, price must be > 0. Named constraints (chk_discount_percent) provide clear error messages when violations attempted. Pattern: analyze existing data → fix violations → add constraints. For large tables: identify violating rows with query matching constraint condition, batch update violations, then add constraint.

🎯 Example 2: Adding UNIQUE and PRIMARY KEY Constraints

-- Table without primary key
CREATE TABLE customers_pk_add (
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    first_name VARCHAR(50)
);

INSERT INTO customers_pk_add (customer_id, email, username, first_name) 
VALUES 
    (1, 'john@example.com', 'john_doe', 'John'),
    (2, 'jane@example.com', 'jane_smith', 'Jane');

-- Add PRIMARY KEY constraint
ALTER TABLE customers_pk_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT pk_customers PRIMARY KEY (customer_id);

-- Add UNIQUE constraints on email and username
ALTER TABLE customers_pk_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT unique_email UNIQUE (email);

ALTER TABLE customers_pk_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT unique_username UNIQUE (username);

-- Verify constraints created indexes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'customers_pk_add'
ORDER BY indexname;
Result (indexes created by constraints):
       indexname        |                          indexdef
------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------
 pk_customers           | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX pk_customers ON customers_pk_add USING btree (customer_id)
 unique_email           | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX unique_email ON customers_pk_add USING btree (email)
 unique_username        | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX unique_username ON customers_pk_add USING btree (username)
(3 rows)
Explanation: Added PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints to existing table - all create unique B-tree indexes automatically. pk_customers ensures customer_id unique and NOT NULL (PK requirement). unique_email and unique_username prevent duplicate emails/usernames. Indexes created by constraints: pk_customers (unique index on customer_id), unique_email (unique index on email), unique_username (unique index on username). Constraints enforce uniqueness + provide index for fast lookups. Can't add PK if: column contains NULLs (must be NOT NULL first), column has duplicates (must deduplicate first). After addition, customer_id becomes definitive row identifier, email and username must be unique across all rows.

🎯 Example 3: Adding FOREIGN KEY Constraints

-- Parent table
CREATE TABLE categories_fk_add (
    category_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    category_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

-- Child table without FK initially
CREATE TABLE products_fk_add (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    category_id INTEGER NOT NULL
);

-- Insert data
INSERT INTO categories_fk_add (category_name) 
VALUES ('Electronics'), ('Clothing');

INSERT INTO products_fk_add (product_name, category_id) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1), ('T-Shirt', 2);

-- Add foreign key constraint
ALTER TABLE products_fk_add 
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_product_category 
FOREIGN KEY (category_id) 
REFERENCES categories_fk_add(category_id)
ON DELETE RESTRICT
ON UPDATE CASCADE;

-- Create index on FK column for join performance
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category_id ON products_fk_add(category_id);

-- Verify FK working (try invalid insert)
-- INSERT INTO products_fk_add (product_name, category_id) 
-- VALUES ('Widget', 999);
-- ERROR: insert violates foreign key constraint "fk_product_category"
-- DETAIL: Key (category_id)=(999) is not present in table "categories_fk_add"

SELECT 
    p.product_name,
    c.category_name
FROM products_fk_add p
INNER JOIN categories_fk_add c ON p.category_id = c.category_id;
Result:
 product_name | category_name
--------------+---------------
 Laptop       | Electronics
 T-Shirt      | Clothing
(2 rows)
Explanation: Added FOREIGN KEY constraint to existing table - validates all existing category_id values reference valid categories. FK addition scanned all products_fk_add rows, verified category_id 1 and 2 exist in categories_fk_add. ON DELETE RESTRICT prevents deleting categories with products, ON UPDATE CASCADE propagates category_id changes. Created separate index on category_id for join performance (FK doesn't auto-index). FK enforces referential integrity: can't insert product with invalid category_id, can't delete category with products (unless CASCADE). Pattern: ensure data integrity before adding FK (all child rows reference valid parents), add FK, create index for query performance.

🎯 Example 4: Using NOT VALID for Large Tables

-- Simulate large table
CREATE TABLE orders_large (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_date DATE NOT NULL,
    total_amount NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL
);

-- Insert sample data
INSERT INTO orders_large (order_date, total_amount, status) 
SELECT 
    CURRENT_DATE - (random() * 365)::INTEGER,
    (random() * 1000 + 10)::NUMERIC(12,2),
    (ARRAY['pending', 'shipped', 'delivered'])[floor(random() * 3 + 1)::INT]
FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Add constraint with NOT VALID (doesn't validate existing rows)
ALTER TABLE orders_large 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_total_positive CHECK (total_amount > 0) NOT VALID;

-- Constraint active for new/updated rows but existing rows not validated yet
-- Insert with invalid value fails even though NOT VALID
-- INSERT INTO orders_large (order_date, total_amount, status) 
-- VALUES (CURRENT_DATE, -100, 'pending');
-- ERROR: new row violates check constraint "chk_total_positive"

-- Validate existing rows separately (can run during low-traffic period)
ALTER TABLE orders_large 
VALIDATE CONSTRAINT chk_total_positive;

-- Query constraint status
SELECT 
    conname AS constraint_name,
    convalidated AS is_validated
FROM pg_constraint
WHERE conname = 'chk_total_positive';
Result:
 constraint_name     | is_validated
---------------------+--------------
 chk_total_positive  | t
(1 row)
Explanation: NOT VALID adds constraint without validating existing rows - reduces lock time on large tables. Constraint immediately enforces on new INSERT/UPDATE but doesn't scan existing data. VALIDATE CONSTRAINT later scans table to verify existing rows comply (acquires lighter lock, doesn't block writes). Pattern for production: ADD CONSTRAINT NOT VALID during deployment (instant), VALIDATE CONSTRAINT during maintenance window (slower but non-blocking). is_validated=t after VALIDATE (was 'f' before). Benefits: reduced downtime (ADD CONSTRAINT instant), background validation (VALIDATE can run during low traffic), rollback safety (can DROP constraint before VALIDATE if issues found). Use for: large tables (millions of rows), tight maintenance windows, gradual constraint enforcement.

🎯 Example 5: Adding Multiple Constraints Atomically

-- Employee table needing multiple constraints
CREATE TABLE employees_multi_constraint (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    salary NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    hire_date DATE NOT NULL,
    manager_id INTEGER,
    department VARCHAR(50)
);

INSERT INTO employees_multi_constraint (email, salary, hire_date, department) 
VALUES 
    ('john@company.com', 75000, '2025-01-15', 'Engineering'),
    ('jane@company.com', 85000, '2025-02-01', 'Sales');

-- Add multiple constraints in single statement (atomic operation)
ALTER TABLE employees_multi_constraint 
ADD CONSTRAINT unique_email UNIQUE (email),
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_salary_positive CHECK (salary > 0),
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_hire_date_valid CHECK (hire_date >= '2000-01-01'),
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_manager 
    FOREIGN KEY (manager_id) 
    REFERENCES employees_multi_constraint(employee_id)
    ON DELETE SET NULL;

-- Query all constraints on table
SELECT 
    conname AS constraint_name,
    contype AS constraint_type,
    pg_get_constraintdef(oid) AS definition
FROM pg_constraint
WHERE conrelid = 'employees_multi_constraint'::regclass
ORDER BY contype, conname;
Result (table constraints):
      constraint_name       | constraint_type |                          definition
----------------------------+-----------------+---------------------------------------------------------------
 chk_hire_date_valid        | c               | CHECK ((hire_date >= '2000-01-01'::date))
 chk_salary_positive        | c               | CHECK ((salary > (0)::numeric))
 fk_manager                 | f               | FOREIGN KEY (manager_id) REFERENCES employees_multi_constraint(employee_id) ON DELETE SET NULL
 employees_multi_constraint_pkey | p          | PRIMARY KEY (employee_id)
 unique_email               | u               | UNIQUE (email)
(5 rows)
Explanation: Added multiple constraints atomically - all succeed or all rolled back (transaction safety). unique_email prevents duplicate emails, chk_salary_positive validates salary > 0, chk_hire_date_valid ensures reasonable hire dates, fk_manager self-referencing FK for org hierarchy (manager is also employee). Single ALTER TABLE more efficient than multiple statements (one table scan vs multiple). Constraint types: 'c'=CHECK, 'f'=FOREIGN KEY, 'p'=PRIMARY KEY, 'u'=UNIQUE. pg_get_constraintdef() shows complete constraint definition. Self-referencing FK (manager_id references employee_id in same table) enables hierarchical data. All constraints validated against existing data during addition - fails if any row violates any constraint. Best practice for schema migrations: group related constraints in single ALTER TABLE.

5.7.4 Removing a Constraint

📖 Full Description

Removing a Constraint relaxes data integrity rules using ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT. Dropping constraints doesn't modify data - existing rows remain unchanged, but future inserts/updates no longer validated against dropped rules. Common reasons to drop constraints: business rules changed (price can now be negative for refunds), constraint too restrictive (preventing legitimate data), temporary removal for bulk data loading (drop CHECK/FK, load data, re-add constraints for speed), migrating to different validation approach (application-level vs database-level).

Constraint removal requires knowing constraint name - use information_schema.table_constraints or \\d table_name in psql to list constraints and their names. Named constraints (created with CONSTRAINT name ...) easily removed by name; unnamed constraints have system-generated names like table_column_key. Dropping PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraints also drops associated unique indexes - may impact query performance if index used for lookups. Dropping FOREIGN KEY constraints doesn't affect referenced table or data.

RESTRICT vs CASCADE behavior applies: RESTRICT (default) prevents dropping constraints if other objects depend on them, CASCADE drops dependent objects automatically. For most constraints, dependencies rare; but dropping UNIQUE constraint might affect views using that column in GROUP BY. DROP CONSTRAINT operations are fast (catalog update only) and reversible via transaction rollback. Best practice: drop constraints in transactions (BEGIN...COMMIT) enabling rollback, recreate constraint immediately after data modification if temporary removal, document why constraints removed to avoid confusion.

Drop Constraint Syntax
-- Basic DROP CONSTRAINT
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP CONSTRAINT constraint_name;

-- Drop constraint IF EXISTS (no error if doesn't exist)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP CONSTRAINT IF EXISTS constraint_name;

-- Drop constraint with RESTRICT (default - fail if dependencies)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP CONSTRAINT constraint_name RESTRICT;

-- Drop constraint with CASCADE (drop dependent objects)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP CONSTRAINT constraint_name CASCADE;

-- Drop multiple constraints
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP CONSTRAINT constraint1,
DROP CONSTRAINT constraint2;

-- List constraints to find names
SELECT 
    conname AS constraint_name,
    contype AS constraint_type
FROM pg_constraint
WHERE conrelid = 'table_name'::regclass;

-- Common constraint types:
-- 'c' = CHECK constraint
-- 'f' = FOREIGN KEY constraint
-- 'p' = PRIMARY KEY constraint
-- 'u' = UNIQUE constraint
-- 'x' = EXCLUSION constraint

-- Query constraint definition
SELECT pg_get_constraintdef(oid) 
FROM pg_constraint 
WHERE conname = 'constraint_name';

-- Common patterns:

-- Drop CHECK constraint
ALTER TABLE products
