DROP CONSTRAINT chk_price_positive;

-- Drop FOREIGN KEY
ALTER TABLE orders 
DROP CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer;

-- Drop UNIQUE constraint (and its index)
ALTER TABLE users 
DROP CONSTRAINT unique_email;

-- Drop PRIMARY KEY (rare - usually bad idea)
ALTER TABLE table_name 
DROP CONSTRAINT table_name_pkey CASCADE;

Dropping Constraint Considerations

Impact on Data:

  • No data changes: Existing rows unchanged, constraint simply no longer enforced
  • Future operations: INSERT/UPDATE no longer validated against dropped rule
  • Data can become invalid: Without constraint, invalid data may accumulate
  • Indexes dropped: UNIQUE/PK/EXCLUSION constraints drop associated indexes
  • Foreign keys unaffected: Dropping FK doesn't delete data in either table

Finding Constraint Names:

  • Named constraints: Use name from CREATE TABLE or ADD CONSTRAINT
  • Unnamed constraints: Query pg_constraint or use \\d table_name (psql)
  • Auto-generated names: table_column_key, table_column_check patterns
  • information_schema: table_constraints view lists all constraints

Performance:

  • Fast operation: Catalog update only, no table scan
  • No lock duration: Brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock (milliseconds)
  • Index removal: Dropping UNIQUE/PK also drops index (instant)
  • Can impact queries: Dropping index from UNIQUE constraint may slow lookups

Use Cases:

  • Bulk data loading: Drop FK/CHECK, load data fast, re-add constraints
  • Business rule changes: Requirements changed, old constraint no longer valid
  • Performance tuning: Remove unnecessary constraints, keep only essential ones
  • Schema refactoring: Replacing constraint with different validation approach
  • Temporary relaxation: Allow exceptional data temporarily, re-add constraint later

Safety:

  • Use transactions: BEGIN; DROP CONSTRAINT; test; COMMIT or ROLLBACK
  • Document removals: Note why constraint dropped in migration comments
  • Re-add if temporary: Plan to recreate constraint after data operation
  • Check dependencies: UNIQUE constraints might be referenced by foreign keys elsewhere

🎯 Example 1: Dropping CHECK Constraints

-- Table with restrictive constraints
CREATE TABLE products_drop_check (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    discount_percent NUMERIC(5,2) DEFAULT 0,
    CONSTRAINT chk_price_positive CHECK (price > 0),
    CONSTRAINT chk_discount_range CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 50)
);

INSERT INTO products_drop_check (product_name, price, discount_percent) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99, 10), ('Mouse', 29.99, 20);

-- Business rule changed: allow up to 75% discount
-- Try inserting with 60% discount (fails with current constraint)
-- INSERT INTO products_drop_check (product_name, price, discount_percent) 
-- VALUES ('Clearance Item', 99.99, 60);
-- ERROR: new row violates check constraint "chk_discount_range"

-- Drop old discount constraint
ALTER TABLE products_drop_check 
DROP CONSTRAINT chk_discount_range;

-- Add new constraint with updated range
ALTER TABLE products_drop_check 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_discount_updated CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 75);

-- Now 60% discount allowed
INSERT INTO products_drop_check (product_name, price, discount_percent) 
VALUES ('Clearance Item', 99.99, 60);

SELECT * FROM products_drop_check;
Result:
 product_id |  product_name  |  price  | discount_percent
------------+----------------+---------+------------------
          1 | Laptop         | 1299.99 |            10.00
          2 | Mouse          |   29.99 |            20.00
          3 | Clearance Item |   99.99 |            60.00
(3 rows)
Explanation: Dropped old CHECK constraint (discount 0-50%) to accommodate business rule change. After DROP CONSTRAINT chk_discount_range, discount validation removed - could insert any value. Added new constraint chk_discount_updated (0-75%) with relaxed range. Pattern: drop old constraint → add new constraint (don't leave validation gap). Alternative: DROP and ADD in single ALTER TABLE statement for atomic update. Existing rows (10%, 20%) still valid under new constraint. New row (60%) now allowed. Best practice: name constraints clearly (chk_discount_range) for easy identification when dropping/modifying. price constraint (chk_price_positive) remains active.

🎯 Example 2: Dropping FOREIGN KEY Constraints

-- Parent and child tables
CREATE TABLE suppliers_fk_drop (
    supplier_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    supplier_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE products_fk_drop (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    supplier_id INTEGER,
    CONSTRAINT fk_product_supplier 
        FOREIGN KEY (supplier_id) 
        REFERENCES suppliers_fk_drop(supplier_id)
);

INSERT INTO suppliers_fk_drop (supplier_name) VALUES ('Tech Corp');
INSERT INTO products_fk_drop (product_name, supplier_id) VALUES ('Laptop', 1);

-- Try inserting with invalid supplier_id (fails)
-- INSERT INTO products_fk_drop (product_name, supplier_id) VALUES ('Widget', 999);
-- ERROR: insert violates foreign key constraint "fk_product_supplier"

-- Drop foreign key constraint
ALTER TABLE products_fk_drop 
DROP CONSTRAINT fk_product_supplier;

-- Now can insert with invalid supplier_id (no validation)
INSERT INTO products_fk_drop (product_name, supplier_id) VALUES ('Widget', 999);

-- Query shows orphaned reference
SELECT 
    p.product_id,
    p.product_name,
    p.supplier_id,
    s.supplier_name
FROM products_fk_drop p
LEFT JOIN suppliers_fk_drop s ON p.supplier_id = s.supplier_id;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | supplier_id | supplier_name
------------+--------------+-------------+---------------
          1 | Laptop       |           1 | Tech Corp
          2 | Widget       |         999 | NULL
(2 rows)
Explanation: Dropped FOREIGN KEY constraint fk_product_supplier - referential integrity no longer enforced. Widget has supplier_id=999 (doesn't exist in suppliers_fk_drop) - orphaned reference now allowed. LEFT JOIN shows Widget with NULL supplier_name (no matching supplier). Use cases for dropping FKs: bulk data loading (load products without suppliers, fix references later), data migration (temporary invalid references during import), performance (FK checks on large inserts slow), archival (keep product data after supplier deleted). Danger: orphaned records cause data inconsistency, breaks referential integrity guarantees. Best practice: drop FK temporarily during bulk load, re-add after data cleanup. Alternative: keep FK but set supplier_id NULL during load.

🎯 Example 3: Dropping UNIQUE Constraints and Indexes

-- Table with UNIQUE constraint
CREATE TABLE users_unique_drop (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT unique_email UNIQUE (email),
    CONSTRAINT unique_username UNIQUE (username)
);

INSERT INTO users_unique_drop (email, username) 
VALUES ('john@example.com', 'john_doe'), ('jane@example.com', 'jane_smith');

-- Check indexes created by UNIQUE constraints
SELECT indexname, indexdef 
FROM pg_indexes 
WHERE tablename = 'users_unique_drop';

-- Try duplicate email (fails)
-- INSERT INTO users_unique_drop (email, username) VALUES ('john@example.com', 'john_doe2');
-- ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "unique_email"

-- Drop UNIQUE constraint (also drops its index)
ALTER TABLE users_unique_drop 
DROP CONSTRAINT unique_email;

-- Check indexes again (unique_email index gone)
SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'users_unique_drop';

-- Now can insert duplicate emails
INSERT INTO users_unique_drop (email, username) 
VALUES ('john@example.com', 'john_doe2');

SELECT user_id, email, username FROM users_unique_drop;
Result (indexes before DROP):
      indexname      |                        indexdef
---------------------+--------------------------------------------------------
 users_unique_drop_pkey | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... PRIMARY KEY (user_id)
 unique_email        | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX unique_email ON ... (email)
 unique_username     | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX unique_username ON ... (username)
(3 rows)
Result (indexes after DROP):
      indexname
---------------------
 users_unique_drop_pkey
 unique_username
(2 rows)
Result (duplicate emails allowed):
 user_id |       email        |  username
---------+--------------------+-------------
       1 | john@example.com   | john_doe
       2 | jane@example.com   | jane_smith
       3 | john@example.com   | john_doe2
(3 rows)
Explanation: Dropping UNIQUE constraint unique_email also dropped its underlying unique index. Before DROP: 3 indexes (PK, unique_email, unique_username). After DROP: 2 indexes (PK, unique_username). Email duplicates now allowed - users 1 and 3 both have john@example.com. Performance impact: queries filtering by email (WHERE email = 'john@example.com') no longer benefit from index (sequential scan). username still unique (unique_username constraint remains). Reason to drop UNIQUE: allow temporary duplicates during data consolidation, merge accounts, relaxed business rules. Best practice: if need index for queries but not uniqueness, drop UNIQUE constraint and CREATE regular index: DROP CONSTRAINT unique_email; CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users(email);

🎯 Example 4: Temporary Constraint Removal for Bulk Loading

-- Orders table with constraints
CREATE TABLE orders_bulk_load (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_number VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    total_amount NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT chk_total_positive CHECK (total_amount > 0)
);

-- Create foreign key to customers (simulate)
CREATE TABLE customers_bulk (customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100));
INSERT INTO customers_bulk (name) VALUES ('Customer 1');

ALTER TABLE orders_bulk_load 
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer 
FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers_bulk(customer_id);

-- BULK LOAD PATTERN:

-- Step 1: Drop constraints that slow bulk insert
ALTER TABLE orders_bulk_load 
DROP CONSTRAINT chk_total_positive,
DROP CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer;

-- Step 2: Bulk load data (much faster without validation)
INSERT INTO orders_bulk_load (order_number, customer_id, total_amount) 
SELECT 
    'ORD-' || generate_series,
    1,
    (random() * 1000 + 10)::NUMERIC(12,2)
FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Step 3: Validate data quality
SELECT COUNT(*) AS invalid_total 
FROM orders_bulk_load 
WHERE total_amount <= 0;

SELECT COUNT(*) AS invalid_customer 
FROM orders_bulk_load o 
LEFT JOIN customers_bulk c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id 
WHERE c.customer_id IS NULL;

-- Step 4: Re-add constraints (validates all data)
ALTER TABLE orders_bulk_load 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_total_positive CHECK (total_amount > 0),
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer 
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers_bulk(customer_id);

SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_orders FROM orders_bulk_load;
Result:
 invalid_total
---------------
             0
(1 row)

 invalid_customer
------------------
                0
(1 row)

 total_orders
--------------
         1000
(1 row)
Explanation: Bulk load pattern for performance: STEP 1 drop constraints (CHECK, FK) that validate each row during INSERT, STEP 2 bulk load data (10-100x faster without per-row validation), STEP 3 validate data quality with SELECT queries (find invalid rows), STEP 4 re-add constraints (validates all data in single table scan). Re-adding constraints fails if any rows invalid - must fix data first. Benefits: faster bulk loads (no per-row FK lookups, no CHECK evaluation), single validation pass at end, same final integrity guarantees. Risks: if constraint re-addition fails, data stuck in invalid state. Alternative: use COPY command which is fast even with constraints, or keep constraints and accept slower inserts. Pattern common for: large data imports, ETL processes, database migrations.

🎯 Example 5: Safe Constraint Removal with Validation

-- Products table with constraint to remove
CREATE TABLE products_safe_drop (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    category VARCHAR(50),
    CONSTRAINT chk_price_range CHECK (price BETWEEN 1 AND 10000),
    CONSTRAINT chk_category_valid CHECK (category IN ('Electronics', 'Clothing', 'Books'))
);

INSERT INTO products_safe_drop (product_name, price, category) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99, 'Electronics'), ('Novel', 19.99, 'Books');

-- SAFE REMOVAL WORKFLOW:

-- Step 1: List all constraints
SELECT 
    conname,
    pg_get_constraintdef(oid) AS definition
FROM pg_constraint
WHERE conrelid = 'products_safe_drop'::regclass AND contype = 'c';

-- Step 2: Test removal in transaction (can rollback)
BEGIN;

-- Drop constraint
ALTER TABLE products_safe_drop 
DROP CONSTRAINT chk_category_valid;

-- Test that operations work
INSERT INTO products_safe_drop (product_name, price, category) 
VALUES ('Widget', 50.00, 'NewCategory');

-- Verify change acceptable
SELECT * FROM products_safe_drop;

-- If satisfied, COMMIT; otherwise ROLLBACK
COMMIT;

-- Step 3: Verify final state
SELECT 
    conname,
    contype
FROM pg_constraint
WHERE conrelid = 'products_safe_drop'::regclass
ORDER BY conname;
Result (constraints before):
      conname         |                     definition
----------------------+----------------------------------------------------
 chk_category_valid   | CHECK ((category IN ('Electronics', 'Clothing', 'Books')))
 chk_price_range      | CHECK ((price >= 1::numeric) AND (price <= 10000::numeric))
(2 rows)
Result (after COMMIT):
      conname      | contype
-------------------+---------
 chk_price_range   | c
 products_safe_drop_pkey | p
(2 rows)
Explanation: Safe constraint removal workflow: STEP 1 list constraints to verify target exists and understand definition, STEP 2 execute in transaction (BEGIN) enabling rollback if issues, STEP 3 test operations that were previously blocked (INSERT with NewCategory), verify results acceptable, COMMIT if satisfied or ROLLBACK if problems. Transaction safety critical: if dropping constraint causes unexpected issues (application errors, bad data), can ROLLBACK to restore constraint. chk_category_valid dropped successfully, NewCategory now allowed. chk_price_range remains (price still must be 1-10000). Best practice for production: wrap all DDL in transactions, test thoroughly before COMMIT, document why constraints removed, monitor application behavior after deployment.

5.7.5 Changing a Column's Default Value

📖 Full Description

Changing a Column's Default Value modifies the automatic value assigned to a column when INSERT doesn't specify a value, using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET DEFAULT or DROP DEFAULT. Changing defaults only affects future INSERT operations - existing rows unchanged. This enables safe schema evolution: update default without rewriting table data, no impact on current data, no validation of existing rows required. Fast operation regardless of table size since only catalog metadata updated.

SET DEFAULT replaces current default with new value or expression. DROP DEFAULT removes default entirely, making column NULL on INSERT (unless NOT NULL, which causes error). Defaults can be: literals (DEFAULT 0, DEFAULT 'pending'), expressions (DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP), function calls (DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()), sequence values (DEFAULT nextval('seq')). PostgreSQL 11+ stores simple defaults in catalog without rewriting table - instant operation even on huge tables. Complex or volatile defaults require evaluation on each INSERT.

Common use cases: update timestamps (change DEFAULT now() to now() + INTERVAL '1 hour'), change status values (DEFAULT 'pending' to DEFAULT 'active'), adjust numeric defaults (DEFAULT 0 to DEFAULT 100), remove outdated defaults (DROP DEFAULT when application now always specifies values). Best practice: combine DEFAULT change with data backfill in separate transaction if wanting existing rows to match new default: first UPDATE existing rows, then ALTER TABLE SET DEFAULT for future rows.

Alter Column Default Syntax
-- Set new default value
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ALTER COLUMN column_name SET DEFAULT value;

-- Set default with expression
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ALTER COLUMN column_name SET DEFAULT expression;

-- Remove default
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ALTER COLUMN column_name DROP DEFAULT;

-- Common default patterns:

-- Literal defaults
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price SET DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN status SET DEFAULT 'pending';
ALTER TABLE settings ALTER COLUMN is_active SET DEFAULT true;

-- Function-based defaults
ALTER TABLE logs ALTER COLUMN created_at SET DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN user_id SET DEFAULT gen_random_uuid();

-- Expression defaults
ALTER TABLE discounts ALTER COLUMN expires_at SET DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '30 days');

-- Sequence defaults
ALTER TABLE customers ALTER COLUMN customer_number SET DEFAULT nextval('customer_seq');

-- Remove default (NULLable column)
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN notes DROP DEFAULT;

-- Common migration pattern:
-- 1. Update existing rows
UPDATE table_name SET column = new_value WHERE column IS NULL;
-- 2. Set new default for future rows
ALTER TABLE table_name ALTER COLUMN column SET DEFAULT new_value;

Default Value Considerations

Effect on Data:

  • Existing rows unchanged: SET/DROP DEFAULT only affects future INSERTs
  • No validation: Existing data not checked against new default
  • Instant operation: Catalog update only, no table rewrite
  • Backfill pattern: UPDATE existing + SET DEFAULT future = consistent data

Default Types:

  • Literal values: Numbers, strings, booleans (DEFAULT 42, DEFAULT 'text', DEFAULT true)
  • Current timestamp: DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, DEFAULT now()
  • Expressions: Calculations, concatenations (DEFAULT (price * 0.1))
  • Functions: UUID generation, sequences (DEFAULT gen_random_uuid())
  • NULL: Explicit DEFAULT NULL or DROP DEFAULT for nullable columns

Performance:

  • Fast in PG 11+: Simple defaults stored in catalog, no table rewrite
  • Evaluated on INSERT: Volatile functions (now(), random()) computed per row
  • No lock contention: Brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock for catalog update

🎯 Example 1: Changing Status Defaults (Consolidated Examples)

-- Example demonstrates changing literal, function, and expression defaults
CREATE TABLE orders_defaults (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_number VARCHAR(20),
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'draft',
    priority INTEGER DEFAULT 5,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    expires_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP + INTERVAL '7 days')
);

-- Insert with old defaults
INSERT INTO orders_defaults (order_number) VALUES ('ORD-001');

-- CHANGE 1: Update status default (business rule changed)
ALTER TABLE orders_defaults ALTER COLUMN status SET DEFAULT 'pending';

-- CHANGE 2: Update priority default
ALTER TABLE orders_defaults ALTER COLUMN priority SET DEFAULT 3;

-- CHANGE 3: Change expiration period
ALTER TABLE orders_defaults 
ALTER COLUMN expires_at SET DEFAULT (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP + INTERVAL '30 days');

-- Insert with new defaults
INSERT INTO orders_defaults (order_number) VALUES ('ORD-002');

-- Insert with explicit values (overrides defaults)
INSERT INTO orders_defaults (order_number, status, priority) 
VALUES ('ORD-003', 'shipped', 1);

-- Compare results
SELECT 
    order_id,
    order_number,
    status,
    priority,
    TO_CHAR(created_at, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS created,
    EXTRACT(DAY FROM (expires_at - created_at)) AS days_to_expire
FROM orders_defaults
ORDER BY order_id;
Result:
 order_id | order_number | status  | priority |  created   | days_to_expire
----------+--------------+---------+----------+------------+----------------
        1 | ORD-001      | draft   |        5 | 2026-01-25 |              7
        2 | ORD-002      | pending |        3 | 2026-01-25 |             30
        3 | ORD-003      | shipped |        1 | 2026-01-25 |             30
(3 rows)
Explanation: Changed multiple defaults: status 'draft'→'pending', priority 5→3, expires_at 7 days→30 days. Existing row (ORD-001) keeps old defaults (draft, 5, 7 days). New row (ORD-002) gets new defaults (pending, 3, 30 days). Explicit values (ORD-003: shipped, 1) override defaults. Pattern shows: defaults only affect INSERTs without values, existing data unchanged, explicit INSERT values always win. Days_to_expire column calculates difference showing 7 vs 30 days. Use cases: adjust business rules (new orders pending not draft), tune settings (priority levels), extend time windows (30-day expiration).

🎯 Example 2: Removing and Restoring Defaults

-- Products with defaults
CREATE TABLE products_drop_default (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
    is_featured BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
    notes TEXT DEFAULT 'No notes'
);

INSERT INTO products_drop_default (product_name) VALUES ('Laptop');

-- DROP DEFAULT makes column NULL on INSERT (unless NOT NULL)
ALTER TABLE products_drop_default ALTER COLUMN notes DROP DEFAULT;

-- Insert without notes (becomes NULL now)
INSERT INTO products_drop_default (product_name, price) VALUES ('Mouse', 29.99);

-- Try to DROP DEFAULT on NOT NULL column without default causes issues
-- ALTER TABLE products_drop_default ALTER COLUMN product_name DROP DEFAULT;
-- Future INSERTs must provide product_name or error

-- Restore default
ALTER TABLE products_drop_default ALTER COLUMN notes SET DEFAULT 'No additional information';

-- Insert with restored default
INSERT INTO products_drop_default (product_name, price) VALUES ('Keyboard', 89.99);

SELECT product_id, product_name, price, notes FROM products_drop_default;
Result:
 product_id | product_name |  price  |          notes
------------+--------------+---------+--------------------------
          1 | Laptop       |    0.00 | No notes
          2 | Mouse        |   29.99 | NULL
          3 | Keyboard     |   89.99 | No additional information
(3 rows)
Explanation: DROP DEFAULT removed 'No notes' default from notes column. Laptop (before DROP) has 'No notes', Mouse (after DROP) has NULL (no default available), Keyboard (after restore) has new default 'No additional information'. Demonstrates lifecycle: original default → DROP DEFAULT → NULL on INSERT → SET new DEFAULT. Dropping defaults on NOT NULL columns dangerous - forces explicit values on every INSERT or errors occur. Pattern for removing obsolete defaults: DROP DEFAULT when application now always provides values, or keep DEFAULT for backward compatibility even if rarely used.

5.7.6 Changing a Column's Data Type

📖 Full Description

Changing a Column's Data Type converts existing column to different type using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... TYPE new_type. Type changes may require table rewrite (slow on large tables) if data must be reformatted or validated. Automatic conversions work when PostgreSQL knows how to cast: INTEGER to BIGINT, VARCHAR(50) to VARCHAR(100), TEXT to JSONB (if valid JSON). Explicit USING clause required for complex conversions: extract substring, parse formats, compute new values.

Performance varies: some type changes instant (VARCHAR expanding, adding/removing array dimensions), others rewrite entire table (numeric precision changes, TEXT to INTEGER). Rewrite operations slow and lock-heavy on large tables. Use NOT NULL, defaults, and constraints carefully - new type must satisfy all existing constraints. Best practice: test on copy of production data, plan for downtime on large tables, consider creating new column + migrating data gradually instead of ALTER TYPE on huge tables.

Alter Column Type Syntax
-- Simple type change
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ALTER COLUMN column_name TYPE new_type;

-- Type change with USING expression
ALTER TABLE table_name 
ALTER COLUMN column_name TYPE new_type 
USING expression;

-- Examples:
-- Expand VARCHAR
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN email TYPE VARCHAR(200);

-- Integer to BIGINT
ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN order_id TYPE BIGINT;

-- TEXT to JSONB with validation
ALTER TABLE logs ALTER COLUMN data TYPE JSONB USING data::JSONB;

-- Extract substring during conversion
ALTER TABLE products 
ALTER COLUMN sku TYPE VARCHAR(10) 
USING substring(sku FROM 1 FOR 10);

-- Parse string to numeric
ALTER TABLE items 
ALTER COLUMN price_text TYPE NUMERIC(10,2) 
USING price_text::NUMERIC;

🎯 Example: Type Conversions (Combined)

CREATE TABLE data_types_change (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    item_code VARCHAR(10),
    quantity INTEGER,
    price_text TEXT
);

INSERT INTO data_types_change (item_code, quantity, price_text) 
VALUES ('ABC', 100, '29.99'), ('DEFGHIJKLMN', 200, '49.99');

-- Expand VARCHAR (no rewrite, instant)
ALTER TABLE data_types_change ALTER COLUMN item_code TYPE VARCHAR(50);

-- Integer to BIGINT (may rewrite depending on version)
ALTER TABLE data_types_change ALTER COLUMN quantity TYPE BIGINT;

-- TEXT to NUMERIC with casting
ALTER TABLE data_types_change 
ALTER COLUMN price_text TYPE NUMERIC(10,2) 
USING price_text::NUMERIC;

-- Rename for clarity after type change
ALTER TABLE data_types_change RENAME COLUMN price_text TO price;

SELECT * FROM data_types_change;
Result:
 id | item_code | quantity |  price
----+-----------+----------+--------
  1 | ABC       |      100 |  29.99
  2 | DEFGHIJKLMN |    200 |  49.99
(2 rows)
Explanation: Expanded VARCHAR(10) to VARCHAR(50) - fast operation. Changed quantity INTEGER to BIGINT - supports larger numbers. Converted price_text (TEXT) to NUMERIC using USING clause - parses '29.99' string to 29.99 numeric. USING required when PostgreSQL can't auto-cast. Failed conversions error: '29.99invalid'::NUMERIC would fail. Type changes validate all data - any row failing conversion aborts operation.

5.7.7 Renaming a Column

📖 Full Description

Renaming a Column changes column identifier without altering data using ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN old_name TO new_name. Instant operation - only catalog metadata updated. Views, functions, and triggers using old name may break unless rewritten. Best practice: coordinate with application deployment - update code to use new name before/after rename.

Rename Column Syntax
-- Rename column
ALTER TABLE table_name 
RENAME COLUMN old_column_name TO new_column_name;

-- Examples:
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN login TO username;
ALTER TABLE products RENAME COLUMN desc TO description;
ALTER TABLE orders RENAME COLUMN amt TO total_amount;

🎯 Example: Column Renaming

CREATE TABLE employees_rename (
    emp_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    fname VARCHAR(50),
    lname VARCHAR(50),
    sal NUMERIC(10,2)
);

INSERT INTO employees_rename (fname, lname, sal) 
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', 75000), ('Jane', 'Smith', 85000);

-- Rename for clarity
ALTER TABLE employees_rename RENAME COLUMN emp_id TO employee_id;
ALTER TABLE employees_rename RENAME COLUMN fname TO first_name;
ALTER TABLE employees_rename RENAME COLUMN lname TO last_name;
ALTER TABLE employees_rename RENAME COLUMN sal TO salary;

SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, salary FROM employees_rename;
Result:
 employee_id | first_name | last_name | salary
-------------+------------+-----------+---------
           1 | John       | Doe       | 75000.00
           2 | Jane       | Smith     | 85000.00
(2 rows)
Explanation: Renamed abbreviated columns to full names: emp_id→employee_id, fname→first_name, lname→last_name, sal→salary. Data unchanged, only identifiers updated. Improves readability and maintainability. Instant operations. Applications must update queries to use new names.

5.7.8 Renaming a Table

📖 Full Description

Renaming a Table changes table identifier using ALTER TABLE old_name RENAME TO new_name. Instant catalog operation. Dependent objects (views, foreign keys, triggers, functions) automatically updated to reference new name. Indexes, constraints, sequences also renamed. Applications must update queries. Best practice: use views to abstract table names, enabling renames without application changes.

Rename Table Syntax
-- Rename table
ALTER TABLE old_table_name RENAME TO new_table_name;

-- Examples:
ALTER TABLE users RENAME TO customers;
ALTER TABLE prod RENAME TO products;
ALTER TABLE order_items RENAME TO line_items;

🎯 Example: Table Renaming

CREATE TABLE temp_data (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    value TEXT
);

INSERT INTO temp_data (value) VALUES ('test');

-- Rename to permanent name
ALTER TABLE temp_data RENAME TO permanent_data;

-- Old name no longer works
-- SELECT * FROM temp_data;
-- ERROR: relation "temp_data" does not exist

-- New name works
SELECT * FROM permanent_data;
Result:
 id | value
----+-------
  1 | test
(1 row)
Explanation: Renamed temp_data to permanent_data. Old name no longer valid. Data, constraints, indexes automatically moved to new table name. Sequence permanent_data_id_seq auto-renamed from temp_data_id_seq. Applications querying temp_data will fail until updated to use permanent_data.

5.8 Privileges

📖 Full Description

Privileges control access to database objects (tables, schemas, functions) using GRANT and REVOKE commands. PostgreSQL implements role-based access control: roles can be users (can login) or groups (contain other roles). Object privileges include SELECT (read), INSERT (add rows), UPDATE (modify rows), DELETE (remove rows), TRUNCATE (fast delete all), REFERENCES (create foreign keys), TRIGGER (create triggers). Schema/database privileges control object creation and usage.

GRANT privilege ON object TO role syntax assigns permissions. REVOKE removes them. PUBLIC refers to all roles. WITH GRANT OPTION allows grantee to grant privilege to others. Owner of object has all privileges automatically. Superusers bypass all permission checks. Default privileges (ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES) set permissions for future objects created by role.

Best practices: principle of least privilege (grant minimum needed), use roles for groups not individual users, REVOKE PUBLIC access on sensitive tables, grant through roles not directly to users, use schemas to organize and control access, document privilege requirements clearly, audit privilege grants regularly.

Privileges Syntax
-- Grant table privileges
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON table_name TO role_name;
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON table_name TO role_name;

-- Revoke privileges
REVOKE SELECT, INSERT ON table_name FROM role_name;
REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON table_name FROM role_name;

-- Grant with grant option (role can grant to others)
GRANT SELECT ON table_name TO role_name WITH GRANT OPTION;

-- Grant to PUBLIC (all roles)
GRANT SELECT ON table_name TO PUBLIC;

-- Schema privileges
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA schema_name TO role_name;
GRANT CREATE ON SCHEMA schema_name TO role_name;

-- Database privileges
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE database_name TO role_name;
GRANT CREATE ON DATABASE database_name TO role_name;

-- Sequence privileges
GRANT USAGE, SELECT ON SEQUENCE sequence_name TO role_name;

-- Function privileges
GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION function_name(arg_types) TO role_name;

-- Create roles
CREATE ROLE role_name;              -- Group role
CREATE ROLE username WITH LOGIN;     -- User role (can login)
CREATE ROLE username WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret';

-- Grant role membership
GRANT role_name TO username;

-- Alter default privileges (for future objects)
ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA schema_name
GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON TABLES TO role_name;

🎯 Example: Privilege Management (Consolidated)

-- Create roles
CREATE ROLE app_readonly;
CREATE ROLE app_readwrite;
CREATE ROLE analyst WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'analyst_pass';

-- Create sample tables
CREATE TABLE products_priv (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    price NUMERIC(10,2)
);

CREATE TABLE orders_priv (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_id INTEGER,
    quantity INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO products_priv (product_name, price) VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99);
INSERT INTO orders_priv (product_id, quantity) VALUES (1, 5);

-- GRANT privileges to roles
-- Read-only: can SELECT
GRANT SELECT ON products_priv, orders_priv TO app_readonly;

-- Read-write: can SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON products_priv, orders_priv TO app_readwrite;

-- Grant sequence usage for INSERT
GRANT USAGE, SELECT ON SEQUENCE products_priv_product_id_seq TO app_readwrite;
GRANT USAGE, SELECT ON SEQUENCE orders_priv_order_id_seq TO app_readwrite;

-- Grant role membership (analyst gets readonly permissions)
GRANT app_readonly TO analyst;

-- Check privileges
SELECT 
    table_name,
    grantee,
    privilege_type
FROM information_schema.table_privileges
WHERE table_name IN ('products_priv', 'orders_priv')
ORDER BY table_name, grantee, privilege_type;
Result (partial - showing key grants):
  table_name    |    grantee     | privilege_type
----------------+----------------+----------------
 orders_priv    | app_readonly   | SELECT
 orders_priv    | app_readwrite  | SELECT
 orders_priv    | app_readwrite  | INSERT
 orders_priv    | app_readwrite  | UPDATE
 orders_priv    | app_readwrite  | DELETE
 products_priv  | app_readonly   | SELECT
 products_priv  | app_readwrite  | SELECT
 products_priv  | app_readwrite  | INSERT
(8 rows)
Explanation: Created roles: app_readonly (group for read access), app_readwrite (group for write access), analyst (user inherits from app_readonly). Granted SELECT to app_readonly, SELECT+INSERT+UPDATE+DELETE to app_readwrite. Sequence USAGE grants required for INSERT with SERIAL columns. Analyst user inherits app_readonly permissions via GRANT app_readonly TO analyst. Pattern: group roles define privilege sets, user roles gain permissions through membership. Separates permission structure from individual users. To revoke: REVOKE SELECT ON products_priv FROM app_readonly.

5.9 Row Security Policies

📖 Full Description

Row Security Policies (RLS) filter rows based on current user, enabling row-level permissions beyond table-level GRANT/REVOKE. Policies use WHERE clauses to control which rows visible/modifiable per user. Enable RLS with ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, then CREATE POLICY defines rules. Policies can restrict SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE separately. Common use cases: multi-tenant applications (users see only their data), hierarchical access (managers see subordinates' data), data classification (users see data at their security level).

Policy expressions use current_user, session variables, or functions to determine access. USING clause filters visible rows (SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE). WITH CHECK validates new/modified rows (INSERT/UPDATE). Policies applied to non-owner roles; table owner and superusers bypass RLS unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY set. Multiple policies combine with OR logic. Default-deny: enable RLS without policies blocks all access except owners.

Row Security Syntax
-- Enable row security on table
ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Create policy
CREATE POLICY policy_name ON table_name
FOR SELECT | INSERT | UPDATE | DELETE | ALL
TO role_name | PUBLIC
USING (condition)          -- Filters visible rows
WITH CHECK (condition);    -- Validates new/modified rows

-- Examples:
-- Users see only their own data
CREATE POLICY user_data_policy ON orders
FOR ALL
USING (user_id = current_user);

-- Managers see team data
CREATE POLICY manager_policy ON employees
FOR SELECT
USING (manager_id = (SELECT employee_id FROM employees WHERE username = current_user));

-- Drop policy
DROP POLICY policy_name ON table_name;

-- Disable row security
ALTER TABLE table_name DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

🎯 Example: Row-Level Security

-- Create roles
CREATE ROLE user1 WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'pass1';
CREATE ROLE user2 WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'pass2';

-- Create table
CREATE TABLE documents_rls (
    doc_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    owner TEXT NOT NULL,
    title VARCHAR(100),
    content TEXT
);

-- Enable RLS
ALTER TABLE documents_rls ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Policy: users see only their documents
CREATE POLICY doc_owner_policy ON documents_rls
FOR ALL
USING (owner = current_user);

-- Grant table access
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON documents_rls TO user1, user2;
GRANT USAGE ON SEQUENCE documents_rls_doc_id_seq TO user1, user2;

-- Insert data as different users (simulated)
INSERT INTO documents_rls (owner, title, content) 
VALUES ('user1', 'User1 Doc', 'Private content'),
       ('user2', 'User2 Doc', 'Other content');

-- As user1, would only see:
SELECT * FROM documents_rls;  -- Shows only owner='user1' rows

-- Policy prevents access to other users' data
-- UPDATE documents_rls SET title='Modified' WHERE owner='user2';
-- Result: 0 rows updated (policy filtered them out)
Result (as user1):
 doc_id | owner | title      | content
--------+-------+------------+----------------
      1 | user1 | User1 Doc  | Private content
(1 row)
Explanation: Row-level security filters rows based on owner column. user1 sees only documents where owner='user1', user2 sees only owner='user2'. Policy enforces multi-tenant data isolation automatically - no need to add WHERE owner=current_user to every query. INSERT with owner='user3' by user1 would fail WITH CHECK. Superusers see all rows unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. Use cases: SaaS applications (tenant isolation), collaborative platforms (sharing controls), compliance (data classification).

5.10 Schemas

📖 Overview

Schemas are namespaces within databases that organize tables, views, functions, and other objects. Schemas prevent naming conflicts, organize large databases, support multi-tenant applications, and provide security boundaries. Every object belongs to one schema. Qualified names (schema.table) explicitly reference objects; unqualified names search schemas in search_path order. Best practices: create dedicated schemas for applications, avoid public for production, use schemas for logical separation, grant schema-level privileges for access management.

5.10.1 Creating a Schema

📖 Full Description

Creating Schemas: CREATE SCHEMA name establishes new namespace. CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS prevents errors on duplicate creation. Schema owner automatically has all privileges. CREATE SCHEMA AUTHORIZATION username sets specific owner. Optionally include object creation statements in same command for atomic schema setup.

Schema Creation Syntax
-- Basic schema creation
CREATE SCHEMA schema_name;

-- Create with specific owner
CREATE SCHEMA schema_name AUTHORIZATION username;

-- Create if not exists (safe for scripts)
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS schema_name;

-- Create schema with objects in single command
CREATE SCHEMA app_schema
    CREATE TABLE users (user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, username VARCHAR(50))
    CREATE TABLE posts (post_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, user_id INTEGER);

🎯 Example: Creating Application Schema

-- Create schema for application
CREATE SCHEMA app_sales;

-- Create tables in the schema
CREATE TABLE app_sales.customers (
    customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE app_sales.orders (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER REFERENCES app_sales.customers(customer_id),
    order_date DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    total_amount NUMERIC(10,2)
);

-- Query using qualified names
SELECT c.customer_name, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
FROM app_sales.customers c
LEFT JOIN app_sales.orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_name;

5.10.2 The Public Schema

📖 Full Description

Public Schema: Default schema where unqualified objects created. All users have CREATE and USAGE privileges by default. Security consideration: in production, REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public FROM PUBLIC to prevent unauthorized object creation. Public schema useful for development/testing but risky in multi-user environments.

🎯 Example: Securing Public Schema

-- Check current privileges on public schema
SELECT nspname, nspacl FROM pg_namespace WHERE nspname = 'public';

-- Revoke CREATE privilege from public (best practice for production)
REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public FROM PUBLIC;

-- Create role and grant specific access
CREATE ROLE app_user WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'secure_pass';
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO app_user;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO app_user;

5.10.3 The Schema Search Path

📖 Full Description

Search Path: Determines schema search order for unqualified object names. Default: "$user", public. SET search_path = schema1, schema2 changes order for session. ALTER DATABASE/ROLE SET search_path sets persistent default. First matching object found is used. Qualified names (schema.table) bypass search_path.

Search Path Syntax
-- View current search path
SHOW search_path;

-- Set for current session
SET search_path TO schema1, schema2, public;

-- Set for specific user (persistent)
ALTER ROLE username SET search_path = schema1, public;

-- Set for database (all connections)
ALTER DATABASE dbname SET search_path = schema1, public;

🎯 Example: Multi-Tenant Search Path

-- Create tenant schemas
CREATE SCHEMA tenant_x;
CREATE SCHEMA tenant_y;

-- Create identical table structures
CREATE TABLE tenant_x.data (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, value TEXT);
CREATE TABLE tenant_y.data (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, value TEXT);

-- Insert different data
INSERT INTO tenant_x.data (value) VALUES ('Tenant X Data');
INSERT INTO tenant_y.data (value) VALUES ('Tenant Y Data');

-- Set search path for tenant_x
SET search_path TO tenant_x, public;
SELECT * FROM data;  -- Returns Tenant X Data

-- Switch to tenant_y
SET search_path TO tenant_y, public;
SELECT * FROM data;  -- Returns Tenant Y Data

5.10.4 Schemas and Privileges

📖 Full Description

Schema Privileges: USAGE allows accessing schema objects (SELECT, INSERT, etc.). CREATE allows creating new objects in schema. Both required for full access. ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES sets permissions on future objects. Schema owner has all privileges automatically.

🎯 Example: Schema-Level Access Control

-- Create schema and roles
CREATE SCHEMA reporting;
CREATE ROLE report_reader WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'reader_pass';
CREATE ROLE report_writer WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'writer_pass';

-- Grant schema access
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA reporting TO report_reader, report_writer;
GRANT CREATE ON SCHEMA reporting TO report_writer;

-- Grant table privileges
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA reporting TO report_reader;
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA reporting TO report_writer;

-- Set default privileges for future tables
ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA reporting 
GRANT SELECT ON TABLES TO report_reader;

ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA reporting 
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLES TO report_writer;

5.10.5 The System Catalog Schema

📖 Full Description

System Catalog: pg_catalog schema contains system tables and views. Always first in search_path regardless of setting. information_schema provides SQL-standard views of metadata. Both provide database introspection capabilities. Query these for object discovery, dependency analysis, and system monitoring.

🎯 Example: Querying System Catalog

-- List all schemas
SELECT nspname AS schema_name, nspowner::regrole AS owner
FROM pg_namespace
WHERE nspname NOT LIKE 'pg_%' AND nspname != 'information_schema'
ORDER BY nspname;

-- List all tables in specific schema
SELECT table_schema, table_name, table_type
FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_schema = 'app_sales'
ORDER BY table_name;

-- Get table column details
SELECT column_name, data_type, is_nullable, column_default
FROM information_schema.columns
WHERE table_schema = 'app_sales' AND table_name = 'orders'
ORDER BY ordinal_position;

5.10.6 Usage Patterns

📖 Full Description

Common Schema Patterns: 1) Application Isolation (app1, app2 schemas for different apps), 2) Multi-Tenancy (tenant schemas with identical structures), 3) Versioning (v1, v2 schemas for API versions), 4) Functional Grouping (sales, hr, finance schemas), 5) Data Lifecycle (current, archived schemas). Choose pattern based on security, scalability, and maintenance requirements.

🎯 Example: Version Management Pattern

-- Create version schemas
CREATE SCHEMA api_v1;
CREATE SCHEMA api_v2;

-- V1 schema structure
CREATE TABLE api_v1.users (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

-- V2 schema structure (enhanced)
CREATE TABLE api_v2.users (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Applications can connect to specific version
SET search_path TO api_v2, public;  -- New apps use V2
-- Legacy apps use: SET search_path TO api_v1, public;

5.10.7 Portability

📖 Full Description

SQL Standard Compliance: SQL standard defines schemas but implementations vary across databases. PostgreSQL-specific features: inheritance, search_path configuration, ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES. For maximum portability: use explicit schema qualification (schema.table), avoid PostgreSQL extensions in schema DDL, test across target databases. Migration tools (pg_dump, AWS DMS) handle schema translation.

🎯 Example: Portable Schema Design

-- Portable schema creation (works on most SQL databases)
CREATE SCHEMA myapp;

-- Use qualified names (portable)
CREATE TABLE myapp.customers (
    customer_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);

-- Avoid PostgreSQL-specific features for portability:
-- AVOID: SERIAL (use INTEGER + sequence manually)
-- AVOID: INHERITS (not standard SQL)
-- AVOID: PostgreSQL-specific data types (JSONB, ARRAY)

-- Standard SQL approach
CREATE SEQUENCE myapp.customer_seq;
CREATE TABLE myapp.customers_portable (
    customer_id INTEGER DEFAULT NEXTVAL('myapp.customer_seq') PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);

5.11 Inheritance

📖 Full Description

Table Inheritance allows tables to inherit columns from parent tables using INHERITS clause. Child tables include parent columns plus additional columns. Queries on parent return rows from parent and all children (unless ONLY keyword used). Inheritance useful for: shared common columns across related tables, polymorphic queries (query parent to get all subtypes), partitioning (legacy approach, declarative partitioning preferred now). Constraints and indexes NOT inherited - must be recreated on children.

Caveats: PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints not inherited. Foreign keys don't span inheritance hierarchy. INSERT on parent doesn't route to children. Inheritance was original PostgreSQL partitioning approach but declarative partitioning (Section 5.12) now recommended for most uses. Inheritance still useful for polymorphic data models where child tables have distinct structures.

Inheritance Syntax
-- Create parent table
CREATE TABLE parent_table (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    common_column TEXT
);

-- Create child table inheriting from parent
CREATE TABLE child_table (
    child_specific_column TEXT
) INHERITS (parent_table);

-- Query parent (includes child rows)
SELECT * FROM parent_table;

-- Query only parent (excludes children)
SELECT * FROM ONLY parent_table;

-- Query specific child
SELECT * FROM child_table;

🎯 Example: Table Inheritance

-- Parent table for all employees
CREATE TABLE employees_inherit (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    hire_date DATE NOT NULL
);

-- Child table for managers (additional columns)
CREATE TABLE managers (
    department VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    bonus_pct NUMERIC(5,2)
) INHERITS (employees_inherit);

-- Child table for engineers (additional columns)
CREATE TABLE engineers (
    programming_language VARCHAR(50),
    certification VARCHAR(100)
) INHERITS (employees_inherit);

-- Insert into parent
INSERT INTO employees_inherit (first_name, last_name, hire_date) 
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', '2025-01-15');

-- Insert into children
INSERT INTO managers (first_name, last_name, hire_date, department, bonus_pct) 
VALUES ('Jane', 'Smith', '2024-06-01', 'Sales', 15.00);

INSERT INTO engineers (first_name, last_name, hire_date, programming_language, certification) 
VALUES ('Bob', 'Johnson', '2025-03-01', 'Python', 'AWS Certified');

-- Query parent (returns all: parent + children)
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, 'All Employees' AS type FROM employees_inherit;

-- Query only parent (excludes children)
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, 'Base Employees Only' AS type FROM ONLY employees_inherit;

-- Query specific child types
SELECT first_name, last_name, department FROM managers
UNION ALL
SELECT first_name, last_name, programming_language FROM engineers;
Result (all employees):
 employee_id | first_name | last_name |      type
-------------+------------+-----------+------------------
           1 | John       | Doe       | All Employees
           1 | Jane       | Smith     | All Employees
           1 | Bob        | Johnson   | All Employees
(3 rows)
Result (only base):
 employee_id | first_name | last_name |        type
-------------+------------+-----------+----------------------
           1 | John       | Doe       | Base Employees Only
(1 row)
Explanation: Inheritance creates type hierarchy: employees_inherit parent, managers and engineers children. Children inherit employee_id, first_name, last_name, hire_date plus add specific columns (department, programming_language). Query on employees_inherit returns ALL rows (parent + children). ONLY employees_inherit excludes children, returns just parent rows. Caveats: PRIMARY KEY on parent NOT inherited (each child has separate employee_id sequence starting at 1), no constraint inheritance, no automatic routing. Modern alternative: use single table with type discriminator column or declarative partitioning for performance, inheritance mainly for polymorphic data models.

5.11.1 Caveats

📖 Full Description

Inheritance Limitations: While table inheritance provides polymorphic querying, several important constraints and behaviors don't work as expected. PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints are NOT inherited - each child table has independent constraints. CHECK constraints CAN be inherited but must be explicitly defined. Foreign key constraints don't span the inheritance hierarchy - references to parent table don't automatically include children.

Key Issues: INSERT into parent doesn't automatically route to appropriate child table (manual routing needed). UPDATE/DELETE on parent affects only parent rows unless propagated to children. Indexes are not inherited - must create separately on each child. Query performance can degrade with many children. Modern alternatives like declarative partitioning (5.12) or single-table inheritance with discriminator column often preferred.

Common Inheritance Issues
-- Issue 1: PRIMARY KEY not inherited
CREATE TABLE parent (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE child () INHERITS (parent);
-- Result: child table has separate id sequence, duplicate IDs possible!

-- Issue 2: UNIQUE constraints not inherited
CREATE TABLE parent_unique (email VARCHAR UNIQUE, name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE child_unique () INHERITS (parent_unique);
-- Result: email uniqueness enforced per table, duplicates across inheritance!

-- Issue 3: Foreign keys don't work with inheritance
CREATE TABLE orders_ref (order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY);
CREATE TABLE items_ref (
    item_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id INTEGER REFERENCES orders_ref(order_id)  -- Only references parent!
);
CREATE TABLE special_orders () INHERITS (orders_ref);
-- Result: items_ref foreign key doesn't recognize special_orders rows!

-- Modern Alternative: Single table with discriminator
CREATE TABLE employees_modern (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    employee_type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,  -- 'base', 'manager', 'engineer'
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    -- Common columns for all
    department VARCHAR(50),  -- Null for non-managers
    programming_language VARCHAR(50),  -- Null for non-engineers
    CONSTRAINT valid_type CHECK (employee_type IN ('base', 'manager', 'engineer'))
);

-- Query specific types using WHERE clause
SELECT * FROM employees_modern WHERE employee_type = 'manager';

🎯 Example: PRIMARY KEY Not Inherited Problem

-- Create parent with PRIMARY KEY
CREATE TABLE products_inherit (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    base_price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL
);

-- Create child tables
CREATE TABLE physical_products (
    weight_kg NUMERIC(6,2),
    dimensions VARCHAR(50)
) INHERITS (products_inherit);

CREATE TABLE digital_products (
    file_size_mb NUMERIC(8,2),
    download_url TEXT
) INHERITS (products_inherit);

-- Insert data
INSERT INTO products_inherit (product_name, base_price) 
VALUES ('Generic Product', 29.99);

INSERT INTO physical_products (product_name, base_price, weight_kg, dimensions) 
VALUES ('Laptop', 1299.99, 2.5, '35x25x2cm');

INSERT INTO digital_products (product_name, base_price, file_size_mb, download_url) 
VALUES ('Software License', 99.99, 150.5, 'https://example.com/download');

-- Problem: Each table has independent product_id sequence!
SELECT product_id, product_name, 'Parent' AS source FROM ONLY products_inherit
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id, product_name, 'Physical' AS source FROM physical_products
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id, product_name, 'Digital' AS source FROM digital_products
ORDER BY source, product_id;
Result:
 product_id |   product_name    |  source
------------+-------------------+-----------
 1          | Digital Product   | Digital
 1          | Generic Product   | Parent
 1          | Laptop           | Physical
(3 rows)
Explanation: All three tables have product_id = 1 because each has separate SERIAL sequence. PRIMARY KEY constraint not inherited means no uniqueness enforcement across inheritance hierarchy. Query on parent products_inherit returns all rows but with duplicate IDs. This breaks relational model assumptions (unique identifiers). Solutions: 1) Use single sequence for all tables (nextval('shared_seq')), 2) Implement discriminator column in single table, 3) Use declarative partitioning if goal is performance. Inheritance suited for polymorphic data models where child structures differ significantly, not for partitioning performance.

5.12 Table Partitioning

📖 Overview

Table Partitioning divides large tables into smaller physical pieces (partitions) while appearing as single logical table. Improves query performance (partition pruning scans only relevant partitions), enables efficient data management (drop old partitions instead of DELETE), and supports maintenance operations on subsets. PostgreSQL supports declarative partitioning (PostgreSQL 10+, recommended) with RANGE, LIST, and HASH strategies.

5.12.1 Overview

📖 Full Description

Partitioning Concepts: Partitioning splits large table into smaller pieces based on partition key. Each partition is separate table storing subset of data. Parent table routes queries to appropriate partitions. Benefits include improved performance (scan fewer rows), easier maintenance (VACUUM/ANALYZE smaller tables), archival (drop old partitions), and parallelism (concurrent partition operations).

When to Partition: Tables > 100GB, time-series data (logs, events), write-heavy workloads, archival requirements, query patterns matching partition key. Don't partition small tables (< 100GB) - overhead outweighs benefits. Choose partition key carefully - should match common WHERE clauses.

Partition Types
-- RANGE partitioning (continuous ranges)
-- Use for: dates, numbers, timestamps
PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date)

-- LIST partitioning (discrete values)
-- Use for: regions, categories, status codes
PARTITION BY LIST (region)

-- HASH partitioning (even distribution)
-- Use for: uniform data distribution, load balancing
PARTITION BY HASH (customer_id)

5.12.2 Declarative Partitioning

📖 Full Description

Declarative Partitioning: Modern partitioning method (PostgreSQL 10+). Create parent with PARTITION BY clause, create children with PARTITION OF. PostgreSQL automatically routes INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE to correct partition. Supports partition pruning (query optimizer eliminates irrelevant partitions). Preferred over inheritance-based partitioning for new applications.

Declarative Partitioning Syntax
-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    columns...
) PARTITION BY RANGE (column);  -- or LIST/HASH

-- Create range partition
CREATE TABLE partition_name PARTITION OF table_name
FOR VALUES FROM (start) TO (end);

-- Create list partition
CREATE TABLE partition_name PARTITION OF table_name
FOR VALUES IN (value1, value2);

-- Create hash partition
CREATE TABLE partition_name PARTITION OF table_name
FOR VALUES WITH (MODULUS x, REMAINDER y);

🎯 Example: Declarative Range Partitioning

-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE orders_by_month (
    order_id SERIAL,
    order_date DATE NOT NULL,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    total_amount NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (order_date, order_id)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date);

-- Create monthly partitions
CREATE TABLE orders_2026_01 PARTITION OF orders_by_month
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-01-01') TO ('2026-02-01');

CREATE TABLE orders_2026_02 PARTITION OF orders_by_month
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-02-01') TO ('2026-03-01');

-- Insert automatically routes to correct partition
INSERT INTO orders_by_month (order_date, customer_id, total_amount)
VALUES ('2026-01-15', 1001, 299.99);

5.12.3 Partitioning Using Inheritance

📖 Full Description

Inheritance-Based Partitioning: Legacy approach using table inheritance + triggers for routing. Child tables INHERIT from parent, CHECK constraints define ranges, triggers route INSERTs. More complex setup than declarative partitioning. Requires constraint_exclusion = on for partition pruning. Use declarative partitioning for new applications - inheritance method mainly for legacy systems.

Inheritance Partitioning Pattern
-- Create parent table
CREATE TABLE parent (id SERIAL, created_date DATE);

-- Create child with CHECK constraint
CREATE TABLE child_2026_01 (
    CHECK (created_date >= '2026-01-01' AND created_date < '2026-02-01')
) INHERITS (parent);

-- Create trigger function for routing
CREATE FUNCTION insert_trigger() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    IF NEW.created_date >= '2026-01-01' AND NEW.created_date < '2026-02-01' THEN
        INSERT INTO child_2026_01 VALUES (NEW.*);
    ELSE
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'No partition for date %', NEW.created_date;
    END IF;
    RETURN NULL;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Attach trigger to parent
CREATE TRIGGER insert_trigger BEFORE INSERT ON parent
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION insert_trigger();

5.12.4 Partition Pruning

📖 Full Description

Partition Pruning: Query optimization technique where planner eliminates irrelevant partitions from scan. Happens at plan time (static pruning) or execution time (dynamic pruning). Requires queries with WHERE clauses on partition key. Check EXPLAIN output to verify pruning working. Enable with enable_partition_pruning = on (default in PostgreSQL 11+).

🎯 Example: Verifying Partition Pruning

-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE logs_by_day (
    log_id BIGSERIAL,
    log_date DATE NOT NULL,
    message TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (log_date, log_id)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (log_date);

-- Create daily partitions
CREATE TABLE logs_2026_01_25 PARTITION OF logs_by_day
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-01-25') TO ('2026-01-26');

CREATE TABLE logs_2026_01_26 PARTITION OF logs_by_day
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-01-26') TO ('2026-01-27');

-- Query with pruning (EXPLAIN shows only 1 partition scanned)
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM logs_by_day WHERE log_date = '2026-01-25';
-- Output: Seq Scan on logs_2026_01_25 (1 partition scanned, 1 filtered out)

-- Query without pruning (scans all partitions)
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM logs_by_day WHERE message LIKE '%error%';
-- Output: Append -> Seq Scan on logs_2026_01_25, Seq Scan on logs_2026_01_26

5.12.5 Partitioning and Constraint Exclusion

📖 Full Description

Constraint Exclusion: Older optimization technique for inheritance-based partitioning. Planner checks CHECK constraints to exclude partitions. Requires constraint_exclusion = partition or on. Less efficient than partition pruning (used by declarative partitioning). Only needed for legacy inheritance-based partitions. Declarative partitioning uses partition pruning automatically - constraint exclusion not needed.

Constraint Exclusion Settings
-- Check current setting
SHOW constraint_exclusion;

-- Enable for inheritance partitioning (partition value recommended)
SET constraint_exclusion = partition;  -- Only for partitioned tables
-- or
SET constraint_exclusion = on;         -- For all CHECK constraints (slower)

5.12.6 Best Practices for Declarative Partitioning

📖 Full Description

Partition Key Selection: Choose key matching common WHERE clauses in queries. Use columns with natural ranges (dates, IDs). Avoid frequently updated columns (causes row movement across partitions). Consider cardinality - too many partitions (>100) hurts performance.

Maintenance Best Practices: Create indexes on partitions after creation (inherited from parent). Use ATTACH/DETACH PARTITION for archival. Pre-create future partitions. Monitor partition sizes - rebalance if skewed. Use DEFAULT partition for overflow data. Test query plans (EXPLAIN) to verify pruning. Partition key must be in PRIMARY KEY/UNIQUE constraints.

🎯 Example: Complete Partitioning Workflow

-- 1. Create partitioned table (partition key in PK)
CREATE TABLE sales_by_quarter (
    sale_id BIGSERIAL,
    sale_date DATE NOT NULL,
    amount NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL,
    region VARCHAR(50),
    PRIMARY KEY (sale_date, sale_id)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date);

-- 2. Create partitions
CREATE TABLE sales_2026_q1 PARTITION OF sales_by_quarter
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-01-01') TO ('2026-04-01');

CREATE TABLE sales_2026_q2 PARTITION OF sales_by_quarter
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-04-01') TO ('2026-07-01');

-- 3. Create indexes (after partitions exist, inherited to all)
CREATE INDEX idx_sales_region ON sales_by_quarter (region);

-- 4. Insert data (auto-routed)
INSERT INTO sales_by_quarter (sale_date, amount, region) VALUES
('2026-01-15', 1000.00, 'US'),
('2026-05-20', 2000.00, 'EU');

-- 5. Archive old partition
ALTER TABLE sales_by_quarter DETACH PARTITION sales_2026_q1;
-- Now can pg_dump, archive, and DROP sales_2026_q1

-- 6. Add future partition
CREATE TABLE sales_2026_q3 PARTITION OF sales_by_quarter
FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-10-01');

-- 7. Verify pruning
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM sales_by_quarter WHERE sale_date = '2026-01-15';

5.13 Foreign Data

📖 Full Description

Foreign Data Wrappers (FDW) access external data sources as if they were PostgreSQL tables. Query remote databases, files, or APIs through SQL interface. Common FDWs: postgres_fdw (remote PostgreSQL), file_fdw (CSV files), mysql_fdw, oracle_fdw. Use cases: federated queries (join local and remote data), data integration (query multiple databases), migration (access legacy systems), real-time reporting (query production without replication).

Workflow: CREATE EXTENSION for FDW, CREATE SERVER (connection info), CREATE USER MAPPING (credentials), CREATE FOREIGN TABLE (schema definition). Query foreign tables like regular tables - PostgreSQL pushes down predicates/joins where possible for performance. Foreign tables support SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (if FDW implements), but no indexes, constraints, or triggers on foreign tables themselves.

Foreign Data Syntax
-- Enable postgres_fdw extension
CREATE EXTENSION postgres_fdw;

-- Create server (remote database connection)
CREATE SERVER remote_server
FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER postgres_fdw
OPTIONS (host 'remote.example.com', port '5432', dbname 'remote_db');

-- Create user mapping (credentials)
CREATE USER MAPPING FOR current_user
SERVER remote_server
OPTIONS (user 'remote_user', password 'remote_pass');

-- Create foreign table (maps to remote table)
CREATE FOREIGN TABLE remote_products (
    product_id INTEGER,
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    price NUMERIC(10,2)
) SERVER remote_server
OPTIONS (schema_name 'public', table_name 'products');

-- Query foreign table
SELECT * FROM remote_products WHERE price > 100;

5.14 Other Database Objects

📖 Full Description

Other Database Objects beyond tables include: Views (virtual tables from queries, CREATE VIEW), Materialized Views (cached query results, REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW), Indexes (B-tree, Hash, GiST, GIN, BRIN for performance), Functions (stored procedures in PL/pgSQL, SQL, Python, etc.), Triggers (automatic functions on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), Sequences (auto-incrementing numbers for PRIMARY KEY), Types (composite types, enums, domains), Operators (custom operators like @> for arrays).

These objects work together: views simplify complex queries, indexes speed up WHERE/JOIN clauses, functions encapsulate logic, triggers enforce business rules automatically, sequences generate unique IDs, custom types model domain-specific data. All follow CREATE/ALTER/DROP pattern and belong to schemas for organization and access control.

5.15 Dependency Tracking

📖 Full Description

Dependency Tracking manages relationships between database objects. PostgreSQL tracks dependencies in pg_depend catalog: views depend on tables, foreign keys depend on referenced tables, functions depend on types. DROP operations check dependencies: RESTRICT (default) fails if dependents exist, CASCADE drops object and all dependents recursively.

Common Dependencies: Views → tables/columns (view breaks if column dropped), Foreign keys → referenced tables (can't drop referenced table), Functions → tables/types (function breaks if type changed), Triggers → functions (trigger needs function), Indexes → tables (dropped with table). Query pg_depend or information_schema views to inspect dependencies before schema changes.

Dependency Tracking Syntax
-- Drop with RESTRICT (fails if dependencies)
DROP TABLE table_name RESTRICT;

-- Drop with CASCADE (drops dependents)
DROP TABLE table_name CASCADE;

-- Query dependencies
SELECT 
    dependent.relname AS dependent_object,
    dependent.relkind AS object_type,
    referenced.relname AS referenced_object
FROM pg_depend d
JOIN pg_class dependent ON d.objid = dependent.oid
JOIN pg_class referenced ON d.refobjid = referenced.oid
WHERE referenced.relname = 'your_table';

🎯 Example: Dependency Management

-- Create base table
CREATE TABLE products_deps (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL
);

-- Create view depending on table
CREATE VIEW expensive_products AS
SELECT * FROM products_deps WHERE price > 1000;

-- Create dependent table with foreign key
CREATE TABLE reviews_deps (
    review_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_id INTEGER REFERENCES products_deps(product_id),
    rating INTEGER
);

-- Try to drop products_deps with RESTRICT (fails)
-- DROP TABLE products_deps RESTRICT;
-- ERROR: cannot drop table products_deps because other objects depend on it
-- DETAIL: view expensive_products depends on table products_deps
--         constraint reviews_deps_product_id_fkey on table reviews_deps depends on table products_deps

-- Drop with CASCADE (removes table, view, and FK constraint)
DROP TABLE products_deps CASCADE;
-- NOTICE: drop cascades to view expensive_products
-- NOTICE: drop cascades to constraint reviews_deps_product_id_fkey on table reviews_deps

-- View and foreign key removed, reviews_deps table remains but FK gone
SELECT * FROM reviews_deps;  -- Works, FK constraint dropped
Result (after CASCADE):
 review_id | product_id | rating
-----------+------------+--------
(0 rows)
Explanation: Dependency tracking prevents orphaned objects. products_deps has dependents: expensive_products view and reviews_deps foreign key. DROP RESTRICT fails listing dependencies. DROP CASCADE removes products_deps + view + FK constraint (but not reviews_deps table). reviews_deps remains but product_id no longer constrained. CASCADE powerful but dangerous - can drop unexpected objects. Best practice: query dependencies before dropping (check pg_depend), drop dependents manually first for control, use CASCADE only when understanding full impact, test on staging, keep database backups. Dependency chain can be deep: table → view → function → trigger.

📝 Chapter 6: Data Manipulation

About Data Manipulation

Data Manipulation Language (DML) comprises SQL commands for inserting, updating, and deleting data in database tables. This chapter covers the core operations for modifying data: INSERT (adding new rows), UPDATE (modifying existing rows), DELETE (removing rows), and the RETURNING clause (retrieving modified data).

Data manipulation is fundamental to database operations - after defining tables with DDL (Chapter 5), you use DML to populate tables with data, maintain accuracy through updates, remove obsolete records, and retrieve information about changes. PostgreSQL provides powerful DML features including multi-row operations, subquery support, conflict resolution, and transaction safety.

➕

INSERT

Add new rows to tables with single or bulk operations

✏️

UPDATE

Modify existing data with conditional updates

🗑️

DELETE

Remove rows based on conditions or truncate entire tables

↩️

RETURNING

Retrieve modified data in single operation

6.1 Inserting Data

📖 Full Description

INSERT Statement adds new rows to tables. Basic syntax: INSERT INTO table_name (columns) VALUES (values). PostgreSQL supports multiple insert methods: single-row inserts, multi-row inserts (comma-separated VALUES), INSERT from SELECT queries, and INSERT with ON CONFLICT for upsert operations. Columns can be explicitly listed or omitted (inserts into all columns in order).

Key Features: Default values used for omitted columns, SERIAL/IDENTITY columns auto-generate values, constraints validated during insert, foreign keys checked, triggers fired. INSERT returns affected row count. Use RETURNING clause to get inserted data (including generated values). INSERT is atomic - succeeds completely or rolls back entirely within transaction.

Best Practices: Always specify column names (avoids breakage if table structure changes), use prepared statements to prevent SQL injection, batch multiple inserts into single statement for performance, handle constraint violations gracefully, use COPY command for bulk loading large datasets (faster than INSERT).

INSERT Syntax
-- Basic INSERT
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, ...)
VALUES (value1, value2, ...);

-- Multi-row INSERT
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2)
VALUES 
    (value1a, value2a),
    (value1b, value2b),
    (value1c, value2c);

-- INSERT from SELECT
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2)
SELECT column1, column2
FROM other_table
WHERE condition;

-- INSERT with DEFAULT values
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2)
VALUES (value1, DEFAULT);

-- INSERT all columns (omit column list)
INSERT INTO table_name
VALUES (value1, value2, value3, ...);

-- INSERT with RETURNING
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2)
VALUES (value1, value2)
RETURNING *;  -- or specific columns

-- INSERT with ON CONFLICT (upsert)
INSERT INTO table_name (id, column1, column2)
VALUES (1, value1, value2)
ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE
SET column1 = EXCLUDED.column1, column2 = EXCLUDED.column2;

🎯 Example 1: Basic Single-Row INSERT

-- Insert new customer
INSERT INTO customers (first_name, last_name, email, city, country)
VALUES ('Alice', 'Johnson', 'alice.johnson@email.com', 'Seattle', 'USA');

-- Insert with auto-generated customer_id (SERIAL)
-- Insert with default values for optional columns
INSERT INTO customers (first_name, last_name, email)
VALUES ('Bob', 'Smith', 'bob.smith@email.com');

-- Verify inserts
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, city, country, registration_date
FROM customers
WHERE email IN ('alice.johnson@email.com', 'bob.smith@email.com')
ORDER BY customer_id;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |          email           |  city   | country |     registration_date
-------------+------------+-----------+--------------------------+---------+---------+---------------------------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | alice.johnson@email.com  | Seattle | USA     | 2026-01-25 10:30:00
        1002 | Bob        | Smith     | bob.smith@email.com      | NULL    | USA     | 2026-01-25 10:30:01
(2 rows)
Explanation: First INSERT provides all five columns - customer_id auto-generated by SERIAL, registration_date uses DEFAULT (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP). Second INSERT only provides required columns (first_name, last_name, email) - city remains NULL, country uses table default ('USA'). Both inserts succeed, return 1 row affected. Use explicit column lists to avoid issues when table structure changes. SERIAL columns (customer_id) automatically increment - no need to specify value.

🎯 Example 2: Multi-Row INSERT (Bulk Insert)

-- Insert multiple products in single statement
INSERT INTO products (product_name, category_id, price, stock_quantity)
VALUES 
    ('Wireless Mouse', 1, 29.99, 150),
    ('USB-C Cable', 1, 12.99, 300),
    ('Laptop Stand', 2, 49.99, 75),
    ('Desk Lamp', 2, 34.99, 100),
    ('Notebook Set', 3, 15.99, 200);

-- Verify bulk insert
SELECT product_id, product_name, category_id, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
WHERE product_name IN ('Wireless Mouse', 'USB-C Cable', 'Laptop Stand', 'Desk Lamp', 'Notebook Set')
ORDER BY product_id;
Result:
 product_id |  product_name   | category_id | price | stock_quantity
------------+-----------------+-------------+-------+----------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse  |           1 | 29.99 |            150
       2002 | USB-C Cable     |           1 | 12.99 |            300
       2003 | Laptop Stand    |           2 | 49.99 |             75
       2004 | Desk Lamp       |           2 | 34.99 |            100
       2005 | Notebook Set    |           3 | 15.99 |            200
(5 rows)

INSERT 0 5
Explanation: Multi-row INSERT adds 5 products in single statement - much more efficient than 5 separate INSERTs (reduces round-trips, transaction overhead). All rows inserted atomically - either all succeed or all fail. PostgreSQL returns "INSERT 0 5" indicating 5 rows inserted (0 is OID, deprecated). Product_id values auto-generated sequentially. Multi-row syntax: VALUES clause with comma-separated tuples. Performance tip: batch large inserts into groups of 100-1000 rows for optimal performance.

🎯 Example 3: INSERT from SELECT (Copy Data Between Tables)

-- Create archive table for old orders
CREATE TABLE orders_archive (
    order_id INTEGER,
    customer_id INTEGER,
    order_date DATE,
    status VARCHAR(20),
    total_amount NUMERIC(10,2)
);

-- Insert orders from 2025 into archive
INSERT INTO orders_archive (order_id, customer_id, order_date, status, total_amount)
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, status, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE order_date < '2026-01-01';

-- Verify archived orders
SELECT COUNT(*) AS archived_orders, MIN(order_date) AS oldest, MAX(order_date) AS newest
FROM orders_archive;
Result:
 archived_orders |  oldest    |  newest
-----------------+------------+------------
             143 | 2025-01-05 | 2025-12-31
(1 row)

INSERT 0 143
Explanation: INSERT...SELECT copies data from SELECT query into target table. Useful for data archival, creating backups, populating summary tables, migrating data between tables. SELECT can include WHERE clause (filter), JOIN (combine data), aggregate functions (summarize). Number of columns and data types must match between SELECT and INSERT. Transaction ensures atomic operation - all 143 rows inserted together. After archiving, original orders could be deleted: DELETE FROM orders WHERE order_date < '2026-01-01'. INSERT...SELECT more efficient than cursor-based row-by-row copying.

🎯 Example 4: INSERT with RETURNING Clause

-- Insert order and immediately return generated ID
INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, order_date, status, total_amount)
VALUES (1001, CURRENT_DATE, 'pending', 0.00)
RETURNING order_id, order_date, status;

-- Insert multiple order items and return all inserted data
INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price)
VALUES 
    (3001, 2001, 2, 29.99),
    (3001, 2003, 1, 49.99),
    (3001, 2005, 3, 15.99)
RETURNING item_id, order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price, 
          (quantity * unit_price) AS line_total;
Result (first INSERT):
 order_id | order_date |  status
----------+------------+---------
     3001 | 2026-01-25 | pending
(1 row)
Result (second INSERT):
 item_id | order_id | product_id | quantity | unit_price | line_total
---------+----------+------------+----------+------------+------------
    5001 |     3001 |       2001 |        2 |      29.99 |      59.98
    5002 |     3001 |       2003 |        1 |      49.99 |      49.99
    5003 |     3001 |       2005 |        3 |      15.99 |      47.97
(3 rows)
Explanation: RETURNING clause retrieves inserted data in same operation - eliminates need for separate SELECT query. Returns generated SERIAL values (order_id, item_id), default values (order_date), and inserted values. RETURNING * returns all columns, or specify columns explicitly. Can include expressions/calculations (quantity * unit_price). Useful for: getting auto-generated IDs for subsequent inserts, confirming inserted values, audit logging, returning data to application layer. Works with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. Reduces round-trips to database - improves performance.

🎯 Example 5: INSERT with ON CONFLICT (Upsert)

-- Create product inventory summary table
CREATE TABLE product_summary (
    product_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    total_sold INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    last_updated TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Insert or update product summary (upsert)
INSERT INTO product_summary (product_id, product_name, total_sold)
VALUES (2001, 'Wireless Mouse', 10)
ON CONFLICT (product_id) 
DO UPDATE SET 
    total_sold = product_summary.total_sold + EXCLUDED.total_sold,
    last_updated = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
RETURNING product_id, product_name, total_sold, last_updated;

-- Try upsert again (will update existing row)
INSERT INTO product_summary (product_id, product_name, total_sold)
VALUES (2001, 'Wireless Mouse', 5)
ON CONFLICT (product_id)
DO UPDATE SET 
    total_sold = product_summary.total_sold + EXCLUDED.total_sold,
    last_updated = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
RETURNING product_id, product_name, total_sold, last_updated;
Result (first upsert - INSERT):
 product_id |  product_name  | total_sold |     last_updated
------------+----------------+------------+-------------------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse |         10 | 2026-01-25 10:35:00
(1 row)
Result (second upsert - UPDATE):
 product_id |  product_name  | total_sold |     last_updated
------------+----------------+------------+-------------------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse |         15 | 2026-01-25 10:35:05
(1 row)
Explanation: INSERT...ON CONFLICT implements upsert (INSERT or UPDATE): first execution inserts new row (product_id 2001 doesn't exist), second execution updates existing row (conflict on PRIMARY KEY). ON CONFLICT (product_id) specifies conflict target (must be unique constraint or index). DO UPDATE uses EXCLUDED.column to reference values from INSERT attempt. Use case: maintaining aggregate tables, preventing duplicates, idempotent operations. Alternative: DO NOTHING ignores conflicts without error. More efficient than SELECT-then-INSERT/UPDATE pattern - atomic operation, fewer round-trips. EXCLUDED pseudo-table contains values that would have been inserted.

6.2 Updating Data

📖 Full Description
