----------+------------------+---------------+-------------+-----------
 1        | Premium          |             3 |         245 |     66.66
 1        | Standard         |             5 |        1000 |     24.38
 1        | All Price Tiers  |             8 |        1245 |     39.50
 2        | Premium          |             3 |         275 |     59.99
 2        | Standard         |             2 |         375 |     29.99
 2        | All Price Tiers  |             5 |         650 |     45.99
 3        | Standard         |             3 |         425 |     24.99
 3        | All Price Tiers  |             3 |         425 |     24.99
 All Categories| Premium      |             6 |         520 |     63.33
 All Categories| Standard     |            10 |        1800 |     25.79
 All Categories| All Price Tiers|          16 |        2320 |     38.27
(11 rows)
Explanation: CUBE (category_id, price_tier) generates all combinations: (category,tier), (category), (tier), (). Category 1 Premium: 3 products, avg $66.66. Category 1 total: 8 products (Premium+Standard). All categories Premium: 6 products, avg $63.33. Grand total: 16 products, avg $38.27. CUBE shows data from every angle - by category, by price tier, by both, and overall. Powerful for dashboards and analytics showing multiple dimensions simultaneously. Warning: CUBE(a,b,c) produces 8 groupings, CUBE(a,b,c,d) produces 16 - grows exponentially.

🎯 Example 4: GROUPING Function for Identifying Aggregation Levels

-- Sales report with clear aggregation indicators
SELECT 
    category_id,
    EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_date) AS month,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    GROUPING(category_id) AS cat_total,
    GROUPING(EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_date)) AS month_total,
    CASE
        WHEN GROUPING(category_id) = 1 AND GROUPING(EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_date)) = 1 
            THEN 'GRAND TOTAL'
        WHEN GROUPING(category_id) = 1 THEN 'CATEGORY TOTAL'
        WHEN GROUPING(EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_date)) = 1 THEN 'MONTH TOTAL'
        ELSE 'DETAIL'
    END AS row_type
FROM products
WHERE created_date >= '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY ROLLUP (category_id, EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_date))
ORDER BY category_id NULLS LAST, month NULLS LAST;
Result:
 category_id | month | product_count | cat_total | month_total | row_type
-------------+-------+---------------+-----------+-------------+---------------
           1 |     1 |             3 |         0 |           0 | DETAIL
           1 |  NULL |             3 |         0 |           1 | MONTH TOTAL
           2 |     1 |             2 |         0 |           0 | DETAIL
           2 |  NULL |             2 |         0 |           1 | MONTH TOTAL
           3 |     1 |             1 |         0 |           0 | DETAIL
           3 |  NULL |             1 |         0 |           1 | MONTH TOTAL
        NULL |  NULL |             6 |         1 |           1 | GRAND TOTAL
(7 rows)
Explanation: GROUPING function crucial for distinguishing NULL from aggregation vs actual NULL values. cat_total=0 means category_id is actual value, =1 means it's aggregated (NULL represents total). Detail rows: both GROUPING=0 (category 1, month 1, 3 products). Month total rows: cat_total=0, month_total=1 (category 1 total across all months). Grand total: both GROUPING=1 (overall total). CASE expression labels row types for report clarity. Use GROUPING_ID(col1, col2) for bitmask of all columns: GROUPING_ID returns integer representing which columns aggregated. Essential for formatting hierarchical reports correctly.

🎯 Example 5: Combining GROUPING SETS with HAVING and Complex Filters

-- Advanced sales analysis with multiple dimensions and filters
WITH sales_data AS (
    SELECT 
        c.country,
        o.status,
        DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS order_month,
        o.total_amount
    FROM orders o
    INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    WHERE o.order_date >= '2025-06-01'
)
SELECT 
    COALESCE(country, 'All Countries') AS country,
    COALESCE(status, 'All Statuses') AS status,
    TO_CHAR(order_month, 'YYYY-MM') AS month,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    SUM(total_amount)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS total_sales,
    AVG(total_amount)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS avg_order_value
FROM sales_data
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
    (country, status, order_month),  -- Detail by all dimensions
    (country, status),                -- Subtotal by country and status
    (country, order_month),           -- Subtotal by country and month
    (country),                        -- Country totals
    ()                                -- Grand total
)
HAVING SUM(total_amount) > 100      -- Filter out small groupings
ORDER BY 
    GROUPING(country),
    country,
    GROUPING(status),
    status,
    order_month NULLS LAST
LIMIT 15;
Result:
 country | status     | month   | order_count | total_sales | avg_order_value
---------+------------+---------+-------------+-------------+-----------------
 USA     | confirmed  | NULL    |           5 |     1234.50 |          246.90
 USA     | pending    | NULL    |           3 |      845.67 |          281.89
 USA     | shipped    | NULL    |           8 |     2156.88 |          269.61
 USA     | All Statuses|2025-06 |           4 |      987.45 |          246.86
 USA     | All Statuses|2025-07 |           6 |     1589.23 |          264.87
 USA     | All Statuses|2025-08 |           6 |     1660.37 |          276.73
 USA     | All Statuses| NULL   |          16 |     4237.05 |          264.82
 All Countries|All Statuses|NULL|          16 |     4237.05 |          264.82
(8 rows)
Explanation: GROUPING SETS with 5 different grouping levels provides flexible multi-dimensional analysis. Detail level: country+status+month shows granular data. Intermediate levels: country+status (status performance by country), country+month (monthly trends by country). Summary levels: country totals, grand total. HAVING SUM(total_amount) > 100 filters out insignificant groupings (applies to all grouping levels). CTE simplifies main query. USA confirmed status: 5 orders, $1234.50 total. USA June 2025: 4 orders, $987.45. GROUPING SETS more flexible than ROLLUP/CUBE - specify exactly which combinations needed without generating unnecessary groupings.

7.2.5 Window Function Processing

📖 Full Description

Window Functions perform calculations across sets of rows related to current row - without collapsing rows like GROUP BY. Syntax: function() OVER (window_specification). Window specification includes PARTITION BY (divide into groups), ORDER BY (sort within partition), and frame clause (define calculation range). Window functions enable running totals, rankings, moving averages, lag/lead values, and sophisticated analytics while preserving individual rows.

Common Window Functions: Ranking: ROW_NUMBER() (unique sequential numbers), RANK() (same rank for ties, gaps), DENSE_RANK() (no gaps), NTILE(n) (divide into n buckets). Aggregate: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX over window. Offset: LAG() (previous row value), LEAD() (next row value), FIRST_VALUE(), LAST_VALUE(), NTH_VALUE(). Statistical: PERCENT_RANK(), CUME_DIST().

Frame Specification: Defines which rows in partition to include in calculation. ROWS BETWEEN start AND end or RANGE BETWEEN. UNBOUNDED PRECEDING (partition start), CURRENT ROW (current row), UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING (partition end), n PRECEDING/FOLLOWING (n rows before/after). Default frame: RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW. Critical for running totals and moving averages.

Window Function Syntax
-- Basic window function
SELECT column,
       function() OVER (PARTITION BY column ORDER BY column) AS result
FROM table;

-- Window specification components
function() OVER (
    PARTITION BY col1, col2       -- Optional: divide into groups
    ORDER BY col3 [ASC|DESC]      -- Optional: sort within partition
    [frame_clause]                -- Optional: define row range
)

-- Frame clause options
ROWS BETWEEN start AND end
RANGE BETWEEN start AND end

-- Frame boundaries
UNBOUNDED PRECEDING    -- Start of partition
n PRECEDING            -- n rows before current
CURRENT ROW            -- Current row
n FOLLOWING            -- n rows after current
UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING    -- End of partition

-- Common patterns
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY col)                    -- Unique row numbers
RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY cat ORDER BY val)         -- Ranking within groups
SUM(col) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING)  -- Running total
AVG(col) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)  -- Moving avg
LAG(col, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date)                    -- Previous row value
LEAD(col, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date)                   -- Next row value

-- Named window (reusable)
SELECT col, SUM(val) OVER w, AVG(val) OVER w
FROM table
WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY cat ORDER BY date);

🎯 Example 1: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK

-- Ranking products by price within each category
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    category_id,
    price,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category_id ORDER BY price DESC) AS row_num,
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category_id ORDER BY price DESC) AS rank,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category_id ORDER BY price DESC) AS dense_rank
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY category_id, price DESC;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | category_id | price | row_num | rank | dense_rank
------------+---------------------+-------------+-------+---------+------+------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |           1 | 79.99 |       1 |    1 |          1
       2007 | Monitor Stand       |           1 | 39.99 |       2 |    2 |          2
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |           1 | 32.99 |       3 |    3 |          3
       2009 | USB Hub             |           1 | 32.99 |       4 |    3 |          3
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |           1 | 14.29 |       5 |    5 |          4
       2010 | Laptop Bag          |           2 | 69.99 |       1 |    1 |          1
       2003 | Laptop Stand        |           2 | 49.99 |       2 |    2 |          2
       2007 | Monitor Stand       |           2 | 39.99 |       3 |    3 |          3
       2008 | Desk Organizer      |           2 | 24.99 |       4 |    4 |          4
       2005 | Notebook Set        |           3 | 34.99 |       1 |    1 |          1
       2012 | Pen Set             |           3 | 24.99 |       2 |    2 |          2
       2013 | Sticky Notes        |           3 | 15.99 |       3 |    3 |          3
(12 rows)
Explanation: PARTITION BY category_id creates separate ranking within each category. ORDER BY price DESC ranks most expensive first. ROW_NUMBER() assigns unique sequential numbers (1,2,3,4,5) - even for ties (Wireless Mouse and USB Hub both $32.99 get different row_num). RANK() gives same rank to ties, then skips numbers (3,3,5). DENSE_RANK() gives same rank to ties without gaps (3,3,4). Category 1: Mechanical Keyboard ranked 1st ($79.99), tied products both rank 3rd. Use ROW_NUMBER for unique numbering, RANK for competition-style ranking (1st, 2nd, 2nd, 4th), DENSE_RANK for dense ranking (1st, 2nd, 2nd, 3rd).

🎯 Example 2: Running Totals and Cumulative Aggregates

-- Calculate running total of sales by date
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount,
    SUM(total_amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date, order_id) AS running_total,
    AVG(total_amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date, order_id) AS running_avg,
    COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY order_date, order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-20'
ORDER BY order_date, order_id;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | order_date | total_amount | running_total | running_avg | order_count
----------+-------------+------------+--------------+---------------+-------------+-------------
     3007 |        1005 | 2026-01-20 |       445.00 |        445.00 |      445.00 |           1
     3001 |        1001 | 2026-01-21 |       157.94 |        602.94 |      301.47 |           2
     3006 |        1004 | 2026-01-22 |       159.50 |        762.44 |      254.15 |           3
     3004 |        1001 | 2026-01-23 |       334.94 |       1097.38 |      274.35 |           4
     3005 |        1003 | 2026-01-24 |       289.99 |       1387.37 |      277.47 |           5
(5 rows)
Explanation: Window functions without PARTITION BY operate on entire result set. ORDER BY order_date, order_id establishes calculation sequence. SUM() OVER calculates running total: first order $445.00, after second order $445.00 + $157.94 = $602.94. Default frame RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING means sum all rows from start to current. running_avg shows average of all orders up to current row. order_count shows cumulative count. Each row retains detail (unlike GROUP BY which collapses). Running totals critical for financial reports, cumulative metrics, dashboard analytics. Add ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for explicit frame.

🎯 Example 3: LAG and LEAD for Row Comparison

-- Compare each order with previous and next orders
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount,
    LAG(total_amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS previous_order_amount,
    LEAD(total_amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_order_amount,
    total_amount - LAG(total_amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS change_from_previous,
    CASE 
        WHEN total_amount > LAG(total_amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) THEN 'Increased'
        WHEN total_amount < LAG(total_amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) THEN 'Decreased'
        ELSE 'Same/First'
    END AS trend
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-20'
ORDER BY order_date;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | order_date | total_amount | previous_order_amount | next_order_amount | change_from_previous | trend
----------+-------------+------------+--------------+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------+-----------
     3007 |        1005 | 2026-01-20 |       445.00 |                  NULL |            157.94 |                 NULL | Same/First
     3001 |        1001 | 2026-01-21 |       157.94 |                445.00 |            159.50 |              -287.06 | Decreased
     3006 |        1004 | 2026-01-22 |       159.50 |                157.94 |            334.94 |                 1.56 | Increased
     3004 |        1001 | 2026-01-23 |       334.94 |                159.50 |            289.99 |               175.44 | Increased
     3005 |        1003 | 2026-01-24 |       289.99 |                334.94 |              NULL |               -44.95 | Decreased
(5 rows)
Explanation: LAG(column, offset) retrieves value from previous row (offset rows back). LEAD(column, offset) retrieves value from next row. LAG(total_amount, 1) gets previous order's amount - NULL for first row. LEAD returns next order's amount - NULL for last row. change_from_previous calculates difference from previous order ($157.94 - $445.00 = -$287.06). CASE expression determines trend. LAG/LEAD essential for time-series analysis, trend detection, period-over-period comparisons. Can specify default value: LAG(total_amount, 1, 0) returns 0 instead of NULL.

🎯 Example 4: Moving Averages with Frame Specification

-- Calculate 3-day moving average of order amounts
SELECT 
    order_date,
    order_id,
    total_amount,
    AVG(total_amount) OVER (
        ORDER BY order_date, order_id
        ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    )::NUMERIC(10,2) AS moving_avg_3day,
    SUM(total_amount) OVER (
        ORDER BY order_date, order_id
        ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    )::NUMERIC(10,2) AS rolling_sum_3day,
    COUNT(*) OVER (
        ORDER BY order_date, order_id
        ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS window_size
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-20'
ORDER BY order_date, order_id;
Result:
 order_date | order_id | total_amount | moving_avg_3day | rolling_sum_3day | window_size
------------+----------+--------------+-----------------+------------------+-------------
 2026-01-20 |     3007 |       445.00 |          445.00 |           445.00 |           1
 2026-01-21 |     3001 |       157.94 |          301.47 |           602.94 |           2
 2026-01-22 |     3006 |       159.50 |          254.15 |           762.44 |           3
 2026-01-23 |     3004 |       334.94 |          217.46 |           652.38 |           3
 2026-01-24 |     3005 |       289.99 |          261.48 |           784.43 |           3
(5 rows)
Explanation: ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW defines frame: include current row plus 2 rows before. First row: only itself (window_size 1), avg $445.00. Second row: 2 rows (previous + current), avg $301.47. Third row onward: 3 rows (2 previous + current). moving_avg_3day smooths fluctuations: row 4 averages $159.50, $334.94, $289.99 = $261.48. Different from cumulative average (uses all previous rows). Frame types: ROWS (physical rows), RANGE (logical range based on ORDER BY values). Moving averages essential for trend analysis, smoothing noisy data, detecting patterns. Adjust window size (2 PRECEDING for 3-row window, 6 PRECEDING for 7-row window).

🎯 Example 5: NTILE, PERCENT_RANK, FIRST_VALUE, LAST_VALUE

-- Advanced analytics: percentiles, quartiles, and value access
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    category_id,
    price,
    NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY price) AS price_quartile,
    PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price)::NUMERIC(5,4) AS percent_rank,
    FIRST_VALUE(product_name) OVER (PARTITION BY category_id ORDER BY price DESC) AS most_expensive_in_category,
    LAST_VALUE(product_name) OVER (
        PARTITION BY category_id 
        ORDER BY price DESC
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    ) AS least_expensive_in_category
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 10;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | category_id | price | price_quartile | percent_rank | most_expensive_in_category | least_expensive_in_category
------------+---------------------+-------------+-------+----------------+--------------+----------------------------+-----------------------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |           1 | 79.99 |              4 |       1.0000 | Mechanical Keyboard        | USB-C Cable
       2010 | Laptop Bag          |           2 | 69.99 |              4 |       0.9091 | Laptop Bag                 | Desk Organizer
       2003 | Laptop Stand        |           2 | 49.99 |              3 |       0.8182 | Laptop Bag                 | Desk Organizer
       2007 | Monitor Stand       |           1 | 39.99 |              3 |       0.7273 | Mechanical Keyboard        | USB-C Cable
       2005 | Notebook Set        |           3 | 34.99 |              3 |       0.6364 | Notebook Set               | Sticky Notes
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |           1 | 32.99 |              2 |       0.5455 | Mechanical Keyboard        | USB-C Cable
       2009 | USB Hub             |           1 | 32.99 |              2 |       0.5455 | Mechanical Keyboard        | USB-C Cable
       2012 | Pen Set             |           3 | 24.99 |              2 |       0.3636 | Notebook Set               | Sticky Notes
       2008 | Desk Organizer      |           2 | 24.99 |              1 |       0.2727 | Laptop Bag                 | Desk Organizer
       2013 | Sticky Notes        |           3 | 15.99 |              1 |       0.1818 | Notebook Set               | Sticky Notes
(10 rows)
Explanation: NTILE(4) divides products into 4 equal groups (quartiles) by price: quartile 4 = most expensive 25%, quartile 1 = least expensive 25%. PERCENT_RANK() calculates percentile: 0.0 = lowest, 1.0 = highest. Mechanical Keyboard at 100th percentile (most expensive). FIRST_VALUE gets first value in window: most_expensive_in_category returns category's priciest product. LAST_VALUE requires ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING - default frame only goes to CURRENT ROW. LAST_VALUE returns category's cheapest product. These functions power advanced analytics: customer segmentation (NTILE for RFM analysis), percentile calculations, comparison to group extremes. Named window (WINDOW w AS ...) can simplify repeated window specs.

7.3 Select Lists

📖 About Select Lists

Select Lists specify output columns in queries. Can include table columns, expressions, constants, aggregate functions, window functions, subqueries, and wildcards (*). This section covers select-list items, column labeling, and DISTINCT for removing duplicates.

7.3.1 Select-List Items

📖 Full Description

Select-List Items define query output columns. Types: column references (table.column), expressions (price * 1.1), aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM), window functions (ROW_NUMBER), scalar subqueries (SELECT returning single value), constants ('literal'), wildcards (* or table.*). Each item becomes output column. Use commas to separate items. Can mix different item types in same query.

Expressions: Combine columns, operators, functions: price * quantity AS total, UPPER(name), EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date), CASE expressions. PostgreSQL evaluates expressions for each output row. Expressions can reference any column from FROM clause (after WHERE filtering). Cannot reference column aliases from same SELECT (except in ORDER BY).

Best Practices: Use explicit column names (not *) for production queries, give expressions meaningful aliases, qualify column names when joining tables (table.column), use * only for ad-hoc queries, avoid expensive expressions in SELECT if not needed, use subqueries sparingly (performance impact), order SELECT items logically (identifiers first, then data, then calculations).

Select-List Syntax
-- Column references
SELECT column1, column2, table1.column3
FROM table1;

-- Wildcard
SELECT * FROM table1;
SELECT table1.*, table2.column FROM table1 JOIN table2;

-- Expressions
SELECT 
    column1,
    column2 * 1.1 AS adjusted,
    UPPER(column3) AS upper_name,
    CASE WHEN column4 > 10 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Low' END AS category
FROM table1;

-- Aggregate functions
SELECT category, COUNT(*), AVG(price), SUM(quantity)
FROM products
GROUP BY category;

-- Window functions
SELECT column1, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY column2)
FROM table1;

-- Scalar subqueries
SELECT 
    column1,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table2 WHERE table2.id = table1.id) AS count
FROM table1;

-- Constants
SELECT 'Literal String', 123, TRUE, NULL;

🎯 Example 1: Column References and Wildcards

-- Wildcard selects all columns
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1 LIMIT 3;

-- Specific columns
SELECT product_id, product_name, price FROM products WHERE category_id = 1 LIMIT 3;

-- Mix wildcard with specific columns (from different tables)
SELECT 
    c.*,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE c.city = 'Seattle'
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name, c.email, c.city, c.country, c.registration_date
LIMIT 3;
Result (specific columns):
 product_id |    product_name     | price
------------+---------------------+-------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99
       2002 | USB-C Cable         | 14.29
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99
(3 rows)
Explanation: SELECT * returns all table columns - convenient for exploration but problematic for production (column order changes break applications, returns unnecessary data). Specific column selection more efficient and maintainable. c.* expands to all customers columns. When using *, must include all non-aggregated columns in GROUP BY. Explicit column lists self-document queries and protect against schema changes.

🎯 Example 2: Expressions and Calculations

-- Arithmetic expressions
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price AS original_price,
    price * 0.9 AS discounted_price,
    price * 1.08 AS price_with_tax,
    stock_quantity,
    price * stock_quantity AS inventory_value,
    ROUND(price * stock_quantity, 2) AS inventory_value_rounded
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
ORDER BY inventory_value DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | original_price | discounted_price | price_with_tax | stock_quantity | inventory_value | inventory_value_rounded
------------+---------------------+----------------+------------------+----------------+----------------+-----------------+-------------------------
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |          14.29 |            12.86 |          15.43 |            295 |         4215.55 |                 4215.55
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |          79.99 |            71.99 |          86.39 |             50 |         3999.50 |                 3999.50
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |          32.99 |            29.69 |          35.63 |            145 |         4783.55 |                 4783.55
(3 rows)
Explanation: Expressions perform calculations on each row. price * 0.9 applies 10% discount. price * 1.08 adds 8% tax. price * stock_quantity calculates total inventory value. ROUND() function rounds to 2 decimal places. Expressions can use arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /), functions (ROUND, ABS, POWER), and column values. Each expression becomes output column. Use aliases (AS discounted_price) for readability. Expressions evaluated after WHERE but before ORDER BY.

🎯 Example 3: String Functions and CASE Expressions

-- String manipulation and conditional logic
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
    UPPER(last_name) AS last_name_upper,
    LOWER(email) AS email_lower,
    LENGTH(email) AS email_length,
    CASE 
        WHEN city IS NULL THEN 'Location Unknown'
        ELSE city || ', ' || country
    END AS location,
    CASE 
        WHEN registration_date >= '2026-01-01' THEN 'New Customer'
        WHEN registration_date >= '2025-01-01' THEN 'Recent Customer'
        ELSE 'Established Customer'
    END AS customer_type
FROM customers
ORDER BY customer_id
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |   full_name   | last_name_upper |      email_lower        | email_length |      location      |   customer_type
-------------+------------+-----------+---------------+-----------------+-------------------------+--------------+--------------------+---------------------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | Alice Johnson | JOHNSON         | alice.johnson@email.com |           23 | Seattle, USA       | Established Customer
        1002 | Bob        | Smith     | Bob Smith     | SMITH           | bob.smith@email.com     |           19 | NULL               | Established Customer
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     | Charlie Brown | BROWN           | charlie.brown@email.com |           23 | Denver, USA        | New Customer
(3 rows)
Explanation: String concatenation with || operator: first_name || ' ' || last_name creates full name. UPPER/LOWER convert case. LENGTH returns character count. CASE expression provides conditional logic: checks registration_date to categorize customers. CASE evaluates conditions sequentially, returns first match. ELSE provides default. NULL handling: city IS NULL returns 'Location Unknown'. String functions (UPPER, LOWER, SUBSTRING, TRIM) and CASE expressions essential for data formatting and categorization.

🎯 Example 4: Scalar Subqueries in SELECT

-- Subqueries returning single values per row
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) 
     FROM orders o 
     WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) AS total_orders,
    (SELECT COALESCE(SUM(total_amount), 0)
     FROM orders o 
     WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) AS total_spent,
    (SELECT MAX(order_date)
     FROM orders o
     WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) AS last_order_date,
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) = 0 
            THEN 'No Orders'
        WHEN (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) >= CURRENT_DATE - 30 
            THEN 'Active'
        ELSE 'Inactive'
    END AS customer_status
FROM customers c
WHERE c.city = 'Seattle'
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;
Result:
 customer_id | customer_name | total_orders | total_spent | last_order_date | customer_status
-------------+---------------+--------------+-------------+-----------------+-----------------
        1001 | Alice Johnson |            3 |      647.82 | 2026-01-23      | Active
        1008 | Henry Ford    |            2 |      389.50 | 2026-01-15      | Active
        1015 | Oscar Wilde   |            0 |        0.00 | NULL            | No Orders
(3 rows)
Explanation: Scalar subqueries in SELECT must return single value (one row, one column). Correlated subqueries reference outer query (o.customer_id = c.customer_id). First subquery counts customer's orders. Second sums spending (COALESCE handles NULL for zero orders). Third finds latest order date. Fourth determines customer status based on activity. Subqueries execute once per outer row - performance consideration for large datasets. Alternative: JOIN with GROUP BY typically faster. Use subqueries when logic requires it or for clarity.

🎯 Example 5: Combining Multiple Select-List Item Types

-- Complex SELECT with various item types
SELECT 
    p.product_id,                                    -- Column reference
    p.product_name,                                  -- Column reference
    'Product' AS item_type,                          -- Constant
    p.price,                                         -- Column
    p.price * 1.2 AS markup_price,                   -- Expression
    CASE 
        WHEN p.stock_quantity = 0 THEN 'Out of Stock'
        WHEN p.stock_quantity < 50 THEN 'Low Stock'
        ELSE 'In Stock'
    END AS availability,                             -- CASE expression
    (SELECT AVG(price) 
     FROM products 
     WHERE category_id = p.category_id) AS category_avg_price,  -- Scalar subquery
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY p.category_id ORDER BY p.price DESC) AS price_rank_in_category,  -- Window function
    NOW() AS report_timestamp                        -- Function call
FROM products p
WHERE p.stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY p.category_id, p.price DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | item_type | price | markup_price | availability | category_avg_price | price_rank_in_category |     report_timestamp
------------+---------------------+-----------+-------+--------------+--------------+--------------------+------------------------+-------------------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | Product   | 79.99 |        95.99 | In Stock     |              39.50 |                      1 | 2026-01-25 14:30:00
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | Product   | 39.99 |        47.99 | In Stock     |              39.50 |                      2 | 2026-01-25 14:30:00
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | Product   | 32.99 |        39.59 | In Stock     |              39.50 |                      3 | 2026-01-25 14:30:00
       2010 | Laptop Bag          | Product   | 69.99 |        83.99 | In Stock     |              45.99 |                      1 | 2026-01-25 14:30:00
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | Product   | 49.99 |        59.99 | In Stock     |              45.99 |                      2 | 2026-01-25 14:30:00
(5 rows)
Explanation: SELECT list combines all item types: plain columns (product_id, product_name), constants ('Product'), arithmetic expressions (price * 1.2), CASE logic (availability check), scalar subquery (category average), window function (ranking), function calls (NOW()). Each type serves different purpose. Constants useful for report labels. Expressions perform calculations. Subqueries fetch related data. Window functions provide analytics. Mix types as needed - PostgreSQL handles all seamlessly. Each item evaluated according to query processing order (after FROM/WHERE, before ORDER BY).

7.3.2 Column Labels

📖 Full Description

Column Labels (Aliases) rename output columns using AS keyword or implicit naming. Syntax: expression AS alias_name or expression alias_name. Labels improve readability, enable reference in ORDER BY, and provide meaningful names for expressions. Without aliases, expressions show as "?column?" or the full expression text. Labels appear in query results and client applications.

Alias Rules: Can contain letters, numbers, underscores. Must quote if contains spaces or special characters: "Full Name". Case-sensitive when quoted. Cannot reference aliases from same SELECT in WHERE or HAVING (evaluated before SELECT). Can reference in ORDER BY, HAVING when using GROUP BY. Avoid SQL keywords as aliases (SELECT, FROM, etc.) - quote if necessary.

Best Practices: Always use aliases for expressions and calculations, use AS keyword for clarity (though optional), choose descriptive names (total_price not tp), use snake_case or camelCase consistently, avoid spaces in aliases (use underscores), don't reuse table column names as aliases (can confuse), document complex expressions with clear aliases.

Column Label Syntax
-- AS keyword (recommended)
SELECT column AS alias_name FROM table;
SELECT expression AS alias_name FROM table;

-- Implicit alias (AS optional)
SELECT column alias_name FROM table;

-- Quoted aliases (for spaces/special chars)
SELECT column AS "Column Name" FROM table;
SELECT column AS "User's Email" FROM table;

-- Multiple aliases
SELECT 
    column1 AS alias1,
    column2 AS alias2,
    expression AS alias3
FROM table;

-- Referencing aliases
SELECT 
    price,
    quantity,
    price * quantity AS total
FROM table
ORDER BY total DESC;  -- Can reference alias in ORDER BY

🎯 Example 1: Basic Column Aliasing

-- Without aliases (unclear)
SELECT product_id, product_name, price, price * 0.9, price * 1.08, stock_quantity * price
FROM products
LIMIT 3;

-- With clear aliases
SELECT 
    product_id AS id,
    product_name AS name,
    price AS original_price,
    price * 0.9 AS sale_price,
    price * 1.08 AS price_with_tax,
    stock_quantity * price AS inventory_value
FROM products
ORDER BY inventory_value DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result (with aliases):
  id  |        name         | original_price | sale_price | price_with_tax | inventory_value
------+---------------------+----------------+------------+----------------+-----------------
 2001 | Wireless Mouse      |          32.99 |      29.69 |          35.63 |         4783.55
 2002 | USB-C Cable         |          14.29 |      12.86 |          15.43 |         4215.55
 2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |          79.99 |      71.99 |          86.39 |         3999.50
(3 rows)
Explanation: Without aliases, expressions appear as "?column?" or full expression text - unclear in results. With aliases, columns have meaningful names: sale_price (10% discount), price_with_tax (8% tax). Aliases improve result readability and make ORDER BY clearer (ORDER BY inventory_value vs ORDER BY stock_quantity * price). Aliases appear in client applications, reports, and API responses. AS keyword optional but recommended for clarity.

🎯 Example 2: Aliases in ORDER BY and Complex Expressions

-- Aliases enable clean ORDER BY
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
    EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(registration_date)) AS years_member,
    CASE 
        WHEN registration_date >= '2026-01-01' THEN 'New'
        WHEN registration_date >= '2025-01-01' THEN 'Recent'
        ELSE 'Established'
    END AS membership_status
FROM customers
ORDER BY years_member DESC, full_name ASC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 customer_id |   full_name   | years_member | membership_status
-------------+---------------+--------------+-------------------
        1025 | Aaron Williams|            5 | Established
        1012 | Betty White   |            4 | Established
        1018 | David Miller  |            3 | Established
        1001 | Alice Johnson |            1 | Recent
        1003 | Charlie Brown |            0 | New
(5 rows)
Explanation: ORDER BY can reference SELECT aliases: years_member and full_name instead of repeating complex expressions. Without aliases: ORDER BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(registration_date)) DESC, first_name || ' ' || last_name ASC - verbose and error-prone. Aliases evaluated in SELECT clause, then available in ORDER BY. Cannot use in WHERE: WHERE years_member > 2 fails (WHERE evaluated before SELECT). Use aliases to simplify ORDER BY, improve maintainability, and avoid expression duplication.

🎯 Example 3: Quoted Aliases for Special Characters

-- Quoted aliases for spaces and special characters
SELECT 
    product_id AS "Product ID",
    product_name AS "Product Name",
    price AS "Unit Price ($)",
    stock_quantity AS "Stock Qty",
    CASE 
        WHEN stock_quantity > 100 THEN 'High'
        WHEN stock_quantity > 50 THEN 'Medium'
        ELSE 'Low'
    END AS "Stock Level",
    price * stock_quantity AS "Inventory Value ($)"
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
ORDER BY "Inventory Value ($)" DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 Product ID |     Product Name    | Unit Price ($) | Stock Qty | Stock Level | Inventory Value ($)
------------+---------------------+----------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |          32.99 |       145 | High        |             4783.55
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |          14.29 |       295 | High        |             4215.55
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |          79.99 |        50 | Medium      |             3999.50
(3 rows)
Explanation: Double quotes allow spaces and special characters in aliases: "Product Name", "Unit Price ($)". Useful for report-style output matching business terminology. Quoted aliases are case-sensitive: "Product ID" != "product id". ORDER BY must use exact quoted name. Warning: quoted aliases more fragile (typos, case mismatches cause errors). Prefer unquoted snake_case aliases in application queries (product_id), use quoted aliases only for end-user reports. Unquoted aliases case-insensitive: product_id = PRODUCT_ID = Product_Id.

🎯 Example 4: Aliases for Aggregate Functions and Window Functions

-- Meaningful aliases for aggregates and window functions
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    AVG(price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS average_price,
    MIN(price) AS cheapest_product_price,
    MAX(price) AS most_expensive_price,
    SUM(stock_quantity) AS total_units_in_stock,
    STDDEV(price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS price_std_deviation,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS popularity_rank
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
GROUP BY category_id
ORDER BY popularity_rank;
Result:
 category_id | product_count | average_price | cheapest_product_price | most_expensive_price | total_units_in_stock | price_std_deviation | popularity_rank
-------------+---------------+---------------+------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+---------------------+-----------------
           1 |             8 |         39.50 |                  14.29 |                79.99 |                 1245 |               22.45 |               1
           2 |             5 |         45.99 |                  24.99 |                69.99 |                  650 |               18.32 |               2
           3 |             3 |         24.99 |                  15.99 |                34.99 |                  425 |                9.50 |               3
(3 rows)
Explanation: Aggregate functions return single value per group - aliases essential for clarity. product_count clearer than "count". average_price better than "avg". Window function ROW_NUMBER() benefits from descriptive alias (popularity_rank). Without aliases, aggregate columns might show as "?column?" or function name. Aliases make results self-documenting: total_units_in_stock immediately clear. Use domain-specific terminology in aliases (popularity_rank for business context). Cast to NUMERIC for cleaner decimal output. Aliases critical when results feed dashboards, reports, or APIs.

🎯 Example 5: Alias Limitations and Workarounds

-- CANNOT reference alias in WHERE (evaluated before SELECT)
-- This FAILS:
-- SELECT price * quantity AS total FROM order_items WHERE total > 100;

-- Workaround 1: Repeat expression
SELECT 
    item_id,
    product_id,
    quantity,
    unit_price,
    quantity * unit_price AS line_total
FROM order_items
WHERE quantity * unit_price > 100  -- Must repeat expression
ORDER BY line_total DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Workaround 2: Use subquery or CTE
WITH calculated AS (
    SELECT 
        item_id,
        product_id,
        quantity,
        unit_price,
        quantity * unit_price AS line_total
    FROM order_items
)
SELECT * 
FROM calculated
WHERE line_total > 100  -- Now can use alias
ORDER BY line_total DESC;
Result:
 item_id | product_id | quantity | unit_price | line_total
---------+------------+----------+------------+------------
    5001 |       2001 |        2 |      29.99 |      59.98
    5004 |       2006 |        2 |      79.99 |     159.98
    5007 |       2003 |        3 |      49.99 |     149.97
    5009 |       2006 |        2 |      79.99 |     159.98
(4 rows)
Explanation: WHERE clause evaluated before SELECT - cannot reference SELECT aliases. WHERE total > 100 fails because total doesn't exist yet. Workaround 1: repeat expression in WHERE (quantity * unit_price > 100). Acceptable for simple expressions but duplicates code. Workaround 2: CTE or subquery - calculate alias first, then filter. CTE approach cleaner for complex expressions: define once (line_total), use everywhere (WHERE, ORDER BY). CTEs improve maintainability when same calculation used multiple times. HAVING with GROUP BY can reference aliases because HAVING evaluated after SELECT.

7.3.3 DISTINCT

📖 Full Description

DISTINCT eliminates duplicate rows from result set. Syntax: SELECT DISTINCT columns FROM table. Compares all selected columns - rows identical across all columns removed (keeps one). DISTINCT operates after WHERE, before ORDER BY. Can significantly reduce result size when querying non-unique columns. Performance consideration: requires sort or hash operation to identify duplicates.

DISTINCT ON: PostgreSQL extension returns first row for each distinct value of specified expressions. Syntax: SELECT DISTINCT ON (expression) columns ORDER BY expression. Must ORDER BY the DISTINCT ON expression(s) first. Useful for getting one row per group (alternative to ROW_NUMBER() = 1). DISTINCT ON more flexible than standard DISTINCT - can specify which columns determine uniqueness.

Best Practices: Use DISTINCT when duplicates are problem (category names, customer cities), prefer GROUP BY for aggregates over DISTINCT (more flexible), combine with ORDER BY for predictable results, use DISTINCT ON carefully (non-standard SQL), consider if duplicates indicate data model issue, avoid DISTINCT on large result sets (performance cost), use indexes on DISTINCT columns when possible.

DISTINCT Syntax
-- Basic DISTINCT
SELECT DISTINCT column1, column2
FROM table;

-- DISTINCT with all columns
SELECT DISTINCT *
FROM table;

-- DISTINCT on specific columns
SELECT DISTINCT category_id
FROM products;

-- DISTINCT with ORDER BY
SELECT DISTINCT city
FROM customers
ORDER BY city;

-- DISTINCT with aggregate (usually use GROUP BY instead)
SELECT DISTINCT category_id, COUNT(*)
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id;

-- DISTINCT ON (PostgreSQL-specific)
SELECT DISTINCT ON (column1) column1, column2, column3
FROM table
ORDER BY column1, column2;

🎯 Example 1: Basic DISTINCT for Unique Values

-- Without DISTINCT: all customer cities (with duplicates)
SELECT city FROM customers ORDER BY city LIMIT 10;

-- With DISTINCT: unique cities only
SELECT DISTINCT city 
FROM customers 
WHERE city IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY city;

-- Count unique cities
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS unique_cities FROM customers;

-- Distinct country-city combinations
SELECT DISTINCT country, city
FROM customers
WHERE city IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY country, city
LIMIT 10;
Result (DISTINCT cities):
   city
----------
 Boston
 Chicago
 Denver
 Houston
 Los Angeles
 Miami
 New York
 Phoenix
 Portland
 Seattle
(10 rows)
Result (count unique):
 unique_cities
---------------
            15
(1 row)
Explanation: Without DISTINCT, query returns all customer cities including duplicates (Seattle appears 5 times if 5 customers from Seattle). DISTINCT removes duplicates, returns each city once. COUNT(DISTINCT city) counts unique cities (15 distinct cities). DISTINCT on multiple columns (country, city) removes rows duplicate across both columns - (USA, Seattle) and (USA, Boston) both kept (different cities), but duplicate (USA, Seattle) removed. DISTINCT useful for dropdown lists, unique value analysis, deduplication. NULL considered distinct value - IS NOT NULL filter excludes it.

🎯 Example 2: DISTINCT vs GROUP BY

-- DISTINCT: Just unique categories
SELECT DISTINCT category_id
FROM products
ORDER BY category_id;

-- GROUP BY: Same result but enables aggregates
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    AVG(price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id
ORDER BY category_id;

-- DISTINCT with expressions
SELECT DISTINCT 
    CASE 
        WHEN price < 30 THEN 'Budget'
        WHEN price < 70 THEN 'Mid-Range'
        ELSE 'Premium'
    END AS price_tier
FROM products
ORDER BY price_tier;
Result (DISTINCT category_id):
 category_id
-------------
           1
           2
           3
(3 rows)
Result (GROUP BY with aggregates):
 category_id | product_count | avg_price
-------------+---------------+-----------
           1 |             8 |     39.50
           2 |             5 |     45.99
           3 |             3 |     24.99
(3 rows)
Result (DISTINCT on expression):
 price_tier
------------
 Budget
 Mid-Range
 Premium
(3 rows)
Explanation: DISTINCT and GROUP BY can produce same results when just listing unique values. GROUP BY more powerful - enables aggregate functions (COUNT, AVG). Use DISTINCT for simple unique value lists, GROUP BY when need aggregates or complex grouping. DISTINCT works on expressions: CASE expression categorizes prices, DISTINCT returns unique tiers. DISTINCT simpler syntax for deduplication, GROUP BY provides analytical capabilities. Both can use indexes for performance. Prefer GROUP BY in practice - more flexible, clearer intent for aggregation.

🎯 Example 3: DISTINCT ON for First Row Per Group

-- Get most recent order per customer
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id)
    customer_id,
    order_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount,
    status
FROM orders
ORDER BY customer_id, order_date DESC;

-- Get highest priced product per category
SELECT DISTINCT ON (category_id)
    category_id,
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price
FROM products
ORDER BY category_id, price DESC;
Result (most recent order per customer):
 customer_id | order_id | order_date | total_amount | status
-------------+----------+------------+--------------+------------
        1001 |     3004 | 2026-01-23 |       334.94 | shipped
        1003 |     3005 | 2026-01-24 |       289.99 | pending
        1004 |     3006 | 2026-01-22 |       159.50 | confirmed
        1005 |     3007 | 2026-01-20 |       445.00 | shipped
(4 rows)
Result (highest priced per category):
 category_id | product_id |    product_name     | price
-------------+------------+---------------------+-------
           1 |       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99
           2 |       2010 | Laptop Bag          | 69.99
           3 |       2005 | Notebook Set        | 34.99
(3 rows)
Explanation: DISTINCT ON (customer_id) returns first row for each customer_id. ORDER BY customer_id, order_date DESC ensures most recent order selected (first row after sorting by date descending). Must ORDER BY DISTINCT ON columns first. Returns one order per customer (most recent). Alternative: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) = 1. DISTINCT ON simpler for this pattern. PostgreSQL-specific feature (not standard SQL). Useful for "latest per group", "top per category" queries. Critical: ORDER BY determines which row kept.

🎯 Example 4: DISTINCT with Multiple Columns and NULL Handling

-- Distinct combinations of status and country
SELECT DISTINCT 
    o.status,
    c.country
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
ORDER BY c.country, o.status;

-- Show how DISTINCT handles NULL
SELECT DISTINCT city, country
FROM customers
ORDER BY city NULLS FIRST;

-- Count distinct with condition
SELECT 
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS total_customers,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN city IS NOT NULL THEN customer_id END) AS customers_with_city,
    COUNT(DISTINCT city) AS unique_cities
FROM customers;
Result (status-country combinations):
   status    | country
-------------+---------
 confirmed   | Canada
 pending     | Canada
 shipped     | Canada
 confirmed   | USA
 pending     | USA
 processing  | USA
 shipped     | USA
(7 rows)
Result (count distinct):
 total_customers | customers_with_city | unique_cities
-----------------+---------------------+---------------
              50 |                  42 |            15
(1 row)
Explanation: DISTINCT on multiple columns removes rows duplicate across all selected columns. (pending, USA) and (shipped, USA) both kept (different status). Multiple (pending, USA) collapsed to one. DISTINCT treats NULL as distinct value - NULL city included once. COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values. 50 total customers, 42 have non-NULL city, 15 unique cities. CASE WHEN in COUNT filters before counting. DISTINCT with JOIN: examines joined result combinations. NULLs with DISTINCT: one NULL per column kept, rest removed.

🎯 Example 5: DISTINCT Performance Considerations

-- Example showing when to use DISTINCT vs alternatives

-- Scenario 1: Large table with many duplicates - DISTINCT can be expensive
-- Instead of:
-- SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders; (scans all orders)

-- Better: Use GROUP BY with index
SELECT customer_id
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

-- Scenario 2: DISTINCT with ORDER BY (requires sort)
SELECT DISTINCT category_id, 
       COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY category_id) AS product_count
FROM products
ORDER BY category_id;

-- Better: Use window function or GROUP BY
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id
ORDER BY category_id;

-- Scenario 3: Check existence (not all values)
-- Instead of: SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders WHERE order_date > '2026-01-01'
-- Better: Use EXISTS
SELECT c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM orders o 
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id 
      AND o.order_date > '2026-01-01'
);
Result (efficient GROUP BY):
 category_id | product_count
-------------+---------------
           1 |             8
           2 |             5
           3 |             3
(3 rows)
Explanation: DISTINCT requires finding all unique values - potentially expensive on large tables. Requires sort or hash operation. GROUP BY often more efficient, especially with indexes. DISTINCT on indexed column uses index scan. DISTINCT with ORDER BY requires additional sort (unless order matches). For existence checks, EXISTS more efficient than DISTINCT (stops at first match). When aggregating, always prefer GROUP BY over DISTINCT (enables COUNT, SUM, etc.). Use EXPLAIN to check query plan. DISTINCT fine for small result sets or indexed columns. Consider alternatives for large tables or when aggregation needed.

7.4 Combining Queries (UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT)

📖 Full Description

Set Operations combine results from multiple SELECT queries. UNION combines rows from both queries (removing duplicates), UNION ALL includes duplicates, INTERSECT returns common rows, EXCEPT returns rows in first query but not second. All queries must have same number of columns with compatible data types. Column names from first query used in result. Set operations process after individual queries complete.

UNION vs UNION ALL: UNION removes duplicates (like DISTINCT), requires sort or hash operation. UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates - faster, no deduplication overhead. Use UNION when duplicates are problem, UNION ALL when duplicates acceptable or known not to exist. UNION ALL more efficient for large result sets.

Best Practices: Use UNION ALL unless specifically need deduplication, ensure column counts match between queries, verify data types compatible, use same column order in all queries, consider performance: UNION slower than UNION ALL, combine with ORDER BY at end (applies to final result), use parentheses for complex combinations: (query1 UNION query2) INTERSECT query3.

Set Operations Syntax
-- UNION (removes duplicates)
SELECT columns FROM table1
UNION
SELECT columns FROM table2;

-- UNION ALL (keeps duplicates - faster)
SELECT columns FROM table1
UNION ALL
SELECT columns FROM table2;

-- INTERSECT (common rows)
SELECT columns FROM table1
INTERSECT
SELECT columns FROM table2;

-- EXCEPT (in first, not in second)
SELECT columns FROM table1
EXCEPT
SELECT columns FROM table2;

-- ORDER BY on combined result
SELECT columns FROM table1
UNION
SELECT columns FROM table2
ORDER BY column1;

-- Complex combinations with parentheses
(SELECT columns FROM table1 UNION SELECT columns FROM table2)
INTERSECT
SELECT columns FROM table3;

🎯 Example 1: UNION and UNION ALL

-- UNION: Combine customers from different sources (remove duplicates)
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, 'Seattle' AS source
FROM customers
WHERE city = 'Seattle'
UNION
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, 'Boston' AS source
FROM customers
WHERE city = 'Boston'
ORDER BY last_name
LIMIT 5;

-- UNION ALL: Keep all rows (faster, includes duplicates if any)
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, 'Has Orders' AS status
FROM customers
WHERE customer_id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders)
UNION ALL
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, 'No Orders' AS status
FROM customers
WHERE customer_id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders)
ORDER BY customer_id
LIMIT 8;
Result (UNION):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | source
-------------+------------+-----------+---------
        1015 | Oscar      | Brown     | Boston
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     | Seattle
        1008 | Henry      | Ford      | Seattle
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | Seattle
        1012 | Michael    | Jordan    | Boston
(5 rows)
Result (UNION ALL):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |          email           |   status
-------------+------------+-----------+--------------------------+-------------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | alice.johnson@email.com  | Has Orders
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     | charlie.brown@email.com  | Has Orders
        1004 | Diana      | Prince    | diana.prince@email.com   | Has Orders
        1005 | Edward     | Norton    | edward.norton@email.com  | Has Orders
        1015 | Oscar      | Wilde     | oscar.wilde@email.com    | No Orders
        1020 | Patricia   | Moore     | patricia.moore@email.com | No Orders
(6 rows)
Explanation: UNION combines Seattle and Boston customers, removes duplicates (if customer somehow in both). UNION ALL combines customers with/without orders, keeps all rows (no duplicates possible - customer either has orders or doesn't). Both queries must return same column count and types. Column names from first query used (customer_id, first_name, last_name, source/email, status). ORDER BY applies to combined result. UNION performs deduplication (slower), UNION ALL faster (no deduplication). Use UNION ALL when results guaranteed unique or duplicates acceptable.

🎯 Example 2: INTERSECT for Common Rows

-- Find customers who ordered in both 2025 and 2026
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = 2025
INTERSECT
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = 2026
ORDER BY customer_id;

-- Products in both Electronics (1) and ordered in last 30 days
SELECT product_id, product_name
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
INTERSECT
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_id, p.product_name
FROM products p
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
INNER JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 30;
Result:
 product_id | product_name
------------+----------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard
(2 rows)
Explanation: INTERSECT returns rows present in both queries. First query finds customers with 2025 orders, second finds customers with 2026 orders, INTERSECT returns customers in both (ordered in both years). Second example finds Electronics products (category 1) that were also ordered in last 30 days - intersection shows popular Electronics items. INTERSECT automatically removes duplicates (like UNION). Alternative: use WHERE with multiple conditions or EXISTS subqueries. INTERSECT clearer for "in both sets" logic. Both queries must return same columns with compatible types.

🎯 Example 3: EXCEPT for Difference

-- Customers who registered but never ordered
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email
FROM customers
EXCEPT
SELECT c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name, c.email
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
ORDER BY customer_id
LIMIT 5;

-- Products in category 1 that have never been ordered
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
EXCEPT
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_id, p.product_name, p.price
FROM products p
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
ORDER BY product_id;
Result (customers never ordered):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |          email
-------------+------------+-----------+--------------------------
        1015 | Oscar      | Wilde     | oscar.wilde@email.com
        1020 | Patricia   | Moore     | patricia.moore@email.com
        1025 | Quinn      | Adams     | quinn.adams@email.com
        1030 | Rachel     | Green     | rachel.green@email.com
        1035 | Samuel     | Taylor    | samuel.taylor@email.com
(5 rows)
Explanation: EXCEPT (also called MINUS in some databases) returns rows in first query not in second. First query gets all customers, second gets customers with orders, EXCEPT returns customers without orders (never ordered). Second query finds Electronics products never ordered - useful for identifying dead stock. EXCEPT order matters: A EXCEPT B != B EXCEPT A. Alternative: NOT EXISTS or NOT IN subqueries. EXCEPT clearer for "in first but not second" logic. Automatically removes duplicates from result.

🎯 Example 4: Combining Multiple Set Operations

-- Complex combination: customers from Seattle OR Boston, AND who have orders
(
    SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
    FROM customers
    WHERE city = 'Seattle'
    UNION
    SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
    FROM customers
    WHERE city = 'Boston'
)
INTERSECT
SELECT c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name, c.city
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
ORDER BY city, last_name;

-- Products ordered in January but not in December 2025
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_id, p.product_name
FROM products p
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
INNER JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-01' AND o.order_date < '2026-02-01'
EXCEPT
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_id, p.product_name
FROM products p
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
INNER JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2025-12-01' AND o.order_date < '2026-01-01'
ORDER BY product_name;
Result (Seattle OR Boston AND has orders):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |  city
-------------+------------+-----------+---------
        1015 | Oscar      | Brown     | Boston
        1012 | Michael    | Jordan    | Boston
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     | Seattle
        1008 | Henry      | Ford      | Seattle
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | Seattle
(5 rows)
Explanation: Parentheses control set operation order. First example: (Seattle UNION Boston) creates combined city set, then INTERSECT with customers who have orders filters to customers from these cities with orders. Without parentheses, operations execute left-to-right. Second example uses EXCEPT to find products new in January - ordered this month but not last month (new customer interest). Complex set operations enable sophisticated filtering logic. Order BY applies to final result. Use parentheses for clarity when combining multiple operations.

🎯 Example 5: Practical Use Cases and Performance

-- Use case 1: Merge data from multiple tables with same structure
SELECT 'Current' AS status, order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01'
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Archived' AS status, order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders_archive
WHERE order_date < '2026-01-01'
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Use case 2: Generate report combining different metrics
SELECT 'Total Orders' AS metric, COUNT(*)::TEXT AS value, NULL::NUMERIC AS amount
FROM orders
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Total Customers', COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id)::TEXT, NULL
FROM orders
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Total Revenue', NULL, SUM(total_amount)::NUMERIC(10,2)
FROM orders
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Average Order Value', NULL, AVG(total_amount)::NUMERIC(10,2)
FROM orders;
Result (report metrics):
       metric        | value  |  amount
---------------------+--------+----------
 Total Orders        | 156    |     NULL
 Total Customers     | 45     |     NULL
 Total Revenue       | NULL   | 34567.89
 Average Order Value | NULL   |   221.59
(4 rows)
Explanation: UNION ALL merges current orders with archived orders - single query returns all historical data. Useful when data partitioned across tables. Second example creates summary report by unioning different aggregate queries - each row different metric. Must cast to compatible types (::TEXT, ::NUMERIC). NULL used for non-applicable columns. UNION ALL efficient for combining non-overlapping datasets (current vs archived). Common patterns: merging staging/production data, combining different time periods, creating pivot-style reports, consolidating similar tables. Performance: UNION ALL faster than UNION (no deduplication), but both require combining full result sets before filtering/sorting.

7.5 Sorting Rows (ORDER BY)

📖 Full Description

ORDER BY sorts query results. Syntax: ORDER BY expression [ASC|DESC] [NULLS {FIRST|LAST}]. Can sort by columns, expressions, column positions, or aliases. ASC (ascending, default) sorts low to high, DESC (descending) high to low. Multiple sort expressions separate with commas - sorts by first expression, ties broken by second, etc. NULL handling: NULLS FIRST places NULLs before non-NULLs, NULLS LAST after (default varies by ASC/DESC).

Sort Options: ASC NULLS LAST (default for ASC), DESC NULLS FIRST (default for DESC). Override with explicit NULLS FIRST/LAST. Can sort by expressions not in SELECT list. Position syntax: ORDER BY 1, 2 sorts by first then second SELECT column (discouraged - fragile). Collation: use COLLATE for locale-specific sorting (ORDER BY name COLLATE "en_US").

Best Practices: Always use ORDER BY for predictable results (relational model has no inherent order), specify NULL handling explicitly when important, use column names not positions, avoid expensive expressions in ORDER BY (pre-calculate in SELECT), use indexes on ORDER BY columns for performance, combine multiple sort columns for tie-breaking, consider LIMIT with ORDER BY for pagination.

ORDER BY Syntax
-- Basic ORDER BY
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column1 [ASC|DESC];

-- Multiple sort columns
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column1 DESC, column2 ASC;

-- NULL handling
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column1 NULLS FIRST;
ORDER BY column1 DESC NULLS LAST;

-- Sort by expression
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY LENGTH(column1) DESC;

-- Sort by alias
SELECT column1, column2 * 1.1 AS adjusted
FROM table
ORDER BY adjusted DESC;

-- Sort by position (not recommended)
SELECT column1, column2 FROM table
ORDER BY 1, 2 DESC;

-- Collation
SELECT name FROM table
ORDER BY name COLLATE "en_US";

🎯 Example 1: Basic Sorting - ASC and DESC

-- Ascending (default) - lowest to highest
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY price ASC
LIMIT 5;

-- Descending - highest to lowest
SELECT product_id, product_name, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Multiple columns - primary and secondary sort
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
FROM customers
ORDER BY city ASC, last_name ASC, first_name ASC
LIMIT 10;
Result (price DESC):
 product_id |    product_name     | price | stock_quantity
------------+---------------------+-------+----------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99 |             50
       2010 | Laptop Bag          | 69.99 |             80
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99 |             75
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99 |            100
       2005 | Notebook Set        | 34.99 |            200
(5 rows)
Result (city, last_name, first_name):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |    city
-------------+------------+-----------+------------
        1015 | Oscar      | Brown     | Boston
        1012 | Michael    | Jordan    | Boston
        1018 | Karen      | Smith     | Boston
        1022 | Linda      | Davis     | Chicago
        1025 | Tom        | Wilson    | Chicago
(5 rows)
Explanation: ORDER BY price ASC sorts from cheapest to most expensive (ASC default, can omit). ORDER BY price DESC reverses order - most expensive first. Multiple columns: ORDER BY city, last_name, first_name sorts by city first (Boston before Chicago), within same city sorts by last_name (Brown before Jordan), within same last_name sorts by first_name. Each subsequent column breaks ties in previous. Without ORDER BY, row order undefined (database may return different order each time). Always use ORDER BY for predictable results.

🎯 Example 2: NULL Handling with NULLS FIRST/LAST

-- Default NULL behavior with ASC (NULLS LAST)
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
FROM customers
ORDER BY city ASC
LIMIT 8;

-- Explicit NULLS FIRST with ASC
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
FROM customers
ORDER BY city ASC NULLS FIRST
LIMIT 8;

-- DESC with NULLS LAST (not default for DESC)
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city, registration_date
FROM customers
ORDER BY registration_date DESC NULLS LAST
LIMIT 5;
Result (ASC NULLS FIRST):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | city
-------------+------------+-----------+------
        1002 | Bob        | Smith     | NULL
        1018 | Karen      | Wilson    | NULL
        1027 | Thomas     | Anderson  | NULL
        1015 | Oscar      | Brown     | Boston
        1012 | Michael    | Jordan    | Boston
        1022 | Linda      | Davis     | Chicago
        1025 | Tom        | Wilson    | Chicago
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     | Denver
(8 rows)
Explanation: Default NULL behavior: ASC sorts NULLS LAST (non-NULL values first, then NULLs), DESC sorts NULLS FIRST (NULLs first, then non-NULL values descending). NULLS FIRST explicitly places NULL cities at top. NULLS LAST with DESC places NULLs at bottom. Important for reports where NULL handling affects meaning (NULL = unknown vs NULL = not applicable). Explicit NULLS FIRST/LAST ensures consistent behavior across PostgreSQL versions and other databases. Use when NULL position matters for business logic.

🎯 Example 3: Sorting by Expressions and Calculations

-- Sort by calculated expression
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    stock_quantity,
    (price * stock_quantity) AS inventory_value
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY (price * stock_quantity) DESC  -- Sort by expression
LIMIT 5;

-- Sort by string length
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
    LENGTH(first_name || ' ' || last_name) AS name_length
FROM customers
ORDER BY LENGTH(first_name || ' ' || last_name) DESC, full_name ASC
LIMIT 8;

-- Sort by date part
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    EXTRACT(DOW FROM order_date) AS day_of_week
FROM orders
ORDER BY EXTRACT(DOW FROM order_date), order_date
LIMIT 10;
Result (inventory value):
 product_id |    product_name     | price | stock_quantity | inventory_value
------------+---------------------+-------+----------------+-----------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99 |            145 |         4783.55
       2002 | USB-C Cable         | 14.29 |            295 |         4215.55
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99 |             50 |         3999.50
       2005 | Notebook Set        | 34.99 |            200 |         6998.00
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99 |            100 |         3999.00
(5 rows)
Explanation: ORDER BY can sort by expressions not just columns. (price * stock_quantity) calculates inventory value for sorting without requiring alias. LENGTH(first_name || ' ' || last_name) sorts by full name length - longest names first. Multiple sort criteria: by length DESC, then alphabetically ASC (tie-breaker). EXTRACT(DOW FROM order_date) sorts by day of week (0=Sunday, 6=Saturday). Expressions evaluated for sorting even if not in SELECT. Can sort by SELECT aliases: ORDER BY inventory_value DESC equivalent to ORDER BY (price * stock_quantity) DESC. Sorting by expression can be expensive - consider pre-calculating in SELECT if expression complex.

🎯 Example 4: Sorting with Aliases and CASE Expressions

-- Sort by alias
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    CASE 
        WHEN price < 30 THEN 'Budget'
        WHEN price < 70 THEN 'Mid-Range'
        ELSE 'Premium'
    END AS price_category
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY price_category, price ASC;

-- Custom sort order with CASE
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    status,
    total_amount,
    CASE status
        WHEN 'pending' THEN 1
        WHEN 'processing' THEN 2
        WHEN 'shipped' THEN 3
        WHEN 'delivered' THEN 4
        ELSE 5
    END AS status_order
FROM orders
ORDER BY status_order, order_date DESC
LIMIT 10;
Result (price_category then price):
 product_id |    product_name     | price | price_category
------------+---------------------+-------+----------------
       2002 | USB-C Cable         | 14.29 | Budget
       2013 | Sticky Notes        | 15.99 | Budget
       2008 | Desk Organizer      | 24.99 | Budget
       2012 | Pen Set             | 24.99 | Budget
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99 | Mid-Range
       2005 | Notebook Set        | 34.99 | Mid-Range
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99 | Mid-Range
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99 | Mid-Range
       2010 | Laptop Bag          | 69.99 | Premium
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99 | Premium
(10 rows)
Explanation: ORDER BY price_category sorts by alias - Budget, then Mid-Range, then Premium (alphabetical). Within each category, ORDER BY price ASC sorts from cheapest to most expensive. Second query uses CASE for custom sort order - pending (1) before processing (2) before shipped (3), etc. Without CASE, status would sort alphabetically (delivered, pending, processing, shipped) - not business logic order. CASE in ORDER BY powerful for non-alphabetical sorting (priority levels, custom sequences, status workflows). Can reference aliases or use expressions directly in ORDER BY.

🎯 Example 5: Performance Considerations and Index Usage

-- Efficient: ORDER BY indexed column
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email
FROM customers
ORDER BY customer_id  -- customer_id is PRIMARY KEY (indexed)
LIMIT 10;

-- Less efficient: ORDER BY non-indexed column
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
FROM customers
ORDER BY city  -- If city not indexed, requires sort
LIMIT 10;

-- Efficient with LIMIT: Index scan + stop early
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;  -- Returns after finding 5, doesn't sort all rows

-- Multi-column index benefit
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 1001
ORDER BY order_date DESC  -- Index on (customer_id, order_date) very efficient
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | order_date | total_amount
----------+-------------+------------+--------------
     3004 |        1001 | 2026-01-23 |       334.94
     3001 |        1001 | 2026-01-21 |       157.94
     3012 |        1001 | 2026-01-15 |       445.00
(3 rows)
Explanation: ORDER BY performance depends on indexes. Indexed columns sort efficiently (index already sorted). Non-indexed columns require full table scan + sort operation (expensive on large tables). LIMIT with ORDER BY optimized - PostgreSQL can stop after finding requested rows (top-N heapsort). Multi-column index (customer_id, order_date) enables efficient filtering + sorting in single operation. Use EXPLAIN to verify index usage: "Index Scan" good, "Sort" operation indicates sorting overhead. Create indexes on frequently sorted columns, especially with LIMIT. Composite indexes benefit queries filtering + sorting on multiple columns. Sorting large result sets expensive - use pagination with LIMIT/OFFSET and indexed ORDER BY columns.

7.6 LIMIT and OFFSET

📖 Full Description

LIMIT and OFFSET restrict query results for pagination. LIMIT specifies maximum rows to return, OFFSET specifies rows to skip. Syntax: LIMIT count OFFSET start. Alternative SQL standard syntax: OFFSET start ROWS FETCH FIRST count ROWS ONLY. LIMIT/OFFSET applied after ORDER BY - always use with ORDER BY for consistent pagination. Without ORDER BY, row order undefined, pagination results unpredictable.

Pagination Pattern: Page 1: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 0, Page 2: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10, Page 3: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20. Calculate: OFFSET = (page_number - 1) * page_size. LIMIT enables "top N" queries (LIMIT 10 for top 10). OFFSET enables skipping rows (OFFSET 100 starts at row 101). Combine for pagination, use LIMIT alone for top results.

Best Practices: Always use ORDER BY with LIMIT/OFFSET (predictable results), use indexed ORDER BY columns (performance), consider alternatives for large OFFSETs (keyset pagination), validate LIMIT/OFFSET parameters (prevent SQL injection), use FETCH FIRST for standard SQL compatibility, avoid large OFFSETs (database still processes skipped rows), consider cursors or keyset pagination for large datasets.

LIMIT and OFFSET Syntax
-- LIMIT only (top N rows)
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column
LIMIT 10;

-- LIMIT with OFFSET (skip rows)
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;  -- Skip 20, return next 10 (rows 21-30)

-- SQL standard syntax (equivalent)
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column
OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY;

-- LIMIT 0 (count only, no rows)
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table;  -- Better
SELECT columns FROM table LIMIT 0;  -- Returns structure, no data

-- Dynamic pagination
SELECT columns FROM table
ORDER BY column
LIMIT page_size OFFSET (page_number - 1) * page_size;

🎯 Example 1: Basic LIMIT - Top N Results

-- Top 5 most expensive products
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Top 10 customers by spending
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Most recent 5 orders
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount, status
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_date DESC, order_id DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result (top 5 expensive products):
 product_id |    product_name     | price
------------+---------------------+-------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99
       2010 | Laptop Bag          | 69.99
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99
       2005 | Notebook Set        | 34.99
(5 rows)
Explanation: LIMIT 5 returns first 5 rows after sorting. Must use ORDER BY for meaningful "top N" - ORDER BY price DESC gives top 5 most expensive. Without ORDER BY, LIMIT returns arbitrary 5 rows. Second query finds top 10 spending customers - GROUP BY aggregates, ORDER BY total_spent DESC sorts by spending, LIMIT 10 takes top 10. Third query gets 5 most recent orders - ORDER BY order_date DESC, order_id DESC ensures consistent ordering (date tie-breaker). LIMIT efficient with indexes - database stops after finding requested rows.

🎯 Example 2: LIMIT with OFFSET for Pagination

-- Page 1: First 10 products
SELECT product_id, product_name, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 0;  -- OFFSET 0 can be omitted

-- Page 2: Next 10 products (skip first 10)
SELECT product_id, product_name, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10;

-- Page 3: Next 10 products (skip first 20)
SELECT product_id, product_name, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;

-- Calculate total pages
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS total_products,
    CEIL(COUNT(*) / 10.0) AS total_pages
FROM products;
Result (Page 2 - OFFSET 10):
 product_id |    product_name     | price | stock_quantity
------------+---------------------+-------+----------------
       2011 | Wireless Keyboard   | 45.99 |             60
       2012 | Pen Set             | 24.99 |            150
       2013 | Sticky Notes        | 15.99 |            275
       2014 | Desk Chair          | 189.99|             25
       2015 | File Cabinet        | 129.99|             15
       2016 | Desk Lamp           | 34.99 |             80
       2017 | Mouse Pad           | 9.99  |            400
       2018 | Webcam              | 79.99 |             45
       2019 | Headset             | 59.99 |             90
       2020 | Cable Organizer     | 12.99 |            200
(10 rows)
Explanation: OFFSET skips specified rows. LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10 skips first 10, returns next 10 (rows 11-20). Pagination formula: OFFSET = (page_num - 1) * page_size. Page 1: OFFSET 0 (skip 0), Page 2: OFFSET 10 (skip 10), Page 3: OFFSET 20 (skip 20). Must use consistent ORDER BY across pages for stable pagination. CEIL(COUNT(*) / 10.0) calculates total pages. Warning: large OFFSET inefficient - database processes all skipped rows. For large datasets (OFFSET > 1000), use keyset pagination (WHERE id > last_seen_id LIMIT 10).

🎯 Example 3: SQL Standard Syntax - FETCH FIRST

-- SQL standard equivalent to LIMIT/OFFSET
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
ORDER BY price DESC
OFFSET 5 ROWS FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY;

-- Same as: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 5

-- FETCH FIRST without OFFSET (top N)
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers
ORDER BY registration_date DESC
FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS ONLY;

-- FETCH NEXT (equivalent to FETCH FIRST)
SELECT order_id, customer_id, total_amount
FROM orders
ORDER BY total_amount DESC
FETCH NEXT 3 ROWS ONLY;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | price
------------+---------------------+-------
       2005 | Notebook Set        | 34.99
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99
       2009 | USB Hub             | 32.99
       2012 | Pen Set             | 24.99
       2008 | Desk Organizer      | 24.99
       2013 | Sticky Notes        | 15.99
       2002 | USB-C Cable         | 14.29
(7 rows)
