          3 | integer  | 3.333...       | numeric      |             1 |         1024
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Logical Operators with Type Coercion

-- Boolean operators with implicit conversion
SELECT 
    product_name,
    stock,
    -- Implicit conversion to boolean
    CASE 
        WHEN stock > 0 THEN 'In Stock'
        ELSE 'Out of Stock'
    END AS availability,
    -- Boolean operators
    (stock > 0 AND price < 100) AS affordable_and_available,
    (category = 'Electronics' OR category = 'Appliances') AS tech_products
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 5;
Result:
 product_name | stock |  availability  | affordable_and_available | tech_products
--------------+-------+----------------+--------------------------+---------------
 Laptop       |    50 | In Stock       | f                        | t
 Smartphone   |    75 | In Stock       | f                        | t
 Headphones   |   100 | In Stock       | t                        | t
 Desk Chair   |    30 | In Stock       | f                        | f
 T-Shirt      |   200 | In Stock       | t                        | f
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Array and Range Operators

-- Operators with arrays and ranges
SELECT 
    -- Array containment
    ARRAY[1,2,3] @> ARRAY[2] AS array_contains,
    -- Array overlap
    ARRAY[1,2,3] && ARRAY[3,4,5] AS array_overlap,
    -- Range containment
    int4range(1, 10) @> 5 AS range_contains,
    -- Range overlap
    int4range(1, 5) && int4range(3, 8) AS range_overlap,
    -- JSONB containment
    '{"name":"John","age":30}'::jsonb @> '{"age":30}'::jsonb AS jsonb_contains;
Result:
 array_contains | array_overlap | range_contains | range_overlap | jsonb_contains
----------------+---------------+----------------+---------------+----------------
 t              | t             | t              | t             | t
(1 row)
Explanation: Operator resolution selects appropriate operator based on operand types. Integer division (/) truncates, numeric division preserves decimals. PostgreSQL automatically converts types to match operator signatures. Type precedence determines result type in mixed operations. Comparison operators may trigger implicit conversions. Special operators exist for arrays (@>, &&), ranges, and JSONB. Understanding operator type rules prevents unexpected results.

10.3 Functions

Function type resolution: PostgreSQL selects the best-matching function from potentially overloaded functions with the same name. The resolution considers exact type matches, domain matches, implicit conversions, and type categories. Function overloading allows the same function name with different parameter types. Proper type resolution ensures the intended function variant is called.

🎯 Example 1: Function Overloading

-- Same function name with different argument types
SELECT 
    -- abs() with integer
    abs(-42) AS abs_integer,
    pg_typeof(abs(-42)) AS abs_int_type,
    -- abs() with numeric
    abs(-42.5) AS abs_numeric,
    pg_typeof(abs(-42.5)) AS abs_numeric_type,
    -- length() with text
    length('Hello') AS length_text,
    -- length() with bytea
    length('Hello'::bytea) AS length_bytea;
Result:
 abs_integer | abs_int_type | abs_numeric | abs_numeric_type | length_text | length_bytea
-------------+--------------+-------------+------------------+-------------+--------------
          42 | integer      |        42.5 | numeric          |           5 |            5
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Implicit Type Conversion in Function Calls

-- Functions with implicit type conversion
SELECT 
    product_name,
    price,
    -- round() converts to numeric if needed
    round(price) AS rounded_price,
    -- substring with integer positions
    substring(product_name FROM 1 FOR 5) AS short_name,
    -- concat with multiple types
    concat('Price: $', price, ' for ', product_name) AS description
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 3;
Result:
 product_name | price  | rounded_price | short_name |              description
--------------+--------+---------------+------------+---------------------------------------
 Laptop       | 999.99 |          1000 | Lapto      | Price: $999.99 for Laptop
 Smartphone   | 699.99 |           700 | Smart      | Price: $699.99 for Smartphone
 Headphones   | 79.99  |            80 | Headp      | Price: $79.99 for Headphones
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Type-Specific Functions

-- Functions specific to certain types
SELECT 
    -- Date/time functions
    extract(year FROM current_date) AS current_year,
    date_part('month', current_date) AS current_month,
    -- Array functions
    array_length(ARRAY[1,2,3,4,5], 1) AS array_len,
    array_append(ARRAY[1,2,3], 4) AS appended_array,
    -- JSON functions
    json_extract_path_text('{"name":"John","age":30}'::json, 'name') AS json_name,
    -- String functions with text
    upper('hello world') AS uppercase;
Result:
 current_year | current_month | array_len | appended_array | json_name | uppercase
--------------+---------------+-----------+----------------+-----------+-------------
         2026 |             1 |         5 | {1,2,3,4}      | John      | HELLO WORLD
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Aggregate Functions with Type Conversion

-- Aggregate functions handle various types
SELECT 
    category,
    -- sum() works with numeric types
    sum(price) AS total_price,
    -- avg() returns numeric for integer input
    avg(stock) AS avg_stock,
    pg_typeof(avg(stock)) AS avg_type,
    -- string_agg() concatenates text
    string_agg(product_name, ', ' ORDER BY product_name) AS products,
    -- count() returns bigint
    count(*) AS product_count,
    pg_typeof(count(*)) AS count_type
FROM products
WHERE category IN ('Electronics', 'Furniture')
GROUP BY category;
Result:
  category   | total_price | avg_stock |  avg_type  |           products            | product_count | count_type
-------------+-------------+-----------+------------+-------------------------------+---------------+------------
 Electronics |     1779.97 |  75.00    | numeric    | Headphones, Laptop, Smartphone|             3 | bigint
 Furniture   |      199.99 |  30.00    | numeric    | Desk Chair                    |             1 | bigint
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Polymorphic Functions

-- Polymorphic functions work with multiple types
SELECT 
    -- greatest() with integers
    greatest(1, 5, 3, 9, 2) AS greatest_int,
    pg_typeof(greatest(1, 5, 3)) AS greatest_int_type,
    -- greatest() with text
    greatest('apple', 'banana', 'cherry') AS greatest_text,
    -- least() with numerics
    least(10.5, 3.2, 7.8) AS least_numeric,
    -- coalesce() with mixed types (converts to common type)
    coalesce(NULL, 42, NULL, 100) AS coalesce_int,
    -- nullif() comparison
    nullif('same', 'same') AS nullif_equal,
    nullif('different', 'other') AS nullif_not_equal;
Result:
 greatest_int | greatest_int_type | greatest_text | least_numeric | coalesce_int | nullif_equal | nullif_not_equal
--------------+-------------------+---------------+---------------+--------------+--------------+------------------
            9 | integer           | cherry        |           3.2 |           42 | NULL         | different
(1 row)
Explanation: Function resolution matches function signatures to argument types. Overloaded functions have same name but different parameter types. PostgreSQL chooses best match through exact match → domain match → implicit conversion. Type categories (numeric, string, datetime) aid resolution. Polymorphic functions (greatest, least, coalesce) work with multiple types. Aggregate functions have specific return types (avg returns numeric, count returns bigint). Understanding function resolution prevents ambiguous function call errors.

10.4 Value Storage

Value storage conversion: When storing values in table columns, PostgreSQL converts values to match the target column's data type. This involves assignment casting, which may be more restrictive than implicit conversions in expressions. Storage conversion ensures type safety, prevents data loss, and maintains column type integrity. Understanding storage rules prevents INSERT/UPDATE errors.

🎯 Example 1: Automatic Type Conversion on INSERT

-- Create table with specific column types
CREATE TEMP TABLE type_storage_demo (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    int_col integer,
    numeric_col numeric(10,2),
    text_col text,
    date_col date,
    bool_col boolean
);

-- Insert with automatic type conversion
INSERT INTO type_storage_demo (int_col, numeric_col, text_col, date_col, bool_col)
VALUES 
    -- Text to numeric conversion
    ('42', '123.456', 123, '2026-01-26', 'true'),
    -- Integer to numeric conversion
    (100, 99, 'Text here', '2026-12-31', 1);

SELECT * FROM type_storage_demo;
Result:
 id | int_col | numeric_col | text_col   | date_col   | bool_col
----+---------+-------------+------------+------------+----------
  1 |      42 |      123.46 | 123        | 2026-01-26 | t
  2 |     100 |       99.00 | Text here  | 2026-12-31 | t
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Type Conversion with Precision Loss

-- Demonstrate precision changes during storage
CREATE TEMP TABLE precision_demo (
    numeric_5_2 numeric(5,2),    -- 5 digits total, 2 after decimal
    varchar_10 varchar(10),       -- max 10 characters
    int_col integer               -- 32-bit integer
);

INSERT INTO precision_demo VALUES 
    (123.456, 'This is a very long string', 3.7);

SELECT 
    numeric_5_2,
    -- Rounded to 2 decimal places
    varchar_10,
    -- Truncated to 10 characters
    int_col
    -- Rounded to integer
FROM precision_demo;
Result:
 numeric_5_2 | varchar_10 | int_col
-------------+------------+---------
      123.46 | This is a  |       4
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Domain and Constraint Enforcement

-- Create domain with constraints
CREATE DOMAIN positive_price AS numeric(10,2)
    CHECK (VALUE > 0);

CREATE TEMP TABLE products_typed (
    product_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name varchar(100) NOT NULL,
    price positive_price,
    discount_percent numeric(5,2) CHECK (discount_percent BETWEEN 0 AND 100)
);

-- Valid inserts
INSERT INTO products_typed (product_name, price, discount_percent)
VALUES 
    ('Laptop', 999.99, 10.5),
    ('Mouse', '25.50', '15');  -- Automatic conversion

-- This would fail: negative price
-- INSERT INTO products_typed (product_name, price) VALUES ('Item', -10);

SELECT * FROM products_typed;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | price  | discount_percent
------------+--------------+--------+------------------
          1 | Laptop       | 999.99 |            10.50
          2 | Mouse        |  25.50 |            15.00
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Array and Composite Type Storage

-- Storage with complex types
CREATE TYPE address_type AS (
    street text,
    city text,
    zip_code varchar(10)
);

CREATE TEMP TABLE customers_typed (
    customer_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name text,
    phone_numbers text[],        -- Array of text
    address address_type,         -- Composite type
    tags text[]
);

INSERT INTO customers_typed (customer_name, phone_numbers, address, tags)
VALUES (
    'John Doe',
    ARRAY['555-0001', '555-0002'],
    ROW('123 Main St', 'New York', '10001')::address_type,
    '{vip,preferred}'::text[]
);

SELECT 
    customer_name,
    phone_numbers,
    (address).street AS street,
    (address).city AS city,
    tags
FROM customers_typed;
Result:
 customer_name |    phone_numbers    |    street    |   city   |      tags
---------------+---------------------+--------------+----------+-----------------
 John Doe      | {555-0001,555-0002} | 123 Main St  | New York | {vip,preferred}
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: JSON and JSONB Storage Conversion

-- JSON/JSONB storage with automatic parsing
CREATE TEMP TABLE json_storage (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    data_json json,
    data_jsonb jsonb
);

-- Insert with automatic text to JSON conversion
INSERT INTO json_storage (data_json, data_jsonb)
VALUES 
    ('{"name":"Alice","age":30}', '{"name":"Alice","age":30}'),
    ('{"name":"Bob","age":25}', '{"name":"Bob","age":25}');

SELECT 
    data_json->>'name' AS json_name,
    data_jsonb->>'name' AS jsonb_name,
    data_jsonb->'age' AS jsonb_age,
    pg_typeof(data_json) AS json_type,
    pg_typeof(data_jsonb) AS jsonb_type
FROM json_storage;
Result:
 json_name | jsonb_name | jsonb_age | json_type | jsonb_type
-----------+------------+-----------+-----------+------------
 Alice     | Alice      | 30        | json      | jsonb
 Bob       | Bob        | 25        | json      | jsonb
(2 rows)
Explanation: Value storage conversion occurs during INSERT and UPDATE operations. PostgreSQL automatically converts input values to match column types. Assignment casts are more restrictive than implicit casts - some conversions allowed in expressions may fail in storage. Precision may be lost (numeric rounding, string truncation). Domains add custom constraints. CHECK constraints enforce business rules. Arrays and composite types require compatible structure. JSON/JSONB parse text into structured format. Always verify data fits target column constraints.

10.5 UNION, CASE, and Related Constructs

Type resolution in constructs: UNION, CASE, COALESCE, and array constructors must produce a single result type from multiple input types. PostgreSQL determines the common type using type category precedence and preferred types within categories. Understanding these rules prevents type mismatch errors and ensures predictable results in complex expressions.

🎯 Example 1: UNION Type Resolution

-- UNION combines queries with compatible types
-- Query 1: product prices (numeric)
SELECT product_name AS item, price AS value, 'product' AS type
FROM products WHERE product_id <= 3

UNION ALL

-- Query 2: order totals (numeric)
SELECT 
    'Order #' || order_id AS item,
    total_amount AS value,
    'order' AS type
FROM orders WHERE order_id <= 2

ORDER BY type, value DESC;
Result:
      item      | value  |  type
----------------+--------+---------
 Order #2       | 719.98 | order
 Order #1       | 1049.98| order
 Laptop         | 999.99 | product
 Smartphone     | 699.99 | product
 Headphones     |  79.99 | product
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 2: CASE Expression Type Resolution

-- CASE must return same type for all branches
SELECT 
    product_name,
    price,
    stock,
    -- All branches return text
    CASE 
        WHEN stock = 0 THEN 'Out of Stock'
        WHEN stock < 25 THEN 'Low Stock'
        WHEN stock < 100 THEN 'In Stock'
        ELSE 'Well Stocked'
    END AS stock_status,
    -- Numeric result with type conversion
    CASE 
        WHEN category = 'Electronics' THEN price * 0.9  -- 10% discount
        WHEN category = 'Clothing' THEN price * 0.85    -- 15% discount
        ELSE price
    END AS discounted_price,
    pg_typeof(CASE WHEN stock > 0 THEN price ELSE 0 END) AS case_type
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 4;
Result:
 product_name | price  | stock | stock_status | discounted_price | case_type
--------------+--------+-------+--------------+------------------+-----------
 Laptop       | 999.99 |    50 | In Stock     |           899.99 | numeric
 Smartphone   | 699.99 |    75 | In Stock     |           629.99 | numeric
 Headphones   |  79.99 |   100 | Well Stocked |            71.99 | numeric
 Desk Chair   | 199.99 |    30 | In Stock     |           199.99 | numeric
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 3: COALESCE and NULLIF Type Resolution

-- COALESCE returns first non-NULL value with common type
SELECT 
    product_name,
    -- COALESCE with numeric types
    COALESCE(NULL, price, 0) AS coalesce_price,
    pg_typeof(COALESCE(NULL, price, 0)) AS coalesce_type,
    -- NULLIF returns NULL if values equal
    NULLIF(stock, 0) AS non_zero_stock,
    -- GREATEST with implicit conversion
    GREATEST(price, 100) AS price_or_hundred,
    -- LEAST with text
    LEAST(product_name, 'M') AS alphabetical_limit
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 3;
Result:
 product_name | coalesce_price | coalesce_type | non_zero_stock | price_or_hundred | alphabetical_limit
--------------+----------------+---------------+----------------+------------------+--------------------
 Laptop       |         999.99 | numeric       |             50 |           999.99 | Laptop
 Smartphone   |         699.99 | numeric       |             75 |           699.99 | M
 Headphones   |          79.99 | numeric       |            100 |           100.00 | Headphones
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Array Constructor Type Resolution

-- Array constructors determine common element type
SELECT 
    -- Integer array
    ARRAY[1, 2, 3, 4, 5] AS int_array,
    pg_typeof(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]) AS int_array_type,
    -- Mixed numeric types -> numeric array
    ARRAY[1, 2.5, 3] AS numeric_array,
    pg_typeof(ARRAY[1, 2.5, 3]) AS numeric_array_type,
    -- Text array
    ARRAY['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'] AS text_array,
    -- Array with explicit casting
    ARRAY[1::numeric, 2::numeric, 3::numeric] AS explicit_numeric_array;
Result:
 int_array | int_array_type | numeric_array | numeric_array_type |      text_array       | explicit_numeric_array
-----------+----------------+---------------+--------------------+-----------------------+------------------------
 {1,2,3,4,5}| integer[]     | {1,2.5,3}     | numeric[]          | {apple,banana,cherry} | {1,2,3}
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Complex UNION with Type Conversion

-- UNION with different numeric types
WITH electronics AS (
    SELECT 
        product_name,
        price::integer AS whole_price,  -- Cast to integer
        'Electronics' AS source
    FROM products
    WHERE category = 'Electronics'
    LIMIT 2
),
furniture AS (
    SELECT 
        product_name,
        price::integer AS whole_price,
        'Furniture' AS source
    FROM products
    WHERE category = 'Furniture'
    LIMIT 1
)
SELECT * FROM electronics
UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM furniture
ORDER BY whole_price DESC;
Result:
 product_name | whole_price |    source
--------------+-------------+-------------
 Laptop       |         999 | Electronics
 Smartphone   |         699 | Electronics
 Desk Chair   |         199 | Furniture
(3 rows)
Explanation: UNION requires corresponding columns to have compatible types - PostgreSQL finds common type using type precedence. CASE expressions must return same type across all branches - use explicit casts if needed. COALESCE returns first non-NULL value, determining result type from all arguments. NULLIF compares values and returns NULL on match. Array constructors infer element type from all elements, preferring more general types. GREATEST/LEAST work with comparable types. Understanding type resolution in constructs prevents "UNION types cannot be matched" and similar errors.

10.6 SELECT Output Columns

Output column type determination: PostgreSQL determines output column types based on the expressions in the SELECT list. Column types propagate from source tables, are determined by function return types, or result from expression evaluation. Understanding output types is crucial for views, prepared statements, client applications, and query composition.

🎯 Example 1: Output Column Type Inheritance

-- SELECT inherits types from source columns
SELECT 
    product_id,
    pg_typeof(product_id) AS id_type,
    product_name,
    pg_typeof(product_name) AS name_type,
    price,
    pg_typeof(price) AS price_type,
    created_at,
    pg_typeof(created_at) AS date_type
FROM products
LIMIT 1;
Result:
 product_id | id_type | product_name | name_type | price  | price_type |       created_at       |     date_type
------------+---------+--------------+-----------+--------+------------+------------------------+--------------------
          1 | integer | Laptop       | text      | 999.99 | numeric    | 2026-01-15 10:00:00+00 | timestamp with...
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Computed Column Types

-- Output types determined by expressions
SELECT 
    product_name,
    price,
    -- Arithmetic expression type
    price * 1.1 AS price_with_tax,
    pg_typeof(price * 1.1) AS tax_type,
    -- String concatenation
    'Product: ' || product_name AS description,
    pg_typeof('Product: ' || product_name) AS concat_type,
    -- Conditional expression
    CASE WHEN stock > 50 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Low' END AS stock_level,
    pg_typeof(CASE WHEN stock > 50 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Low' END) AS case_type
FROM products
WHERE product_id = 1;
Result:
 product_name | price  | price_with_tax | tax_type |    description    | concat_type | stock_level | case_type
--------------+--------+----------------+----------+-------------------+-------------+-------------+-----------
 Laptop       | 999.99 |        1099.99 | numeric  | Product: Laptop   | text        | Low         | text
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Function Return Types

-- Output types from function return types
SELECT 
    -- String functions
    upper(product_name) AS upper_name,
    pg_typeof(upper(product_name)) AS upper_type,
    -- Aggregate functions
    count(*) AS total_count,
    pg_typeof(count(*)) AS count_type,
    sum(price) AS total_price,
    pg_typeof(sum(price)) AS sum_type,
    avg(price) AS average_price,
    pg_typeof(avg(price)) AS avg_type
FROM products;
Result:
 upper_name | upper_type | total_count | count_type | total_price | sum_type | average_price | avg_type
------------+------------+-------------+------------+-------------+----------+---------------+----------
 LAPTOP     | text       |          10 | bigint     |     2599.89 | numeric  |       259.99  | numeric
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: View Column Types

-- Create view with determined column types
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW product_summary AS
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    category,
    price,
    stock,
    price * stock AS inventory_value,
    CASE 
        WHEN stock = 0 THEN 'Out of Stock'
        WHEN stock < 50 THEN 'Low Stock'
        ELSE 'In Stock'
    END AS availability_status,
    now() AS report_time
FROM products;

-- Check view column types
SELECT 
    column_name,
    data_type,
    udt_name
FROM information_schema.columns
WHERE table_name = 'product_summary'
ORDER BY ordinal_position;
Result:
    column_name     |       data_type        |      udt_name
--------------------+------------------------+--------------------
 product_id         | integer                | int4
 product_name       | text                   | text
 category           | text                   | text
 price              | numeric                | numeric
 stock              | integer                | int4
 inventory_value    | numeric                | numeric
 availability_status| text                   | text
 report_time        | timestamp with tz      | timestamptz
(8 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Subquery Output Types

-- Subquery column types affect outer query
WITH product_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        category,
        count(*) AS product_count,
        avg(price) AS avg_price,
        max(stock) AS max_stock,
        string_agg(product_name, ', ' ORDER BY product_name) AS products
    FROM products
    GROUP BY category
)
SELECT 
    category,
    product_count,
    pg_typeof(product_count) AS count_type,
    round(avg_price, 2) AS rounded_avg,
    pg_typeof(round(avg_price, 2)) AS round_type,
    max_stock,
    pg_typeof(max_stock) AS stock_type,
    left(products, 30) AS product_sample,
    pg_typeof(products) AS agg_type
FROM product_stats
WHERE category = 'Electronics';
Result:
  category   | product_count | count_type | rounded_avg | round_type | max_stock | stock_type |      product_sample       | agg_type
-------------+---------------+------------+-------------+------------+-----------+------------+---------------------------+----------
 Electronics |             3 | bigint     |      593.32 | numeric    |       100 | integer    | Headphones, Laptop, Smart | text
(1 row)
Explanation: SELECT output column types are determined by source column types, function return types, and expression evaluation rules. Column types are inherited directly from tables. Computed expressions determine type through operator/function resolution. Aggregate functions have specific return types (count→bigint, avg→numeric, string_agg→text). Views store column types at creation. Subquery types propagate to outer queries. Understanding output types is essential for application integration, prepared statements, and query composition. Use pg_typeof() to verify types during development.

Chapter 11: Indexes

Overview

Indexes are critical database structures that dramatically improve query performance by allowing rapid data retrieval without scanning entire tables. PostgreSQL provides multiple index types (B-tree, Hash, GiST, SP-GiST, GIN, BRIN) optimized for different use cases. Understanding when and how to use indexes is essential for building high-performance applications.

11.1 Introduction

Index fundamentals: Indexes are separate data structures that maintain pointers to table rows, enabling fast lookups. They trade storage space and write performance for dramatically faster reads. Without indexes, queries perform sequential scans (reading every row). With appropriate indexes, queries can use index scans (jumping directly to matching rows). Understanding index basics prevents slow queries and performance bottlenecks.

🎯 Example 1: Performance Without Index

-- Query without index (sequential scan)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_name = 'Laptop';

-- Check execution plan
EXPLAIN (FORMAT TEXT, ANALYZE FALSE)
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category = 'Electronics';
Execution Plan (Without Index):
Seq Scan on products  (cost=0.00..1.12 rows=1 width=...)
  Filter: (product_name = 'Laptop'::text)
  Planning Time: 0.052 ms
  Execution Time: 0.123 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Creating Basic Index

-- Create index on product_name
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name ON products(product_name);

-- Create index on category
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category);

-- List all indexes on products table
SELECT 
    indexname,
    indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'products'
ORDER BY indexname;
Result:
       indexname        |                    indexdef
------------------------+------------------------------------------------
 idx_products_category  | CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON...
 idx_products_name      | CREATE INDEX idx_products_name ON products...
 products_pkey          | CREATE UNIQUE INDEX products_pkey ON...
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Performance With Index

-- Same query now uses index scan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_name = 'Laptop';

-- Compare execution plans
EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON)
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category = 'Electronics';
Execution Plan (With Index):
Index Scan using idx_products_name on products  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1)
  Index Cond: (product_name = 'Laptop'::text)
  Planning Time: 0.089 ms
  Execution Time: 0.045 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Index Size and Statistics

-- Check index sizes
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    idx_scan AS times_used,
    idx_tup_read AS tuples_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS tuples_fetched
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'products'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename |      indexname       | index_size | times_used | tuples_read | tuples_fetched
------------+-----------+----------------------+------------+------------+-------------+----------------
 public     | products  | products_pkey        | 16 kB      |        450 |         450 |            450
 public     | products  | idx_products_name    | 16 kB      |         35 |          35 |             35
 public     | products  | idx_products_category| 16 kB      |        120 |         480 |            450
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Dropping Unused Indexes

-- Find unused indexes
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0
  AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%_pkey'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;

-- Drop unused index
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_products_unused;

-- Verify index was dropped
SELECT count(*) AS remaining_indexes
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'products';
Unused Indexes:
 schemaname | tablename |    indexname      | idx_scan | index_size
------------+-----------+-------------------+----------+------------
 public     | products  | idx_old_column    |        0 | 24 kB
(1 row)
After Dropping:
 remaining_indexes
-------------------
                 3
(1 row)
Explanation: Indexes dramatically speed up queries by avoiding full table scans. Sequential scans read every row; index scans jump directly to matching rows. Create indexes on frequently queried columns. Each index consumes disk space and slows INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations. Use EXPLAIN to see query execution plans. Monitor index usage with pg_stat_user_indexes. Drop unused indexes to reduce overhead. Balance query performance with maintenance costs.

11.2 Index Types

Index type selection: PostgreSQL offers six index types, each optimized for specific workloads: B-tree (default, general-purpose), Hash (equality only), GiST (geometric/full-text), SP-GiST (partitioned data), GIN (multi-value columns like arrays/JSONB), BRIN (very large tables with natural ordering). Choosing the right index type can provide 10-100x performance improvements for specific query patterns.

11.2.1 B-Tree

B-Tree indexes: The default and most common index type. B-tree handles <, <=, =, >=, > comparisons and BETWEEN, IN, IS NULL. Supports ORDER BY and maintains sorted order. Works for all data types with comparison operators. Ideal for range queries, sorted results, and general equality searches. Use unless you have specific needs for other index types.

🎯 Example 1: Basic B-Tree Index Creation

-- Create B-tree index (default type)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);

-- Explicitly specify B-tree (same as above)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_date ON orders(order_date) USING btree;

-- Check index types
SELECT 
    tablename,
    indexname,
    indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename IN ('products', 'orders')
  AND indexname LIKE 'idx_%';
Result:
 tablename |      indexname       |                indexdef
-----------+----------------------+----------------------------------------
 products  | idx_products_price   | CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON...
 orders    | idx_orders_date      | CREATE INDEX idx_orders_date ON orders...
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 2: B-Tree Range Queries

-- Range query using B-tree index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price BETWEEN 100 AND 500
ORDER BY price;

-- Greater than query
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price > 200
LIMIT 5;
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_products_price on products  (cost=0.15..12.30 rows=3)
  Index Cond: ((price >= 100) AND (price <= 500))
  Planning Time: 0.125 ms
  Execution Time: 0.067 ms
(4 rows)
Result:
 product_name  | price
---------------+--------
 Desk Chair    | 299.99
 Coffee Maker  | 279.99
 Microwave     | 349.99
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: B-Tree ORDER BY Optimization

-- B-tree provides pre-sorted data
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Combine with WHERE clause
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category = 'Electronics'
ORDER BY price
LIMIT 3;
Execution Plan:
Limit  (cost=0.15..1.45 rows=10)
  -> Index Scan Backward using idx_products_price on products
     Planning Time: 0.089 ms
     Execution Time: 0.045 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 4: B-Tree NULL Handling

-- B-tree indexes can find NULL values
CREATE INDEX idx_products_discount ON products(discount_percent);

-- Query for NULL values (index can be used)
SELECT product_name, discount_percent
FROM products
WHERE discount_percent IS NULL
LIMIT 3;

-- Query for NOT NULL
SELECT product_name, discount_percent
FROM products
WHERE discount_percent IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY discount_percent DESC
LIMIT 3;
NULL Results:
 product_name | discount_percent
--------------+------------------
 Laptop       | NULL
 Mouse        | NULL
 Keyboard     | NULL
(3 rows)
NOT NULL Results:
 product_name  | discount_percent
---------------+------------------
 T-Shirt       |            25.00
 Headphones    |            15.00
 Desk Chair    |            10.00
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: B-Tree Pattern Matching (Prefix)

-- B-tree supports LIKE with prefix (no leading wildcard)
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_name ON customers(customer_name);

-- Prefix search uses index (efficient)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT customer_name, email
FROM customers
WHERE customer_name LIKE 'John%';

-- Leading wildcard cannot use standard B-tree
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT customer_name
FROM customers
WHERE customer_name LIKE '%Smith';
Prefix Search (Uses Index):
Index Scan using idx_customers_name on customers  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1)
  Index Cond: ((customer_name >= 'John') AND (customer_name < 'Joio'))
  Filter: (customer_name ~~ 'John%'::text)
(3 rows)
Suffix Search (Sequential Scan):
Seq Scan on customers  (cost=0.00..2.50 rows=1)
  Filter: (customer_name ~~ '%Smith'::text)
(2 rows)
Explanation: B-tree is the default, most versatile index type. Supports all comparison operators (<, <=, =, >=, >), range queries (BETWEEN), sorting (ORDER BY), and NULL searches (IS NULL). B-tree maintains balanced tree structure, keeping data sorted. Excellent for range scans, equality lookups, sorted retrieval. Supports prefix pattern matching (LIKE 'prefix%') but not suffix/infix. Use B-tree for 90% of indexing needs - only choose specialized types for specific requirements.

11.2.2 Hash

Hash indexes: Optimized for simple equality comparisons only (=). Cannot handle range queries, sorting, or inequality comparisons. Generally B-tree performs similarly for equality, so hash indexes are rarely needed. Use hash when: (1) you only need equality lookups, (2) values are very large (hashing is faster), (3) write-heavy workload where hash's simpler structure helps.

🎯 Example 1: Creating Hash Index

-- Create hash index for equality-only lookups
CREATE INDEX idx_products_sku_hash ON products(sku) USING hash;

-- Check index type
SELECT 
    tablename,
    indexname,
    indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE indexname = 'idx_products_sku_hash';
Result:
 tablename |       indexname        |                indexdef
-----------+------------------------+----------------------------------------
 products  | idx_products_sku_hash  | CREATE INDEX idx_products_sku_hash ON...
                                     | USING hash
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Hash Index Equality Queries

-- Equality query uses hash index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products WHERE sku = 'SKU-001';

-- Multiple equality conditions
SELECT product_name, sku, price
FROM products
WHERE sku IN ('SKU-001', 'SKU-002', 'SKU-003');
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_products_sku_hash on products  (cost=0.00..8.02 rows=1)
  Index Cond: (sku = 'SKU-001'::text)
  Planning Time: 0.095 ms
  Execution Time: 0.042 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Hash Index Limitations

-- Hash cannot handle range queries (uses seq scan instead)
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM products WHERE sku > 'SKU-100';

-- Hash cannot handle LIKE queries
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM products WHERE sku LIKE 'SKU-%';
Result (Cannot Use Hash):
Seq Scan on products  (cost=0.00..1.15 rows=1)
  Filter: (sku > 'SKU-100'::text)
  -- Hash index NOT used for range queries
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Comparing Hash vs B-Tree

-- Create both types for comparison
CREATE INDEX idx_email_btree ON customers(email) USING btree;
CREATE INDEX idx_email_hash ON customers(email) USING hash;

-- Check sizes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE 'idx_email%'
ORDER BY indexname;
Result:
    indexname     | size
------------------+-------
 idx_email_btree  | 16 kB
 idx_email_hash   | 16 kB
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: When to Use Hash

-- Use hash for: unique identifiers with only equality checks
-- Example: session tokens, UUIDs with only exact lookups

CREATE TABLE sessions (
    session_id uuid PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id integer,
    data jsonb,
    created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

-- Hash index for session lookups (equality only)
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_id_hash ON sessions(session_id) USING hash;

-- Query by session ID (exact match)
SELECT session_id, user_id, created_at
FROM sessions
WHERE session_id = 'a0eebc99-9c0b-4ef8-bb6d-6bb9bd380a11'::uuid;
Result:
            session_id              | user_id |       created_at
----------------------------------------+---------+------------------------
 a0eebc99-9c0b-4ef8-bb6d-6bb9bd380a11   |     101 | 2026-01-26 10:00:00+00
(1 row)
Explanation: Hash indexes support only equality (=) operator. Cannot do range scans, sorting, or inequality comparisons. Hash function maps values to buckets - fast for exact matches. Historically had limitations (no WAL logging, crash recovery issues) but fully supported since PostgreSQL 10. B-tree usually equally fast for equality while supporting more operations. Use hash only when: (1) exclusively equality lookups, (2) very large keys where hashing is faster, (3) proven performance benefit. In most cases, stick with B-tree.

11.2.3 GiST

GiST indexes: Generalized Search Tree - extensible framework supporting various data types and operators. Built-in support for geometric types (points, boxes, polygons), full-text search (tsvector), IP networks (inet), ranges. Custom GiST indexes can be created for specialized types. Ideal for spatial queries, nearest-neighbor searches, complex containment checks.

🎯 Example 1: GiST for Geometric Data

-- Create table with geometric column
CREATE TABLE stores (
    store_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    store_name text,
    location point
);

INSERT INTO stores (store_name, location) VALUES
    ('Downtown Store', point(40.7128, -74.0060)),
    ('Uptown Store', point(40.7589, -73.9851)),
    ('Brooklyn Store', point(40.6782, -73.9442));

-- Create GiST index for geometric queries
CREATE INDEX idx_stores_location ON stores USING gist(location);

-- Find nearest store to a point
SELECT store_name, location
FROM stores
ORDER BY location <-> point(40.7500, -73.9800)
LIMIT 1;
Result:
   store_name   |      location
----------------+--------------------
 Uptown Store   | (40.7589,-73.9851)
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: GiST for Full-Text Search

-- Add full-text search column
ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN search_vector tsvector;

UPDATE products
SET search_vector = to_tsvector('english', 
    coalesce(product_name, '') || ' ' || coalesce(description, ''));

-- Create GiST index for full-text search
CREATE INDEX idx_products_search ON products USING gist(search_vector);

-- Full-text search query
SELECT product_name, description
FROM products
WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'electronic & device')
LIMIT 3;
Result:
 product_name  |           description
---------------+----------------------------------
 Laptop        | High-performance electronic device
 Smartphone    | Mobile electronic device
 Tablet        | Portable electronic device
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: GiST for Range Types

-- Create table with date ranges
CREATE TABLE events (
    event_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    event_name text,
    event_period daterange
);

INSERT INTO events (event_name, event_period) VALUES
    ('Summer Sale', '[2026-06-01,2026-08-31]'::daterange),
    ('Holiday Season', '[2026-11-15,2026-12-31]'::daterange),
    ('Spring Promo', '[2026-03-01,2026-05-31]'::daterange);

-- GiST index for range operations
CREATE INDEX idx_events_period ON events USING gist(event_period);

-- Find events containing a specific date
SELECT event_name, event_period
FROM events
WHERE event_period @> '2026-07-15'::date;
Result:
  event_name  |      event_period
--------------+-------------------------
 Summer Sale  | [2026-06-01,2026-08-31)
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: GiST for IP Networks

-- Table with IP network ranges
CREATE TABLE ip_blocks (
    block_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    network inet,
    description text
);

INSERT INTO ip_blocks (network, description) VALUES
    ('192.168.1.0/24', 'Internal Network A'),
    ('10.0.0.0/8', 'Private Network'),
    ('172.16.0.0/12', 'Internal Network B');

-- GiST index for network containment
CREATE INDEX idx_ip_blocks_network ON ip_blocks USING gist(network);

-- Check if IP is in any block
SELECT description, network
FROM ip_blocks
WHERE network >> '192.168.1.50'::inet;
Result:
    description     |     network
--------------------+-----------------
 Internal Network A | 192.168.1.0/24
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: GiST Index Statistics

-- Check GiST index usage
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan,
    idx_tup_read,
    idx_tup_fetch
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE '%gist%'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename |       indexname        | idx_scan | idx_tup_read | idx_tup_fetch
------------+-----------+------------------------+----------+--------------+---------------
 public     | stores    | idx_stores_location    |       45 |           45 |            45
 public     | products  | idx_products_search    |       28 |          120 |           115
 public     | events    | idx_events_period      |       12 |           18 |            18
(3 rows)
Explanation: GiST (Generalized Search Tree) is extensible framework for specialized data types. Supports geometric data (points, polygons - nearest neighbor, containment), full-text search (tsvector), ranges (overlap, containment), network addresses. GiST uses lossy compression - may require rechecking matches. Slower than B-tree for simple equality but essential for complex spatial/containment queries. Use GiST for: PostGIS geometric queries, full-text search, range overlaps, custom types requiring containment/proximity operators.

11.2.4 SP-GiST

SP-GiST indexes: Space-Partitioned Generalized Search Tree - supports partitioned search trees like quad-trees, k-d trees, radix trees. Efficient for data with natural clustering or hierarchical structure: phone numbers, IP addresses, geographic coordinates. Better than GiST for certain data distributions. Supports geometric types, text (prefix searches), inet/cidr types.

🎯 Example 1: SP-GiST for Text Prefix Searches

-- SP-GiST excels at prefix searches
CREATE TABLE phone_directory (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    name text,
    phone_number text
);

INSERT INTO phone_directory (name, phone_number) VALUES
    ('Alice Smith', '+1-555-0100'),
    ('Bob Jones', '+1-555-0200'),
    ('Carol White', '+1-555-0150'),
    ('Dave Brown', '+1-555-0250');

-- SP-GiST index for prefix searches
CREATE INDEX idx_phone_spgist ON phone_directory USING spgist(phone_number);

-- Efficient prefix search
SELECT name, phone_number
FROM phone_directory
WHERE phone_number LIKE '+1-555-01%';
Result:
     name      | phone_number
---------------+--------------
 Alice Smith   | +1-555-0100
 Carol White   | +1-555-0150
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 2: SP-GiST for IP Addresses

-- Table with IP addresses
CREATE TABLE access_log (
    log_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    ip_address inet,
    accessed_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

INSERT INTO access_log (ip_address) VALUES
    ('192.168.1.10'),
    ('192.168.1.25'),
    ('10.0.0.5'),
    ('172.16.0.100');

-- SP-GiST for IP address searches
CREATE INDEX idx_access_ip_spgist ON access_log USING spgist(ip_address);

-- Range queries on IP addresses
SELECT ip_address, accessed_at
FROM access_log
WHERE ip_address << '192.168.1.0/24'::inet
ORDER BY accessed_at DESC;
Result:
  ip_address   |       accessed_at
---------------+------------------------
 192.168.1.25  | 2026-01-26 14:30:25+00
 192.168.1.10  | 2026-01-26 14:30:10+00
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: SP-GiST for Point Data

-- Geographic coordinates with SP-GiST
CREATE TABLE landmarks (
    landmark_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    name text,
    coordinates point
);

INSERT INTO landmarks (name, coordinates) VALUES
    ('City Hall', point(40.7128, -74.0060)),
    ('Central Park', point(40.7829, -73.9654)),
    ('Times Square', point(40.7580, -73.9855));

-- SP-GiST for spatial partitioning
CREATE INDEX idx_landmarks_coords ON landmarks USING spgist(coordinates);

-- Spatial query
SELECT name, coordinates
FROM landmarks
WHERE coordinates <@ box(point(40.70, -74.01), point(40.80, -73.95));
Result:
     name      |      coordinates
---------------+----------------------
 City Hall     | (40.7128,-74.006)
 Times Square  | (40.758,-73.9855)
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 4: SP-GiST vs B-Tree Performance

-- Compare index types for text searches
CREATE INDEX idx_phone_btree ON phone_directory(phone_number) USING btree;
CREATE INDEX idx_phone_spgist2 ON phone_directory(phone_number) USING spgist;

-- Analyze query plans
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM phone_directory WHERE phone_number LIKE '+1-555%';

-- Check index sizes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'phone_directory';
Index Comparison:
     indexname      | size
--------------------+-------
 idx_phone_btree    | 16 kB
 idx_phone_spgist   | 16 kB
 idx_phone_spgist2  | 16 kB
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: SP-GiST for Range Types

-- SP-GiST can also handle ranges
CREATE TABLE reservations (
    reservation_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    room_number integer,
    reserved_period daterange
);

INSERT INTO reservations (room_number, reserved_period) VALUES
    (101, '[2026-01-20,2026-01-25]'::daterange),
    (102, '[2026-01-22,2026-01-28]'::daterange),
    (103, '[2026-01-26,2026-01-30]'::daterange);

-- SP-GiST for range overlaps
CREATE INDEX idx_reservations_period ON reservations USING spgist(reserved_period);

-- Find overlapping reservations
SELECT room_number, reserved_period
FROM reservations
WHERE reserved_period && '[2026-01-24,2026-01-27]'::daterange;
Result:
 room_number |     reserved_period
-------------+-------------------------
         101 | [2026-01-20,2026-01-25)
         102 | [2026-01-22,2026-01-28)
         103 | [2026-01-26,2026-01-30)
(3 rows)
Explanation: SP-GiST (Space-Partitioned GiST) uses partitioning strategies like quad-trees, k-d trees, radix trees. Excellent for naturally clustered data: phone numbers (radix tree), IP addresses (quad-tree), geographic points (k-d tree). More efficient than regular GiST for certain data distributions. Supports text prefix searches, geometric data, IP networks, ranges. Use SP-GiST when: data has hierarchical structure, prefix searches are common, point data has spatial clustering. Choose between GiST and SP-GiST based on data distribution and query patterns.

11.2.5 GIN

GIN indexes: Generalized Inverted Index - optimized for multi-value columns where single value can match multiple rows. Perfect for: arrays (containment, overlap), JSONB (key/value searches), full-text search (tsvector), hstore. GIN creates entries for each element/key. Larger than B-tree but dramatically faster for containment queries. Essential for array and JSONB operations.

🎯 Example 1: GIN for Array Operations

-- Table with array column
CREATE TABLE articles (
    article_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text,
    tags text[]
);

INSERT INTO articles (title, tags) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL Tutorial', ARRAY['database', 'sql', 'postgresql']),
    ('Python Guide', ARRAY['programming', 'python', 'tutorial']),
    ('Web Development', ARRAY['html', 'css', 'javascript', 'web']);

-- GIN index for array operations
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_tags ON articles USING gin(tags);

-- Find articles with specific tag (containment)
SELECT title, tags
FROM articles
WHERE tags @> ARRAY['postgresql'];
Result:
       title        |            tags
--------------------+-----------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| {database,sql,postgresql}
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: GIN for JSONB Queries

-- Table with JSONB data
CREATE TABLE products_json (
    product_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    product_data jsonb
);

INSERT INTO products_json (product_data) VALUES
    ('{"name":"Laptop","specs":{"cpu":"Intel i7","ram":"16GB"}}'::jsonb),
    ('{"name":"Phone","specs":{"cpu":"Snapdragon","ram":"8GB"}}'::jsonb),
    ('{"name":"Tablet","specs":{"cpu":"Apple M1","ram":"8GB"}}'::jsonb);

-- GIN index for JSONB containment
CREATE INDEX idx_products_data ON products_json USING gin(product_data);

-- Query by JSONB containment
SELECT product_data->>'name' AS name,
       product_data->'specs'->>'ram' AS ram
FROM products_json
WHERE product_data @> '{"specs":{"ram":"8GB"}}';
Result:
  name  |  ram
--------+------
 Phone  | 8GB
 Tablet | 8GB
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: GIN for Full-Text Search

-- Full-text search with GIN
CREATE TABLE documents (
    doc_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text,
    content text,
    search_vector tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS 
        (to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(content,''))) STORED
);

INSERT INTO documents (title, content) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL Guide', 'Learn about database indexing and optimization'),
    ('Python Tutorial', 'Introduction to Python programming language'),
    ('Data Science', 'Analysis and visualization using Python and SQL');

-- GIN index for fast full-text search
CREATE INDEX idx_documents_search ON documents USING gin(search_vector);

-- Full-text search
SELECT title, content
FROM documents
WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'database | python');
Result:
      title       |                  content
------------------+---------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Guide | Learn about database indexing and optimization
 Python Tutorial  | Introduction to Python programming language
 Data Science     | Analysis and visualization using Python and SQL
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: GIN Index with jsonb_path_ops

-- Standard GIN vs jsonb_path_ops (smaller, faster for @> queries)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_data_ops ON products_json USING gin(product_data jsonb_path_ops);

-- Compare index sizes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'products_json'
ORDER BY indexname;
Result:
        indexname         | size
--------------------------+-------
 idx_products_data        | 24 kB
 idx_products_data_ops    | 16 kB  -- Smaller!
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: GIN Performance Statistics

-- Check GIN index usage
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan AS scans,
    idx_tup_read AS tuples_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS tuples_fetched,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE '%gin%'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename    |       indexname        | scans | tuples_read | tuples_fetched | size
------------+--------------+------------------------+-------+-------------+----------------+------
 public     | articles     | idx_articles_tags      |   145 |         289 |            275 | 16kB
 public     | products_json| idx_products_data      |    89 |         178 |            165 | 24kB
 public     | documents    | idx_documents_search   |    56 |         223 |            210 | 32kB
(3 rows)
Explanation: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) excels at multi-value data. Creates index entry for each element/key - one array element can point to multiple rows. Essential for: array containment (@>, &&, <@), JSONB key/value searches (@>, @?, @@), full-text search (@@), hstore operations. Two operator classes: standard (supports more operators) and jsonb_path_ops (smaller, faster, but only @> operator). GIN larger than B-tree but 100x+ faster for containment. Use GIN for arrays, JSONB, full-text search - mandatory for these data types.

11.2.6 BRIN

BRIN indexes: Block Range Index - stores min/max values for data ranges (blocks). Extremely small, fast to scan. Perfect for very large tables with natural ordering (timestamps, sequential IDs). Typical BRIN index is 1000x smaller than B-tree. Not suitable for random data or exact lookups. Use BRIN for: time-series data, log tables, append-only tables with monotonic values.

🎯 Example 1: BRIN for Time-Series Data

-- Large time-series table
CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
    reading_id bigserial,
    sensor_id integer,
    temperature numeric(5,2),
    recorded_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

-- Insert sample time-series data
INSERT INTO sensor_readings (sensor_id, temperature, recorded_at)
SELECT 
    (random() * 100)::integer,
    (random() * 50)::numeric(5,2),
    now() - (random() * interval '365 days')
FROM generate_series(1, 10000);

-- BRIN index for timestamp column
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_time_brin ON sensor_readings USING brin(recorded_at);

-- Range query uses BRIN efficiently
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT avg(temperature)
FROM sensor_readings
WHERE recorded_at >= now() - interval '30 days';
Execution Plan:
Bitmap Heap Scan on sensor_readings  (cost=12.00..89.50 rows=850)
  Recheck Cond: (recorded_at >= (now() - '30 days'::interval))
  -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_readings_time_brin
       Index Cond: (recorded_at >= (now() - '30 days'::interval))
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 2: BRIN Index Size Comparison

-- Create both B-tree and BRIN for comparison
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_time_btree ON sensor_readings(recorded_at) USING btree;
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_time_brin2 ON sensor_readings(recorded_at) USING brin;

-- Compare sizes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('sensor_readings')) AS table_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'sensor_readings'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;
Result:
        indexname         | index_size | table_size
--------------------------+------------+------------
 idx_readings_time_btree  | 224 kB     | 896 kB
 idx_readings_time_brin   | 24 kB      | 896 kB  -- 10x smaller!
 idx_readings_time_brin2  | 24 kB      | 896 kB
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: BRIN for Sequential IDs

-- Log table with sequential IDs
CREATE TABLE application_logs (
    log_id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
    log_level text,
    message text,
    created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

-- Insert sequential log entries
INSERT INTO application_logs (log_level, message)
SELECT 
    CASE (random() * 3)::integer
        WHEN 0 THEN 'INFO'
        WHEN 1 THEN 'WARNING'
        ELSE 'ERROR'
    END,
    'Log message ' || generate_series
FROM generate_series(1, 50000);

-- BRIN on sequential ID
CREATE INDEX idx_logs_id_brin ON application_logs USING brin(log_id);

-- Range query on ID
SELECT count(*)
FROM application_logs
WHERE log_id BETWEEN 10000 AND 20000;
Result:
 count
-------
 10001
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: BRIN Pages Per Range

-- Create BRIN with custom pages_per_range
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_brin_128 ON sensor_readings 
USING brin(recorded_at) WITH (pages_per_range = 128);  -- default

CREATE INDEX idx_readings_brin_64 ON sensor_readings 
USING brin(recorded_at) WITH (pages_per_range = 64);  -- finer granularity

-- Compare sizes and effectiveness
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE '%brin%'
ORDER BY indexname;
Result:
       indexname        | size
------------------------+-------
 idx_readings_brin_128  | 24 kB
 idx_readings_brin_64   | 32 kB  -- Larger but more precise
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: When NOT to Use BRIN

-- BRIN performs poorly on random/unordered data
CREATE TABLE random_data (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    random_value integer
);

INSERT INTO random_data (random_value)
SELECT (random() * 1000000)::integer
FROM generate_series(1, 10000);

-- BRIN on random data (inefficient)
CREATE INDEX idx_random_brin ON random_data USING brin(random_value);

-- Query may scan many blocks due to overlapping ranges
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM random_data WHERE random_value = 500000;

-- B-tree would be much better for random data
CREATE INDEX idx_random_btree ON random_data(random_value) USING btree;
BRIN on Random Data (Poor):
Bitmap Heap Scan on random_data  (cost=12.00..125.50 rows=10)
  Recheck Cond: (random_value = 500000)
  Rows Removed by Index Recheck: 9850  -- Many false positives!
  -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_random_brin
       Index Cond: (random_value = 500000)
(5 rows)
Explanation: BRIN (Block Range Index) stores min/max values for block ranges (default 128 pages). Extremely compact - typically 1000x smaller than B-tree. Perfect for naturally ordered data: timestamps (append-only logs), sequential IDs, monotonically increasing values. BRIN scans block ranges that might contain matching values. Inefficient for random data (many false positives require rechecking). Tuning: smaller pages_per_range = larger index but more precise. Use BRIN for: very large tables (TB+), time-series data, append-only tables, when index size is critical. Avoid for: randomly distributed data, exact lookups, frequent updates.

11.3 Multicolumn Indexes

Multicolumn indexes: Index on multiple columns (composite/compound index). B-tree, GiST, GIN, BRIN support multiple columns. Column order matters! Leftmost columns must be used in WHERE clause. Useful for queries filtering on multiple columns together. Maximum 32 columns per index. Consider single-column indexes vs multicolumn based on query patterns.

🎯 Example 1: Basic Multicolumn Index

-- Composite index on (category_id, price)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category_price ON products(category_id, price);

-- Query uses the index (leftmost column present)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
  AND price BETWEEN 50 AND 100;
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_products_category_price on products
  Index Cond: ((category_id = 1) AND (price >= 50) AND (price <= 100))
  Planning Time: 0.125 ms
  Execution Time: 0.089 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Column Order Matters

-- Same index: (category_id, price)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category_price ON products(category_id, price);

-- Query 1: Uses index (category_id is leftmost)
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Query 2: Uses index (both columns)
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1 AND price > 50;

-- Query 3: Cannot use index efficiently (price alone, not leftmost)
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 50;
Results:
-- Query 1: Index Scan using idx_products_category_price
-- Query 2: Index Scan using idx_products_category_price
-- Query 3: Seq Scan on products  (index not used!)

🎯 Example 3: Multi-Column Index for Sorting

-- Index supports both filtering and sorting
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_date ON orders(customer_id, order_date DESC);

-- Efficient: filter + sort using index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 1
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 10;
Execution Plan:
Limit  (cost=0.29..1.85 rows=10)
  -> Index Scan using idx_orders_customer_date on orders
       Index Cond: (customer_id = 1)
       -- No separate Sort step needed!
Planning Time: 0.098 ms
Execution Time: 0.067 ms
(6 rows)

🎯 Example 4: When to Use Multiple Single-Column Indexes

-- Different query patterns - use separate indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_stock ON products(stock_quantity);

-- Query 1: Uses idx_products_category
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Query 2: Uses idx_products_price
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100;

-- Query 3: PostgreSQL combines both indexes (bitmap scan)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products 
WHERE category_id = 1 AND price > 100;
Bitmap Index Scan (Combines Indexes):
Bitmap Heap Scan on products
  Recheck Cond: ((category_id = 1) AND (price > 100))
  -> BitmapAnd
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_category
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_price
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Three-Column Index

-- Index on three columns for complex queries
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_status_date 
ON orders(customer_id, status, order_date);

-- Query uses all three columns
SELECT order_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 1
  AND status = 'shipped'
  AND order_date >= '2024-01-01';

-- Check index usage
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan,
    idx_tup_read,
    idx_tup_fetch
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname = 'idx_orders_customer_status_date';
Index Statistics:
 schemaname | tablename |           indexname             | idx_scan | idx_tup_read | idx_tup_fetch
------------+-----------+---------------------------------+----------+--------------+---------------
 public     | orders    | idx_orders_customer_status_date |      234 |         1156 |          1098
(1 row)
Explanation: Multicolumn indexes optimize queries filtering on multiple columns. Column order critical - query must use leftmost column(s). Index (A,B,C) supports: WHERE A, WHERE A AND B, WHERE A AND B AND C. Does NOT support: WHERE B, WHERE C, WHERE B AND C. Choose column order: most selective/frequently queried first, then by frequency of column combinations. Alternative: multiple single-column indexes - PostgreSQL can combine using bitmap scans. Trade-off: multicolumn index faster for specific combinations but less flexible; single indexes more flexible but slower for combined queries. Choose based on actual query patterns.

11.4 Indexes and ORDER BY

Indexes and sorting: Indexes can eliminate or reduce sorting operations. B-tree indexes naturally maintain order. Index column order must match ORDER BY columns. Can specify ASC/DESC per column. NULLS FIRST/LAST also matters. Significant performance benefit for queries with ORDER BY + LIMIT.

🎯 Example 1: Index Eliminates Sort

-- Without index: must sort
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers
ORDER BY last_name
LIMIT 10;
Without Index (Requires Sort):
Limit  (cost=89.83..89.85 rows=10)
  -> Sort  (cost=89.83..92.33 rows=1000)  -- Expensive sort!
       Sort Key: last_name
       Sort Method: top-N heapsort  Memory: 25kB
       -> Seq Scan on customers  (cost=0.00..65.00 rows=1000)
Planning Time: 0.102 ms
Execution Time: 2.456 ms
-- Create index on ORDER BY column
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_lastname ON customers(last_name);

-- With index: no sort needed!
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers
ORDER BY last_name
LIMIT 10;
With Index (No Sort Needed):
Limit  (cost=0.28..1.12 rows=10)
  -> Index Scan using idx_customers_lastname on customers
       -- No Sort step!
Planning Time: 0.089 ms
Execution Time: 0.067 ms  -- 36x faster!

🎯 Example 2: Index with DESC Order

-- Index with descending order
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_date_desc ON orders(order_date DESC);

-- Query benefits from DESC index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 20;
Execution Plan:
Limit  (cost=0.29..1.45 rows=20)
  -> Index Scan using idx_orders_date_desc on orders
       -- Index already in DESC order
Planning Time: 0.078 ms
Execution Time: 0.056 ms
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Multi-Column ORDER BY

-- Index matching ORDER BY columns and order
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category_price_desc 
ON products(category_id ASC, price DESC);

-- Query uses index for both filtering and sorting
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
ORDER BY category_id ASC, price DESC
LIMIT 5;
Execution Plan:
Limit  (cost=0.29..0.95 rows=5)
  -> Index Scan using idx_products_category_price_desc on products
       Index Cond: (category_id = 1)
       -- No separate sort needed
Planning Time: 0.101 ms
Execution Time: 0.043 ms
(6 rows)

🎯 Example 4: NULLS FIRST/LAST

-- Index with NULL handling
CREATE INDEX idx_products_stock_nulls_last 
ON products(stock_quantity DESC NULLS LAST);

-- Query matching NULL ordering
SELECT product_name, stock_quantity
FROM products
ORDER BY stock_quantity DESC NULLS LAST
LIMIT 10;
Result:
   product_name   | stock_quantity
------------------+----------------
 Laptop Pro       |           1500
 Wireless Mouse   |           1200
 USB-C Cable      |            980
 Monitor 27"      |            750
 Keyboard Mech    |            620
 Phone Case       |            450
 Tablet Stand     |            280
 Headphones       |            125
 Webcam HD        |             85
 Mouse Pad        |             40
(10 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Index Scan Backward

-- Index supports both ASC and DESC scans
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_email ON customers(email);

-- Forward scan (ASC)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT email FROM customers ORDER BY email ASC LIMIT 5;

-- Backward scan (DESC) - same index!
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT email FROM customers ORDER BY email DESC LIMIT 5;
Both Directions Supported:
-- ASC:
Index Scan using idx_customers_email on customers

-- DESC:
Index Scan Backward using idx_customers_email on customers
-- PostgreSQL scans index in reverse
Explanation: B-tree indexes maintain sorted order - can eliminate sorting operations. Index must match ORDER BY: same columns, same order (ASC/DESC), same NULL handling. PostgreSQL can scan B-tree forward (ASC) or backward (DESC) - single index supports both directions. For DESC queries on large tables, explicit DESC index slightly faster. Multi-column: index (A ASC, B DESC) only helps ORDER BY A ASC, B DESC - not other combinations. Huge performance win with ORDER BY + LIMIT - avoids sorting entire table. Check EXPLAIN: "Index Scan" with no "Sort" step confirms index usage.

11.5 Combining Multiple Indexes

Bitmap index scans: PostgreSQL can combine multiple single-column indexes for complex queries. Uses bitmap AND/OR operations. Alternative to multicolumn indexes. Useful when different queries use different column combinations. Bitmap scan has small overhead but more flexible than multicolumn indexes.

🎯 Example 1: Bitmap AND (Multiple Conditions)

-- Create separate indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);

-- Query with AND condition - combines both indexes
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name, price, category_id
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
  AND price BETWEEN 50 AND 100;
Bitmap Index Scan (Combines Indexes):
Bitmap Heap Scan on products  (cost=8.27..32.45 rows=15)
  Recheck Cond: ((category_id = 1) AND (price >= 50) AND (price <= 100))
  -> BitmapAnd  (cost=8.27..8.27 rows=15)
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_category
            Index Cond: (category_id = 1)
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_price
            Index Cond: (price >= 50) AND (price <= 100)
Planning Time: 0.234 ms
Execution Time: 0.156 ms
(9 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Bitmap OR (Multiple Alternatives)

-- Indexes on different columns
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer ON orders(customer_id);

-- Query with OR condition
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, status, customer_id
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
   OR customer_id = 1;
Bitmap OR Scan:
Bitmap Heap Scan on orders  (cost=12.45..45.78 rows=25)
  Recheck Cond: ((status = 'pending') OR (customer_id = 1))
  -> BitmapOr  (cost=12.45..12.45 rows=25)
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_orders_status
            Index Cond: (status = 'pending')
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_orders_customer
            Index Cond: (customer_id = 1)
(7 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Three Indexes Combined

-- Three separate indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_stock ON products(stock_quantity);

-- Query combines all three
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
  AND price < 100
  AND stock_quantity > 0;
Three-Way Bitmap AND:
Bitmap Heap Scan on products  (cost=15.67..42.89 rows=12)
  Recheck Cond: ((category_id = 1) AND (price < 100) AND (stock_quantity > 0))
  -> BitmapAnd  (cost=15.67..15.67 rows=12)
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_category
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_price
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_stock
(7 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Bitmap Scan vs Multicolumn Index

-- Test data
CREATE TABLE test_data (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    col_a integer,
    col_b integer,
    col_c text
);

INSERT INTO test_data (col_a, col_b, col_c)
SELECT 
    (random() * 100)::integer,
    (random() * 100)::integer,
    md5(random()::text)
FROM generate_series(1, 10000);

-- Option 1: Two single-column indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_test_cola ON test_data(col_a);
CREATE INDEX idx_test_colb ON test_data(col_b);

-- Option 2: One multicolumn index
CREATE INDEX idx_test_cola_colb ON test_data(col_a, col_b);

-- Compare performance
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM test_data WHERE col_a = 50 AND col_b = 75;
Performance Comparison:
-- With bitmap scan (two single indexes):
Bitmap Heap Scan  (cost=8.45..25.67 rows=1)  Time: 0.234 ms

-- With multicolumn index:
Index Scan using idx_test_cola_colb  (cost=0.29..8.31 rows=1)  Time: 0.089 ms
-- Multicolumn index faster for this specific combination

🎯 Example 5: When Bitmap Scans Are Optimal

-- Different query patterns - single indexes better
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_supplier ON products(supplier_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_brand ON products(brand_id);

-- Query 1: Uses idx_products_category
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Query 2: Uses idx_products_supplier
SELECT * FROM products WHERE supplier_id = 5;

-- Query 3: Uses idx_products_brand
SELECT * FROM products WHERE brand_id = 3;

-- Query 4: Combines idx_products_category + idx_products_supplier
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1 AND supplier_id = 5;

-- Query 5: Combines idx_products_category + idx_products_brand
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1 AND brand_id = 3;
Flexible Index Usage:
-- Three single-column indexes support:
-- - Each column individually
-- - Any two-column combination (bitmap AND)
-- - All three together (bitmap AND)

-- Would need 7 multicolumn indexes for same flexibility:
-- (A), (B), (C), (A,B), (A,C), (B,C), (A,B,C)
Explanation: PostgreSQL automatically combines multiple indexes using bitmap scans. Bitmap AND for conjunctions (WHERE A AND B), Bitmap OR for disjunctions (WHERE A OR B). Each index creates bitmap of matching row locations, then combines bitmaps before heap access. Small overhead vs multicolumn index but much more flexible. Use single-column indexes when: many different query patterns, no dominant column combination, want maximum flexibility. Use multicolumn indexes when: specific combination queried frequently, need maximum performance for that combination. Bitmap scan particularly effective for selective queries (small result sets) and complex multi-condition WHERE clauses.

11.6 Unique Indexes

Unique indexes: Enforce uniqueness constraint + provide index for queries. Created automatically for PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints. Can also create manually with CREATE UNIQUE INDEX. Reject duplicate values. Support NULL values (multiple NULLs allowed - each NULL is distinct). More efficient than regular index + constraint check.

🎯 Example 1: Unique Index Basics

-- Create unique index manually
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_customers_email_unique ON customers(email);

-- Try inserting duplicate - fails
INSERT INTO customers (first_name, last_name, email)
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', 'john@example.com');

INSERT INTO customers (first_name, last_name, email)
VALUES ('Jane', 'Smith', 'john@example.com');  -- Duplicate!
Error:
ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "idx_customers_email_unique"
DETAIL:  Key (email)=(john@example.com) already exists.

🎯 Example 2: Unique Constraint vs Unique Index

-- Method 1: UNIQUE constraint (creates unique index automatically)
ALTER TABLE customers ADD CONSTRAINT customers_email_key UNIQUE (email);

-- Method 2: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX (manual)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_customers_email ON customers(email);

-- Both enforce uniqueness, but constraint shows in \d output
\d customers
Table Definition:
Table "public.customers"
   Column    |  Type   | Nullable
-------------+---------+----------
 customer_id | integer | not null
 email       | text    |
Indexes:
    "customers_pkey" PRIMARY KEY, btree (customer_id)
    "customers_email_key" UNIQUE CONSTRAINT, btree (email)
    "idx_customers_email" UNIQUE, btree (email)

🎯 Example 3: Composite Unique Index

-- Unique combination of multiple columns
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_order_items_unique 
ON order_items(order_id, product_id);

-- Same product can appear in different orders
INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, price_each)
VALUES (1, 101, 2, 29.99);  -- OK

-- But not twice in same order
INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, price_each)
VALUES (1, 101, 1, 29.99);  -- Duplicate!
Error:
ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "idx_order_items_unique"
DETAIL:  Key (order_id, product_id)=(1, 101) already exists.

🎯 Example 4: Unique Index with NULLs

-- Create table with nullable unique column
CREATE TABLE employees (
    employee_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    name text NOT NULL,
    phone text
);

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_employees_phone ON employees(phone);

-- Multiple NULLs are allowed (each NULL is distinct)
INSERT INTO employees (name, phone) VALUES ('Alice', NULL);
INSERT INTO employees (name, phone) VALUES ('Bob', NULL);  -- OK!
INSERT INTO employees (name, phone) VALUES ('Charlie', '555-1234');
INSERT INTO employees (name, phone) VALUES ('David', '555-1234');  -- Fails!

SELECT * FROM employees;
Result:
 employee_id |   name   |   phone
-------------+----------+-----------
           1 | Alice    | NULL
           2 | Bob      | NULL
           3 | Charlie  | 555-1234
(3 rows)

ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "idx_employees_phone"

🎯 Example 5: Unique Index Performance

-- Unique index used for lookups
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_products_sku ON products(sku);

-- Fast lookup using unique index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE sku = 'LAP-001';

-- Check index statistics
SELECT 
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan,
    idx_tup_read,
    idx_tup_fetch
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname = 'idx_products_sku';
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_products_sku on products  (cost=0.29..8.31 rows=1)
  Index Cond: (sku = 'LAP-001')
Planning Time: 0.067 ms
Execution Time: 0.045 ms  -- Very fast!

 tablename |     indexname      | idx_scan | idx_tup_read | idx_tup_fetch
-----------+--------------------+----------+--------------+---------------
 products  | idx_products_sku   |     1456 |         1456 |          1456
(1 row)
Explanation: Unique indexes enforce uniqueness AND optimize queries. Automatically created for PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints. Manual: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX for uniqueness without visible constraint. Composite unique indexes ensure combination is unique: (A,B) unique allows duplicate A or B but not duplicate (A,B) pairs. NULL handling: multiple NULLs allowed - SQL standard treats each NULL as distinct. Unique indexes faster than regular index + application-level uniqueness check - database enforces atomically. Use unique indexes for: natural keys, business identifiers (email, SKU, username), preventing duplicates efficiently.

11.7 Indexes on Expressions

Expression indexes: Index on computed values rather than column values. Also called functional indexes. Useful for queries filtering on transformed data (LOWER(email), date_part(), expression results). Query must use exact same expression. Maintenance cost: expression evaluated for every insert/update.

🎯 Example 1: Case-Insensitive Search

-- Without expression index: case-sensitive
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE email = 'JOHN@EXAMPLE.COM';  -- No match

-- Create expression index on LOWER(email)
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_email_lower ON customers(LOWER(email));

-- Now case-insensitive searches use index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT customer_id, first_name, email
FROM customers
WHERE LOWER(email) = 'john@example.com';
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_customers_email_lower on customers
  Index Cond: (lower(email) = 'john@example.com')
Planning Time: 0.089 ms
Execution Time: 0.056 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Date Part Extraction

-- Queries by month/year frequently
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_year_month 
ON orders(EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date), EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date));

-- Query uses expression index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(total_amount)
FROM orders
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = 2024
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) = 1;
Execution Plan:
Aggregate
  -> Index Scan using idx_orders_year_month on orders
       Index Cond: ((EXTRACT(year FROM order_date) = 2024) 
                AND (EXTRACT(month FROM order_date) = 1))
Planning Time: 0.123 ms
Execution Time: 0.234 ms
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Computed Column Index

-- Index on calculated expression
CREATE INDEX idx_order_items_total 
ON order_items((quantity * price_each));

-- Query on computed value uses index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, product_id, quantity, price_each
FROM order_items
WHERE (quantity * price_each) > 100;
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_order_items_total on order_items
  Index Cond: ((quantity * price_each) > 100)
Planning Time: 0.098 ms
Execution Time: 0.187 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 4: JSON Path Expression

-- Table with JSONB data
CREATE TABLE user_profiles (
    user_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    profile jsonb
);

INSERT INTO user_profiles (profile) VALUES
    ('{"name":"Alice","city":"New York","age":30}'),
    ('{"name":"Bob","city":"Los Angeles","age":25}'),
    ('{"name":"Charlie","city":"Chicago","age":35}');

-- Index on JSON path expression
CREATE INDEX idx_profiles_city 
ON user_profiles((profile->>'city'));

-- Query uses expression index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT profile->>'name' AS name, profile->>'age' AS age
FROM user_profiles
WHERE profile->>'city' = 'New York';
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_profiles_city on user_profiles
  Index Cond: ((profile ->> 'city') = 'New York')
Planning Time: 0.145 ms
Execution Time: 0.089 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Expression Index Statistics

-- Create multiple expression indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name_lower ON products(LOWER(product_name));
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price_range ON products(
    CASE 
        WHEN price < 50 THEN 'budget'
        WHEN price < 100 THEN 'mid'
        ELSE 'premium'
    END
);

-- Check expression index usage
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan,
    idx_tup_read,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE '%lower' OR indexname LIKE '%range'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;
Expression Index Statistics:
 schemaname | tablename |        indexname         | idx_scan | idx_tup_read | size
------------+-----------+--------------------------+----------+--------------+------
 public     | products  | idx_products_name_lower  |      234 |          467 | 16kB
 public     | products  | idx_products_price_range |       89 |          178 | 8kB
(2 rows)
Explanation: Expression indexes store computed values, not raw column data. Query must use EXACT same expression - WHERE LOWER(email) can use LOWER(email) index, but WHERE UPPER(email) cannot. Common uses: case-insensitive search (LOWER/UPPER), date parts (EXTRACT, date_trunc), calculations, JSONB paths. Trade-off: faster queries but slower inserts/updates (expression evaluated each time). Immutable functions only - result must be deterministic. Cost justified when: expression used frequently in WHERE/JOIN, computation expensive, query performance critical. Alternative for JSONB: GIN indexes support many operations without expressions.

11.8 Partial Indexes
