Partial indexes: Index only subset of rows based on WHERE condition. Smaller, faster, cheaper to maintain than full index. Perfect for queries consistently filtering on specific condition. Common uses: active records only, recent data, non-NULL values, specific status values. Query WHERE clause must be subset of index WHERE clause.

🎯 Example 1: Index Active Records Only

-- Most queries filter for active customers
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_active ON customers(email)
WHERE active = true;

-- Index used for active customers
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE active = true AND email = 'john@example.com';

-- Index NOT used for inactive customers  
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE active = false AND email = 'jane@example.com';
Active Query Uses Partial Index:
Index Scan using idx_customers_active on customers
  Index Cond: (email = 'john@example.com')
  Filter: active = true
Execution Time: 0.067 ms

-- Inactive query doesn't use partial index:
Seq Scan on customers
  Filter: (active = false) AND (email = 'jane@example.com')
Execution Time: 1.234 ms

🎯 Example 2: Index Pending Orders Only

-- Most operations on pending orders (90% of queries)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending ON orders(customer_id, order_date)
WHERE status = 'pending';

-- Check index size comparison
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_all ON orders(customer_id, order_date);  -- Full index

SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'orders'
ORDER BY indexname;
Size Comparison:
     indexname      |  size
--------------------+--------
 idx_orders_all     | 184 kB  -- Full index
 idx_orders_pending |  24 kB  -- 87% smaller!
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Index Recent Data Only

-- Index only last 30 days (most queries use recent data)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_recent ON orders(order_date, customer_id)
WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days';

-- Query on recent data uses partial index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
  AND customer_id = 1;
Execution Plan:
Index Scan using idx_orders_recent on orders
  Index Cond: ((order_date >= (CURRENT_DATE - '30 days')) AND (customer_id = 1))
Planning Time: 0.089 ms
Execution Time: 0.123 ms
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Index Non-NULL Values Only

-- Many rows have NULL, queries always filter WHERE column IS NOT NULL
CREATE INDEX idx_products_discount ON products(discount_percent)
WHERE discount_percent IS NOT NULL;

-- Query uses partial index
SELECT product_name, price, discount_percent
FROM products
WHERE discount_percent IS NOT NULL
  AND discount_percent > 20;
Result:
   product_name   | price | discount_percent
------------------+-------+------------------
 Laptop Pro       | 999   |               25
 Wireless Mouse   | 29    |               30
 USB-C Cable      | 19    |               35
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Multiple Partial Indexes by Status

-- Different indexes for different statuses
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending ON orders(customer_id) WHERE status = 'pending';
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_shipped ON orders(customer_id) WHERE status = 'shipped';
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_delivered ON orders(customer_id) WHERE status = 'delivered';

-- Query automatically picks correct partial index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, order_date FROM orders 
WHERE customer_id = 1 AND status = 'shipped';

-- Check partial index usage
SELECT 
    indexname,
    idx_scan,
    idx_tup_read,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE 'idx_orders_%'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;
Partial Index Statistics:
      indexname       | idx_scan | idx_tup_read | size
----------------------+----------+--------------+------
 idx_orders_pending   |     1234 |         2456 | 24kB
 idx_orders_shipped   |      856 |         1678 | 32kB
 idx_orders_delivered |      445 |          889 | 56kB
(3 rows)
Explanation: Partial indexes index only rows matching WHERE condition. Benefits: smaller size (faster scans, less disk space), faster updates (fewer index entries to maintain), focused on hot data. Query must include index WHERE condition (or stricter). Example: index WHERE active = true helps WHERE active = true AND email = 'x', but not WHERE email = 'x' alone. Common patterns: status flags (active/pending), date ranges (recent data), non-NULL values, high-value customers. Trade-off: very effective when queries consistently filter same condition; useless for queries without that filter. Can replace full index if 90%+ queries use same filter.

11.9 Index-Only Scans and Covering Indexes

Index-only scans: Query retrieves all needed columns directly from index without accessing table heap. Dramatically faster - no disk I/O to table. Requires: all query columns in index, visibility map up to date (VACUUM), index includes all SELECT/WHERE columns. Create covering indexes by including extra columns with INCLUDE clause.

🎯 Example 1: Index-Only Scan Basics

-- Regular index: must access heap
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_email ON customers(email);

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT email FROM customers WHERE email = 'john@example.com';

-- Include first_name in index for covering
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_email_covering ON customers(email) INCLUDE (first_name);

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT email, first_name FROM customers WHERE email = 'john@example.com';
Comparison:
-- Regular index (heap access):
Index Scan using idx_customers_email on customers
  Index Cond: (email = 'john@example.com')
Execution Time: 0.145 ms

-- Covering index (index-only scan):
Index Only Scan using idx_customers_email_covering on customers
  Index Cond: (email = 'john@example.com')
  Heap Fetches: 0  -- No heap access!
Execution Time: 0.067 ms  -- 2x faster!

🎯 Example 2: INCLUDE Clause for Covering Index

-- Create covering index with INCLUDE
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category_covering 
ON products(category_id) INCLUDE (product_name, price);

-- Query retrieves included columns - index-only scan
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1;
Index-Only Scan:
Index Only Scan using idx_products_category_covering on products
  Index Cond: (category_id = 1)
  Heap Fetches: 0
  Buffers: shared hit=12  -- Only index blocks read!
Planning Time: 0.089 ms
Execution Time: 0.234 ms
(6 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Visibility Map and VACUUM

-- Create covering index
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_covering 
ON orders(customer_id) INCLUDE (order_date, total_amount);

-- Query before VACUUM - may need heap fetches
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 1;

-- Run VACUUM to update visibility map
VACUUM orders;

-- Query after VACUUM - pure index-only scan
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 1;
Before VACUUM:
Index Only Scan using idx_orders_customer_covering on orders
  Index Cond: (customer_id = 1)
  Heap Fetches: 23  -- Must check heap for visibility!
  Buffers: shared hit=45
Execution Time: 0.456 ms

After VACUUM:
Index Only Scan using idx_orders_customer_covering on orders
  Index Cond: (customer_id = 1)
  Heap Fetches: 0  -- Pure index-only!
  Buffers: shared hit=12
Execution Time: 0.189 ms  -- 2.4x faster!

🎯 Example 4: Multicolumn Covering Index

-- Common query pattern: filter by status, retrieve multiple columns
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_covering 
ON orders(status) 
INCLUDE (order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount);

-- Index-only scan retrieves all columns from index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 10;
Execution Plan:
Limit  (cost=0.29..1.56 rows=10)
  -> Index Only Scan using idx_orders_status_covering on orders
       Index Cond: (status = 'pending')
       Heap Fetches: 0
Planning Time: 0.098 ms
Execution Time: 0.123 ms
(6 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Index-Only Scan Statistics

-- Check which indexes enable index-only scans
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan AS total_scans,
    idx_tup_read AS tuples_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS heap_fetches,
    CASE 
        WHEN idx_tup_fetch = 0 THEN '100%'
        ELSE ROUND(100.0 * (idx_tup_read - idx_tup_fetch) / NULLIF(idx_tup_read, 0), 2)::text || '%'
    END AS index_only_ratio,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan > 0
ORDER BY (idx_tup_read - idx_tup_fetch) DESC
LIMIT 10;
Index-Only Scan Statistics:
 schemaname | tablename |         indexname              | total_scans | tuples_read | heap_fetches | index_only_ratio | size
------------+-----------+--------------------------------+-------------+-------------+--------------+------------------+------
 public     | products  | idx_products_category_covering |        1234 |        2456 |            0 | 100%             | 48kB
 public     | orders    | idx_orders_status_covering     |         856 |        1678 |            0 | 100%             | 64kB
 public     | customers | idx_customers_email_covering   |         445 |         889 |          112 | 87.40%           | 32kB
(3 rows)
Explanation: Index-only scans avoid heap access by retrieving all data from index. Requirements: (1) all SELECT columns in index, (2) visibility map current (recent VACUUM), (3) query columns covered. INCLUDE clause adds non-key columns to index leaf nodes - useful for covering but not for ordering/searching. Benefits: fewer disk I/O, faster queries (2-10x), reduced buffer cache pressure. Costs: larger indexes, slightly slower updates. Use for: frequently queried columns, read-heavy workloads, queries selecting few columns. Check Heap Fetches in EXPLAIN - should be 0 for pure index-only scan. Run VACUUM regularly to maintain visibility map accuracy.

11.10 Operator Classes and Operator Families

Operator classes: Define how index type handles specific data type. Determine which operators use the index (=, <, >, etc.). Each index type has default operator class per data type. Can specify non-default for specialized behavior: case-insensitive text (text_pattern_ops), varchar patterns (varchar_pattern_ops), custom collations. Important for LIKE queries and non-C locale ordering.

🎯 Example 1: Default vs Pattern Operator Class

-- Default text operator class
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_name_default ON customers(last_name);

-- Pattern operator class for LIKE queries
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_name_pattern ON customers(last_name text_pattern_ops);

-- LIKE query without pattern operator class - may not use index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE last_name LIKE 'Smi%';

-- With pattern operator class - uses index efficiently
DROP INDEX idx_customers_name_default;
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_name_pattern ON customers(last_name text_pattern_ops);

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE last_name LIKE 'Smi%';
Comparison:
-- Default operator class (locale-dependent):
Seq Scan on customers  -- May not use index!
  Filter: (last_name ~~ 'Smi%')

-- Pattern operator class (C locale):
Index Scan using idx_customers_name_pattern on customers
  Index Cond: ((last_name ~>=~ 'Smi') AND (last_name ~<~ 'Smj'))
  Filter: (last_name ~~ 'Smi%')
Execution Time: 0.089 ms  -- Much faster!

🎯 Example 2: List Available Operator Classes

-- Show operator classes for B-tree indexes
SELECT 
    opc.opcname AS operator_class,
    opc.opcintype::regtype AS input_type,
    am.amname AS index_method
FROM pg_opclass opc
JOIN pg_am am ON opc.opcmethod = am.oid
WHERE am.amname = 'btree'
  AND opc.opcname LIKE '%text%'
ORDER BY operator_class;
Text Operator Classes:
    operator_class    | input_type | index_method
----------------------+------------+--------------
 text_ops             | text       | btree
 text_pattern_ops     | text       | btree
 varchar_ops          | varchar    | btree
 varchar_pattern_ops  | varchar    | btree
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Operator Class for Different Data Types

-- Numeric operator classes
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price_default ON products(price);  -- Uses numeric_ops

-- Array operator class
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_tags_array ON articles(tags);  -- Uses array_ops

-- JSONB operator classes
CREATE INDEX idx_products_data_jsonb ON products_json(product_data);  -- Uses jsonb_ops
CREATE INDEX idx_products_data_ops ON products_json(product_data jsonb_path_ops);  -- Alternative

-- View index details including operator class
SELECT 
    indexname,
    tablename,
    indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
  AND indexname LIKE '%tags%' OR indexname LIKE '%jsonb%';
Operator Class Usage:
         indexname          | tablename |                    indexdef
----------------------------+-----------+------------------------------------------------
 idx_articles_tags_array    | articles  | CREATE INDEX ... USING btree (tags)
 idx_products_data_jsonb    | products  | CREATE INDEX ... USING gin (product_data)
 idx_products_data_ops      | products  | CREATE INDEX ... USING gin (product_data jsonb_path_ops)
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Case-Insensitive Unique Index

-- Case-insensitive unique constraint using expression + operator class
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_username_lower 
ON users(LOWER(username) text_pattern_ops);

-- Prevents duplicates with different cases
INSERT INTO users (username) VALUES ('JohnDoe');   -- OK
INSERT INTO users (username) VALUES ('johndoe');   -- Fails!
INSERT INTO users (username) VALUES ('JOHNDOE');   -- Fails!
Result:
INSERT 0 1

ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "idx_users_username_lower"
DETAIL:  Key (lower(username))=(johndoe) already exists.

ERROR:  duplicate key value violates unique constraint "idx_users_username_lower"
DETAIL:  Key (lower(username))=(johndoe) already exists.

🎯 Example 5: Operator Class Performance Comparison

-- Create test table
CREATE TABLE test_strings (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    value text
);

INSERT INTO test_strings (value)
SELECT md5(random()::text)
FROM generate_series(1, 10000);

-- Compare operator classes
CREATE INDEX idx_test_default ON test_strings(value);
CREATE INDEX idx_test_pattern ON test_strings(value text_pattern_ops);

-- LIKE query performance
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT * FROM test_strings WHERE value LIKE 'abc%';

-- Check index sizes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'test_strings';
Results:
      indexname      |  size
---------------------+--------
 idx_test_default    | 248 kB
 idx_test_pattern    | 248 kB  -- Same size, different behavior

-- Pattern operator class enables efficient LIKE:
Index Scan using idx_test_pattern on test_strings
  Index Cond: ((value ~>=~ 'abc') AND (value ~<~ 'abd'))
  Filter: (value ~~ 'abc%')
  Rows Removed by Filter: 0
Execution Time: 0.123 ms
Explanation: Operator classes define how indexes handle data types and which operators they support. Default operator class chosen by PostgreSQL based on data type. Specify non-default for specialized behavior: text_pattern_ops for LIKE queries (C locale ordering), jsonb_path_ops for smaller GIN indexes (only @> operator), custom collations for locale-specific sorting. Pattern operator classes critical for LIKE with non-C locale database. Check pg_opclass catalog for available classes. Most indexes use default automatically. Specify explicitly only when: using LIKE frequently, need specific collation, want alternative JSONB behavior (jsonb_ops vs jsonb_path_ops).

11.11 Indexes and Collations

Indexes and collations: Collation determines string comparison and sorting order. Index must match query collation to be usable. Different collations require different indexes. Important for international applications with multiple locales. Default collation set at database/column level. Can specify collation in index definition.

🎯 Example 1: Default Collation Index

-- Check database default collation
SHOW lc_collate;

-- Create index with default collation
CREATE INDEX idx_customers_name ON customers(last_name);

-- Query uses index (matches default collation)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM customers 
WHERE last_name = 'Smith'
ORDER BY last_name;
Default Collation:
 lc_collate
----------------
 en_US.UTF-8
(1 row)

Index Scan using idx_customers_name on customers
  Index Cond: (last_name = 'Smith')
Execution Time: 0.089 ms

🎯 Example 2: Explicit Collation in Index

-- Create indexes with different collations
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name_en ON products(product_name COLLATE "en_US");
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name_fr ON products(product_name COLLATE "fr_FR");
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name_de ON products(product_name COLLATE "de_DE");

-- Query with specific collation uses matching index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name
FROM products
ORDER BY product_name COLLATE "fr_FR"
LIMIT 10;
Uses Matching Collation Index:
Limit  (cost=0.29..1.45 rows=10)
  -> Index Scan using idx_products_name_fr on products
       -- Uses French collation index
Planning Time: 0.098 ms
Execution Time: 0.067 ms
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Case-Insensitive Collation

-- Create case-insensitive collation (PostgreSQL 12+)
CREATE COLLATION case_insensitive (
    provider = icu,
    locale = 'und-u-ks-level2',
    deterministic = false
);

-- Index with case-insensitive collation
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_ci ON users(email COLLATE case_insensitive);

-- Case-insensitive search uses index
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM users 
WHERE email COLLATE case_insensitive = 'JOHN@EXAMPLE.COM';
Case-Insensitive Search:
Index Scan using idx_users_email_ci on users
  Index Cond: ((email)::text = 'JOHN@EXAMPLE.COM')
  -- Matches 'john@example.com', 'JOHN@EXAMPLE.COM', etc.
Execution Time: 0.078 ms
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: List Available Collations

-- Show all available collations
SELECT 
    collname AS collation,
    collprovider AS provider,
    collcollate AS lc_collate,
    collctype AS lc_ctype
FROM pg_collation
WHERE collname LIKE '%en%' OR collname LIKE '%fr%'
ORDER BY collname
LIMIT 10;
Available Collations:
   collation   | provider | lc_collate  |   lc_ctype
---------------+----------+-------------+---------------
 en_AG         | c        | en_AG       | en_AG
 en_AU         | c        | en_AU.utf8  | en_AU.utf8
 en_GB         | c        | en_GB.utf8  | en_GB.utf8
 en_US         | c        | en_US.utf8  | en_US.utf8
 fr_BE         | c        | fr_BE.utf8  | fr_BE.utf8
 fr_CA         | c        | fr_CA.utf8  | fr_CA.utf8
 fr_FR         | c        | fr_FR.utf8  | fr_FR.utf8
(7 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Collation Mismatch Problem

-- Index with en_US collation
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name_en ON products(product_name COLLATE "en_US");

-- Query with different collation - CANNOT use index!
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products
ORDER BY product_name COLLATE "fr_FR"
LIMIT 10;

-- Must create matching index or use default
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name_fr ON products(product_name COLLATE "fr_FR");

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products
ORDER BY product_name COLLATE "fr_FR"
LIMIT 10;
Collation Mismatch:
-- Without matching collation:
Limit  (cost=89.83..89.85 rows=10)
  -> Sort  (cost=89.83..92.33 rows=1000)  -- Must sort!
       Sort Key: (product_name COLLATE "fr_FR")
       -> Seq Scan on products
Execution Time: 2.456 ms

-- With matching collation:
Limit  (cost=0.29..1.12 rows=10)
  -> Index Scan using idx_products_name_fr on products
Execution Time: 0.067 ms  -- 36x faster!
Explanation: Collation determines string comparison rules and sort order. Index collation must match query collation - mismatch prevents index use. Default collation from database settings unless explicitly specified. Different collations produce different orderings: en_US vs fr_FR vs de_DE sort differently. Use cases: (1) international apps - index per locale, (2) case-insensitive search - ICU collation with level2, (3) legacy systems - C collation for ASCII-only speed. Collation affects: comparisons (=, <, >), sorting (ORDER BY), LIKE patterns, unique constraints. Check with SHOW lc_collate. Pattern operator classes (text_pattern_ops) use C collation internally regardless of database default.

11.12 Examining Index Usage

Monitoring indexes: Critical for database performance optimization. Check: which indexes are used/unused, scan statistics, index bloat, effectiveness. Use pg_stat_user_indexes, pg_statio_user_indexes, EXPLAIN ANALYZE. Identify unused indexes to drop, missing indexes to add, bloated indexes to rebuild.

🎯 Example 1: Index Usage Statistics

-- Comprehensive index usage report
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan AS index_scans,
    idx_tup_read AS tuples_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS tuples_fetched,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename)) AS table_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;
Index Usage Report:
 schemaname | tablename |           indexname            | index_scans | tuples_read | tuples_fetched | index_size | table_size
------------+-----------+--------------------------------+-------------+-------------+----------------+------------+------------
 public     | customers | idx_customers_email            |       12456 |       12456 |          12456 | 32 kB      | 896 kB
 public     | orders    | idx_orders_customer            |        8934 |       45678 |          42345 | 184 kB     | 2048 kB
 public     | products  | idx_products_category          |        5678 |       23456 |          21234 | 64 kB      | 1024 kB
 public     | order_items | idx_order_items_order        |        3456 |       89012 |          87654 | 248 kB     | 4096 kB
 public     | suppliers | idx_suppliers_name             |           0 |           0 |              0 | 16 kB      | 128 kB
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Find Unused Indexes

-- Identify indexes that are never used
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    idx_scan AS scans,
    pg_stat_get_tuples_inserted(relid) + 
    pg_stat_get_tuples_updated(relid) + 
    pg_stat_get_tuples_deleted(relid) AS table_modifications
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes sui
JOIN pg_stat_user_tables sut ON sui.relid = sut.relid
WHERE idx_scan = 0
  AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%pkey'  -- Exclude primary keys
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;
Unused Indexes (Candidates for Removal):
 schemaname | tablename  |        indexname         | index_size | scans | table_modifications
------------+------------+--------------------------+------------+-------+---------------------
 public     | suppliers  | idx_suppliers_name       | 16 kB      |     0 |                 234
 public     | categories | idx_categories_parent    | 8 kB       |     0 |                  45
 public     | logs       | idx_logs_level           | 248 kB     |     0 |              145678
(3 rows)

-- Consider dropping these indexes!

🎯 Example 3: Index Cache Hit Ratio

-- Check how often indexes are read from cache vs disk
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_blks_read AS disk_reads,
    idx_blks_hit AS cache_hits,
    CASE 
        WHEN (idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read) = 0 THEN 0
        ELSE ROUND(100.0 * idx_blks_hit / (idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read), 2)
    END AS cache_hit_ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_indexes
WHERE idx_blks_read + idx_blks_hit > 0
ORDER BY cache_hit_ratio ASC;
Index Cache Performance:
 schemaname | tablename |         indexname          | disk_reads | cache_hits | cache_hit_ratio
------------+-----------+----------------------------+------------+------------+-----------------
 public     | logs      | idx_logs_timestamp         |       2345 |       1234 |           34.48  -- Low! Consider more RAM
 public     | products  | idx_products_category      |        123 |       8765 |           98.62  -- Excellent!
 public     | customers | idx_customers_email        |         45 |      12456 |           99.64  -- Excellent!
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Index Bloat Detection

-- Estimate index bloat
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    ROUND(100 * (pg_relation_size(indexrelid) - 
          pg_relation_size(indexrelid, 'main'))::numeric / 
          NULLIF(pg_relation_size(indexrelid), 0), 2) AS bloat_ratio,
    CASE 
        WHEN pg_relation_size(indexrelid) > 100000000 THEN 'Consider REINDEX'
        ELSE 'OK'
    END AS recommendation
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE pg_relation_size(indexrelid) > 10000000  -- Indexes > 10MB
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;
Index Bloat Report:
 schemaname | tablename  |          indexname          | index_size | bloat_ratio |  recommendation
------------+------------+-----------------------------+------------+-------------+------------------
 public     | logs       | idx_logs_timestamp          | 456 MB     |       23.45 | Consider REINDEX
 public     | orders     | idx_orders_customer         | 234 MB     |       12.34 | Consider REINDEX
 public     | products   | idx_products_name           | 89 MB      |        5.67 | OK
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Query Plan Analysis with Indexes

-- Analyze query to see index usage
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, VERBOSE)
SELECT 
    c.first_name,
    c.last_name,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE c.active = true
  AND o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Get recommendations for missing indexes
SELECT 
    'CREATE INDEX idx_' || tablename || '_' || 
    replace(replace(indexdef, ' ', '_'), '.', '_') AS recommendation
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE schemaname = 'public';
Query Plan with Index Usage:
Limit  (cost=245.67..245.69 rows=10)
  -> Sort  (cost=245.67..248.17 rows=1000)
       Sort Key: (sum(o.total_amount)) DESC
       -> HashAggregate  (cost=189.45..234.56 rows=1000)
             -> Hash Left Join  (cost=45.67..156.78 rows=1234)
                   Hash Cond: (c.customer_id = o.customer_id)
                   -> Bitmap Heap Scan on customers c  (cost=12.34..34.56 rows=500)
                         Recheck Cond: (active = true)
                         -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_customers_active
                               Index Cond: (active = true)
                   -> Hash  (cost=28.90..28.90 rows=345)
                         -> Index Scan using idx_orders_date on orders o
                               Index Cond: (order_date >= (CURRENT_DATE - '1 year'))
Planning Time: 2.345 ms
Execution Time: 5.678 ms
Buffers: shared hit=234

Missing index detected: Consider creating index on orders(customer_id, order_date)
Explanation: Regular index monitoring essential for optimization. Key metrics: idx_scan (usage count), idx_tup_read/fetch (rows returned), cache hit ratio (memory efficiency), size (disk usage). Unused indexes (idx_scan=0) waste space and slow writes - consider dropping. Low cache hit ratio indicates insufficient memory or large indexes. Index bloat from updates - rebuild with REINDEX CONCURRENTLY. Use EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to verify index usage in queries. Monitor pg_stat_user_indexes regularly, reset statistics after major changes with pg_stat_reset(). Tools: pg_stat_statements for query-level analysis, auto_explain for production monitoring. Balance: more indexes improve reads but slow writes.

Chapter 12: Full Text Search

Full Text Search: PostgreSQL provides powerful full-text search capabilities for searching natural-language documents. Unlike simple pattern matching with LIKE, full-text search understands language-specific rules, word stemming, and ranking. It can efficiently search large text collections, handle multiple languages, and rank results by relevance. Full-text search uses specialized data types (tsvector, tsquery) and operators (@@) for performance.

12.1 Introduction

Full-text search basics: Involves preprocessing documents into a searchable format (tsvector), creating queries (tsquery), and matching them. Documents are tokenized, normalized, and converted to lexemes. Queries support boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) and phrase search. The @@ operator performs the match. Configuration determines language-specific rules for parsing and stemming.

12.1.1 What Is a Document?

🎯 Example 1: Simple Document

-- A document is any textual data you want to search
-- Can be from single column or multiple columns combined

SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name || ' ' || description AS document
FROM products
LIMIT 3;
Documents:
 product_id |                    document
------------+--------------------------------------------------
          1 | Laptop Pro High-performance laptop with 16GB RAM
          2 | Wireless Mouse Ergonomic wireless mouse with USB
          3 | USB-C Cable Fast charging cable for all devices
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Multi-Column Document

-- Combine multiple columns into single searchable document
CREATE TABLE articles (
    article_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text,
    author text,
    content text,
    published_date date
);

INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content, published_date) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL Tutorial', 'John Smith', 'Learn PostgreSQL database management and SQL queries', '2024-01-15'),
    ('Python Programming', 'Jane Doe', 'Introduction to Python programming language basics', '2024-02-20'),
    ('Web Development Guide', 'Bob Wilson', 'Complete guide to modern web development with HTML CSS JavaScript', '2024-03-10');

-- Document combines title, author, and content
SELECT 
    title,
    title || ' ' || author || ' ' || content AS full_document
FROM articles
LIMIT 1;
Combined Document:
       title        |                           full_document
--------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| PostgreSQL Tutorial John Smith Learn PostgreSQL database management and SQL queries
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Document Preprocessing

-- Convert document to tsvector (searchable format)
SELECT to_tsvector('english', 
    'PostgreSQL is a powerful, open source object-relational database system.'
) AS document_vector;
Result (tsvector):
                           document_vector
---------------------------------------------------------------------
 'databas':7 'object':6 'object-rel':5 'open':3 'postgresql':1 
 'power':2 'relat':6 'sourc':4 'system':8
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Document with Position Information

-- tsvector stores lexemes with position information
SELECT to_tsvector('english', 
    'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog'
) AS vector_with_positions;
Lexemes with Positions:
           vector_with_positions
--------------------------------------------
 'brown':3 'dog':9 'fox':4 'jump':5 'lazi':8 'quick':2
(1 row)

-- Numbers after colons are word positions
-- Stop words like 'the', 'over' are removed

🎯 Example 5: Document Structure Comparison

-- Raw text vs tsvector
SELECT 
    'PostgreSQL Database' AS raw_text,
    to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL Database') AS as_tsvector,
    length('PostgreSQL Database') AS text_bytes,
    length(to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL Database')::text) AS vector_bytes;
Comparison:
      raw_text       |    as_tsvector     | text_bytes | vector_bytes
---------------------+--------------------+------------+--------------
 PostgreSQL Database | 'databas':2 'postgresql':1 |         19 |           30
(1 row)

-- tsvector is normalized and indexed form
Explanation: A document in full-text search is any textual data you want to search - can be single column or combination of multiple columns. Documents are converted to tsvector format: tokenized into words, normalized (lowercased), stemmed (running→run), and stop words removed (the, a, an). Each lexeme stores position information for phrase matching. tsvector is the searchable representation optimized for text search operations. Document preprocessing happens via to_tsvector() function which requires language configuration.

12.1.2 Basic Text Matching

🎯 Example 1: Simple Text Match with @@ Operator

-- Create search query with to_tsquery
-- Match using @@ operator
SELECT 
    title,
    content
FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql');
Matching Documents:
       title        |                     content
--------------------+---------------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| Learn PostgreSQL database management and SQL queries
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Boolean Operators in Queries

-- AND operator: &
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'python & programming');

-- OR operator: |
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql | python');

-- NOT operator: !
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'programming & !python');
Results:
-- AND (both terms required):
       title
--------------------
 Python Programming

-- OR (either term):
       title
--------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial
 Python Programming

-- NOT (first but not second):
       title
-------------------
 Web Development Guide

🎯 Example 3: Phrase Search

-- Search for phrases with <-> (followed by)
INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content) VALUES
    ('Database Tips', 'Alice Brown', 'PostgreSQL database performance tips and tricks');

-- Find "database performance" as phrase
SELECT title, content
FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'database <-> performance');
Phrase Match:
     title      |                    content
----------------+-----------------------------------------------
 Database Tips  | PostgreSQL database performance tips and tricks
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Case Insensitivity and Stemming

-- Full-text search is case-insensitive and handles word forms
INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content) VALUES
    ('Running Guide', 'Tom Runner', 'Tips for runners who enjoy running daily');

-- All these queries match the same document
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'run');

SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'running');

SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'RUNNER');
All Match (Stemming):
     title
---------------
 Running Guide
 Running Guide
 Running Guide
(3 results - same document)

-- 'run', 'running', 'runner' all stem to 'run'

🎯 Example 5: LIKE vs Full-Text Search Comparison

-- LIKE pattern matching (slow, no stemming)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE content LIKE '%database%';

-- Full-text search (faster with index, supports stemming)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'database');
Performance Comparison:
-- LIKE:
Seq Scan on articles  (cost=0.00..25.50 rows=1)
  Filter: (content ~~ '%database%')
Execution Time: 2.345 ms

-- Full-text search:
Seq Scan on articles  (cost=0.00..28.75 rows=1)
  Filter: (to_tsvector('english', content) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'database'))
Execution Time: 1.234 ms

-- With GIN index, full-text search is 100x+ faster!
Explanation: Basic text matching uses @@ operator to match tsvector against tsquery. to_tsquery() creates search queries with boolean operators: & (AND), | (OR), ! (NOT), <-> (followed by). Full-text search is case-insensitive and applies stemming - "running", "runs", "ran" all match query "run". Phrase search with <-> requires words in specific order. Much more powerful than LIKE: understands language, finds word variants, ranks by relevance. Performance: without index similar to LIKE; with GIN index dramatically faster for large datasets.

12.1.3 Configurations

🎯 Example 1: Available Text Search Configurations

-- List all available text search configurations
SELECT cfgname, cfgowner::regrole, cfgparser::regparser
FROM pg_ts_config
ORDER BY cfgname;
Text Search Configurations:
    cfgname    | cfgowner | cfgparser
---------------+----------+-----------
 arabic        | postgres | default
 danish        | postgres | default
 dutch         | postgres | default
 english       | postgres | default
 finnish       | postgres | default
 french        | postgres | default
 german        | postgres | default
 greek         | postgres | default
 italian       | postgres | default
 portuguese    | postgres | default
 russian       | postgres | default
 simple        | postgres | simple
 spanish       | postgres | default
 swedish       | postgres | default
 turkish       | postgres | default
(15 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Using Different Language Configurations

-- English configuration
SELECT to_tsvector('english', 'The running runners ran quickly') AS english_vector;

-- French configuration
SELECT to_tsvector('french', 'Les coureurs courent rapidement') AS french_vector;

-- Simple configuration (no stemming, basic stop words)
SELECT to_tsvector('simple', 'The running runners ran quickly') AS simple_vector;
Different Configurations:
      english_vector
------------------------
 'quick':4 'ran':3 'run':1,2

      french_vector
-----------------------
 'cour':2 'coureur':1 'rapid':3

      simple_vector
---------------------------------
 'quickly':5 'ran':4 'runners':3 'running':2
-- Simple keeps all words, no stemming

🎯 Example 3: Default Configuration

-- Check default text search configuration
SHOW default_text_search_config;

-- Using default configuration (implicit)
SELECT to_tsvector('PostgreSQL is powerful') AS with_default;

-- Same as explicitly using 'english' if that's the default
SELECT to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL is powerful') AS explicit_english;
Default Configuration:
 default_text_search_config
----------------------------
 pg_catalog.english
(1 row)

       with_default        |      explicit_english
---------------------------+---------------------------
 'postgresql':1 'power':3  | 'postgresql':1 'power':3
(Both produce same result)

🎯 Example 4: Configuration Affects Stemming

-- Word "organization" in different configurations
SELECT 
    'english' AS config,
    to_tsvector('english', 'organizations') AS vector
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'simple',
    to_tsvector('simple', 'organizations');

-- Word "running" in different configurations  
SELECT 
    'english' AS config,
    to_tsvector('english', 'running runners') AS vector
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'french',
    to_tsvector('french', 'running runners');
Configuration Impact:
 config  |      vector
---------+------------------
 english | 'organ':1
 simple  | 'organizations':1

 config  |      vector
---------+------------------
 english | 'run':1,2
 french  | 'runner':2 'running':1
-- English stems 'running'→'run', French doesn't recognize English words

🎯 Example 5: Custom Configuration Setup

-- Create custom configuration based on english
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_config (COPY = english);

-- View configuration details
SELECT 
    ts_config_name,
    ts_config_owner::regrole
FROM pg_ts_config_map
WHERE ts_config_name = 'my_config'
LIMIT 5;

-- Use custom configuration
SELECT to_tsvector('my_config', 'Customized text search example');
Custom Configuration:
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION

 ts_config_name | ts_config_owner
----------------+-----------------
 my_config      | postgres

             to_tsvector
--------------------------------------
 'custom':1 'exampl':4 'search':3 'text':2
(1 row)
Explanation: Text search configurations determine language-specific processing rules: stop words (the, a, is), stemming algorithms (running→run), dictionary mappings. PostgreSQL includes 15+ language configurations (english, french, spanish, etc.) plus 'simple' (minimal processing). Configuration specified in to_tsvector() and to_tsquery() functions. Default configuration set by default_text_search_config parameter. Different languages have different stemming rules and stop words. Can create custom configurations with CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION. Choosing correct configuration critical for accurate search - use language matching your documents.

12.2 Tables and Indexes

Full-text search in tables: Store tsvector in table column for better performance. Can use generated column (automatic) or trigger-maintained column. Create GIN or GiST index on tsvector column for fast searching. GIN index preferred for static data (faster search), GiST for frequently updated data (faster updates). Proper indexing makes full-text search scale to millions of documents.

12.2.1 Searching a Table

🎯 Example 1: Basic Table Search Without Stored tsvector

-- Search directly on text columns (slow for large tables)
SELECT 
    article_id,
    title,
    author
FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database');
Direct Search Result:
 article_id |       title        |   author
------------+--------------------+-------------
          1 | PostgreSQL Tutorial| John Smith
          4 | Database Tips      | Alice Brown
(2 rows)

-- Problem: to_tsvector computed on every query (expensive!)

🎯 Example 2: Table with Stored tsvector Column

-- Add tsvector column to store preprocessed documents
ALTER TABLE articles ADD COLUMN tsv tsvector;

-- Update existing rows
UPDATE articles 
SET tsv = to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(content,''));

-- Check the stored vectors
SELECT 
    title,
    tsv
FROM articles
LIMIT 2;
Stored tsvector:
       title        |                              tsv
--------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| 'databas':6 'john':3 'learn':4 'manag':7 'postgresql':1,5 'queri':10 'smith':2 'sql':9 'tutorial':2
 Python Programming | 'basic':9 'doe':3 'introduct':4 'jane':2 'languag':8 'program':1,6,7 'python':1,5
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Searching Stored tsvector (Faster)

-- Search using stored tsvector column
SELECT 
    article_id,
    title,
    author,
    published_date
FROM articles
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql | python')
ORDER BY published_date DESC;
Fast Search Result:
 article_id |       title        |   author   | published_date
------------+--------------------+------------+----------------
          2 | Python Programming | Jane Doe   | 2024-02-20
          1 | PostgreSQL Tutorial| John Smith | 2024-01-15
(2 rows)

-- Much faster: tsvector already computed!

🎯 Example 4: Generated tsvector Column (Automatic)

-- Create table with generated tsvector column
CREATE TABLE blog_posts (
    post_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text NOT NULL,
    body text NOT NULL,
    author text,
    tsv tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(body,''))
    ) STORED
);

-- Insert data - tsv automatically generated
INSERT INTO blog_posts (title, body, author) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL Performance', 'Tips for optimizing PostgreSQL queries', 'Alice'),
    ('Python Basics', 'Learn Python programming from scratch', 'Bob'),
    ('Web Security', 'Best practices for securing web applications', 'Charlie');

-- Query automatically uses generated tsvector
SELECT title, author
FROM blog_posts
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql | python');
Generated Column Result:
        title         | author
----------------------+--------
 PostgreSQL Performance| Alice
 Python Basics        | Bob
(2 rows)

-- tsv maintained automatically on INSERT/UPDATE!

🎯 Example 5: Weighted Search Across Multiple Columns

-- Different columns with different weights (A=highest, D=lowest)
CREATE TABLE documents (
    doc_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text,
    abstract text,
    body text,
    tsv tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'')), 'A') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(abstract,'')), 'B') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(body,'')), 'D')
    ) STORED
);

INSERT INTO documents (title, abstract, body) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL Guide', 'Database management system', 'Detailed PostgreSQL documentation and examples'),
    ('Python Tutorial', 'Programming language basics', 'Python is used for web development and data science');

-- Search with weighted vector
SELECT 
    title,
    ts_rank(tsv, query) AS rank
FROM documents, to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
ORDER BY rank DESC;
Weighted Results (Ranked):
      title       |   rank
------------------+----------
 PostgreSQL Guide | 0.607927  -- Higher rank (found in title=weight A)
(1 row)

-- Title matches score higher than body matches
Explanation: For production use, store tsvector in dedicated column rather than computing on-the-fly. Three approaches: (1) Manual column with triggers, (2) GENERATED ALWAYS AS column (automatic, PostgreSQL 12+), (3) Trigger-maintained column. Generated columns recommended for simplicity and reliability. Use setweight() to assign importance to different fields (A > B > C > D). Title matches more important than body matches. Combining multiple columns: concatenate with || operator. Stored tsvector enables indexing for fast search. Memory trade-off: larger table size but dramatically faster queries.

12.2.2 Creating Indexes

🎯 Example 1: GIN Index on tsvector Column

-- Create GIN index for fast full-text search
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_tsv ON articles USING gin(tsv);

-- Check index usage
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT title, author
FROM articles
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database');
With GIN Index:
Bitmap Heap Scan on articles  (cost=12.25..23.52 rows=5)
  Recheck Cond: (tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database'))
  -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_articles_tsv
       Index Cond: (tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database'))
Planning Time: 0.234 ms
Execution Time: 0.156 ms

-- Index scan used! Much faster than Seq Scan

🎯 Example 2: GIN vs GiST Index Comparison

-- Create both index types for comparison
CREATE INDEX idx_blog_gin ON blog_posts USING gin(tsv);
CREATE INDEX idx_blog_gist ON blog_posts USING gist(tsv);

-- Compare index sizes
SELECT 
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size,
    idx_scan AS scans
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'blog_posts'
ORDER BY indexname;
Index Comparison:
    indexname    | size  | scans
-----------------+-------+-------
 idx_blog_gin    | 24 kB |   125
 idx_blog_gist   | 16 kB |    87
(2 rows)

-- GIN: Larger but faster for searches
-- GiST: Smaller, faster updates, good for frequently changing data

🎯 Example 3: Index on Computed tsvector

-- Create index directly on computed tsvector (no stored column)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_fulltext ON products 
USING gin(to_tsvector('english', product_name || ' ' || description));

-- Query must use exact same expression
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT product_name
FROM products
WHERE to_tsvector('english', product_name || ' ' || description) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'laptop');
Expression Index Usage:
Bitmap Heap Scan on products  (cost=8.25..15.50 rows=2)
  Recheck Cond: (to_tsvector('english', product_name || ' ' || description) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'laptop'))
  -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_products_fulltext
       Index Cond: (to_tsvector('english', product_name || ' ' || description) @@ to_tsquery('english', 'laptop'))
Execution Time: 0.189 ms

-- Works but storing tsvector is usually better

🎯 Example 4: Index Build Time and Size

-- Insert larger dataset
INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content)
SELECT 
    'Article ' || generate_series,
    'Author ' || (random() * 100)::int,
    md5(random()::text) || ' ' || md5(random()::text) || ' ' || md5(random()::text)
FROM generate_series(1, 10000);

-- Update tsvector
UPDATE articles SET tsv = to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(content,''));

-- Time index creation
\timing on
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_tsv_large ON articles USING gin(tsv);
\timing off

-- Check index size
SELECT 
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('articles')) AS table_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('idx_articles_tsv_large')) AS index_size;
Index Build Statistics:
Time: 234.567 ms

 table_size | index_size
------------+------------
 1456 kB    | 384 kB
(1 row)

-- GIN index is ~26% of table size for text data

🎯 Example 5: Index Performance Benefit

-- Query without index (after dropping)
DROP INDEX idx_articles_tsv_large;

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT count(*)
FROM articles
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'article');

-- Query with index
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_tsv_large ON articles USING gin(tsv);

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT count(*)
FROM articles
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'article');
Performance Comparison:
-- WITHOUT INDEX:
Aggregate  (cost=325.00..325.01 rows=1)
  -> Seq Scan on articles  (cost=0.00..320.00 rows=1000)
       Filter: (tsv @@ to_tsquery('english', 'article'))
Buffers: shared hit=189
Execution Time: 45.678 ms

-- WITH GIN INDEX:
Aggregate  (cost=48.25..48.26 rows=1)
  -> Bitmap Heap Scan on articles  (cost=12.00..45.00 rows=1000)
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on idx_articles_tsv_large
Buffers: shared hit=23
Execution Time: 2.345 ms

-- 19x faster with index!
Explanation: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) is the preferred index type for full-text search. Creates inverted index: each lexeme maps to documents containing it. GIN characteristics: fast searches, slower updates, larger size (~25-40% of text data). Alternative: GiST (Generalized Search Tree) - smaller, faster updates, but slower searches. Choose GIN for mostly-read data, GiST for frequently updated data. Can index stored tsvector column or computed expression. Stored column + GIN index is typical production setup. Index makes search scale: 10-100x speedup on large tables. Essential for production full-text search.

12.3 Controlling Text Search

Advanced text search control: Fine-tune search behavior through document parsing, query parsing, result ranking, and highlighting. Parse documents with ts_parse() and ts_debug(). Create complex queries with boolean operators, proximity, and negation. Rank results by relevance using ts_rank() and ts_rank_cd(). Highlight matching terms in results with ts_headline(). These tools enable sophisticated search experiences.

12.3.1 Parsing Documents

🎯 Example 1: Token Types with ts_debug()

-- Analyze how text is parsed into tokens
SELECT * FROM ts_debug('english', 
    'PostgreSQL 16.2 is the latest version! Email: info@postgresql.org'
);
Token Analysis:
   alias   |   description   |    token     |  dictionaries  |  dictionary  |  lexemes
-----------+-----------------+--------------+----------------+--------------+-----------
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | PostgreSQL   | {english_stem} | english_stem | {postgresql}
 blank     | Space symbols   |              | {}             |              |
 uint      | Unsigned integer| 16           | {simple}       | simple       | {16}
 blank     | Space symbols   |              | {}             |              |
 uint      | Unsigned integer| 2            | {simple}       | simple       | {2}
 blank     | Space symbols   |              | {}             |              |
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | is           | {english_stem} | english_stem | {}
 blank     | Space symbols   |              | {}             |              |
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | the          | {english_stem} | english_stem | {}
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | latest       | {english_stem} | english_stem | {latest}
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | version      | {english_stem} | english_stem | {version}
 blank     | Space symbols   |              | {}             |              |
 email     | Email address   | info@post... | {simple}       | simple       | {info@post...}
(13 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Raw Parsing with ts_parse()

-- Low-level token parsing without dictionary processing
SELECT tokid, token 
FROM ts_parse('default', 'The quick brown fox jumps over 123 items')
LIMIT 8;
Raw Tokens:
 tokid |  token
-------+---------
     1 | The
    12 |
     1 | quick
    12 |
     1 | brown
    12 |
     1 | fox
    12 |
(8 rows)

-- tokid 1 = asciiword, tokid 12 = blank space

🎯 Example 3: Token Type Details

-- View all token type definitions for default parser
SELECT tokid, alias, description
FROM ts_token_type('default')
ORDER BY tokid;
Token Types:
 tokid |    alias    |          description
-------+-------------+-------------------------------
     1 | asciiword   | Word, all ASCII
     2 | word        | Word, all letters
     3 | numword     | Word, letters and digits
     4 | email       | Email address
     5 | url         | URL
     6 | host        | Host
     7 | sfloat      | Scientific notation
     8 | version     | Version number
     9 | hword_numpart| Hyphenated word part, letters and digits
    10 | hword_part  | Hyphenated word part, all letters
    11 | hword_asciipart| Hyphenated word part, all ASCII
    12 | blank       | Space symbols
    13 | tag         | XML tag
    14 | protocol    | Protocol head
    15 | numhword    | Hyphenated word, letters and digits
    16 | asciihword  | Hyphenated word, all ASCII
    17 | hword       | Hyphenated word, all letters
    18 | url_path    | URL path
    19 | file        | File or path name
    20 | float       | Decimal notation
    21 | int         | Signed integer
    22 | uint        | Unsigned integer
    23 | entity      | XML entity
(23 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Parsing HTML Content

-- Parse HTML while preserving text structure
SELECT * FROM ts_debug('english',
    '
PostgreSQL
A powerful database system.
'
) WHERE token != '';
HTML Parsing:
   alias   |   description   |    token     | lexemes
-----------+-----------------+--------------+----------
 tag       | XML tag         | 
         |
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | PostgreSQL   | {postgresql}
 tag       | XML tag         | 
        |
 tag       | XML tag         | 
          |
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | A            | {}
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | powerful     | {power}
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | database     | {databas}
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | system       | {system}
 tag       | XML tag         | 
         |
(9 rows)

-- Tags recognized but not indexed as lexemes

🎯 Example 5: Custom Document Parsing

-- Parse different document types
CREATE TABLE mixed_content (
    content_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    content_type text,
    content text
);

INSERT INTO mixed_content (content_type, content) VALUES
    ('plain', 'Simple text without markup'),
    ('email', 'Contact us at support@example.com for help'),
    ('url', 'Visit https://www.postgresql.org for documentation'),
    ('code', 'SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE id = 123');

-- Analyze each content type
SELECT 
    content_type,
    array_agg(DISTINCT alias) AS token_types_found
FROM mixed_content,
     LATERAL ts_debug('english', content)
WHERE token != ''
GROUP BY content_type
ORDER BY content_type;
Token Types by Content:
 content_type |          token_types_found
--------------+--------------------------------------
 code         | {asciiword,blank,uint,file}
 email        | {asciiword,blank,email}
 plain        | {asciiword,blank}
 url          | {asciiword,blank,protocol,url,host,url_path}
(4 rows)
Explanation: Document parsing breaks text into tokens and classifies them (words, numbers, emails, URLs, tags). ts_debug() shows complete parsing process: tokenization, classification, dictionary lookup, final lexemes. ts_parse() provides raw tokenization without dictionary processing. Parser recognizes 23 token types including specialized formats (emails, URLs, version numbers, HTML tags). Parser extracts words from complex formats while ignoring markup. Understanding parsing helps debug why searches match/don't match. Use ts_debug() to troubleshoot unexpected search behavior. Parser behavior configured per language configuration.

12.3.2 Parsing Queries

🎯 Example 1: Query Syntax and Operators

-- Different query operators
SELECT 
    query_text,
    to_tsquery('english', query_text) AS parsed_query
FROM (VALUES
    ('postgresql'),                    -- Single term
    ('postgresql & database'),         -- AND operator
    ('postgresql | mysql'),            -- OR operator
    ('!postgresql'),                   -- NOT operator
    ('postgresql <-> database'),       -- Followed by (adjacent)
    ('postgresql <2> database')        -- Followed by (2 positions apart)
) AS queries(query_text);
Query Operators:
       query_text        |      parsed_query
-------------------------+------------------------
 postgresql              | 'postgresql'
 postgresql & database   | 'postgresql' & 'databas'
 postgresql | mysql      | 'postgresql' | 'mysql'
 !postgresql             | !'postgresql'
 postgresql <-> database | 'postgresql' <-> 'databas'
 postgresql <2> database | 'postgresql' <2> 'databas'
(6 rows)

🎯 Example 2: plainto_tsquery() for User Input

-- Convert plain text to query (safer for user input)
SELECT 
    input_text,
    plainto_tsquery('english', input_text) AS simple_query,
    to_tsquery('english', input_text) AS strict_query
FROM (VALUES
    ('postgresql database'),
    ('searching & ranking'),
    ('how to use postgresql?')
) AS inputs(input_text);
Plain Text Queries:
       input_text        |    simple_query     | strict_query
-------------------------+---------------------+--------------
 postgresql database     | 'postgresql' & 'databas' | ERROR
 searching & ranking     | 'search' & 'rank'   | ERROR
 how to use postgresql?  | 'postgresql'        | ERROR
(3 rows)

-- plainto_tsquery() handles punctuation, connects with &
-- to_tsquery() errors on invalid syntax

🎯 Example 3: phraseto_tsquery() for Phrase Search

-- Create phrase queries automatically
SELECT 
    phrase,
    phraseto_tsquery('english', phrase) AS phrase_query,
    plainto_tsquery('english', phrase) AS plain_query
FROM (VALUES
    ('full text search'),
    ('database management system'),
    ('open source software')
) AS phrases(phrase);
Phrase Queries:
          phrase           |         phrase_query          |       plain_query
---------------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------
 full text search          | 'full' <-> 'text' <-> 'search'| 'full' & 'text' & 'search'
 database management system| 'databas' <-> 'manag' <-> 'system'| 'databas' & 'manag' & 'system'
 open source software      | 'open' <-> 'sourc' <-> 'softwar'| 'open' & 'sourc' & 'softwar'
(3 rows)

-- phraseto_tsquery() uses <-> (phrase), plainto_tsquery() uses & (AND)

🎯 Example 4: websearch_to_tsquery() for Web-Style Queries

-- Web search syntax: "quoted phrases", -excluded, OR
SELECT 
    web_query,
    websearch_to_tsquery('english', web_query) AS parsed
FROM (VALUES
    ('postgresql database'),
    ('"full text search"'),
    ('postgresql -mysql'),
    ('postgresql OR mysql'),
    ('"open source" database -commercial')
) AS queries(web_query);
Web Search Queries:
            web_query             |              parsed
--------------------------------------+----------------------------------
 postgresql database                  | 'postgresql' & 'databas'
 "full text search"                   | 'full' <-> 'text' <-> 'search'
 postgresql -mysql                    | 'postgresql' & !'mysql'
 postgresql OR mysql                  | 'postgresql' | 'mysql'
 "open source" database -commercial   | 'open' <-> 'sourc' & 'databas' & !'commerci'
(5 rows)

-- Most user-friendly query function!

🎯 Example 5: Complex Query Building

-- Build complex queries programmatically
SELECT 
    description,
    query,
    to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL is a powerful open source database system') @@ query AS matches
FROM (VALUES
    ('Simple term', to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql')),
    ('AND combination', to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database')),
    ('OR combination', to_tsquery('english', 'mysql | postgresql')),
    ('NOT operator', to_tsquery('english', 'database & !commercial')),
    ('Phrase search', phraseto_tsquery('english', 'open source')),
    ('Complex query', to_tsquery('english', '(postgresql | mysql) & database & !commercial'))
) AS queries(description, query);
Query Matching Results:
   description    |                   query                    | matches
------------------+--------------------------------------------+---------
 Simple term      | 'postgresql'                               | t
 AND combination  | 'postgresql' & 'databas'                   | t
 OR combination   | 'mysql' | 'postgresql'                    | t
 NOT operator     | 'databas' & !'commerci'                    | t
 Phrase search    | 'open' <-> 'sourc'                         | t
 Complex query    | ( 'postgresql' | 'mysql' ) & 'databas' & !'commerci' | t
(6 rows)
Explanation: Query parsing creates tsquery from text input. Four main functions: (1) to_tsquery() - strict syntax with operators, errors on invalid input, (2) plainto_tsquery() - converts plain text, connects terms with &, (3) phraseto_tsquery() - creates phrase queries with <->, (4) websearch_to_tsquery() - Google-style syntax with quoted phrases, - for NOT, OR for alternation. For user-facing search, use websearch_to_tsquery() or plainto_tsquery() - they handle arbitrary input safely. Use to_tsquery() for programmatic queries with precise control. Operators: & (AND), | (OR), ! (NOT), <-> (followed by), (N positions apart). Query parsing applies same stemming as document parsing.

12.3.3 Ranking Search Results

🎯 Example 1: Basic Ranking with ts_rank()

-- Rank search results by relevance
SELECT 
    title,
    ts_rank(tsv, query) AS rank
FROM articles, 
     to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
ORDER BY rank DESC;
Ranked Results:
       title        |   rank
--------------------+----------
 Database Tips      | 0.121661
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| 0.099081
(2 rows)

-- Higher rank = more relevant

🎯 Example 2: Ranking with Weights

-- Different fields have different importance (A > B > C > D)
SELECT 
    title,
    ts_rank(
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', title), 'A') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', content), 'B'),
        to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql')
    ) AS weighted_rank
FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ 
      to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql')
ORDER BY weighted_rank DESC;
Weighted Ranking:
       title        | weighted_rank
--------------------+---------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| 0.607927     -- Title match scores higher
 Database Tips      | 0.075990     -- Body-only match scores lower
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: ts_rank_cd() for Cover Density

-- Cover density ranking (considers proximity of terms)
SELECT 
    title,
    ts_rank(tsv, query) AS basic_rank,
    ts_rank_cd(tsv, query) AS cover_density_rank
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
ORDER BY cover_density_rank DESC;
Ranking Comparison:
       title        | basic_rank | cover_density_rank
--------------------+------------+--------------------
 Database Tips      |  0.121661  |         0.100000
 PostgreSQL Tutorial|  0.099081  |         0.050000
(2 rows)

-- ts_rank_cd() rewards terms appearing close together

🎯 Example 4: Normalization Options

-- Normalization divides rank by document length
-- Options: 0=no norm, 1=divide by length, 2=divide by log(length), 4=divide by mean harmonic distance
SELECT 
    title,
    length(content) AS doc_length,
    ts_rank(tsv, query, 0) AS rank_no_norm,
    ts_rank(tsv, query, 1) AS rank_norm_length,
    ts_rank(tsv, query, 2) AS rank_norm_log
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
ORDER BY rank_norm_length DESC;
Normalization Effects:
       title        | doc_length | rank_no_norm | rank_norm_length | rank_norm_log
--------------------+------------+--------------+------------------+---------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial|         52 |    0.099081  |      0.001906    |    0.024806
 Database Tips      |        102 |    0.075990  |      0.000745    |    0.016472
(2 rows)

-- Normalization prevents long documents from dominating

🎯 Example 5: Custom Ranking with Multiple Factors

-- Combine text rank with other factors
SELECT 
    title,
    published_date,
    ts_rank(tsv, query) AS text_rank,
    EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - published_date)) / 86400 AS days_old,
    -- Combined score: text relevance + recency boost
    ts_rank(tsv, query) * 
    (1.0 / (1.0 + EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - published_date)) / 86400 / 365)) AS combined_score
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'database') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
ORDER BY combined_score DESC;
Combined Ranking:
       title        | published_date | text_rank | days_old | combined_score
--------------------+----------------+-----------+----------+----------------
 Database Tips      | 2024-03-10     |  0.121661 |  322.5   |     0.077234
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| 2024-01-15     |  0.099081 |  377.2   |     0.060011
(2 rows)

-- Newer articles get ranking boost
Explanation: Ranking determines relevance of search results. Two functions: ts_rank() (frequency-based), ts_rank_cd() (cover density - proximity matters). Both return float 0-1, higher = more relevant. Ranking factors: term frequency, term proximity, field weights (A/B/C/D), document length. Normalization options prevent long documents dominating: 0 (none), 1 (divide by length), 2 (divide by log), 4 (harmonic mean). Use setweight() to boost important fields (title > abstract > body). Combine text rank with custom factors: recency, popularity, user preferences. Always ORDER BY rank DESC for best results first. Ranking essential for good search UX.

12.3.4 Highlighting Results

🎯 Example 1: Basic Highlighting with ts_headline()

-- Highlight matching terms in search results
SELECT 
    title,
    ts_headline('english', content, query) AS highlighted_content
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
LIMIT 1;
Highlighted Result:
       title        |              highlighted_content
--------------------+---------------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| Learn PostgreSQL database management and SQL queries
(1 row)

-- Matching terms wrapped in  tags by default

🎯 Example 2: Custom Highlighting Tags

-- Customize start and stop tags
SELECT 
    title,
    ts_headline('english', content, query,
        'StartSel=, StopSel='
    ) AS custom_highlight
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'database & management') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
LIMIT 1;
Custom Tags:
       title        |                    custom_highlight
--------------------+-----------------------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| Learn PostgreSQL database 
                    | management and SQL queries
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Highlighting Options

-- Control fragment length and word count
SELECT 
    title,
    -- Default: MaxWords=35, MinWords=15
    ts_headline('english', content, query) AS default_headline,
    -- Short fragments
    ts_headline('english', content, query, 'MaxWords=10, MinWords=5') AS short_headline,
    -- Show fragment count
    ts_headline('english', content, query, 'MaxFragments=2') AS two_fragments
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql | database') query
WHERE tsv @@ query
LIMIT 1;
Headline Options:
       title        |     default_headline      |  short_headline   |    two_fragments
--------------------+---------------------------+-------------------+-------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial| Learn PostgreSQL   | PostgreSQL | Learn PostgreSQL
                    | database management| database   | database ... SQL
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Highlighting Multiple Columns

-- Highlight in both title and content
SELECT 
    ts_headline('english', title, query, 
        'StartSel=, StopSel='
    ) AS highlighted_title,
    ts_headline('english', content, query,
        'StartSel=, StopSel=, MaxWords=20'
    ) AS highlighted_content
FROM articles,
     websearch_to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql database') query
WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) @@ query;
Multi-Column Highlighting:
         highlighted_title          |           highlighted_content
------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL Tutorial | Learn PostgreSQL 
                                    | database management and SQL
 Database Tips     | PostgreSQL 
                                    | database performance tips
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Context-Aware Highlighting

-- Show context around matches with FragmentDelimiter
INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content) VALUES
    ('Long Article', 'Writer', 'PostgreSQL is powerful. ' || 
     'It has many features. Database systems are complex. ' ||
     'PostgreSQL handles complexity well. ' ||
     'Many developers prefer PostgreSQL for its reliability.');

SELECT 
    title,
    ts_headline('english', content, query,
        'MaxFragments=3, FragmentDelimiter= ... '
    ) AS context_highlights
FROM articles,
     to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql') query
WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ query
  AND title = 'Long Article';
Context Fragments:
    title     |                      context_highlights
--------------+--------------------------------------------------------------------
 Long Article | PostgreSQL is powerful ... PostgreSQL handles 
              | complexity well ... developers prefer PostgreSQL for its
(1 row)
Explanation: ts_headline() generates snippets showing matching terms in context. Wraps matches in tags (default ) for display. Options: StartSel/StopSel (custom tags), MaxWords (fragment length), MinWords (minimum words), MaxFragments (number of snippets), FragmentDelimiter (separator between fragments), HighlightAll (show all or just best fragments). Use custom tags for CSS styling: . Highlighting makes search results user-friendly - shows why document matched. Apply to multiple columns (title, abstract, body) separately. Balance fragment length: too short loses context, too long dilutes matches. Essential for search results pages.

12.4 Additional Features

Advanced full-text features: Manipulate tsvector and tsquery objects for sophisticated search. Combine documents, add/remove lexemes, rewrite queries. Use triggers to automatically maintain tsvector columns. Gather statistics about document composition. These features enable complex search applications with automatic updates and advanced query processing.

12.4.1 Manipulating Documents

🎯 Example 1: Concatenating tsvector

-- Combine multiple tsvector values
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL database') ||
    to_tsvector('english', 'full text search') AS combined_vector;
Combined Vector:
                  combined_vector
----------------------------------------------------
 'databas':2 'full':3 'postgresql':1 'search':5 'text':4
(1 row)

-- Positions automatically adjusted in concatenation

🎯 Example 2: setweight() for Field Importance

-- Assign weights to mark importance (A=highest, D=lowest)
SELECT 
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL Tutorial'), 'A') ||
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', 'Learn database basics'), 'B') ||
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', 'Additional information here'), 'C') AS weighted_doc;
Weighted Document:
                          weighted_doc
----------------------------------------------------------------------
 'addit':7C 'basic':4B 'databas':3B 'inform':8C 'learn':2B 
 'postgresql':1A 'tutorial':2A
(1 row)

-- Letters after positions indicate weights

🎯 Example 3: strip() to Remove Positions

-- Remove position information from tsvector
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL database system') AS with_positions,
    strip(to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL database system')) AS without_positions;
Strip Positions:
        with_positions         |    without_positions
-------------------------------+-------------------------
 'databas':2 'postgresql':1    | 'databas' 'postgresql' 'system'
 'system':3                    |
(1 row)

-- Useful for some matching operations

🎯 Example 4: length() and lexemes

-- Get number of unique lexemes
SELECT 
    doc,
    length(to_tsvector('english', doc)) AS lexeme_count,
    to_tsvector('english', doc) AS vector
FROM (VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL'),
    ('PostgreSQL database'),
    ('PostgreSQL is a powerful database system')
) AS docs(doc);
Lexeme Counts:
                doc                | lexeme_count |           vector
-----------------------------------+--------------+-----------------------------
 PostgreSQL                        |            1 | 'postgresql':1
 PostgreSQL database               |            2 | 'databas':2 'postgresql':1
 PostgreSQL is a powerful database |            4 | 'databas':5 'power':4 
           system                  |              | 'postgresql':1 'system':6
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Custom tsvector Construction

-- Manually construct tsvector (advanced)
SELECT 
    'postgresql':1A || 'database':2B || 'search':3C AS manual_vector,
    to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL database search') AS automatic_vector;
Manual vs Automatic:
        manual_vector         |      automatic_vector
------------------------------+---------------------------
 'databas':2B 'postgresql':1A | 'databas':2 'postgresql':1
 'search':3C                  | 'search':3
(1 row)

-- Manual construction allows precise control
Explanation: tsvector manipulation enables advanced document processing. Concatenation (||) combines vectors, adjusting positions automatically. setweight() assigns importance A/B/C/D to lexemes - used in ranking. strip() removes position info when only lexeme presence matters. length() counts unique lexemes. Can manually construct tsvector with 'lexeme':position format. Manual construction useful for: custom tokenization, preserving special terms unchanged, importing preprocessed data. Most applications use automatic to_tsvector() but manual gives full control. Weighted multi-field vectors common pattern: title (A) + abstract (B) + body (C/D).

12.4.2 Manipulating Queries

🎯 Example 1: Query Operators and Combination

-- Combine queries programmatically
SELECT 
    q1 || q2 AS combined_or,
    q1 && q2 AS combined_and
FROM 
    to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql') AS q1,
    to_tsquery('english', 'database') AS q2;
Query Combination:
        combined_or         |       combined_and
----------------------------+---------------------------
 'postgresql' | 'databas'  | 'postgresql' & 'databas'
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Query Negation

-- Negate entire query
SELECT 
    query,
    !! query AS negated
FROM 
    to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database') AS query;
Negation:
           query            |          negated
----------------------------+----------------------------
 'postgresql' & 'databas'   | !( 'postgresql' & 'databas' )
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: tsquery_phrase() for Proximity

-- Create phrase queries with specific distances
SELECT 
    tsquery_phrase(
        to_tsquery('english', 'full'),
        to_tsquery('english', 'text')
    ) AS adjacent,
    tsquery_phrase(
        to_tsquery('english', 'full'),
        to_tsquery('english', 'text'),
        2
    ) AS two_apart;
Phrase Queries:
     adjacent      |    two_apart
-------------------+------------------
 'full' <-> 'text' | 'full' <2> 'text'
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: querytree() for Query Analysis

-- Analyze complex query structure
SELECT 
    query,
    querytree(query) AS tree_structure
FROM (
    VALUES
        (to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql')),
        (to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database')),
        (to_tsquery('english', '(postgresql | mysql) & !commercial'))
) AS queries(query);
Query Trees:
                query                 |          tree_structure
--------------------------------------+----------------------------------
 'postgresql'                         | 'postgresql'
 'postgresql' & 'databas'             | 'postgresql' & 'databas'
 ( 'postgresql' | 'mysql' ) & !'commercial' | ( 'postgresql' | 'mysql' ) & !'commercial'
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: numnode() for Query Complexity

-- Count nodes in query tree (measure complexity)
SELECT 
    query_text,
    query,
    numnode(query) AS node_count
FROM (
    VALUES
        ('simple', to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql')),
        ('moderate', to_tsquery('english', 'postgresql & database')),
        ('complex', to_tsquery('english', '(postgresql | mysql) & database & !commercial'))
) AS queries(query_text, query);
Query Complexity:
 query_text |                    query                     | node_count
------------+----------------------------------------------+------------
 simple     | 'postgresql'                                 |          1
 moderate   | 'postgresql' & 'databas'                     |          3
 complex    | ( 'postgresql' | 'mysql' ) & 'databas' & !'commercial' |          7
(3 rows)

-- Higher node count = more complex query
Explanation: tsquery manipulation enables dynamic query building. Operators: || (OR), && (AND), !! (NOT). tsquery_phrase() creates proximity queries with custom distances. querytree() visualizes query structure - useful for debugging. numnode() measures query complexity (node count in parse tree). Query rewriting with ts_rewrite() can optimize queries or implement synonyms. Programmatic query building useful for: search suggestions, query expansion, synonym handling, filtering by permissions. Example: expand 'postgres' to 'postgres | postgresql'. Complex queries can impact performance - monitor with numnode().

12.4.3 Triggers for Automatic Updates

🎯 Example 1: Basic Trigger for tsvector Update

-- Create trigger function to maintain tsvector
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION articles_tsv_trigger() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    NEW.tsv := to_tsvector('english', coalesce(NEW.title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(NEW.content,''));
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Attach trigger to table
CREATE TRIGGER tsv_update BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE
ON articles FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION articles_tsv_trigger();

-- Test: INSERT automatically updates tsv
INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content) VALUES
    ('Trigger Test', 'Test Author', 'This tests automatic tsvector updates');

SELECT title, tsv FROM articles WHERE title = 'Trigger Test';
Automatic tsvector:
    title     |                      tsv
--------------+------------------------------------------------
 Trigger Test | 'author':3 'autom':6 'test':1,2,4 'trigger':1 'tsvector':7 'updat':8
(1 row)

-- tsv updated automatically!

🎯 Example 2: Trigger with Weighted Columns

-- Weighted trigger for multi-field search
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION documents_weighted_trigger() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    NEW.tsv := 
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(NEW.title,'')), 'A') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(NEW.abstract,'')), 'B') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(NEW.body,'')), 'D');
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER documents_tsv_update BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE
ON documents FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION documents_weighted_trigger();

-- Test weighted trigger
INSERT INTO documents (title, abstract, body) VALUES
    ('Important Title', 'Brief summary', 'Detailed content here');

SELECT title, tsv FROM documents WHERE title = 'Important Title';
Weighted Result:
     title      |                     tsv
----------------+----------------------------------------------
