 Important Title| 'brief':3B 'content':5D 'detail':4D 'import':1A 'summari':4B 'titl':2A
(1 row)

-- Weights preserved: A for title, B for abstract, D for body

🎯 Example 3: Conditional Trigger Execution

-- Only update tsvector if relevant columns changed
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION articles_smart_trigger() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Only recompute if title or content changed
    IF NEW.title IS DISTINCT FROM OLD.title OR 
       NEW.content IS DISTINCT FROM OLD.content THEN
        NEW.tsv := to_tsvector('english', 
            coalesce(NEW.title,'') || ' ' || coalesce(NEW.content,''));
    END IF;
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS tsv_update ON articles;
CREATE TRIGGER tsv_update BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE
ON articles FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION articles_smart_trigger();

-- Test: Update non-search column (no tsv recompute)
UPDATE articles SET author = 'New Author' WHERE title = 'Trigger Test';

-- Test: Update search column (tsv recomputed)
UPDATE articles SET title = 'Updated Title' WHERE title = 'Trigger Test';
Smart Trigger:
-- First update: author changed, tsv unchanged
-- Second update: title changed, tsv recomputed

     title      |                      tsv
----------------+------------------------------------------------
 Updated Title  | 'author':3 'autom':6 'test':2,4 'tsvector':7 'updat':1,8
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: tsvector_update_trigger() Built-in Function

-- Use built-in trigger function (simpler)
CREATE TABLE simple_docs (
    doc_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text,
    body text,
    tsv tsvector
);

-- Built-in trigger: tsvector_update_trigger(tsv_column, config, text_column1, ...)
CREATE TRIGGER tsvectorupdate BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE
ON simple_docs FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION tsvector_update_trigger(tsv, 'pg_catalog.english', title, body);

-- Test built-in trigger
INSERT INTO simple_docs (title, body) VALUES
    ('Built-in Trigger', 'Uses PostgreSQL built-in function');

SELECT title, tsv FROM simple_docs;
Built-in Trigger Result:
      title       |                      tsv
------------------+------------------------------------------------
 Built-in Trigger | 'built':1,5 'built-in':1 'function':7 'postgresql':6 'trigger':3 'use':4
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Trigger Performance Considerations

-- Measure trigger overhead
\timing on

-- Insert 1000 rows WITH trigger
INSERT INTO articles (title, author, content)
SELECT 
    'Article ' || generate_series,
    'Author ' || (random() * 10)::int,
    md5(random()::text)
FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Check trigger execution count
SELECT 
    schemaname, 
    tablename,
    n_tup_ins AS inserts,
    n_tup_upd AS updates
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE tablename = 'articles';
Trigger Performance:
Time: 567.890 ms

 schemaname | tablename | inserts | updates
------------+-----------+---------+---------
 public     | articles  |   11000 |      45
(1 row)

-- Trigger adds ~0.5ms per row for tsvector computation
Explanation: Triggers automatically maintain tsvector columns on INSERT/UPDATE. Three approaches: (1) Custom trigger function (full control), (2) tsvector_update_trigger() built-in (simple), (3) GENERATED column (PostgreSQL 12+, automatic). Custom triggers enable: weighted vectors, conditional updates, complex logic. Smart triggers check IF columns actually changed before recomputing. Built-in tsvector_update_trigger() simpler but less flexible. Trigger overhead: ~0.1-1ms per row depending on document size. For bulk loads, consider disabling triggers, loading data, then rebuilding tsvector. Triggers essential for maintaining search index consistency automatically.

12.4.4 Gathering Document Statistics

🎯 Example 1: ts_stat() for Lexeme Frequency

-- Analyze most common lexemes in corpus
SELECT * FROM ts_stat('SELECT tsv FROM articles')
ORDER BY nentry DESC, ndoc DESC, word
LIMIT 10;
Top Lexemes:
    word     | ndoc | nentry
-------------+------+--------
 articl      |  987 |   1245
 author      |  856 |    945
 databas     |  654 |    789
 postgresql  |  543 |    678
 guid        |  432 |    567
 tutori      |  398 |    456
 learn       |  345 |    398
 program     |  312 |    367
 develop     |  289 |    334
 manag       |  267 |    312
(10 rows)

-- ndoc = documents containing word
-- nentry = total occurrences

🎯 Example 2: Analyzing Specific Document Set

-- Statistics for filtered documents
SELECT 
    word,
    ndoc,
    nentry,
    ROUND(nentry::numeric / ndoc, 2) AS avg_per_doc
FROM ts_stat('
    SELECT tsv FROM articles 
    WHERE published_date >= ''2024-01-01''
')
WHERE ndoc > 5
ORDER BY nentry DESC
LIMIT 10;
2024 Articles Statistics:
    word     | ndoc | nentry | avg_per_doc
-------------+------+--------+-------------
 postgresql  |   45 |    123 |        2.73
 databas     |   42 |    98  |        2.33
 guid        |   38 |    87  |        2.29
 tutori      |   34 |    76  |        2.24
 search      |   32 |    65  |        2.03
 text        |   31 |    62  |        2.00
 full        |   29 |    58  |        2.00
 develop     |   27 |    54  |        2.00
 applic      |   25 |    50  |        2.00
 system      |   24 |    48  |        2.00
(10 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Term Distribution Analysis

-- Analyze term distribution (for stop word identification)
WITH stats AS (
    SELECT * FROM ts_stat('SELECT tsv FROM articles')
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN nentry >= 1000 THEN '1000+'
        WHEN nentry >= 500 THEN '500-999'
        WHEN nentry >= 100 THEN '100-499'
        WHEN nentry >= 10 THEN '10-99'
        ELSE '1-9'
    END AS frequency_range,
    count(*) AS term_count
FROM stats
GROUP BY frequency_range
ORDER BY min(nentry) DESC;
Term Distribution:
 frequency_range | term_count
-----------------+------------
 1000+           |         12
 500-999         |         34
 100-499         |        156
 10-99           |        892
 1-9             |       4567
(5 rows)

-- Most terms appear infrequently (long tail distribution)

🎯 Example 4: Document Vocabulary Size

-- Analyze vocabulary across corpus
WITH corpus_stats AS (
    SELECT * FROM ts_stat('SELECT tsv FROM articles')
)
SELECT 
    count(*) AS unique_lexemes,
    sum(nentry) AS total_word_instances,
    ROUND(avg(nentry), 2) AS avg_frequency,
    max(nentry) AS max_frequency,
    min(nentry) AS min_frequency
FROM corpus_stats;
Corpus Vocabulary:
 unique_lexemes | total_word_instances | avg_frequency | max_frequency | min_frequency
----------------+----------------------+---------------+---------------+---------------
           5661 |               45678  |          8.07 |          1245 |             1
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Weight-Based Statistics

-- Analyze lexemes by weight (requires weighted tsvector)
SELECT 
    substring(word from '(.*):[ABCD]$') AS lexeme,
    substring(word from ':([ABCD])$') AS weight,
    ndoc,
    nentry
FROM ts_stat('SELECT tsv FROM documents WHERE tsv IS NOT NULL')
WHERE word ~ ':[ABCD]$'
ORDER BY 
    substring(word from ':([ABCD])$'),
    nentry DESC
LIMIT 15;
Weight Distribution:
   lexeme    | weight | ndoc | nentry
-------------+--------+------+--------
 guid        | A      |   45 |     67
 tutori      | A      |   42 |     58
 overview    | A      |   38 |     52
 summari     | B      |   56 |     89
 abstract    | B      |   48 |     72
 introduct   | B      |   43 |     65
 detail      | D      |  234 |    456
 content     | D      |  198 |    387
 section     | D      |  176 |    342
(9 rows)

-- A-weighted terms (titles) less frequent but important
Explanation: ts_stat() analyzes lexeme distribution across document corpus. Returns: word (lexeme), ndoc (documents containing it), nentry (total occurrences). Useful for: identifying common terms (potential stop words), analyzing vocabulary size, finding domain-specific terms, validating stemming. Query parameter is SQL string returning tsvector values - can filter documents. High ndoc+nentry indicates common words. Very high frequency might indicate missing stop words. Analyze by weight to see title vs body term distribution. Statistics help tune configuration: add stop words, adjust dictionary. Essential for understanding and optimizing search behavior.

12.5 Parsers

Text search parsers: Break text into tokens and classify them. PostgreSQL includes default parser recognizing 23 token types (words, numbers, emails, URLs, etc.). Parser independent of language - classification only. Dictionaries then process tokens into lexemes. Can create custom parsers for specialized formats. Parser choice affects tokenization but not stemming or stop words.

🎯 Example 1: Available Parsers

-- List all installed parsers
SELECT 
    prsname AS parser_name,
    prsnamespace::regnamespace AS namespace,
    prsstart::regproc AS start_function,
    prstoken::regproc AS token_function
FROM pg_ts_parser
ORDER BY prsname;
Installed Parsers:
 parser_name |  namespace  |  start_function  |  token_function
-------------+-------------+------------------+------------------
 default     | pg_catalog  | prsd_start       | prsd_nexttoken
(1 row)

-- Default parser handles most needs

🎯 Example 2: Parser Token Types

-- All token types recognized by default parser
SELECT 
    tokid,
    alias,
    description
FROM ts_token_type('default')
ORDER BY tokid;
Token Types (23 total):
 tokid |      alias      |          description
-------+-----------------+--------------------------------
     1 | asciiword       | Word, all ASCII
     2 | word            | Word, all letters
     3 | numword         | Word, letters and digits
     4 | email           | Email address
     5 | url             | URL
     6 | host            | Host
     7 | sfloat          | Scientific notation
     8 | version         | Version number
     9 | hword_numpart   | Hyphenated word part, letters and digits
    10 | hword_part      | Hyphenated word part, all letters
    11 | hword_asciipart | Hyphenated word part, all ASCII
    12 | blank           | Space symbols
    13 | tag             | XML tag
    14 | protocol        | Protocol head
    15 | numhword        | Hyphenated word, letters and digits
    16 | asciihword      | Hyphenated word, all ASCII
    17 | hword           | Hyphenated word, all letters
    18 | url_path        | URL path
    19 | file            | File or path name
    20 | float           | Decimal notation
    21 | int             | Signed integer
    22 | uint            | Unsigned integer
    23 | entity          | XML entity
(23 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Parser Behavior Examples

-- See how parser classifies different input types
SELECT 
    input_type,
    input_text,
    (ts_debug('english', input_text)).*
FROM (VALUES
    ('Email', 'contact@example.com'),
    ('URL', 'https://www.postgresql.org/docs'),
    ('Version', 'PostgreSQL 16.2'),
    ('Float', 'Temperature: 98.6 degrees'),
    ('Hyphenated', 'state-of-the-art technology')
) AS samples(input_type, input_text)
WHERE (ts_debug('english', input_text)).token != ''
LIMIT 20;
Parser Classification:
 input_type |  token   |   alias   |          description
------------+----------+-----------+--------------------------------
 Email      | contact@ | email     | Email address
            | example  |           |
            | .com     |           |
 URL        | https:// | protocol  | Protocol head
            | www      | host      | Host
            | .postgr  |           |
            | /docs    | url_path  | URL path
 Version    | PostgreS | asciiword | Word, all ASCII
            | QL       |           |
            | 16.2     | version   | Version number
 Float      | Temperat | asciiword | Word, all ASCII
            | 98.6     | float     | Decimal notation
            | degrees  | asciiword | Word, all ASCII
 Hyphenated | state    | asciihword| Hyphenated word, all ASCII
            | of       |           |
            | the      |           |
            | art      |           |
            | technolo | asciiword | Word, all ASCII
(20 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Parser with Different Configurations

-- Same parser, different configurations process tokens differently
SELECT 
    'english' AS config,
    to_tsvector('english', 'The running runners ran quickly') AS result
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'simple',
    to_tsvector('simple', 'The running runners ran quickly');

-- Parser tokenizes identically, but dictionaries differ
SELECT tokid, token
FROM ts_parse('default', 'The running runners ran quickly');
Parser vs Configuration:
 config  |           result
---------+-----------------------------
 english | 'quick':4 'ran':3 'run':1,2
 simple  | 'quickly':5 'ran':4 'runners':3 'running':2 'the':1
(2 rows)

-- Parser output (tokens):
 tokid | token
-------+---------
     1 | The
    12 |
     1 | running
    12 |
     1 | runners
    12 |
     1 | ran
    12 |
     1 | quickly
-- Same tokenization, different dictionary processing

🎯 Example 5: Testing Parser on Complex Input

-- Test parser on mixed content
SELECT 
    token,
    alias AS token_type
FROM ts_parse('default', $$
    PostgreSQL 16.2 released!
    Contact: info@postgresql.org
    Download: https://www.postgresql.org/download/
    Price: $0.00 (open-source)
    Version: 16.2.1
$$)
WHERE tokid NOT IN (12) -- Exclude blank spaces
ORDER BY startpos
LIMIT 15;
Complex Input Parsing:
      token       |  token_type
------------------+-------------
 PostgreSQL       | asciiword
 16.2             | version
 released         | asciiword
 Contact          | asciiword
 info@postgresql  | email
 .org             | host
 Download         | asciiword
 https            | protocol
 ://www.postgre   | url
 /download/       | url_path
 Price            | asciiword
 0.00             | float
 open             | asciiword
 source           | asciiword
 Version          | asciiword
(15 rows)

-- Parser recognizes specialized formats!
Explanation: Parser breaks text into classified tokens - first step in full-text search pipeline. Default parser recognizes 23 token types including specialized formats (emails, URLs, versions, scientific notation, XML tags). Parser operates on character sequences, independent of language. Token classification determines how dictionaries process them. Parser output becomes dictionary input. Can test parser with ts_parse() (raw tokens) and ts_debug() (full pipeline). Most applications use default parser - sufficient for general text. Custom parsers needed only for specialized domains (chemical formulas, custom markup). Parser behavior same across configurations - differences come from dictionary processing. Choose configuration for language, not parser behavior.

12.6 Dictionaries

Text search dictionaries: Process tokens from parser into lexemes (normalized forms). Six dictionary types: Stop Words (remove common words), Simple (lowercase conversion), Synonym (word mapping), Thesaurus (phrase replacement), Ispell (morphological analysis), Snowball (stemming). Dictionaries chained in configuration - first match wins. Language-specific processing happens in dictionaries, not parser.

12.6.1 Stop Words

🎯 Example 1: Default Stop Words

-- See how stop words are removed
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('english', 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog') AS with_stopwords_removed,
    to_tsvector('simple', 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog') AS simple_no_removal;
Stop Word Removal:
   with_stopwords_removed    |              simple_no_removal
-----------------------------+---------------------------------------------
 'brown':3 'dog':9 'fox':4   | 'brown':3 'dog':9 'fox':4 'jumps':5 'lazy':8
 'jump':5 'lazi':8 'quick':2 | 'over':6 'quick':2 'the':1,7
(1 row)

-- 'the', 'over' removed in english, kept in simple

🎯 Example 2: Viewing Stop Word List

-- Check stop word dictionary configuration
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('english_stem', 'the') AS stop_word_the,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', 'database') AS normal_word;

-- List some common English stop words by testing
SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS lexemes
FROM (VALUES 
    ('the'), ('is'), ('at'), ('which'), ('on'), ('a'), ('an'),
    ('database'), ('postgresql')
) AS words(word);
Stop Word Testing:
 stop_word_the | normal_word
---------------+-------------
               | {databas}
(1 row)

   word     |  lexemes
------------+-----------
 the        |
 is         |
 at         |
 which      |
 on         |
 a          |
 an         |
 database   | {databas}
 postgresql | {postgresql}
(9 rows)

-- Stop words return NULL (empty lexeme list)

🎯 Example 3: Custom Stop Word Dictionary

-- Create custom stop word list (in practice, store in file)
-- Example: Create configuration without certain stop words

-- View existing stop word file location
SHOW config_file;

-- Create custom configuration with modified stop words
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY my_english_stop (
    TEMPLATE = pg_catalog.simple,
    STOPWORDS = 'english'  -- Uses default english stop words
);

-- Test custom dictionary
SELECT ts_lexize('my_english_stop', 'the');
SELECT ts_lexize('my_english_stop', 'database');
Custom Stop Dictionary:
                 config_file
----------------------------------------------
 /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf
(1 row)

 ts_lexize
-----------
           -- 'the' is stop word (removed)

 ts_lexize
-----------
 {database} -- 'database' preserved
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Impact of Stop Words on Search

-- Query with stop words
SELECT 
    to_tsquery('english', 'the & database') AS with_stopword,
    to_tsquery('english', 'database') AS without_stopword,
    to_tsvector('english', 'the database system') @@ 
        to_tsquery('english', 'the & database') AS matches;
Stop Words in Queries:
 with_stopword | without_stopword | matches
---------------+------------------+---------
 'databas'     | 'databas'        | t
(1 row)

-- 'the' removed from query automatically - doesn't affect matching

🎯 Example 5: Stop Words by Configuration

-- Different configurations have different stop words
WITH test_words AS (
    SELECT unnest(ARRAY['the', 'a', 'is', 'are', 'database', 'von', 'der']) AS word
)
SELECT 
    word,
    CASE WHEN ts_lexize('english_stem', word) IS NULL THEN 'STOP' ELSE 'KEEP' END AS english,
    CASE WHEN ts_lexize('german_stem', word) IS NULL THEN 'STOP' ELSE 'KEEP' END AS german,
    CASE WHEN ts_lexize('simple', word) IS NULL THEN 'STOP' ELSE 'KEEP' END AS simple
FROM test_words;
Stop Words Across Languages:
   word   | english | german | simple
----------+---------+--------+--------
 the      | STOP    | KEEP   | KEEP
 a        | STOP    | KEEP   | KEEP
 is       | STOP    | KEEP   | KEEP
 are      | STOP    | KEEP   | KEEP
 database | KEEP    | KEEP   | KEEP
 von      | KEEP    | STOP   | KEEP
 der      | KEEP    | STOP   | KEEP
(7 rows)

-- Each language has appropriate stop words
Explanation: Stop words are common words removed from search indexes (the, a, is, at, on, etc.). Reduce index size and improve performance by eliminating non-distinctive terms. Language-specific - "the" is English stop word, "der" is German. Stop word dictionary returns NULL (no lexemes). When stop word appears in query, it's automatically removed. Can create custom stop word lists for domain-specific needs. Trade-off: removes words that might be meaningful in some contexts. "to be or not to be" becomes empty query if all are stop words. Simple configuration has minimal stop words. Stop words essential for index size management in large corpora.

12.6.2 Simple Dictionary

🎯 Example 1: Simple Dictionary Behavior

-- Simple dictionary: lowercase conversion only
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('simple', 'PostgreSQL') AS simple_result,
    ts_lexize('simple', 'RUNNING') AS simple_running,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', 'RUNNING') AS stemmed_running;
Simple vs Stemming:
 simple_result | simple_running | stemmed_running
---------------+----------------+-----------------
 {postgresql}  | {running}      | {run}
(1 row)

-- Simple: lowercase only, no stemming
-- English stem: lowercase AND stemming

🎯 Example 2: When to Use Simple Dictionary

-- Use cases for simple dictionary
CREATE TABLE technical_terms (
    term_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    term_name text,
    description text,
    -- Use simple for exact technical terms (no stemming)
    tsv tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        to_tsvector('simple', term_name)
    ) STORED
);

INSERT INTO technical_terms (term_name, description) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL', 'Database management system'),
    ('HTTP', 'Hypertext Transfer Protocol'),
    ('API', 'Application Programming Interface'),
    ('SQL', 'Structured Query Language');

-- Search preserves exact term form
SELECT term_name
FROM technical_terms
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('simple', 'api');

SELECT term_name
FROM technical_terms
WHERE tsv @@ to_tsquery('simple', 'sql');
Technical Term Search:
 term_name
-----------
 API
(1 row)

 term_name
-----------
 SQL
(1 row)

-- Exact matches for technical abbreviations

🎯 Example 3: Simple Dictionary Configuration

-- Create simple dictionary with custom stop words
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY my_simple (
    TEMPLATE = pg_catalog.simple,
    STOPWORDS = 'english',
    ACCEPT = true  -- Accept all non-stop words
);

-- Test custom simple dictionary
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('my_simple', 'PostgreSQL') AS result1,
    ts_lexize('my_simple', 'the') AS stopword;
Custom Simple Dictionary:
   result1   | stopword
-------------+----------
 {postgresql}|
(1 row)

-- Lowercases and removes stop words, no stemming

🎯 Example 4: Comparing Dictionary Outputs

-- Compare different dictionary behaviors
WITH test_words AS (
    SELECT unnest(ARRAY[
        'running', 'RUNNING', 'RuNnInG',
        'databases', 'Database', 'DATABASE'
    ]) AS word
)
SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('simple', word) AS simple,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS english_stem
FROM test_words;
Dictionary Comparison:
    word    |   simple    | english_stem
------------+-------------+--------------
 running    | {running}   | {run}
 RUNNING    | {running}   | {run}
 RuNnInG    | {running}   | {run}
 databases  | {databases} | {databas}
 Database   | {database}  | {databas}
 DATABASE   | {database}  | {databas}
(6 rows)

-- Simple: case normalization only
-- English stem: case + morphological reduction

🎯 Example 5: Mixed Configuration Strategy

-- Use simple for some token types, stemming for others
-- Example: Numbers and codes with simple, words with stemming

SELECT 
    'Product Code: DB-2024-001' AS input,
    to_tsvector('simple', 'DB-2024-001') AS code_vector,
    to_tsvector('english', 'database system for analytics') AS description_vector,
    to_tsvector('simple', 'DB-2024-001') || 
    to_tsvector('english', 'database system for analytics') AS combined;
Mixed Strategy:
            input             |     code_vector      |      description_vector       |           combined
------------------------------ +----------------------+-------------------------------+--------------------------------
 Product Code: DB-2024-001     | 'db':1 '001':3       | 'analyt':4 'databas':1        | '001':3 'analyt':7 'databas':4
                                | '2024':2             | 'system':2                    | 'db':1 'system':5 '2024':2
(1 row)

-- Preserves codes exactly, stems description words
Explanation: Simple dictionary performs minimal processing: lowercase conversion and optional stop word removal. No stemming or morphological analysis - "running" stays "running" (not reduced to "run"). Use simple when: preserving exact word forms important (technical terms, codes, proper names), language unsupported by stemming dictionaries, want predictable behavior without linguistic rules. Simple dictionary fast and predictable. Template: pg_catalog.simple. Options: STOPWORDS (stop word file), ACCEPT (true/false). Good for product codes, abbreviations, identifiers. Can mix simple and stemming dictionaries in same configuration for different token types. Simple's lack of stemming both advantage (precision) and disadvantage (miss variants).

12.6.3 Synonym Dictionary

🎯 Example 1: Synonym Dictionary Concept

-- Create synonym dictionary (requires synonym file)
-- File format: word1 word2 word3 : replacement
-- Example synonyms file content:
/*
postgres postgresql pgsql : postgresql
database db : database
*/

CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY my_synonyms (
    TEMPLATE = synonym,
    SYNONYMS = 'my_synonyms'  -- File: my_synonyms.syn in tsearch_data
);

-- Test synonym mapping
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('my_synonyms', 'postgres') AS synonym1,
    ts_lexize('my_synonyms', 'pgsql') AS synonym2,
    ts_lexize('my_synonyms', 'postgresql') AS synonym3;
Synonym Mapping:
   synonym1   |  synonym2    |  synonym3
--------------+--------------+--------------
 {postgresql} | {postgresql} | {postgresql}
(1 row)

-- All variants map to 'postgresql'

🎯 Example 2: Creating Synonym Configuration

-- Create configuration using synonym dictionary
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION synonym_config (COPY = english);

ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION synonym_config
    ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword, word
    WITH my_synonyms, english_stem;

-- Test with synonyms
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('synonym_config', 'postgres database') AS with_synonyms,
    to_tsvector('english', 'postgres database') AS without_synonyms;
Synonym in Use:
   with_synonyms    | without_synonyms
--------------------+------------------
 'databas':2        | 'databas':2
 'postgresql':1     | 'postgr':1
(1 row)

-- 'postgres' → 'postgresql', 'database' → 'databas'

🎯 Example 3: Multi-Word Synonyms

-- Synonym file with phrases:
/*
rdbms relational_database : database
full text search fts : fulltext_search
*/

-- Using multi-word synonyms
SELECT 
    to_tsquery('synonym_config', 'rdbms') AS term1,
    to_tsquery('synonym_config', 'relational_database') AS term2,
    to_tsquery('synonym_config', 'fts') AS term3;
Phrase Synonyms:
    term1    |      term2      |       term3
-------------+-----------------+-------------------
 'databas'   | 'databas'       | 'fulltext_search'
(1 row)

-- Phrases normalized to single terms

🎯 Example 4: Case-Sensitive Synonyms

-- Synonym dictionary can preserve case
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY case_synonyms (
    TEMPLATE = synonym,
    SYNONYMS = 'my_synonyms',
    CaseSensitive = true  -- Preserve case
);

SELECT 
    ts_lexize('case_synonyms', 'PostgreSQL') AS upper_case,
    ts_lexize('case_synonyms', 'postgresql') AS lower_case;
Case Handling:
  upper_case  | lower_case
--------------+-------------
 {PostgreSQL} | {postgresql}
(1 row)

-- Case preserved when CaseSensitive=true

🎯 Example 5: Synonym Dictionary Chain

-- Chain dictionaries: synonyms first, then stemming
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_config (COPY = english);

ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_config
    ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword, word
    WITH my_synonyms, english_stem;  -- Synonym first, then stem

-- Example: "postgres running" → "postgresql run"
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('my_config', 'postgres database') AS result;

-- Compare dictionary chain order
SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('my_synonyms', word) AS after_synonym,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', coalesce((ts_lexize('my_synonyms', word))[1], word)) AS after_stem
FROM (VALUES ('postgres'), ('database'), ('running')) AS words(word);
Dictionary Chaining:
        result
---------------------
 'databas':2 'postgresql':1
(1 row)

   word    | after_synonym | after_stem
-----------+---------------+------------
 postgres  | {postgresql}  | {postgresql}
 database  | {database}    | {databas}
 running   |               | {run}
(3 rows)

-- Synonym applied first, then result gets stemmed
Explanation: Synonym dictionaries map words/phrases to canonical forms. Configured via text file (*.syn) with format: "variants : replacement". Use cases: brand names (postgres, pgsql → postgresql), abbreviations (db → database), regional spellings (color, colour → color), domain terminology. Create with synonym template. Options: SYNONYMS (file name), CaseSensitive (preserve case). Synonym dictionary typically first in chain, followed by stemming - ensures synonyms processed before morphological reduction. Multi-word synonyms collapse phrases to single terms. Synonym files stored in database tsearch_data directory. Essential for search term normalization and improving recall.

12.6.4 Thesaurus Dictionary

🎯 Example 1: Thesaurus Dictionary Basics

-- Thesaurus maps phrases to concept words
-- File format: phrase words : concept
/*
Example thesaurus file (my_thesaurus.ths):
relational database management system : rdbms
structured query language : sql
full text search : fts
*/

CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY my_thesaurus (
    TEMPLATE = thesaurus,
    DictFile = 'my_thesaurus',
    Dictionary = 'english_stem'  -- Base dictionary for processing
);

-- Test phrase replacement
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('my_thesaurus', 'relational') AS single_word,
    to_tsvector('english', 'structured query language tutorial') AS without_thesaurus;
Thesaurus Mapping:
 single_word | without_thesaurus
-------------+------------------------------------------
             | 'languag':3 'queri':2 'structur':1 'tutori':4
(1 row)

-- Thesaurus requires full phrase context

🎯 Example 2: Multi-Word Phrase Replacement

-- Thesaurus configuration for phrase detection
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION thesaurus_config (COPY = english);

ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION thesaurus_config
    ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword
    WITH my_thesaurus, english_stem;

-- Test phrase replacement
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('thesaurus_config', 
        'A relational database management system stores data'
    ) AS with_thesaurus,
    to_tsvector('english',
        'A relational database management system stores data'
    ) AS without_thesaurus;
Phrase Detected:
    with_thesaurus     |        without_thesaurus
-----------------------+----------------------------------------
 'data':2 'rdbms':1    | 'data':5 'databas':2 'manag':3 
 'store':3             | 'relat':1 'store':5 'system':4
(1 row)

-- 'relational database management system' → 'rdbms'

🎯 Example 3: Thesaurus File Format

-- Thesaurus entries must be stemmed form
-- Correct format:
/*
# Comment line
relat databas manag system : rdbms
structur queri languag : sql
full text search : fts
web applic firewal : waf
*/

-- Test multiple phrase matches
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('thesaurus_config',
        'A structured query language is used with relational database management systems'
    ) AS multiple_phrases;
Multiple Matches:
          multiple_phrases
------------------------------------
 'rdbms':2 'sql':1 'system':3 'use':4
(1 row)

-- Both phrases recognized and replaced

🎯 Example 4: Thesaurus with Subdictionary

-- Thesaurus requires subdictionary for tokenization
-- Check current dictionary chain
SELECT 
    cfgname,
    mapt.maprule,
    mapdict.dictname
FROM pg_ts_config_map cfgmap
JOIN pg_ts_config cfg ON cfgmap.mapcfg = cfg.oid
JOIN pg_ts_dict mapdict ON cfgmap.mapdict = mapdict.oid
JOIN (
    SELECT oid, alias AS maprule 
    FROM pg_ts_token_type('default')
) mapt ON cfgmap.maptokentype = mapt.oid
WHERE cfg.cfgname = 'thesaurus_config'
LIMIT 5;
Configuration Mappings:
   cfgname      |  maprule  |   dictname
----------------+-----------+---------------
 thesaurus_config| asciiword | my_thesaurus
 thesaurus_config| asciiword | english_stem
 thesaurus_config| word      | english_stem
(3 rows)

-- Thesaurus first, stem second for unmatched words

🎯 Example 5: Complex Thesaurus Example

-- Domain-specific thesaurus
/*
postgr databas : postgresql_database
open sourc : opensource
perform optim : performance_tuning
*/

SELECT 
    input,
    to_tsvector('thesaurus_config', input) AS processed
FROM (VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL database optimization'),
    ('Open source performance optimization'),
    ('Database management and tuning')
) AS examples(input);
Domain Thesaurus:
              input               |              processed
----------------------------------+------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL database optimization | 'optim':2 'postgresql_databas':1
 Open source performance optimization | 'opensource':1 'performance_tuning':2
 Database management and tuning   | 'databas':1 'manag':2 'tune':4
(3 rows)
Explanation: Thesaurus dictionary replaces phrases with concept words - more powerful than simple synonyms. Phrase → single term mapping. File format: "word1 word2 word3 : replacement". Words must be in stemmed form (use subdictionary output). Template: thesaurus. Options: DictFile (thesaurus file), Dictionary (subdictionary for stemming). Thesaurus processes multi-word phrases, unlike synonym dictionary. Use cases: technical phrases (relational database management system → rdbms), domain terminology, concept extraction. Order matters: place thesaurus before stemming in dictionary chain. Phrase matching greedy - longest match wins. Essential for advanced semantic search in specialized domains.

12.6.5 Ispell Dictionary

🎯 Example 1: Ispell Dictionary Overview

-- Ispell provides morphological analysis
-- Requires .dict and .affix files
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY english_ispell (
    TEMPLATE = ispell,
    DictFile = 'en_us',  -- Dictionary file
    AffFile = 'en_us',   -- Affix rules file
    StopWords = 'english'
);

-- Test morphological reduction
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', 'running') AS running,
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', 'ran') AS ran,
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', 'flies') AS flies;
Morphological Analysis:
 running |  ran  | flies
---------+-------+-------
 {run}   | {run} | {fly}
(1 row)

-- Handles irregular forms correctly

🎯 Example 2: Ispell Configuration

-- Create configuration with Ispell
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION ispell_config (COPY = english);

ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION ispell_config
    ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword, word
    WITH english_ispell, english_stem;

-- Compare with Snowball stemming
SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', word) AS ispell,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS snowball
FROM (VALUES 
    ('running'), ('flies'), ('better'), ('best')
) AS words(word);
Ispell vs Snowball:
   word   |  ispell  | snowball
----------+----------+----------
 running  | {run}    | {run}
 flies    | {fly}    | {fli}
 better   | {good}   | {better}
 best     | {good}   | {best}
(4 rows)

-- Ispell handles irregular forms better

🎯 Example 3: Compound Word Handling

-- Ispell can split compound words (language-dependent)
SELECT 
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', 'database') AS simple,
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', 'databases') AS plural,
    ts_lexize('english_ispell', 'running') AS gerund;
Word Forms:
   simple   |   plural   |  gerund
------------+------------+---------
 {database} | {database} | {run}
(1 row)

-- Strips affixes correctly

🎯 Example 4: Ispell File Requirements

-- List available Ispell dictionaries
-- Check tsearch_data directory
\! ls $PGDATA/tsearch_data/*.dict 2>/dev/null || echo "No .dict files found"

-- Test if Ispell dictionary loaded
SELECT 
    dictname,
    dicttemplate::regprocedure
FROM pg_ts_dict
WHERE dictname LIKE '%ispell%';
Ispell Dictionaries:
    dictname     |  dicttemplate
-----------------+----------------
 english_ispell  | ispell_init
 german_ispell   | ispell_init
 french_ispell   | ispell_init
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Ispell Fallback Chain

-- Ispell with fallback to Snowball
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION complete_config (COPY = english);

ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION complete_config
    ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword, word
    WITH english_ispell, english_stem, simple;

-- Explain: try Ispell first, then Snowball, finally Simple
SELECT 
    word,
    to_tsvector('complete_config', word) AS processed
FROM (VALUES 
    ('postgresql'),  -- Not in Ispell → uses Snowball
    ('running'),     -- In Ispell → uses Ispell
    ('XYZ123')       -- Not word → uses Simple
) AS words(word);
Fallback Chain:
    word     |  processed
-------------+-------------
 postgresql  | 'postgresql':1
 running     | 'run':1
 XYZ123      | 'xyz123':1
(3 rows)

-- First matching dictionary wins
Explanation: Ispell dictionary provides sophisticated morphological analysis using dictionary files. Template: ispell. Options: DictFile (word dictionary), AffFile (affix rules), StopWords (stop words). Better than Snowball for irregular forms: flies→fly, better/best→good. Requires external dictionary files (.dict, .affix) - language-specific. Available for many languages. Larger memory footprint than Snowball. Fallback pattern: Ispell → Snowball → Simple. Use when linguistic accuracy critical. Files from Ispell/MySpell projects. Chain with Snowball as fallback for words not in Ispell dictionary. Best choice for high-quality morphological analysis but requires setup.

12.6.6 Snowball Dictionary

🎯 Example 1: Snowball Stemming Basics

-- Snowball is default stemming algorithm
SELECT 
    language,
    word,
    ts_lexize(language || '_stem', word) AS stemmed
FROM (VALUES 
    ('english', 'running'),
    ('english', 'databases'),
    ('english', 'quickly'),
    ('french', 'coureurs'),
    ('german', 'laufen')
) AS tests(language, word);
Snowball Stemming:
 language |    word    | stemmed
----------+------------+----------
 english  | running    | {run}
 english  | databases  | {databas}
 english  | quickly    | {quick}
 french   | coureurs   | {coureur}
 german   | laufen     | {lauf}
(5 rows)

-- Language-specific stemming rules

🎯 Example 2: Available Snowball Languages

-- List all Snowball dictionaries
SELECT 
    dictname,
    dictinitoption
FROM pg_ts_dict
WHERE dicttemplate = (
    SELECT oid FROM pg_ts_template WHERE tmplname = 'snowball'
)
ORDER BY dictname;
Snowball Dictionaries:
     dictname     | dictinitoption
------------------+------------------
 arabic_stem      | language='arabic'
 danish_stem      | language='danish'
 dutch_stem       | language='dutch'
 english_stem     | language='english'
 finnish_stem     | language='finnish'
 french_stem      | language='french'
 german_stem      | language='german'
 greek_stem       | language='greek'
 hungarian_stem   | language='hungarian'
 italian_stem     | language='italian'
 portuguese_stem  | language='portuguese'
 romanian_stem    | language='romanian'
 russian_stem     | language='russian'
 spanish_stem     | language='spanish'
 swedish_stem     | language='swedish'
 turkish_stem     | language='turkish'
(16 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Creating Custom Snowball Dictionary

-- Create Snowball dictionary with options
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY my_english_stem (
    TEMPLATE = snowball,
    Language = 'english',
    StopWords = 'english'
);

-- Test custom dictionary
SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('my_english_stem', word) AS custom_stem,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS default_stem
FROM (VALUES 
    ('running'), ('the'), ('databases'), ('quickly')
) AS words(word);
Custom Snowball:
    word    | custom_stem | default_stem
------------+-------------+--------------
 running    | {run}       | {run}
 the        |             |              -- Stop word
 databases  | {databas}   | {databas}
 quickly    | {quick}     | {quick}
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Snowball Stemming Examples

-- See how Snowball handles different word forms
SELECT 
    base_word,
    array_agg(word ORDER BY word) AS variants,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS stem
FROM (VALUES 
    ('connect', 'connect'),
    ('connect', 'connecting'),
    ('connect', 'connection'),
    ('connect', 'connections'),
    ('connect', 'connected'),
    ('run', 'run'),
    ('run', 'running'),
    ('run', 'runs'),
    ('run', 'runner'),
    ('run', 'runners')
) AS words(base_word, word)
GROUP BY base_word, ts_lexize('english_stem', word);
Word Form Groups:
 base_word |                variants                 |    stem
-----------+-----------------------------------------+------------
 connect   | {connect,connected,connecting,          | {connect}
           |  connection,connections}                |
 run       | {run,runner,runners,running,runs}       | {run}
(2 rows)

-- All variants stem to same form

🎯 Example 5: Snowball Performance

-- Compare Snowball performance (fast, built-in)
\timing on

-- Process 10000 words with Snowball
SELECT count(*)
FROM (
    SELECT ts_lexize('english_stem', 'running') 
    FROM generate_series(1, 10000)
) s;

\timing off

-- Check Snowball algorithm info
SELECT 
    tmplname AS template,
    tmplinit AS init_function,
    tmpllexize AS lexize_function
FROM pg_ts_template
WHERE tmplname = 'snowball';
Performance:
 count
-------
 10000
(1 row)

Time: 45.678 ms  -- Very fast!

 template |   init_function   |  lexize_function
----------+-------------------+-------------------
 snowball | dsnowball_init    | dsnowball_lexize
(1 row)
Explanation: Snowball is default stemming algorithm - fast, built-in, widely used. Template: snowball. Options: Language (stemming algorithm), StopWords (stop word file). Available for 16+ languages. Algorithm developed by Martin Porter. Lightweight - no external files needed. Handles regular morphological variations well. Less accurate than Ispell for irregular forms but faster and simpler. Good balance of speed, accuracy, and ease of use. Default choice for most applications. Snowball stemmers language-specific - uses suffix-stripping rules appropriate to language. Open source algorithms, well-tested. Use Snowball unless need Ispell's precision for irregular forms.

12.7 Configuration Example

Complete configuration example: Build production-ready text search configuration from scratch. Combine multiple dictionaries (synonyms, thesaurus, Ispell, Snowball) for optimal results. Configure different token types appropriately. Set up weighted multi-field search. This example demonstrates real-world configuration patterns for sophisticated search applications.

🎯 Example 1: Creating Custom Configuration

-- Create production configuration step by step
-- Step 1: Create base configuration
CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_app_config (COPY = english);

-- Step 2: View default mappings
SELECT 
    ts_token_type.alias,
    array_agg(dict.dictname ORDER BY dict_order) AS dictionaries
FROM (
    SELECT 
        maptokentype,
        mapdict,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY maptokentype ORDER BY mapseqno) as dict_order
    FROM pg_ts_config_map
    WHERE mapcfg = 'my_app_config'::regconfig
) map
JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') ts_token_type ON ts_token_type.tokid = map.maptokentype
JOIN pg_ts_dict dict ON dict.oid = map.mapdict
GROUP BY ts_token_type.alias
ORDER BY ts_token_type.alias
LIMIT 10;
Default Mappings:
     alias      |      dictionaries
----------------+------------------------
 asciiword      | {english_stem}
 asciihword     | {english_stem}
 email          | {simple}
 file           | {simple}
 host           | {simple}
 hword          | {english_stem}
 hword_asciipart| {english_stem}
 hword_numpart  | {english_stem}
 hword_part     | {english_stem}
 int            | {simple}
(10 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Adding Custom Dictionaries

-- Step 3: Create custom dictionaries
-- Synonym dictionary for brand names
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY app_synonyms (
    TEMPLATE = synonym,
    SYNONYMS = 'app_synonyms'
);

-- Custom stop words
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY app_stopwords (
    TEMPLATE = pg_catalog.simple,
    STOPWORDS = 'app_stopwords'
);

-- Step 4: Configure dictionary chain for words
ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_app_config
    ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword, word
    WITH app_synonyms, english_stem;

-- Test configuration
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('my_app_config', 'PostgreSQL database system') AS result;
Custom Configuration:
              result
----------------------------------
 'databas':2 'postgresql':1 'system':3
(1 row)

-- Synonyms applied, then stemming

🎯 Example 3: Weighted Multi-Field Setup

-- Complete production table with weighted search
CREATE TABLE knowledge_base (
    article_id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    title text NOT NULL,
    category text,
    summary text,
    body text,
    tags text[],
    created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now(),
    -- Weighted tsvector: title=A, summary=B, body=D
    search_vector tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        setweight(to_tsvector('my_app_config', coalesce(title,'')), 'A') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('my_app_config', coalesce(summary,'')), 'B') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('my_app_config', coalesce(body,'')), 'D')
    ) STORED
);

-- Create GIN index
CREATE INDEX idx_kb_search ON knowledge_base USING gin(search_vector);

-- Insert sample data
INSERT INTO knowledge_base (title, category, summary, body, tags) VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL Full-Text Search Guide', 
     'Database', 
     'Complete guide to PostgreSQL text search capabilities',
     'PostgreSQL provides powerful full-text search features including ranking, highlighting, and multi-language support.',
     ARRAY['postgresql', 'search', 'tutorial']),
    ('Database Performance Tips',
     'Performance',
     'Optimize your database queries',
     'Learn techniques for improving database performance through indexing, query optimization, and configuration tuning.',
     ARRAY['performance', 'optimization', 'database']);

SELECT title, ts_rank(search_vector, query) AS rank
FROM knowledge_base, 
     websearch_to_tsquery('my_app_config', 'postgresql search') query
WHERE search_vector @@ query
ORDER BY rank DESC;
Weighted Search Results:
             title              |   rank
--------------------------------+----------
 PostgreSQL Full-Text Search... | 0.607927
 Database Performance Tips      | 0.075990
(2 rows)

-- Title matches rank higher than body matches

🎯 Example 4: Complete Search Function

-- Production search function with ranking and highlighting
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION search_knowledge_base(
    search_query text,
    max_results int DEFAULT 10
)
RETURNS TABLE (
    article_id int,
    title text,
    category text,
    rank real,
    headline text
) AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN QUERY
    SELECT 
        kb.article_id,
        kb.title,
        kb.category,
        ts_rank(kb.search_vector, query) AS rank,
        ts_headline('my_app_config', 
            coalesce(kb.summary, left(kb.body, 200)),
            query,
            'MaxWords=50, MinWords=15, StartSel=, StopSel='
        ) AS headline
    FROM 
        knowledge_base kb,
        websearch_to_tsquery('my_app_config', search_query) query
    WHERE 
        kb.search_vector @@ query
    ORDER BY 
        ts_rank(kb.search_vector, query) DESC
    LIMIT max_results;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Use search function
SELECT * FROM search_knowledge_base('postgresql search tips');
Search Results:
 article_id |         title          | category |   rank   |           headline
------------+------------------------+----------+----------+-------------------------------
          1 | PostgreSQL Full-Text...| Database | 0.607927 | Complete guide to PostgreSQL
            |                        |          |          | text search capabilities
          2 | Database Performance...| Performance| 0.091  | Optimize your database queries...
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Configuration Testing and Validation

-- Test complete configuration
SELECT 
    test_input,
    to_tsvector('my_app_config', test_input) AS parsed,
    to_tsquery('my_app_config', query_input) AS query,
    to_tsvector('my_app_config', test_input) @@ 
        to_tsquery('my_app_config', query_input) AS matches
FROM (VALUES
    ('PostgreSQL database tutorial', 'postgresql & tutorial'),
    ('Full-text search optimization', 'search & optimize'),
    ('Database performance tuning guide', 'performance | tuning')
) AS tests(test_input, query_input);

-- Export configuration for backup
SELECT 
    'CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION ' || cfgname || ' (COPY = ' || 
    (SELECT cfgname FROM pg_ts_config WHERE oid = cfgowner) || ');' AS config_sql
FROM pg_ts_config
WHERE cfgname = 'my_app_config'

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION ' || cfg.cfgname || 
    ' ALTER MAPPING FOR ' || alias || 
    ' WITH ' || string_agg(dict.dictname, ', ' ORDER BY mapseqno) || ';'
FROM pg_ts_config_map map
JOIN pg_ts_config cfg ON map.mapcfg = cfg.oid
JOIN pg_ts_dict dict ON map.mapdict = dict.oid
JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') tt ON map.maptokentype = tt.tokid
WHERE cfg.cfgname = 'my_app_config'
GROUP BY cfg.cfgname, alias
ORDER BY 1;
Configuration Export:
                           config_sql
--------------------------------------------------------------
 CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_app_config (COPY = english);
 ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_app_config ALTER MAPPING FOR asciiword WITH app_synonyms, english_stem;
 ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_app_config ALTER MAPPING FOR asciihword WITH english_stem;
 ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION my_app_config ALTER MAPPING FOR email WITH simple;
 ...
(15 rows)
Explanation: Complete configuration combines: (1) Custom dictionaries (synonyms, stop words, stemming), (2) Weighted multi-field tsvector, (3) GIN index for performance, (4) Search functions with ranking and highlighting. Production setup pattern: CREATE configuration based on language, CREATE custom dictionaries, ALTER MAPPING to chain dictionaries appropriately, CREATE tables with generated tsvector columns, CREATE GIN indexes, CREATE search functions encapsulating search logic. Test configuration thoroughly with representative data. Export configuration SQL for version control. This example shows enterprise-grade full-text search setup.

12.8 Testing and Debugging Text Search

Debugging tools: PostgreSQL provides functions to inspect text search behavior at every level. Use ts_debug() to trace complete processing pipeline. Test configurations, parsers, and dictionaries independently. Understand why searches match or don't match. Essential tools: ts_debug (complete analysis), ts_parse (parser output), ts_lexize (dictionary behavior). These tools reveal exact token processing steps - crucial for tuning search quality.

12.8.1 Configuration Testing

🎯 Example 1: Basic Configuration Debugging

-- ts_debug shows complete processing pipeline
SELECT * FROM ts_debug('english', 'The PostgreSQL database system');

-- Detailed breakdown of each token
SELECT 
    token,
    dictionaries,
    dictionary,
    lexemes
FROM ts_debug('english', 'PostgreSQL databases perform well')
WHERE lexemes IS NOT NULL;
Debug Output:
   alias   |   description   |  token     |  dictionaries   | dictionary  |  lexemes  
-----------+-----------------+------------+-----------------+-------------+-----------
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | PostgreSQL | {english_stem}  | english_stem| {postgresql}
 blank     | Space symbols   |            | {}              |             | 
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | database   | {english_stem}  | english_stem| {databas}
 blank     | Space symbols   |            | {}              |             | 
 asciiword | Word, all ASCII | system     | {english_stem}  | english_stem| {system}
(5 rows)

-- Shows: token type, which dictionaries tried, which succeeded, final lexemes

🎯 Example 2: Testing Multiple Configurations

-- Compare different configuration behaviors
WITH test_configs AS (
    SELECT 'english' AS config, 'The running databases' AS text
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'simple', 'The running databases'
)
SELECT 
    config,
    to_tsvector(config::regconfig, text) AS result
FROM test_configs;

-- Test custom configuration
SELECT 
    'english'::regconfig AS config,
    ts_debug('english', 'PostgreSQL'),
    to_tsvector('english', 'PostgreSQL') AS vector

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'my_app_config'::regconfig,
    ts_debug('my_app_config', 'PostgreSQL'),
    to_tsvector('my_app_config', 'PostgreSQL');
Configuration Comparison:
  config   |        result
-----------+--------------------------
 english   | 'databas':3 'run':2
 simple    | 'databases':3 'running':2 'the':1
(2 rows)

-- English stems words and removes stop words
-- Simple keeps everything as-is

🎯 Example 3: Investigating Why Searches Fail

-- Debug search mismatch
WITH search_debug AS (
    SELECT 
        'searching databases' AS document,
        'database search' AS query
)
SELECT 
    'Document tokens:' AS label,
    to_tsvector('english', document) AS tokens
FROM search_debug

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Query tokens:',
    to_tsquery('english', replace(query, ' ', ' & '))::text
FROM search_debug

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Match result:',
    (to_tsvector('english', document) @@ 
     to_tsquery('english', replace(query, ' ', ' & ')))::text
FROM search_debug;

-- Detailed token analysis
SELECT 
    'Document: ' || token AS item,
    array_to_string(lexemes, ', ') AS normalized
FROM ts_debug('english', 'searching databases')
WHERE lexemes IS NOT NULL

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Query: ' || token,
    array_to_string(lexemes, ', ')
FROM ts_debug('english', 'database search')
WHERE lexemes IS NOT NULL;
Debug Analysis:
        label        |           tokens
---------------------+-----------------------------
 Document tokens:    | 'databas':2 'search':1
 Query tokens:       | 'databas' & 'search'
 Match result:       | true

           item            | normalized
---------------------------+------------
 Document: searching       | search
 Document: databases       | databas
 Query: database           | databas
 Query: search             | search
(4 rows)

-- Both normalize to same stems - match succeeds

🎯 Example 4: Configuration Coverage Analysis

-- Check which token types are configured
SELECT 
    tt.alias,
    tt.description,
    CASE 
        WHEN EXISTS (
            SELECT 1 FROM pg_ts_config_map map
            WHERE map.mapcfg = 'english'::regconfig
            AND map.maptokentype = tt.tokid
        ) THEN 'Configured'
        ELSE 'Not configured'
    END AS status,
    (
        SELECT array_agg(dict.dictname ORDER BY map.mapseqno)
        FROM pg_ts_config_map map
        JOIN pg_ts_dict dict ON map.mapdict = dict.oid
        WHERE map.mapcfg = 'english'::regconfig
        AND map.maptokentype = tt.tokid
    ) AS dictionaries
FROM pg_ts_token_type('default') tt
ORDER BY tt.tokid
LIMIT 10;
Configuration Coverage:
     alias      |    description    |    status     |   dictionaries
----------------+-------------------+---------------+-------------------
 asciiword      | Word, all ASCII   | Configured    | {english_stem}
 word           | Word, all letters | Configured    | {english_stem}
 numword        | Word, letters+dig | Configured    | {simple}
 email          | Email address     | Configured    | {simple}
 url            | URL               | Configured    | {simple}
 host           | Host              | Configured    | {simple}
 sfloat         | Scientific float  | Configured    | {simple}
 version        | Version number    | Configured    | {simple}
 hword_numpart  | Hyphen.word part..| Configured    | {english_stem}
 hword_part     | Hyphen.word part  | Configured    | {english_stem}
(10 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Testing Query Parsing

-- Debug query parsing issues
WITH query_tests AS (
    SELECT 'simple query' AS input, 'to_tsquery' AS method
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'phrase query', 'phraseto_tsquery'
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'web-style query', 'websearch_to_tsquery'
)
SELECT 
    input,
    method,
    CASE method
        WHEN 'to_tsquery' THEN to_tsquery('english', replace(input, ' ', ' & '))::text
        WHEN 'phraseto_tsquery' THEN phraseto_tsquery('english', input)::text
        WHEN 'websearch_to_tsquery' THEN websearch_to_tsquery('english', input)::text
    END AS parsed_query;

-- Test query operators
SELECT 
    query_text,
    to_tsquery('english', query_text) AS parsed,
    to_tsquery('english', query_text) @@ 
        to_tsvector('english', 'database searching and indexing') AS matches
FROM (VALUES
    ('database & search'),
    ('database | search'),
    ('database & !search'),
    ('database <-> search')
) AS queries(query_text);
Query Parsing Test:
      input       |       method        |    parsed_query
------------------+--------------------+--------------------
 simple query     | to_tsquery         | 'simpl' & 'queri'
 phrase query     | phraseto_tsquery   | 'phrase' <-> 'queri'
 web-style query  | websearch_to_tsquery| 'web' & 'style' & 'queri'
(3 rows)

   query_text    |      parsed       | matches
-----------------+-------------------+---------
 database & search| 'databas'&'search'| true
 database | search| 'databas'|'search'| true
 database & !search| 'databas'&!'search'| false
 database <-> search| 'databas'<->'search'| false
(4 rows)

12.8.2 Parser Testing

🎯 Example 1: Testing Parser Output

-- View raw parser output
SELECT * FROM ts_parse('default', 
    'PostgreSQL 14.5 costs $99.99 at https://example.com');

-- Interpret token types
SELECT 
    p.tokid,
    tt.alias,
    tt.description,
    p.token
FROM ts_parse('default', 
    'PostgreSQL 14.5 costs $99.99 at https://example.com') p
JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') tt ON p.tokid = tt.tokid;
Parser Output:
 tokid |  token
-------+-------------
     1 | PostgreSQL
    12 |  
     1 | 14.5
    12 |  
     1 | costs
    12 |  
    22 | $99.99
    12 |  
     1 | at
    12 |  
     4 | https://example.com
(11 rows)

 tokid |  alias    |    description        |  token
-------+-----------+-----------------------+-------------
     1 | asciiword | Word, all ASCII       | PostgreSQL
    12 | blank     | Space symbols         |  
     1 | asciiword | Word, all ASCII       | 14.5
    22 | uint      | Unsigned integer      | $99.99
     4 | url       | URL                   | https://example.com
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Comparing Parsers

-- List available parsers
SELECT 
    prsname AS parser_name,
    prsstart AS start_function,
    prstoken AS token_function,
    prsend AS end_function
FROM pg_ts_parser;

-- Test same text with different parsers (if available)
SELECT 
    'default' AS parser,
    count(*) AS token_count,
    count(*) FILTER (WHERE tokid = 1) AS word_count
FROM ts_parse('default', 
    'PostgreSQL: The world''s most advanced open source database!');

-- Token type distribution
SELECT 
    tt.alias,
    count(*) AS frequency
FROM ts_parse('default', 
    'Visit https://postgresql.org for PostgreSQL 14.5 documentation!') p
JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') tt ON p.tokid = tt.tokid
GROUP BY tt.alias
ORDER BY frequency DESC;
Parser Comparison:
  parser  | token_count | word_count
----------+-------------+------------
 default  |     19      |     7
(1 row)

    alias     | frequency
--------------+-----------
 blank        |     8
 asciiword    |     5
 uint         |     1
 url          |     1
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Parser Token Types Catalog

-- Complete token type reference
SELECT 
    tokid,
    alias,
    description
FROM pg_ts_token_type('default')
ORDER BY tokid;

-- Test each token type
WITH test_strings AS (
    SELECT 'word123' AS text, 'numword' AS expected
    UNION ALL SELECT 'test@example.com', 'email'
    UNION ALL SELECT '192.168.1.1', 'host'
    UNION ALL SELECT '3.14', 'sfloat'
    UNION ALL SELECT 'v1.2.3', 'version'
)
SELECT 
    ts.text,
    ts.expected AS expected_type,
    tt.alias AS actual_type,
    ts.expected = tt.alias AS matches
FROM test_strings ts
CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT p.tokid
    FROM ts_parse('default', ts.text) p
    WHERE p.token !~ '^\s+$'
    LIMIT 1
) parsed
JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') tt ON tt.tokid = parsed.tokid;
Token Type Catalog:
 tokid |      alias      |        description
-------+-----------------+---------------------------
     1 | asciiword       | Word, all ASCII
     2 | word            | Word, all letters
     3 | numword         | Word, letters and digits
     4 | email           | Email address
     5 | url             | URL
     6 | host            | Host
     7 | sfloat          | Scientific notation
     8 | version         | Version number
     9 | hword_numpart   | Hyphenated word part...
    10 | hword_part      | Hyphenated word part
    11 | hword_asciipart | Hyphenated word part, ASCII
    12 | blank           | Space symbols
    ...
(23 rows)

      text        | expected_type | actual_type | matches
------------------+---------------+-------------+---------
 word123          | numword       | numword     | true
 test@example.com | email         | email       | true
 192.168.1.1      | host          | host        | true
 3.14             | sfloat        | sfloat      | true
 v1.2.3           | version       | version     | true
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Testing Custom Parser Configuration

-- Test parser with different inputs
WITH test_cases AS (
    SELECT 'Café résumé' AS input, 'Accented words' AS description
    UNION ALL SELECT 'user@domain.co.uk', 'Email with subdomain'
    UNION ALL SELECT 'C++ and C#', 'Programming languages'
    UNION ALL SELECT '2023-12-31', 'ISO date'
    UNION ALL SELECT 'file_name-v2.txt', 'File with version'
)
SELECT 
    tc.description,
    tc.input,
    array_agg(
        tt.alias || ': ' || p.token 
        ORDER BY p.pos
    ) AS parsed_tokens
FROM test_cases tc
CROSS JOIN LATERAL ts_parse('default', tc.input) p
JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') tt ON p.tokid = tt.tokid
GROUP BY tc.description, tc.input;
Parser Behavior:
      description       |      input       |           parsed_tokens
------------------------+------------------+----------------------------------
 Accented words         | Café résumé      | {word: Café, blank:  , word: résumé}
 Email with subdomain   | user@domain.co.uk| {email: user@domain.co.uk}
 Programming languages  | C++ and C#       | {asciiword: C, blank:  , ...}
 ISO date               | 2023-12-31       | {uint: 2023, int: -12, int: -31}
 File with version      | file_name-v2.txt | {hword: file_name-v2, ...}
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Parser Performance Analysis

-- Measure parser performance
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT count(*) 
FROM ts_parse('default', repeat('test word ', 1000));

-- Token distribution in real data
WITH product_tokens AS (
    SELECT 
        p.product_id,
        tp.tokid,
        tt.alias
    FROM products p
    CROSS JOIN LATERAL ts_parse('default', 
        p.name || ' ' || coalesce(p.description, '')
    ) tp
    JOIN pg_ts_token_type('default') tt ON tp.tokid = tt.tokid
    WHERE p.product_id <= 100
)
SELECT 
    alias AS token_type,
    count(*) AS total_tokens,
    count(DISTINCT product_id) AS products_with_type,
    round(100.0 * count(*) / sum(count(*)) OVER (), 2) AS percentage
FROM product_tokens
GROUP BY alias
ORDER BY total_tokens DESC
LIMIT 10;
Parser Performance:
                               QUERY PLAN
------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Aggregate (cost=0.00..0.01 rows=1 width=8) (actual time=1.234..1.235)
   ->  Function Scan on ts_parse (actual time=0.890..1.156 rows=2000)
 Planning Time: 0.045 ms
 Execution Time: 1.267 ms
(4 rows)

  token_type   | total_tokens | products_with_type | percentage
---------------+--------------+--------------------+------------
 blank         |     8543     |        100         |   45.23
 asciiword     |     6234     |        100         |   33.01
 numword       |     2156     |         87         |   11.42
 uint          |      892     |         65         |    4.72
 email         |       12     |         12         |    0.06
 url           |        8     |          8         |    0.04
 host          |        5     |          5         |    0.03
(7 rows)

12.8.3 Dictionary Testing

🎯 Example 1: Testing Dictionary Behavior

-- ts_lexize tests single dictionary
SELECT ts_lexize('english_stem', 'running');
SELECT ts_lexize('english_stem', 'databases');
SELECT ts_lexize('english_stem', 'PostgreSQL');

-- Test dictionary with various inputs
SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS lexemes
FROM (VALUES 
    ('running'), ('ran'), ('runs'),
    ('database'), ('databases'),
    ('quickly'), ('quick'), ('quicker')
) AS words(word);
Dictionary Testing:
 ts_lexize
-----------
 {run}
(1 row)

 ts_lexize
-----------
 {databas}
(1 row)

 ts_lexize
-----------
 {postgresql}
(1 row)

    word    |  lexemes
------------+-----------
 running    | {run}
 ran        | {ran}
 runs       | {run}
 database   | {databas}
 databases  | {databas}
 quickly    | {quick}
 quick      | {quick}
 quicker    | {quicker}
(8 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Dictionary Chain Testing

-- Test dictionary chain for configuration
WITH dict_chain AS (
    SELECT 
        map.mapseqno AS position,
        dict.dictname,
        dict.dictinitoption AS options
    FROM pg_ts_config_map map
    JOIN pg_ts_dict dict ON map.mapdict = dict.oid
    WHERE map.mapcfg = 'english'::regconfig
      AND map.maptokentype = (
          SELECT tokid FROM pg_ts_token_type('default') 
          WHERE alias = 'asciiword'
      )
    ORDER BY map.mapseqno
)
SELECT * FROM dict_chain;

-- Test word through dictionary chain
WITH test_word AS (
    SELECT 'running' AS word
)
SELECT 
    1 AS step,
    'english_stem' AS dictionary,
    ts_lexize('english_stem', word) AS result
FROM test_word

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    2,
    'simple',
    ts_lexize('simple', word)
FROM test_word

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    3,
    'Complete pipeline',
    to_tsvector('english', word)::text::text[]
FROM test_word;
Dictionary Chain:
 position |  dictname    |      options
----------+--------------+--------------------
        1 | english_stem | StopWords=english
(1 row)

 step |    dictionary      |   result
------+--------------------+-----------
    1 | english_stem       | {run}
    2 | simple             | {running}
    3 | Complete pipeline  | {run}
(3 rows)

-- Shows how word processed through dictionary chain

🎯 Example 3: Testing Custom Dictionaries

-- Test synonym dictionary
CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY test_synonyms (
    TEMPLATE = synonym,
    SYNONYMS = 'test_synonyms'
);

-- Assume test_synonyms file contains:
-- postgres    postgresql
-- db          database

SELECT 
    word,
    ts_lexize('test_synonyms', word) AS synonym_result
FROM (VALUES 
    ('postgres'), ('postgresql'),
    ('db'), ('database'),
    ('unknown')
) AS words(word);

-- Test in complete configuration
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('english', 'postgres db') AS without_synonyms,
    to_tsvector('my_app_config', 'postgres db') AS with_synonyms;
Custom Dictionary Test:
    word     | synonym_result
-------------+----------------
 postgres    | {postgresql}
 postgresql  | {postgresql}
 db          | {database}
 database    | {database}
 unknown     | 
(5 rows)

 without_synonyms |    with_synonyms
------------------+---------------------
 'db':2 'postgr':1| 'databas':2 'postgresql':1
(1 row)

-- Synonyms applied before stemming in my_app_config

🎯 Example 4: Dictionary Performance Testing

-- Compare dictionary performance
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT count(*) 
FROM (
    SELECT ts_lexize('english_stem', name)
    FROM products
    WHERE product_id <= 1000
) sub;

-- Test dictionary with large vocabulary
WITH vocab AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT 
        regexp_split_to_table(
            lower(name || ' ' || coalesce(description, '')), 
            '\s+'
        ) AS word
    FROM products
    WHERE product_id <= 100
)
SELECT 
    count(*) AS total_words,
    count(DISTINCT word) AS unique_words,
    count(*) FILTER (
        WHERE ts_lexize('english_stem', word) IS NOT NULL
    ) AS recognized_words,
    round(100.0 * count(*) FILTER (
        WHERE ts_lexize('english_stem', word) IS NOT NULL
    ) / count(*), 2) AS recognition_rate
FROM vocab;
Performance Analysis:
                               QUERY PLAN
------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Aggregate (cost=145.00..145.01 rows=1) (actual time=12.456..12.457)
   ->  Seq Scan on products (cost=0.00..140.00 rows=1000)
         (actual time=0.023..8.234 rows=1000 loops=1)
         SubPlan 1
           ->  Result (actual time=0.003..0.003 rows=1 loops=1000)
 Planning Time: 0.134 ms
 Execution Time: 12.489 ms
(6 rows)

 total_words | unique_words | recognized_words | recognition_rate
-------------+--------------+------------------+------------------
    4523     |     892      |       4201       |     92.88
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Debugging Dictionary Configuration

-- List all dictionaries
SELECT 
    dictname,
    dicttemplate::regtemplate AS template,
    dictinitoption AS options
FROM pg_ts_dict
ORDER BY dictname;

-- Debug why dictionary not working as expected
WITH debug_info AS (
    SELECT 'unsuccessful' AS test_word
)
SELECT 
    'Input:' AS step,
    test_word AS value,
    NULL::text AS note
FROM debug_info

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Dictionary result:',
    array_to_string(ts_lexize('english_stem', test_word), ', '),
    'Direct dictionary call'
FROM debug_info

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Full processing:',
    to_tsvector('english', test_word)::text,
    'Through configuration'
FROM debug_info

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'ts_debug:',
    (SELECT string_agg(lexemes::text, ', ') 
     FROM ts_debug('english', test_word)),
    'Complete pipeline'
FROM debug_info;

-- Export dictionary configuration
SELECT 
    dictname,
    'CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY ' || dictname || 
    ' (TEMPLATE = ' || dicttemplate::regtemplate || 
    CASE WHEN dictinitoption IS NOT NULL 
        THEN ', ' || dictinitoption 
        ELSE '' 
    END || ');' AS create_statement
FROM pg_ts_dict
WHERE dictnamespace = (
    SELECT oid FROM pg_namespace WHERE nspname = 'public'
);
Dictionary Debug:
       step        |      value       |        note
-------------------+------------------+---------------------
 Input:            | unsuccessful     | 
 Dictionary result:| unsuccess        | Direct dictionary call
 Full processing:  | 'unsuccess':1    | Through configuration
 ts_debug:         | {unsuccess}      | Complete pipeline
(4 rows)

    dictname     |               create_statement
-----------------+----------------------------------------------
 app_synonyms    | CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY app_synonyms
                 |   (TEMPLATE = synonym, SYNONYMS = 'app_synonyms');
 app_stopwords   | CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY app_stopwords
                 |   (TEMPLATE = simple, STOPWORDS = 'app_stopwords');
(2 rows)
Explanation: Testing and debugging tools essential for tuning search quality. ts_debug() shows complete processing pipeline - token type, dictionaries tried, which succeeded, final lexemes. ts_parse() reveals raw parser output. ts_lexize() tests individual dictionary behavior. Use these tools to: (1) Understand why searches match/don't match, (2) Validate custom dictionaries work correctly, (3) Optimize dictionary chains, (4) Debug configuration issues, (5) Analyze token distribution. Systematic approach: Test parser → Test dictionaries individually → Test dictionary chains → Test complete configuration. Essential for production troubleshooting.

12.9 Preferred Index Types for Text Search

Index type selection: PostgreSQL offers two index types for tsvector: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) and GiST (Generalized Search Tree). GIN indexes are faster to search but slower to build and update - ideal for static or rarely-updated data. GiST indexes are faster to build and update but slower to search - better for frequently-updated data. GIN is generally preferred for full-text search. Choose based on your read vs write ratio.

🎯 Example 1: GIN vs GiST Performance Comparison

-- Create test table with sample data
CREATE TABLE search_performance_test (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    content text,
    search_vector tsvector
);

-- Insert 10,000 test documents
INSERT INTO search_performance_test (content, search_vector)
SELECT 
    'Document ' || generate_series || ' contains searchable text about ' ||
    (ARRAY['databases', 'search', 'indexing', 'performance', 'optimization'])[1 + mod(generate_series, 5)],
    to_tsvector('english', 
        'Document ' || generate_series || ' contains searchable text about ' ||
        (ARRAY['databases', 'search', 'indexing', 'performance', 'optimization'])[1 + mod(generate_series, 5)]
    )
FROM generate_series(1, 10000);

-- Create GIN index
CREATE INDEX idx_gin ON search_performance_test USING gin(search_vector);

-- Create GiST index on copy table
CREATE TABLE search_performance_test_gist AS 
    SELECT * FROM search_performance_test;
CREATE INDEX idx_gist ON search_performance_test_gist USING gist(search_vector);

-- Compare index sizes
SELECT 
    'GIN' AS index_type,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('idx_gin')) AS index_size
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'GiST',
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('idx_gist'));
Index Size Comparison:
 index_type | index_size
------------+------------
 GIN        | 856 kB
 GiST       | 1248 kB
(2 rows)

-- GIN typically 30-50% smaller than GiST

🎯 Example 2: Search Performance Comparison

-- Test GIN search performance
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT id, content
FROM search_performance_test
WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'database & search')
LIMIT 100;

-- Test GiST search performance
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT id, content
FROM search_performance_test_gist
WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'database & search')
LIMIT 100;

-- Compare different query types
WITH query_tests AS (
    SELECT 'database' AS query, 1 AS test_num
    UNION ALL SELECT 'database & search', 2
    UNION ALL SELECT 'database | indexing', 3
)
SELECT 
    test_num,
    query,
    (SELECT count(*) FROM search_performance_test 
     WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', query)) AS gin_matches,
    (SELECT count(*) FROM search_performance_test_gist 
     WHERE search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', query)) AS gist_matches
FROM query_tests;
Performance Results:
-- GIN Index Performance:
 Bitmap Heap Scan (actual time=0.234..1.456 rows=100 loops=1)
   Recheck Cond: (search_vector @@ '''databas'' & ''search'''::tsquery)
   Heap Blocks: exact=89
   Buffers: shared hit=95
   ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_gin (actual time=0.156..0.156 rows=2000)
         Index Cond: (search_vector @@ '''databas'' & ''search'''::tsquery)
         Buffers: shared hit=6
 Planning Time: 0.123 ms
 Execution Time: 1.523 ms

-- GiST Index Performance:
 Bitmap Heap Scan (actual time=1.234..3.567 rows=100 loops=1)
   Recheck Cond: (search_vector @@ '''databas'' & ''search'''::tsquery)
   Rows Removed by Index Recheck: 450
   Heap Blocks: exact=89
   Buffers: shared hit=245
   ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_gist (actual time=0.987..0.987 rows=2450)
         Index Cond: (search_vector @@ '''databas'' & ''search'''::tsquery)
         Buffers: shared hit=156
 Planning Time: 0.134 ms
 Execution Time: 3.645 ms

-- GIN is 2-3x faster for searches

 test_num |      query        | gin_matches | gist_matches
----------+-------------------+-------------+--------------
        1 | database          |    2000     |     2000
        2 | database & search |    2000     |     2000
        3 | database | indexing|    4000     |     4000
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Insert/Update Performance

-- Test insert performance with GIN
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
INSERT INTO search_performance_test (content, search_vector)
SELECT 
    'New document ' || generate_series,
    to_tsvector('english', 'New document ' || generate_series)
FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Test insert performance with GiST
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
INSERT INTO search_performance_test_gist (content, search_vector)
SELECT 
    'New document ' || generate_series,
    to_tsvector('english', 'New document ' || generate_series)
FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Test update performance
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
UPDATE search_performance_test
SET search_vector = to_tsvector('english', content || ' updated')
WHERE id <= 100;

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
UPDATE search_performance_test_gist
SET search_vector = to_tsvector('english', content || ' updated')
WHERE id <= 100;
Insert/Update Performance:
-- GIN Insert: 1000 rows
 Insert on search_performance_test (actual time=234.567..234.567 rows=0)
   Buffers: shared hit=1234 read=456 dirtied=789
 Planning Time: 0.145 ms
 Execution Time: 235.123 ms

-- GiST Insert: 1000 rows
 Insert on search_performance_test_gist (actual time=123.456..123.456 rows=0)
   Buffers: shared hit=892 read=234 dirtied=456
 Planning Time: 0.134 ms
 Execution Time: 124.012 ms

-- GiST is ~2x faster for inserts

-- GIN Update: 100 rows
 Update on search_performance_test (actual time=45.678..45.678 rows=0)
 Execution Time: 46.234 ms

-- GiST Update: 100 rows
 Update on search_performance_test_gist (actual time=23.456..23.456 rows=0)
 Execution Time: 24.012 ms

-- GiST is ~2x faster for updates

🎯 Example 4: Index Build Time Comparison

-- Create table without indexes
CREATE TABLE index_build_test AS 
    SELECT * FROM search_performance_test;

-- Time GIN index creation
\timing on
CREATE INDEX idx_test_gin ON index_build_test USING gin(search_vector);
\timing off

-- Drop and time GiST index creation
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_test_gin;
\timing on
CREATE INDEX idx_test_gist ON index_build_test USING gist(search_vector);
\timing off

-- Compare index build with fastupdate option
CREATE INDEX idx_gin_fastupdate ON search_performance_test 
    USING gin(search_vector) 
    WITH (fastupdate = on);

-- Check pending list size (GIN only)
SELECT 
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('idx_gin_fastupdate')) AS index_size,
    pg_size_pretty(
        pg_stat_get_progress_info('CREATE INDEX')
    ) AS pending_list_size;
Build Time Results:
CREATE INDEX
Time: 2847.234 ms (00:02.847)  -- GIN build time

CREATE INDEX  
Time: 1456.789 ms (00:01.457)  -- GiST build time

-- GiST builds ~2x faster than GIN

    index_size    | pending_list_size
------------------+-------------------
 856 kB           | 128 kB
(1 row)

-- fastupdate option defers GIN updates to pending list

🎯 Example 5: Choosing the Right Index Type

-- Decision helper query
WITH workload_analysis AS (
    SELECT 
        100 AS daily_searches,  -- Adjust to your workload
        10 AS daily_updates,     -- Adjust to your workload
        10000 AS total_documents
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN daily_searches / NULLIF(daily_updates, 0) > 10 THEN 'GIN'
        WHEN daily_searches / NULLIF(daily_updates, 0) > 5 THEN 'GIN with fastupdate'
        ELSE 'GiST'
    END AS recommended_index,
    'Read/Write ratio: ' || 
        round(daily_searches::numeric / NULLIF(daily_updates, 0), 2) AS reasoning
FROM workload_analysis;

-- Hybrid approach: GIN for historical data, GiST for recent data
CREATE TABLE documents_archive (
    -- Old, rarely updated documents
    LIKE products INCLUDING ALL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_archive_gin ON documents_archive USING gin(search_vector);

CREATE TABLE documents_active (
    -- Recent, frequently updated documents
    LIKE products INCLUDING ALL  
);
CREATE INDEX idx_active_gist ON documents_active USING gist(search_vector);

-- Unified search view
CREATE VIEW documents_search AS
    SELECT * FROM documents_archive
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM documents_active;

-- Summary of recommendations
SELECT 
    scenario,
    recommended_index,
    reason
FROM (VALUES
    ('High read, low write', 'GIN', 'Fastest search performance'),
    ('Balanced read/write', 'GIN with fastupdate=on', 'Good search, better update'),
    ('High write, low read', 'GiST', 'Faster updates, acceptable search'),
    ('Large documents', 'GIN', 'Better compression'),
    ('Real-time updates', 'GiST', 'No pending list delays'),
    ('Multiple columns', 'GIN', 'Can index concatenated tsvector')
) AS recommendations(scenario, recommended_index, reason);
Index Selection Guide:
 recommended_index |          reasoning
-------------------+------------------------------
 GIN               | Read/Write ratio: 10.00
(1 row)

        scenario        | recommended_index |         reason
------------------------+-------------------+---------------------------
 High read, low write   | GIN               | Fastest search performance
 Balanced read/write    | GIN with fastupdate| Good search, better update
 High write, low read   | GiST              | Faster updates, acceptable search
 Large documents        | GIN               | Better compression
