 Real-time updates      | GiST              | No pending list delays
 Multiple columns       | GIN               | Can index concatenated tsvector
(6 rows)
Explanation: Index type selection critical for performance. GIN: 2-3x faster searches, smaller size, but 2x slower updates - best for read-heavy workloads (blogs, documentation, archives). GiST: 2x faster updates, faster builds, but slower searches - best for write-heavy workloads (user-generated content, chat, logs). GIN with fastupdate=on: Compromise - defers index updates to pending list, better update performance than standard GIN, periodic maintenance needed to flush pending list. Hybrid approach: Use GIN for historical/stable data, GiST for recent/active data. Most applications should use GIN - search performance usually more important than update performance. Use GiST only if updates are bottleneck.

12.10 psql Support

psql commands for text search: The psql interactive terminal includes specialized commands for inspecting text search objects. The \dF family of commands lists configurations, parsers, dictionaries, and templates. These commands are invaluable for exploring available text search components and understanding system configuration. Use them for discovery, documentation, and troubleshooting.

🎯 Example 1: Listing Text Search Configurations

-- List all text search configurations
\dF

-- Show detailed configuration info
\dF+

-- List specific configuration
\dF english

-- Show configuration with dictionary details
\dF+ my_app_config
\dF Output:
-- \dF
         List of text search configurations
   Schema   |    Name    |          Description
------------+------------+-------------------------------
 pg_catalog | arabic     | configuration for Arabic
 pg_catalog | danish     | configuration for Danish
 pg_catalog | dutch      | configuration for Dutch
 pg_catalog | english    | configuration for English
 pg_catalog | finnish    | configuration for Finnish
 pg_catalog | french     | configuration for French
 pg_catalog | german     | configuration for German
 pg_catalog | hungarian  | configuration for Hungarian
 public     | my_app_config | custom application config
 ...
(20 rows)

-- \dF+ my_app_config
Text search configuration "public.my_app_config"
Parser: "pg_catalog.default"

      Token      |         Dictionaries
-----------------+------------------------------
 asciiword       | app_synonyms, english_stem
 asciihword      | english_stem
 email           | simple
 file            | simple
 float           | simple
 host            | simple
 hword           | english_stem
 ...
(23 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Listing Parsers

-- List all text search parsers
\dFp

-- Show detailed parser info
\dFp+

-- Specific parser details
\dFp default
\dFp Output:
-- \dFp
        List of text search parsers
   Schema   |  Name   |     Description
------------+---------+---------------------
 pg_catalog | default | default word parser
(1 row)

-- \dFp+ default
Text search parser "pg_catalog.default"
     Method      |    Function    |         Description
-----------------+----------------+-----------------------------
 Start           | prsd_start     | initializes parser
 Gettoken        | prsd_nexttoken | returns next token
 End             | prsd_end       | finishes parsing
 Headline        | prsd_headline  | generates headline
 Lextypes        | prsd_lextype   | returns token type list
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Listing Dictionaries

-- List all dictionaries
\dFd

-- Show detailed dictionary info
\dFd+

-- List specific dictionary
\dFd english_stem

-- Show custom dictionaries only
\dFd public.*
\dFd Output:
-- \dFd
                         List of text search dictionaries
   Schema   |      Name       |                     Description
------------+-----------------+-----------------------------------------------------
 pg_catalog | arabic_stem     | snowball stemmer for arabic
 pg_catalog | danish_stem     | snowball stemmer for danish
 pg_catalog | dutch_stem      | snowball stemmer for dutch
 pg_catalog | english_stem    | snowball stemmer for english
 pg_catalog | finnish_stem    | snowball stemmer for finnish
 pg_catalog | french_stem     | snowball stemmer for french
 pg_catalog | german_stem     | snowball stemmer for german
 pg_catalog | simple          | simple dictionary: just lower case and check for stopword
 public     | app_synonyms    | custom synonym dictionary
 public     | app_stopwords   | custom stop word dictionary
 ...
(30 rows)

-- \dFd+ english_stem
Text search dictionary "pg_catalog.english_stem"
 Template: pg_catalog.snowball
 Language: english
 StopWords: english

🎯 Example 4: Listing Templates

-- List all dictionary templates
\dFt

-- Show detailed template info
\dFt+

-- Specific template
\dFt snowball

-- Templates are used to create dictionaries
\dFt+ synonym
\dFt Output:
-- \dFt
                      List of text search templates
   Schema   |   Name    |                    Description
------------+-----------+---------------------------------------------------
 pg_catalog | ispell    | ispell dictionary
 pg_catalog | simple    | simple dictionary: just lower case and check stopword
 pg_catalog | snowball  | snowball stemmer
 pg_catalog | synonym   | synonym dictionary: replace word by its synonym
 pg_catalog | thesaurus | thesaurus dictionary: phrase by phrase substitution
(5 rows)

-- \dFt+ snowball
    Text search template "pg_catalog.snowball"
    Init    : dsnowball_init
    Lexize  : dsnowball_lexize

-- \dFt+ synonym
    Text search template "pg_catalog.synonym"
    Init    : dsynonym_init
    Lexize  : dsynonym_lexize

🎯 Example 5: Complete psql Reference Guide

-- Complete reference of text search psql commands
/*
Text Search psql Commands Reference:

\dF [PATTERN]      - List text search configurations
\dF+ [PATTERN]     - List configurations with dictionary mappings
\dFp [PATTERN]     - List text search parsers
\dFp+ [PATTERN]    - List parsers with function details
\dFd [PATTERN]     - List text search dictionaries
\dFd+ [PATTERN]    - List dictionaries with templates and options
\dFt [PATTERN]     - List text search templates
\dFt+ [PATTERN]    - List templates with function details

PATTERN can include:
  *         - wildcard for any characters
  schema.*  - all objects in schema
  *_stem    - objects ending with '_stem'

Examples:
*/

-- All English-related configs
\dF *english*

-- All stemming dictionaries
\dFd *_stem

-- Public schema objects only
\dFd public.*

-- Combine with SQL for more complex queries
SELECT 
    '\dF' AS command,
    'List configurations' AS purpose
UNION ALL SELECT '\dFp', 'List parsers'
UNION ALL SELECT '\dFd', 'List dictionaries'
UNION ALL SELECT '\dFt', 'List templates'
UNION ALL SELECT '\dF+', 'Show config details'
UNION ALL SELECT '\dFd+', 'Show dictionary details';

-- Get configuration summary programmatically
\t
\a
\o /tmp/text_search_config.txt
\dF+
\dFd+
\o
\a
\t
psql Command Reference:
 command |        purpose
---------+------------------------
 \dF     | List configurations
 \dFp    | List parsers
 \dFd    | List dictionaries
 \dFt    | List templates
 \dF+    | Show config details
 \dFd+   | Show dictionary details
(6 rows)

-- Output redirected to /tmp/text_search_config.txt

Tip: Combine psql commands with \o to export documentation
     Use \t to toggle tuple-only mode (no headers/footers)
     Use \a to toggle aligned/unaligned output
Explanation: psql provides \dF family of commands for text search inspection. \dF lists configurations - shows available languages and custom configs. \dFp lists parsers - usually just 'default'. \dFd lists dictionaries - shows stemming, stop words, synonyms, etc. \dFt lists templates - shows dictionary types available. Add '+' for detailed info - shows internal functions, options, mappings. Use PATTERN matching: asterisk (*) as wildcard, schema.* for schema objects. These commands invaluable for: (1) Discovering available text search components, (2) Understanding system configuration, (3) Documenting custom setups, (4) Troubleshooting configuration issues. Tip: Redirect output with \o for documentation.

12.11 Limitations

Current limitations: PostgreSQL full-text search is powerful but has some limitations to be aware of. Language support varies - not all languages have full stemming support. Phrase search with position operators can be imprecise with complex document structure. Ranking algorithms are basic compared to specialized search engines. Performance can degrade with very large documents or datasets. Understanding these limitations helps set realistic expectations and plan workarounds.

🎯 Example 1: Language Support Limitations

-- List supported configurations (languages)
SELECT cfgname AS supported_language
FROM pg_ts_config
WHERE cfgnamespace = (SELECT oid FROM pg_namespace WHERE nspname = 'pg_catalog')
ORDER BY cfgname;

-- Not all languages have full support
SELECT 
    'chinese' AS language,
    EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_ts_config WHERE cfgname = 'chinese') AS has_config,
    'Limited - use extensions like zhparser' AS notes

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'japanese',
    EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_ts_config WHERE cfgname = 'japanese'),
    'Limited - use extensions like pg_bigm'

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'korean',
    EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_ts_config WHERE cfgname = 'korean'),
    'Not built-in - requires extensions';

-- Workaround: use simple configuration for unsupported languages
SELECT 
    to_tsvector('simple', '日本語のテキスト') AS simple_config,
    -- Would need extension for proper tokenization
    'Treats as single tokens' AS limitation;
Language Support:
 supported_language
--------------------
 arabic
 danish
 dutch
 english
 finnish
 french
 german
 hungarian
 indonesian
 irish
 italian
 lithuanian
 nepali
 norwegian
 portuguese
 romanian
 russian
 spanish
 swedish
 tamil
 turkish
(21 rows)

 language | has_config |              notes
----------+------------+----------------------------------
 chinese  | false      | Limited - use extensions like zhparser
 japanese | false      | Limited - use extensions like pg_bigm
 korean   | false      | Not built-in - requires extensions
(3 rows)

     simple_config      |      limitation
------------------------+----------------------
 '日本語のテキスト':1  | Treats as single tokens
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Phrase Search Limitations

-- Phrase search with <-> operator
SELECT 
    'database management system' AS document,
    to_tsvector('english', 'database management system') AS tokens,
    phraseto_tsquery('english', 'database management') AS query,
    to_tsvector('english', 'database management system') @@ 
        phraseto_tsquery('english', 'database management') AS matches;

-- Problem: stemming breaks phrase matching
SELECT 
    'databases managed' AS document,
    phraseto_tsquery('english', 'database management') AS query,
    to_tsvector('english', 'databases managed') @@ 
        phraseto_tsquery('english', 'database management') AS matches,
    'Stems to same words but different order' AS issue;

-- Workaround: use websearch_to_tsquery with quotes
SELECT 
    'databases are managed systems' AS document,
    websearch_to_tsquery('english', '"database management"') AS strict_phrase,
    to_tsvector('english', 'databases are managed systems') @@ 
        websearch_to_tsquery('english', '"database management"') AS matches,
    'Still fails - words not adjacent after stemming' AS limitation;

-- Best workaround: combine full-text search with LIKE/regex
SELECT 
    product_id,
    name
FROM products
WHERE 
    search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'database & management')  -- Fast filter
    AND name ~* 'database.*management'  -- Precise phrase check
LIMIT 5;
Phrase Search Issues:
         document          |         tokens         |      query       | matches
---------------------------+------------------------+------------------+---------
 database management system| 'databas':1 'manag':2  | 'databas'<->'manag'| true
                           | 'system':3             |                  |
(1 row)

      document      |       query        | matches |              issue
--------------------+--------------------+---------+------------------------------------
 databases managed  | 'databas'<->'manag'| false   | Stems to same words but different order
(1 row)

          document           |     strict_phrase    | matches |           limitation
-----------------------------+----------------------+---------+--------------------------------
 databases are managed systems| 'databas'<->'manag' | false   | Still fails - words not adjacent
(1 row)

 product_id |          name
------------+------------------------
        123 | Database Management Pro
        456 | Advanced Database Management
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Ranking Limitations

-- ts_rank is simple frequency-based
SELECT 
    doc_name,
    to_tsvector('english', content) AS vector,
    ts_rank(to_tsvector('english', content), 
            to_tsquery('english', 'database')) AS rank
FROM (VALUES
    ('Short relevant', 'database'),
    ('Long spammy', repeat('database ', 100)),
    ('Contextual', 'Our database solution provides excellent database performance')
) AS docs(doc_name, content)
ORDER BY rank DESC;

-- Problem: long documents with repetition rank higher
-- Workaround: use normalization
SELECT 
    doc_name,
    ts_rank(to_tsvector('english', content), 
            to_tsquery('english', 'database'),
            1) AS rank_normalized  -- Divides by document length
FROM (VALUES
    ('Short relevant', 'database'),
    ('Long spammy', repeat('database ', 100)),
    ('Contextual', 'Our database solution provides excellent database performance')
) AS docs(doc_name, content)
ORDER BY rank_normalized DESC;

-- Limitation: No concept of document importance, recency, or authority
-- Workaround: combine with custom scoring
SELECT 
    p.product_id,
    p.name,
    ts_rank(p.search_vector, query) AS text_score,
    log(p.view_count + 1) AS popularity_score,
    extract(days FROM now() - p.created_at) AS age_days,
    -- Combined score
    ts_rank(p.search_vector, query) * 
    log(p.view_count + 1) * 
    (1.0 / (extract(days FROM now() - p.created_at) + 1)) AS combined_score
FROM products p, to_tsquery('english', 'database') query
WHERE p.search_vector @@ query
ORDER BY combined_score DESC
LIMIT 10;
Ranking Issues:
   doc_name    |                vector                  |   rank
---------------+----------------------------------------+----------
 Long spammy   | 'databas':1,2,3,4,5,6...100           | 6.079271
 Contextual    | 'databas':2,7 'excel':5 'perform':8...| 0.121585
 Short relevant| 'databas':1                            | 0.060793
(3 rows)

-- Simple rank favors repetition!

   doc_name    | rank_normalized
---------------+-----------------
 Short relevant|    0.060793
 Contextual    |    0.015198
 Long spammy   |    0.006079
(3 rows)

-- Normalization helps but still basic

 product_id |        name        | text_score | popularity_score | combined_score
------------+--------------------+------------+------------------+----------------
        789 | Database Pro v2    |   0.607927 |         8.517193 |      2.156789
        456 | Database Basics    |   0.455945 |         6.214608 |      0.987654
        123 | Old Database Tool  |   0.607927 |         5.298317 |      0.234567
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Performance Limitations

-- Very large documents can be slow
CREATE TABLE documents (
    id serial PRIMARY KEY,
    content text,
    search_vector tsvector
);

-- Insert large document (1MB text)
INSERT INTO documents (content, search_vector) VALUES
    (repeat('Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet ', 50000),
     to_tsvector('english', repeat('Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet ', 50000)));

-- tsvector has size limit
SELECT 
    id,
    length(content) AS content_bytes,
    length(search_vector::text) AS vector_size,
    pg_column_size(search_vector) AS vector_bytes
FROM documents;

-- Problem: tsvector limited to 1MB and ~16K unique lexemes
-- Workaround: chunk large documents
WITH document_chunks AS (
    SELECT 
        id,
        chunk_num,
        substring(content FROM (chunk_num-1)*10000 + 1 FOR 10000) AS chunk
    FROM documents
    CROSS JOIN generate_series(1, length(content)/10000 + 1) AS chunk_num
    WHERE length(content) > 10000
)
SELECT 
    id,
    chunk_num,
    to_tsvector('english', chunk) AS chunk_vector,
    'Index each chunk separately' AS approach
FROM document_chunks
LIMIT 3;

-- Limitation: Performance degrades with millions of documents
-- Workaround: partition tables by date/category
CREATE TABLE documents_2024_q1 PARTITION OF documents_partitioned
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2024-04-01');

-- Or use separate indexes per partition
CREATE INDEX idx_docs_2024_q1_search 
    ON documents_2024_q1 USING gin(search_vector);
Performance Constraints:
 id | content_bytes | vector_size | vector_bytes
----+---------------+-------------+--------------
  1 |    1350000    |   245678    |   1048576
(1 row)

-- tsvector at maximum size (1MB)
-- Warning: position information may be lost for very large documents

 id | chunk_num |         chunk_vector           |         approach
----+-----------+--------------------------------+---------------------------
  1 |     1     | 'amet':5,10,15... 'dolor':3... | Index each chunk separately
  1 |     2     | 'amet':5,10,15... 'ipsum':2... | Index each chunk separately
  1 |     3     | 'amet':5,10,15... 'lorem':1... | Index each chunk separately
(3 rows)

-- Chunking preserves all content but complicates queries

🎯 Example 5: Workarounds and Alternatives

-- Summary of limitations and solutions
SELECT 
    limitation,
    severity,
    workaround
FROM (VALUES
    ('Limited language support', 
     'Medium', 
     'Use extensions (zhparser, pg_bigm) or external services'),
    
    ('Phrase search imprecision', 
     'Low', 
     'Combine with LIKE/regex for precise phrase matching'),
    
    ('Basic ranking', 
     'Medium', 
     'Implement custom scoring with business logic'),
    
    ('Large document limits', 
     'Low', 
     'Chunk documents or summarize content'),
    
    ('Scale limitations', 
     'Medium', 
     'Use partitioning, external search engine for huge datasets'),
    
    ('No fuzzy search built-in', 
     'Medium', 
     'Use pg_trgm extension or implement edit distance'),
    
    ('No synonym expansion', 
     'Low', 
     'Create synonym dictionaries'),
    
    ('Limited highlighting', 
     'Low', 
     'Implement custom highlighting in application')
) AS limits(limitation, severity, workaround);

-- When to use external search engine instead:
-- ✗ Need advanced features: faceting, suggestions, ML-based ranking
-- ✗ Very large scale (>100M documents)
-- ✗ Complex multi-language requirements
-- ✗ Need advanced analytics and A/B testing

-- When PostgreSQL full-text search is sufficient:
-- ✓ Moderate scale (<10M documents)
-- ✓ Simple to moderate search requirements
-- ✓ Want to avoid external dependencies
-- ✓ Data already in PostgreSQL
-- ✓ Need transactional consistency with search
-- ✓ Limited operations budget

SELECT 
    'PostgreSQL full-text search: Good enough for most applications' AS conclusion;
Limitations Summary:
        limitation        | severity |                  workaround
--------------------------+----------+-----------------------------------------------
 Limited language support | Medium   | Use extensions (zhparser, pg_bigm) or external
 Phrase search imprecision| Low      | Combine with LIKE/regex for precise matching
 Basic ranking            | Medium   | Implement custom scoring with business logic
 Large document limits    | Low      | Chunk documents or summarize content
 Scale limitations        | Medium   | Use partitioning, external search for huge data
 No fuzzy search built-in | Medium   | Use pg_trgm extension or implement edit distance
 No synonym expansion     | Low      | Create synonym dictionaries
 Limited highlighting     | Low      | Implement custom highlighting in application
(8 rows)

                           conclusion
------------------------------------------------------------
 PostgreSQL full-text search: Good enough for most applications
(1 row)
Explanation: PostgreSQL full-text search has limitations but workarounds exist for most. Language support limited to ~21 languages with built-in stemmers - use extensions (zhparser for Chinese, pg_bigm for Japanese/CJK) or simple config for unsupported languages. Phrase search can be imprecise due to stemming - combine FTS with LIKE/regex for precise phrases. Ranking is frequency-based - add custom scoring (popularity, recency, click-through rate). Large documents hit 1MB tsvector limit - chunk content or store summaries. Performance degrades beyond millions of documents - use partitioning or consider Elasticsearch/Solr. No built-in fuzzy search - use pg_trgm extension. Despite limitations, PostgreSQL FTS excellent for most applications - simpler operations, transactional consistency, no external dependencies. Use external search engine only for: advanced features (faceting, ML ranking), massive scale (>100M docs), complex multi-language needs.

Chapter 13. Concurrency Control

🔒 Chapter Overview: This chapter covers concurrency control in PostgreSQL, explaining how multiple transactions can access data simultaneously while maintaining consistency. Learn about transaction isolation levels, explicit locking mechanisms, and how to handle concurrent access patterns safely.

13.1 Introduction

What is Concurrency Control? Concurrency control manages simultaneous database operations to ensure data consistency and integrity. PostgreSQL uses Multiversion Concurrency Control (MVCC) which allows readers and writers to operate without blocking each other, providing excellent performance in multi-user environments.

📊 MVCC Architecture Overview

📖
Readers

Never block writers. See consistent snapshot of data.

✍️
Writers

Never block readers. Create new versions of rows.

🔄
Versions

Multiple row versions coexist. VACUUM cleans old versions.

🎯
Isolation

Transactions see consistent data based on isolation level.

🎯 Example 1: Basic Concurrent Access

-- Session 1: Start a transaction
BEGIN;
SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- product_id | name    | price | stock_quantity
-- -----------+---------+-------+---------------
-- 1          | Laptop  | 999   | 50

-- Session 2: Update the same product (runs concurrently)
BEGIN;
UPDATE products 
SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 
WHERE product_id = 1;
COMMIT;  -- This commits immediately

-- Back to Session 1: Read again
SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- Still sees: stock_quantity = 50 (not 49)
-- This is MVCC in action - consistent snapshot

COMMIT;
Key Concept: Session 1 sees a consistent snapshot throughout its transaction, even though Session 2 modified the data. This is the core benefit of MVCC - readers don't block writers, and each transaction sees a consistent view of the database.

🎯 Example 2: Understanding Transaction IDs (XID)

-- Check current transaction ID
SELECT txid_current();
-- txid_current
-- -------------
-- 1000

-- View system columns that track versions
SELECT 
    product_id,
    name,
    price,
    xmin,  -- Transaction ID that inserted this version
    xmax   -- Transaction ID that deleted this version (0 if active)
FROM products
WHERE product_id = 1;

-- Example output:
-- product_id | name   | price | xmin | xmax
-- -----------+--------+-------+------+------
-- 1          | Laptop | 999   | 995  | 0

-- Update creates a new version
BEGIN;
UPDATE products SET price = 1099 WHERE product_id = 1;

-- New version created:
SELECT product_id, name, price, xmin, xmax
FROM products
WHERE product_id = 1;
-- product_id | name   | price | xmin | xmax
-- -----------+--------+-------+------+------
-- 1          | Laptop | 1099  | 1001 | 0

COMMIT;
Explanation: Every row version has xmin (creating transaction) and xmax (deleting transaction). PostgreSQL uses these to determine which version each transaction should see, enabling MVCC without traditional locking.

13.2 Transaction Isolation

Isolation Levels: PostgreSQL offers four isolation levels as defined by the SQL standard: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, and Serializable. In practice, Read Uncommitted behaves like Read Committed. Each level provides different guarantees about what phenomena can occur.

🔍 Isolation Levels Comparison

Isolation Level Dirty Read Non-Repeatable Read Phantom Read Serialization Anomaly
Read Committed ✓ Prevented ✗ Possible ✗ Possible ✗ Possible
Repeatable Read ✓ Prevented ✓ Prevented ✓ Prevented ✗ Possible
Serializable ✓ Prevented ✓ Prevented ✓ Prevented ✓ Prevented

13.2.1 Read Committed Isolation Level

Default Level: Read Committed is PostgreSQL's default isolation level. Each statement sees a snapshot of data as it existed at the start of the statement. This prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads and phantom reads.

🎯 Example 1: Setting Isolation Level

-- Check current isolation level
SHOW transaction_isolation;
-- transaction_isolation
-- ----------------------
-- read committed

-- Set for current transaction
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;
-- Or equivalently:
BEGIN;
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;

-- Set for session
SET SESSION CHARACTERISTICS AS TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;

-- Set as default (in postgresql.conf or ALTER DATABASE)
ALTER DATABASE ecommerce_db SET default_transaction_isolation = 'read committed';

🎯 Example 2: Non-Repeatable Read Scenario

-- Session 1: Read Committed transaction
BEGIN;
SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- price
-- ------
-- 999

-- Session 2: Update the price
BEGIN;
UPDATE products SET price = 1099 WHERE product_id = 1;
COMMIT;

-- Back to Session 1: Read again in same transaction
SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- price
-- ------
-- 1099  -- Different value! (Non-repeatable read)

COMMIT;
Explanation: In Read Committed, each SELECT sees committed data as of its start time. The second SELECT sees the update from Session 2 because it was committed. This is called a "non-repeatable read" - reading the same data twice gives different results.

🎯 Example 3: Phantom Read Scenario

-- Session 1: Count products in a price range
BEGIN;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 900 AND 1000;
-- count
-- ------
-- 5

-- Session 2: Insert a new product in that range
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO products (name, price, category_id, stock_quantity)
VALUES ('Tablet', 950, 1, 30);
COMMIT;

-- Back to Session 1: Count again
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 900 AND 1000;
-- count
-- ------
-- 6  -- New row appeared! (Phantom read)

COMMIT;
Explanation: A "phantom read" occurs when new rows appear (or disappear) between queries in the same transaction. Session 1's second query sees the row inserted by Session 2.

13.2.2 Repeatable Read Isolation Level

Stronger Guarantees: Repeatable Read ensures that a transaction sees a consistent snapshot of the database from its first query. All queries in the transaction see the same data, preventing non-repeatable reads and phantom reads. However, serialization anomalies can still occur.

🎯 Example 1: Consistent Snapshot Across Queries

-- Session 1: Repeatable Read transaction
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;

SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- price
-- ------
-- 999

-- Session 2: Update and commit
BEGIN;
UPDATE products SET price = 1099 WHERE product_id = 1;
COMMIT;

-- Back to Session 1: Read again
SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- price
-- ------
-- 999  -- Still sees old value! (Consistent snapshot)

-- Count products
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 900 AND 1000;
-- count
-- ------
-- 5

-- Session 2: Insert new product
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO products (name, price, category_id, stock_quantity)
VALUES ('Tablet', 950, 1, 30);
COMMIT;

-- Session 1: Count again
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 900 AND 1000;
-- count
-- ------
-- 5  -- Still sees same count! (No phantom reads)

COMMIT;
Key Benefit: Repeatable Read provides a consistent view of the database throughout the entire transaction. All queries see data as it existed at transaction start, making application logic more predictable.

🎯 Example 2: Update Conflicts in Repeatable Read

-- Session 1: Repeatable Read
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT stock_quantity FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;
-- stock_quantity
-- ---------------
-- 50

-- Session 2: Update and commit
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 10 WHERE product_id = 1;
COMMIT;  -- Success

-- Session 1: Try to update the same row
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 5 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- ERROR: could not serialize access due to concurrent update

ROLLBACK;
Error Output:
ERROR:  could not serialize access due to concurrent update
HINT:  The transaction might succeed if retried.
Explanation: In Repeatable Read, if two transactions try to modify the same row, the second one fails with a serialization error. This prevents lost updates. Your application should be prepared to retry the transaction.

🎯 Example 3: When to Use Repeatable Read

-- Good use case: Generating a report that needs consistent data
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;

-- Calculate total revenue
SELECT 
    SUM(total_amount) AS total_revenue,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    AVG(total_amount) AS avg_order_value
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days';

-- Get top customers (uses same consistent snapshot)
SELECT 
    c.customer_name,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- All queries see consistent data even if orders are being added concurrently
COMMIT;
Use Case: Repeatable Read is ideal for reports, analytics, and any multi-query operation that needs a consistent view of data. It ensures all calculations are based on the same snapshot, even if data changes during the transaction.

13.2.3 Serializable Isolation Level

Strongest Guarantee: Serializable isolation ensures that concurrent transactions produce the same result as if they executed serially (one after another). PostgreSQL uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) to detect and prevent all anomalies, including serialization anomalies that can occur even in Repeatable Read.

⚡ Serializable vs Repeatable Read

Repeatable Read
  • ✓ Prevents dirty reads
  • ✓ Prevents non-repeatable reads
  • ✓ Prevents phantom reads
  • ✗ Serialization anomalies possible
  • 🚀 Better performance
Serializable
  • ✓ Prevents dirty reads
  • ✓ Prevents non-repeatable reads
  • ✓ Prevents phantom reads
  • ✓ Prevents serialization anomalies
  • ⚠️ More serialization failures

🎯 Example 1: Enabling Serializable Isolation

-- Start a serializable transaction
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;

-- Perform operations...

COMMIT;

🎯 Example 2: Serialization Anomaly Prevention

-- Classic example: Two transactions checking and inserting
-- Setup: constraint that total stock across all products cannot exceed 1000
CREATE TABLE inventory_config (
    config_key VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY,
    config_value INTEGER
);
INSERT INTO inventory_config VALUES ('max_total_stock', 1000);

-- Session 1: Serializable transaction
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

-- Check current total
SELECT SUM(stock_quantity) FROM products;
-- sum
-- -----
-- 950

-- Decision: Can add 40 more (950 + 40 = 990 < 1000)

-- Session 2: Also checks (runs concurrently)
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

SELECT SUM(stock_quantity) FROM products;
-- sum
-- -----
-- 950  (Sees same snapshot)

-- Decision: Can add 60 more (950 + 60 = 1010 > 1000... but will try anyway)

-- Session 1: Add stock
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity + 40 WHERE product_id = 1;
COMMIT;  -- Success

-- Session 2: Try to add stock
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity + 60 WHERE product_id = 2;
-- ERROR: could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies

ROLLBACK;
Error Output:
ERROR:  could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions
DETAIL:  Reason code: Canceled on identification as a pivot, during commit attempt.
HINT:  The transaction might succeed if retried.
Explanation: Serializable detected that if both transactions succeeded, the result would violate the business rule (total > 1000). In Repeatable Read, this anomaly could occur. Serializable prevents it by failing one transaction.

🎯 Example 3: Real-World Use Case - Seat Reservation

-- Prevent double-booking with serializable transactions
-- Theater table
CREATE TABLE theater_seats (
    seat_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    row_letter CHAR(1),
    seat_number INTEGER,
    is_reserved BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    reserved_by INTEGER REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
);

-- Session 1: Customer tries to reserve seats A1 and A2
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

-- Check if seats are available
SELECT seat_id, is_reserved 
FROM theater_seats 
WHERE (row_letter = 'A' AND seat_number IN (1, 2))
  AND is_reserved = FALSE;
-- Both available

-- Session 2: Another customer tries to reserve A2 and A3
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

SELECT seat_id, is_reserved 
FROM theater_seats 
WHERE (row_letter = 'A' AND seat_number IN (2, 3))
  AND is_reserved = FALSE;
-- All available (sees same snapshot)

-- Session 1: Reserve seats
UPDATE theater_seats 
SET is_reserved = TRUE, reserved_by = 100
WHERE row_letter = 'A' AND seat_number IN (1, 2);
COMMIT;  -- Success

-- Session 2: Try to reserve (A2 conflicts)
UPDATE theater_seats 
SET is_reserved = TRUE, reserved_by = 101
WHERE row_letter = 'A' AND seat_number IN (2, 3);
-- ERROR: could not serialize

-- Must retry transaction
ROLLBACK;
Use Case: Serializable is perfect for preventing double-booking scenarios. Both customers saw available seats, but Serializable ensures only one transaction succeeds when they try to book overlapping seats.

🎯 Example 4: Handling Serialization Failures

-- Application code pattern (pseudo-code with SQL)
-- Retry logic for serialization failures

DO $$
DECLARE
    max_retries INTEGER := 3;
    retry_count INTEGER := 0;
    success BOOLEAN := FALSE;
BEGIN
    WHILE retry_count < max_retries AND NOT success LOOP
        BEGIN
            -- Start serializable transaction
            SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
            
            -- Your business logic here
            PERFORM reserve_seats(customer_id := 100, seats := ARRAY['A1', 'A2']);
            
            success := TRUE;
            
        EXCEPTION
            WHEN serialization_failure THEN
                retry_count := retry_count + 1;
                RAISE NOTICE 'Serialization failure, retry % of %', retry_count, max_retries;
                -- Add small random delay to reduce contention
                PERFORM pg_sleep(random() * 0.1);
                
            WHEN OTHERS THEN
                -- Other errors should not be retried
                RAISE;
        END;
    END LOOP;
    
    IF NOT success THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Failed after % retries', max_retries;
    END IF;
END $$;
Best Practice: Always implement retry logic when using Serializable isolation. Serialization failures are expected and normal - they indicate PostgreSQL is protecting your data consistency. Most transactions succeed on retry.

13.3 Explicit Locking

Beyond MVCC: While MVCC handles most concurrency automatically, sometimes you need explicit locks for specific use cases. PostgreSQL provides table-level locks, row-level locks, page-level locks, and advisory locks to give you fine-grained control over concurrent access.

🔐 Lock Hierarchy

Table-Level Locks

Lock entire tables - coarsest granularity, affects all rows

Page-Level Locks

Lock 8KB pages - medium granularity, automatic

Row-Level Locks

Lock individual rows - finest granularity, best concurrency

Advisory Locks

Application-defined locks - not tied to table objects

13.3.1 Table-Level Locks

Table Lock Modes: PostgreSQL has 8 table-level lock modes with varying compatibility. LOCK TABLE command explicitly acquires locks. Most operations acquire appropriate locks automatically (e.g., SELECT acquires ACCESS SHARE, UPDATE acquires ROW EXCLUSIVE).

📊 Table Lock Modes

Lock Mode Acquired By Conflicts With
ACCESS SHARE SELECT ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
ROW SHARE SELECT FOR UPDATE/SHARE EXCLUSIVE, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
ROW EXCLUSIVE INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE SHARE, EXCLUSIVE, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
SHARE CREATE INDEX ROW EXCLUSIVE, SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE, EXCLUSIVE, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DROP, TRUNCATE, ALTER TABLE All modes

🎯 Example 1: Explicit Table Locking

-- Lock table in various modes
BEGIN;

-- ACCESS SHARE: Prevents table drops/alterations
LOCK TABLE products IN ACCESS SHARE MODE;
SELECT * FROM products;  -- Safe from schema changes

COMMIT;

-- ROW EXCLUSIVE: Prevents concurrent SHARE locks
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE products IN ROW EXCLUSIVE MODE;
-- Now can INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE safely
UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1 WHERE category_id = 1;
COMMIT;

-- EXCLUSIVE: Prevents all concurrent modifications
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE products IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;
-- Exclusive access for bulk operation
DELETE FROM products WHERE discontinued = TRUE;
INSERT INTO products_archive SELECT * FROM products WHERE discontinued = TRUE;
COMMIT;

-- ACCESS EXCLUSIVE: Most restrictive
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE products IN ACCESS EXCLUSIVE MODE;
-- Complete exclusive access
TRUNCATE products;
COMMIT;

🎯 Example 2: Lock Conflicts

-- Session 1: Acquire SHARE lock
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE products IN SHARE MODE;
-- This allows other SHARE locks but blocks modifications

-- Session 2: Try to update (needs ROW EXCLUSIVE)
BEGIN;
UPDATE products SET price = 100 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- Blocks waiting for Session 1's SHARE lock to release

-- Session 3: Can acquire SHARE lock concurrently
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE products IN SHARE MODE;
-- Success! SHARE mode compatible with other SHARE locks

-- View current locks
SELECT 
    pid,
    usename,
    locktype,
    mode,
    granted,
    query
FROM pg_locks l
JOIN pg_stat_activity a ON l.pid = a.pid
WHERE relation = 'products'::regclass;

-- Session 1: Release lock
COMMIT;  -- Now Session 2's UPDATE proceeds
Lock Status:
 pid  | usename | locktype |      mode       | granted |          query
------+---------+----------+-----------------+---------+-------------------------
 1234 | app_user| relation | ShareLock       | t       | LOCK TABLE products...
 1235 | app_user| relation | RowExclusiveLock| f       | UPDATE products...
 1236 | app_user| relation | ShareLock       | t       | LOCK TABLE products...
Explanation: Multiple SHARE locks can coexist, but ROW EXCLUSIVE (UPDATE) must wait. The granted column shows whether the lock was acquired (t) or is waiting (f).

🎯 Example 3: Preventing Deadlocks with Lock Ordering

-- BAD: Can cause deadlock
-- Session 1
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE products IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;
-- ... later
LOCK TABLE orders IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;  -- Might deadlock

-- Session 2 (concurrent)
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE orders IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;
-- ... later
LOCK TABLE products IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;  -- Deadlock!

-- GOOD: Always lock in same order
-- Session 1
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE orders IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;    -- Alphabetical order
LOCK TABLE products IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;
COMMIT;

-- Session 2
BEGIN;
LOCK TABLE orders IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;    -- Same order
-- Waits for Session 1
LOCK TABLE products IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;
COMMIT;
Best Practice: Always acquire table locks in a consistent order (e.g., alphabetical by table name) to prevent deadlocks. If Session 1 locks A then B, and Session 2 locks B then A, they can deadlock.

13.3.2 Row-Level Locks

Fine-Grained Locking: Row-level locks allow concurrent access to different rows in the same table, providing better concurrency than table locks. PostgreSQL automatically acquires row locks during UPDATE/DELETE. You can explicitly lock rows using SELECT FOR UPDATE/SHARE.

🎯 Row Lock Modes

Lock Mode Command Behavior
FOR UPDATE SELECT FOR UPDATE Exclusive lock - blocks other FOR UPDATE/SHARE
FOR NO KEY UPDATE SELECT FOR NO KEY UPDATE Like FOR UPDATE but allows FOR KEY SHARE
FOR SHARE SELECT FOR SHARE Shared lock - blocks FOR UPDATE but allows other FOR SHARE
FOR KEY SHARE SELECT FOR KEY SHARE Weakest lock - only blocks FOR UPDATE, allows FOR NO KEY UPDATE

🎯 Example 1: SELECT FOR UPDATE

-- Session 1: Lock row for update
BEGIN;

SELECT * FROM products 
WHERE product_id = 1
FOR UPDATE;

-- Row is locked - other sessions cannot modify or lock it
-- Now can safely update based on what we read
UPDATE products 
SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 5
WHERE product_id = 1;

-- Session 2: Try to lock same row (concurrent)
BEGIN;
SELECT * FROM products 
WHERE product_id = 1
FOR UPDATE;
-- Blocks waiting for Session 1

-- Session 1: Finish
COMMIT;  -- Now Session 2's query proceeds

-- Session 2: Now gets the lock
-- Will see updated stock_quantity value
COMMIT;
Use Case: SELECT FOR UPDATE is essential for "read-modify-write" patterns. It prevents lost updates by ensuring no other transaction modifies the row between your read and update.

🎯 Example 2: NOWAIT and SKIP LOCKED

-- NOWAIT: Don't wait for lock, error immediately
BEGIN;
SELECT * FROM products 
WHERE product_id = 1
FOR UPDATE NOWAIT;

-- If row is locked, raises error immediately:
-- ERROR: could not obtain lock on row in relation "products"

ROLLBACK;

-- SKIP LOCKED: Skip rows that are locked
BEGIN;
-- Get next available unprocessed order
SELECT * FROM orders 
WHERE status = 'pending'
ORDER BY order_date
LIMIT 1
FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;

-- Update it
UPDATE orders 
SET status = 'processing', processed_by = 'worker-1'
WHERE order_id = (
    SELECT order_id FROM orders 
    WHERE status = 'pending'
    ORDER BY order_date
    LIMIT 1
    FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
);

COMMIT;
Use Case: SKIP LOCKED is perfect for job queues! Multiple workers can process orders concurrently without blocking each other - each worker gets a different unlocked row.

🎯 Example 3: Queue Processing Pattern

-- Create a job queue table
CREATE TABLE job_queue (
    job_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    job_type VARCHAR(50),
    payload JSONB,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    processed_at TIMESTAMP,
    processed_by VARCHAR(50)
);

-- Insert some jobs
INSERT INTO job_queue (job_type, payload)
VALUES 
    ('email', '{"to": "user1@example.com", "subject": "Welcome"}'),
    ('email', '{"to": "user2@example.com", "subject": "Welcome"}'),
    ('email', '{"to": "user3@example.com", "subject": "Welcome"}');

-- Worker process: Get and process next job
BEGIN;

-- Get next pending job (skip locked ones)
WITH next_job AS (
    SELECT job_id
    FROM job_queue
    WHERE status = 'pending'
    ORDER BY created_at
    LIMIT 1
    FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
)
UPDATE job_queue
SET 
    status = 'processing',
    processed_by = 'worker-' || pg_backend_pid()
WHERE job_id = (SELECT job_id FROM next_job)
RETURNING *;

-- Process the job (pseudo-code)
-- send_email(payload);

-- Mark as complete
UPDATE job_queue
SET 
    status = 'completed',
    processed_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
WHERE status = 'processing' AND processed_by = 'worker-' || pg_backend_pid();

COMMIT;

-- Multiple workers can run this concurrently!
-- Each gets a different job without blocking
Best Practice: The SKIP LOCKED pattern enables efficient concurrent job processing. Multiple workers process different jobs simultaneously without waiting for locks.

🎯 Example 4: FOR SHARE for Read Consistency

-- Scenario: Prevent product deletion while processing order
BEGIN;

-- Lock products in order (shared lock allows other readers)
SELECT p.product_id, p.name, p.price, p.stock_quantity
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE oi.order_id = 1001
FOR SHARE;

-- Now safe to process order knowing products won't be deleted
-- Other sessions can read products but cannot DELETE or UPDATE

-- Process payment...
-- Update inventory...

COMMIT;
Explanation: FOR SHARE allows multiple transactions to read-lock the same rows. It prevents modifications (UPDATE/DELETE) but allows concurrent reads, providing better concurrency than FOR UPDATE when you only need to prevent changes.

13.3.3 Page-Level Locks

Automatic Locks: Page-level locks are acquired automatically during index scans and table scans. A page is an 8KB block of storage. These locks are held very briefly (only while reading/writing the page) and are generally transparent to users. They prevent torn pages during concurrent reads and writes.

🎯 Example: Understanding Pages

-- View page information for a table
SELECT 
    relname AS table_name,
    relpages AS num_pages,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(oid)) AS table_size,
    pg_size_pretty(8192::bigint * relpages) AS calculated_size
FROM pg_class
WHERE relname = 'products';

-- Output shows table spans multiple 8KB pages
-- Example:
-- table_name | num_pages | table_size | calculated_size
-- -----------+-----------+------------+----------------
-- products   | 125       | 1000 kB    | 1000 kB

-- Page-level locks happen automatically during scans
-- View current page locks (rarely visible as they're brief)
SELECT 
    locktype,
    page,
    granted
FROM pg_locks
WHERE locktype = 'page';
Note: Page-level locks are internal and short-lived. You rarely need to worry about them. PostgreSQL manages them automatically to ensure consistent page reads/writes.

13.3.4 Deadlocks

Circular Waiting: A deadlock occurs when two or more transactions wait for each other to release locks, creating a cycle. PostgreSQL automatically detects deadlocks (checks every second by default) and aborts one transaction to break the cycle.

⚠️ Deadlock Scenario

Transaction 1:
  1. Lock Row A ✓
  2. Wait for Row B ⏳
Transaction 2:
  1. Lock Row B ✓
  2. Wait for Row A ⏳
🔄 Deadlock! Each waits for the other.

🎯 Example 1: Creating a Deadlock

-- Session 1: Lock product 1, then try to lock product 2
BEGIN;
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- (Locks row 1)

-- Wait a bit...
pg_sleep(3);

-- Now try to lock product 2
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 WHERE product_id = 2;

-- Session 2: Lock product 2, then try to lock product 1 (concurrent)
BEGIN;
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 WHERE product_id = 2;
-- (Locks row 2)

-- Try to lock product 1
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- DEADLOCK DETECTED!
Error Output:
ERROR:  deadlock detected
DETAIL:  Process 1234 waits for ShareLock on transaction 5678; blocked by process 5679.
Process 5679 waits for ShareLock on transaction 5677; blocked by process 1234.
HINT:  See server log for query details.
CONTEXT:  while updating tuple (0,1) in relation "products"

🎯 Example 2: Preventing Deadlocks - Lock Ordering

-- BAD: Different lock order
-- Session 1: A then B
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 
WHERE product_id IN (1, 2);  -- Locks in random order

-- Session 2: B then A  
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 
WHERE product_id IN (2, 1);  -- Locks in random order

-- GOOD: Consistent lock order
-- Session 1
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 
WHERE product_id IN (1, 2)
ORDER BY product_id;  -- Always locks 1 then 2

-- Session 2
UPDATE products SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 1 
WHERE product_id IN (2, 1)
ORDER BY product_id;  -- Also locks 1 then 2 (waits for Session 1)

🎯 Example 3: Deadlock Timeout Configuration

-- Check current deadlock detection timeout
SHOW deadlock_timeout;
-- deadlock_timeout
-- -----------------
-- 1s (default)

-- Set for session
SET deadlock_timeout = '2s';

-- Set globally (requires superuser, postgresql.conf)
-- deadlock_timeout = 1000ms
Best Practices: 1) Always acquire locks in consistent order. 2) Keep transactions short. 3) Use appropriate isolation levels. 4) Handle deadlock errors with retry logic. 5) Consider using NOWAIT or SKIP LOCKED to avoid waiting.

13.3.5 Advisory Locks

Application-Level Locks: Advisory locks are locks defined by your application, not tied to database objects. PostgreSQL tracks them but doesn't automatically enforce them - your application decides what they mean. Perfect for coordinating application-level resources or implementing custom locking schemes.

🔑 Advisory Lock Functions

Function Scope Description
pg_advisory_lock(bigint) Session Exclusive lock, waits if unavailable
pg_try_advisory_lock(bigint) Session Try to lock, returns immediately
pg_advisory_unlock(bigint) Session Release session-level lock
pg_advisory_xact_lock(bigint) Transaction Auto-released at transaction end
pg_advisory_lock_shared(bigint) Session Shared lock, multiple holders allowed

🎯 Example 1: Basic Advisory Lock Usage

-- Session 1: Acquire advisory lock
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345);
-- Returns immediately, lock acquired

-- Do some work...
SELECT pg_sleep(5);

-- Session 2: Try to acquire same lock (concurrent)
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345);
-- Waits for Session 1 to release

-- Session 1: Release lock
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);
-- Session 2 now acquires the lock

-- Session 2: Release
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);

🎯 Example 2: Non-Blocking Try Lock

-- Try to acquire lock without waiting
SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(12345) AS got_lock;
-- got_lock
-- ---------
-- t (true if acquired, false if already held)

-- Conditional logic based on lock
DO $$
BEGIN
    IF pg_try_advisory_lock(12345) THEN
        -- Got the lock, do exclusive work
        RAISE NOTICE 'Lock acquired, processing...';
        PERFORM pg_sleep(2);
        PERFORM pg_advisory_unlock(12345);
    ELSE
        -- Someone else has lock
        RAISE NOTICE 'Lock busy, skipping...';
    END IF;
END $$;

🎯 Example 3: Preventing Duplicate Job Processing

-- Use advisory locks to ensure only one worker processes each job
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION process_job(job_id_param INTEGER)
RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
DECLARE
    lock_acquired BOOLEAN;
BEGIN
    -- Try to acquire advisory lock for this job
    lock_acquired := pg_try_advisory_lock(job_id_param);
    
    IF NOT lock_acquired THEN
        -- Another worker is processing this job
        RAISE NOTICE 'Job % already being processed', job_id_param;
        RETURN FALSE;
    END IF;
    
    -- We have the lock, process the job
    RAISE NOTICE 'Processing job %', job_id_param;
    
    -- Do actual work here...
    PERFORM pg_sleep(1);
    
    -- Update job status
    UPDATE job_queue 
    SET status = 'completed', processed_at = NOW()
    WHERE job_id = job_id_param;
    
    -- Release lock
    PERFORM pg_advisory_unlock(job_id_param);
    
    RETURN TRUE;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Multiple workers can call this concurrently
SELECT process_job(1);
SELECT process_job(2);
SELECT process_job(3);

🎯 Example 4: Transaction-Scoped Advisory Locks

-- Transaction-level locks auto-release on COMMIT/ROLLBACK
BEGIN;

-- Acquire transaction-scoped lock
SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(99999);

-- Do work within transaction
UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1 WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Lock automatically released on COMMIT
COMMIT;

-- No need to manually unlock!
-- This is safer - no risk of forgetting to unlock

🎯 Example 5: Shared Advisory Locks

-- Multiple readers can hold shared lock
-- Session 1
SELECT pg_advisory_lock_shared(55555);
-- Acquired

-- Session 2
SELECT pg_advisory_lock_shared(55555);
-- Also acquired (shared)

-- Session 3: Try exclusive lock
SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(55555);
-- Returns false - blocked by shared locks

-- Sessions 1 & 2 release
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock_shared(55555);  -- Both sessions

-- Session 3: Now can acquire exclusive
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(55555);
-- Success

🎯 Example 6: View Current Advisory Locks

-- See all advisory locks
SELECT 
    locktype,
    database,
    classid,
    objid,
    mode,
    granted,
    pid,
    pg_blocking_pids(pid) AS blocking_pids
FROM pg_locks
WHERE locktype = 'advisory'
ORDER BY pid;
Use Cases: Advisory locks are perfect for: singleton jobs (ensure only one instance runs), distributed locking (coordinate across multiple application servers), resource allocation (limit concurrent access to external resources), maintenance windows (prevent operations during maintenance).

13.4 Data Consistency Checks at the Application Level

Application Responsibility: While PostgreSQL provides transaction isolation and locking mechanisms, applications must still implement proper consistency checks. This section shows patterns for maintaining data consistency using serializable transactions or explicit locks.

13.4.1 Enforcing Consistency with Serializable Transactions

Automatic Protection: Serializable isolation automatically detects and prevents consistency violations. The application reads data, makes decisions, and writes - if concurrent transactions would cause anomalies, PostgreSQL aborts one and your app retries.

🎯 Example 1: Bank Transfer with Serializable

-- Create accounts table
CREATE TABLE accounts (
    account_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    account_holder VARCHAR(100),
    balance DECIMAL(12, 2),
    CHECK (balance >= 0)  -- No negative balances
);

INSERT INTO accounts (account_holder, balance) VALUES
('Alice', 1000.00),
('Bob', 500.00);

-- Transfer function using serializable
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION transfer_money(
    from_acct INTEGER,
    to_acct INTEGER,
    amount DECIMAL
) RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
DECLARE
    from_balance DECIMAL;
    retries INTEGER := 0;
    max_retries INTEGER := 3;
BEGIN
    LOOP
        BEGIN
            -- Start serializable transaction
            SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
            
            -- Check source balance
            SELECT balance INTO from_balance
            FROM accounts
            WHERE account_id = from_acct;
            
            IF from_balance < amount THEN
                RAISE EXCEPTION 'Insufficient funds';
            END IF;
            
            -- Perform transfer
            UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - amount
            WHERE account_id = from_acct;
            
            UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + amount
            WHERE account_id = to_acct;
            
            RETURN TRUE;
            
        EXCEPTION
            WHEN serialization_failure THEN
                retries := retries + 1;
                IF retries >= max_retries THEN
                    RAISE EXCEPTION 'Transfer failed after % retries', max_retries;
                END IF;
                -- Retry with small delay
                PERFORM pg_sleep(random() * 0.1);
        END;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Test concurrent transfers
SELECT transfer_money(1, 2, 100.00);
Benefit: Serializable handles all edge cases automatically. No matter how many concurrent transfers occur, accounts never go negative and total balance is always conserved.

🎯 Example 2: Inventory Management

-- Prevent overselling with serializable transactions
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

-- Check available stock
SELECT stock_quantity 
FROM products 
WHERE product_id = 1;
-- stock_quantity = 10

-- Customer wants to buy 5 units
-- Another transaction might be buying simultaneously

-- Reserve stock
UPDATE products 
SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 5
WHERE product_id = 1 
AND stock_quantity >= 5;

-- Check if update succeeded
IF NOT FOUND THEN
    ROLLBACK;
    RAISE EXCEPTION 'Insufficient stock';
END IF;

-- Create order
INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, total_amount)
VALUES (100, 250.00);

INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price)
VALUES (currval('orders_order_id_seq'), 1, 5, 50.00);

COMMIT;

-- If another transaction conflicts, one will retry automatically

13.4.2 Enforcing Consistency with Explicit Blocking Locks

Pessimistic Approach: Instead of detecting conflicts and retrying, explicitly lock rows upfront with SELECT FOR UPDATE. This prevents conflicts but may reduce concurrency. Best when conflicts are frequent or retries are expensive.

🎯 Example 1: Bank Transfer with Explicit Locks

-- Same transfer but with explicit locking
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION transfer_money_locked(
    from_acct INTEGER,
    to_acct INTEGER,
    amount DECIMAL
) RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
DECLARE
    from_balance DECIMAL;
BEGIN
    -- Lock both accounts in consistent order (prevent deadlock)
    IF from_acct < to_acct THEN
        SELECT balance INTO from_balance
        FROM accounts
        WHERE account_id = from_acct
        FOR UPDATE;
        
        PERFORM 1 FROM accounts
        WHERE account_id = to_acct
        FOR UPDATE;
    ELSE
        PERFORM 1 FROM accounts
        WHERE account_id = to_acct
        FOR UPDATE;
        
        SELECT balance INTO from_balance
        FROM accounts
        WHERE account_id = from_acct
        FOR UPDATE;
    END IF;
    
    -- Now have exclusive locks, check and transfer
    IF from_balance < amount THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Insufficient funds';
    END IF;
    
    UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - amount
    WHERE account_id = from_acct;
    
    UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + amount
    WHERE account_id = to_acct;
    
    RETURN TRUE;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- No retry needed - locks prevent conflicts
BEGIN;
SELECT transfer_money_locked(1, 2, 100.00);
COMMIT;
Trade-off: Explicit locking avoids retries but reduces concurrency. If many transfers involve the same accounts, they'll queue up waiting for locks. Choose based on your workload.

🎯 Example 2: Seat Reservation with Locks

-- Reserve multiple seats atomically
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION reserve_seats(
    customer_id_param INTEGER,
    seat_ids INTEGER[]
) RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
DECLARE
    reserved_count INTEGER;
BEGIN
    -- Lock seats in order (prevent deadlock)
    PERFORM 1
    FROM theater_seats
    WHERE seat_id = ANY(seat_ids)
    ORDER BY seat_id
    FOR UPDATE;
    
    -- Check all are available
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO reserved_count
    FROM theater_seats
    WHERE seat_id = ANY(seat_ids)
    AND is_reserved = TRUE;
    
    IF reserved_count > 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Some seats already reserved';
    END IF;
    
    -- Reserve all seats
    UPDATE theater_seats
    SET is_reserved = TRUE, reserved_by = customer_id_param
    WHERE seat_id = ANY(seat_ids);
    
    RETURN TRUE;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Usage
BEGIN;
SELECT reserve_seats(100, ARRAY[1, 2, 3]);
COMMIT;

🎯 Example 3: Comparison Table

⚖️ Serializable vs Explicit Locks

Aspect Serializable Explicit Locks
Approach Optimistic (detect & retry) Pessimistic (lock upfront)
Concurrency Higher (read-only txns don't block) Lower (locks held longer)
Retries Required Not needed
Best For Low contention, read-heavy High contention, write-heavy
Complexity Need retry logic Need correct lock order

13.5 Serialization Failure Handling

Handling Failures: When using Repeatable Read or Serializable isolation, applications must handle serialization failures gracefully. These failures indicate conflicts that couldn't be resolved - the solution is to retry the transaction.

🎯 Example 1: Retry Pattern in Application Code

-- PL/pgSQL retry pattern
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION execute_with_retry(
    operation_name TEXT,
    max_attempts INTEGER DEFAULT 5
) RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
DECLARE
    attempt INTEGER := 0;
    backoff_ms INTEGER;
BEGIN
    WHILE attempt < max_attempts LOOP
        attempt := attempt + 1;
        
        BEGIN
            -- Your transaction logic here
            PERFORM your_business_logic();
            
            RETURN TRUE;  -- Success
            
        EXCEPTION
            WHEN serialization_failure OR deadlock_detected THEN
                IF attempt >= max_attempts THEN
                    RAISE EXCEPTION 'Failed after % attempts: %', max_attempts, SQLERRM;
                END IF;
                
                -- Exponential backoff: 10ms, 20ms, 40ms, 80ms, 160ms
                backoff_ms := 10 * POWER(2, attempt - 1);
                RAISE NOTICE 'Attempt % failed, retrying in %ms...', attempt, backoff_ms;
                PERFORM pg_sleep(backoff_ms / 1000.0);
        END;
    END LOOP;
    
    RETURN FALSE;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

🎯 Example 2: Python Application Code

# Python with psycopg2
import psycopg2
import time
import random

def execute_with_retry(conn, operation, max_retries=5):
    """Execute database operation with retry logic."""
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            with conn:  # Auto-commit on success, rollback on exception
                with conn.cursor() as cur:
                    operation(cur)
                return True  # Success
                
        except psycopg2.extensions.TransactionRollbackError as e:
            # Serialization failure or deadlock
            if attempt >= max_retries - 1:
                raise  # Max retries exceeded
                
            # Exponential backoff with jitter
            backoff = (2 ** attempt) * 0.01 * (1 + random.random())
            print(f"Serialization failure, retry {attempt + 1}/{max_retries} after {backoff:.3f}s")
            time.sleep(backoff)
            
    return False

# Usage
def transfer_operation(cur):
    cur.execute("""
        SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
        
        UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - %s WHERE account_id = %s;
        UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + %s WHERE account_id = %s;
    """, (100, 1, 100, 2))

execute_with_retry(conn, transfer_operation)

🎯 Example 3: Identifying Serialization Failure Causes

-- Enable detailed logging
SET log_error_verbosity = 'verbose';

-- View serialization failure details in logs
-- Check postgresql.log for entries like:
/*
ERROR:  could not serialize access due to read/write dependencies among transactions
DETAIL:  Reason code: Canceled on identification as a pivot, during commit attempt.
HINT:  The transaction might succeed if retried.
*/

-- Monitor serialization failures
SELECT 
    datname,
    blks_hit,
    blks_read,
    xact_commit,
    xact_rollback,
    deadlocks
FROM pg_stat_database
WHERE datname = current_database();
Best Practices: 1) Always implement retry logic. 2) Use exponential backoff with jitter. 3) Limit max retries (typically 3-5). 4) Log failures for monitoring. 5) Consider switching to explicit locks if retries are too frequent (>10% of transactions).

13.6 Caveats

Important Limitations: While PostgreSQL's concurrency control is powerful, there are important caveats and edge cases to understand. This section highlights common pitfalls and misconceptions.

⚠️ Caveat 1: Constraint Violations and Serialization

-- UNIQUE constraints can cause serialization failures
CREATE TABLE usernames (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE
);

-- Session 1: Serializable transaction
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
INSERT INTO usernames (username) VALUES ('alice');

-- Session 2: Same username (concurrent)
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
INSERT INTO usernames (username) VALUES ('alice');

-- Session 1 commits first
COMMIT;

-- Session 2 tries to commit
COMMIT;
-- ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint

-- This is NOT a serialization failure, it's a constraint violation
-- Cannot be fixed by retry!
Caveat: Unique constraint violations are not serialization failures. Retrying won't help - handle these separately in your application logic.

⚠️ Caveat 2: Long-Running Transactions

-- Long transactions hold snapshots and can cause issues
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;

-- Transaction runs for hours...
SELECT pg_sleep(3600);

-- Problems:
-- 1. Holds old snapshot, sees stale data
-- 2. Prevents VACUUM from cleaning old row versions
-- 3. Increases database bloat
-- 4. Higher chance of serialization failures

-- Check for long-running transactions
SELECT 
    pid,
    usename,
    state,
    age(clock_timestamp(), xact_start) AS transaction_age,
    query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state != 'idle'
AND xact_start IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xact_start;
Caveat: Keep transactions short! Long-running transactions cause bloat and increase serialization failures. Break large operations into smaller transactions when possible.

⚠️ Caveat 3: SELECT FOR UPDATE and DELETE

-- SELECT FOR UPDATE on deleted rows
BEGIN;

-- Lock row for update
SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_id = 1 FOR UPDATE;

-- Another session deletes it
-- (In concurrent session)
-- DELETE FROM products WHERE product_id = 1;

-- Original session tries to update
UPDATE products SET price = 999 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- No error, but 0 rows updated!

-- Check with GET DIAGNOSTICS
DO $$
DECLARE
    rows_affected INTEGER;
BEGIN
    UPDATE products SET price = 999 WHERE product_id = 1;
    GET DIAGNOSTICS rows_affected = ROW_COUNT;
    
    IF rows_affected = 0 THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Row was deleted by another transaction';
    END IF;
END $$;

⚠️ Caveat 4: Prepared Transactions

-- Prepared transactions require special handling
-- Used for two-phase commit (distributed transactions)

BEGIN;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE account_id = 1;

-- Prepare (not committed yet)
PREPARE TRANSACTION 'transfer-123';

-- Transaction is now in prepared state
-- It holds locks until COMMIT PREPARED or ROLLBACK PREPARED

-- Later (possibly from different connection)
COMMIT PREPARED 'transfer-123';

-- WARNING: Prepared transactions:
-- 1. Hold locks indefinitely if not committed/rolled back
-- 2. Cannot be automatically cleaned up
-- 3. Prevent VACUUM from working
-- 4. Should only be used for true distributed transactions

⚠️ Caveat 5: VACUUM and Concurrency

-- VACUUM is affected by concurrent transactions
-- Oldest active transaction determines what can be cleaned

-- Check vacuum progress
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    relname,
    last_vacuum,
    last_autovacuum,
    n_dead_tup,  -- Dead tuples waiting to be cleaned
    n_live_tup
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE n_dead_tup > 1000
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;
Summary: Key caveats: 1) Constraint violations aren't serialization failures. 2) Keep transactions short. 3) Check affected rows after UPDATE/DELETE. 4) Avoid prepared transactions unless necessary. 5) Long transactions prevent VACUUM from cleaning dead tuples.

13.7 Locking and Indexes

Index Locking: Index operations have their own locking considerations. Understanding how indexes interact with locks is important for maintaining good concurrency during index creation, updates, and queries.

🎯 Example 1: CREATE INDEX Locks

-- Regular CREATE INDEX acquires SHARE lock
-- Blocks INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE but allows SELECT
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
-- Blocks writes for duration of index build

-- CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY - doesn't block writes
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_products_name ON products(name);
-- Allows INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE during build
-- Takes longer but better for production

-- Trade-offs:
-- Regular: Faster, blocks writes
-- CONCURRENT: Slower, doesn't block writes

-- Check index creation progress
SELECT 
    phase,
    blocks_total,
    blocks_done,
    tuples_total,
    tuples_done
FROM pg_stat_progress_create_index
WHERE relid = 'products'::regclass;

🎯 Example 2: Index Bloat and REINDEX

-- REINDEX acquires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock
REINDEX TABLE products;  -- Blocks all access!

-- Better: REINDEX CONCURRENTLY (PostgreSQL 12+)
REINDEX TABLE CONCURRENTLY products;
-- Allows queries during rebuild

-- Or drop and recreate concurrently
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_products_category;
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_products_category ON products(category_id);

🎯 Example 3: Index Scans and Locks

-- Index scans acquire locks on index pages briefly
-- Generally not a concern, but understand the behavior

-- Sequential scan: scans heap pages
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) 
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100;
-- Buffers shows pages accessed

-- Index scan: scans index + heap pages
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1;
-- More pages if index + heap access needed

-- Index-only scan: best performance
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category_price ON products(category_id, price);
VACUUM products;  -- Ensure visibility map is updated

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT category_id, price FROM products WHERE category_id = 1;
-- Index Only Scan - no heap access needed!

🎯 Example 4: Lock Monitoring During Index Operations

-- Monitor locks during index creation
SELECT 
    l.locktype,
    l.relation::regclass AS table_name,
    l.mode,
    l.granted,
    a.query,
    a.state,
    age(clock_timestamp(), a.query_start) AS duration
FROM pg_locks l
JOIN pg_stat_activity a ON l.pid = a.pid
WHERE l.relation = 'products'::regclass
ORDER BY l.granted, duration DESC;

📋 Index Operation Locking Summary

Operation Lock Type Blocks
CREATE INDEX SHARE INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE Other CREATE INDEX
REINDEX ACCESS EXCLUSIVE All operations
REINDEX CONCURRENTLY SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE Other DDL
DROP INDEX ACCESS EXCLUSIVE All operations
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE Other DDL
Best Practices: 1) Always use CONCURRENTLY for index operations on production tables. 2) Schedule regular REINDEX for high-update indexes. 3) Monitor index bloat. 4) Use index-only scans when possible for better performance. 5) Understand lock implications before creating indexes on large tables.

✅ Chapter 13 Summary

This chapter covered PostgreSQL's comprehensive concurrency control mechanisms:

  • MVCC: Readers don't block writers, providing excellent concurrency
  • Isolation Levels: Read Committed (default), Repeatable Read, and Serializable
  • Locking: Table, row, page, and advisory locks for different scenarios
  • Consistency: Use serializable transactions or explicit locks
  • Failure Handling: Always implement retry logic for serialization failures
  • Deadlocks: Prevent with consistent lock ordering
  • Index Operations: Use CONCURRENTLY to avoid blocking production traffic

Key Takeaway: Choose the right tool for your use case - MVCC and appropriate isolation levels handle most scenarios automatically, use explicit locks when you need fine-grained control, and always handle failures gracefully.

Chapter 14. Performance Tips

🚀 Chapter Overview: This chapter provides practical tips for improving PostgreSQL performance. Learn how to use EXPLAIN to understand query execution, how statistics help the planner, techniques for bulk data loading, and configuration options for maximum performance.

14.1 Using EXPLAIN

Understanding Query Execution: EXPLAIN shows how PostgreSQL executes queries - what indexes it uses, join methods, estimated costs, and actual execution times. This is your primary tool for query optimization and performance troubleshooting.

📊 EXPLAIN Command Variants

📋
EXPLAIN

Shows estimated execution plan without running query

⚡
EXPLAIN ANALYZE

Executes query and shows actual timing and row counts

🔍
EXPLAIN (BUFFERS)

Shows buffer usage (cache hits/misses)

📈
EXPLAIN (VERBOSE)

Shows detailed output including column lists

14.1.1 EXPLAIN Basics

Reading Query Plans: EXPLAIN output shows a tree of plan nodes. Each node represents an operation (scan, join, sort, etc.) with estimated cost, rows, and width. Lower cost numbers are better, but costs are relative - only use them to compare different plans for the same query.

🎯 Example 1: Simple Sequential Scan

-- Basic EXPLAIN (doesn't execute the query)
EXPLAIN 
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 500;

-- Output:
-- Seq Scan on products  (cost=0.00..25.50 rows=500 width=100)
--   Filter: (price > 500::numeric)
Explanation of Output:
Seq Scan on products
├─ cost=0.00..25.50    (startup cost .. total cost)
├─ rows=500            (estimated rows returned)
├─ width=100           (estimated avg row width in bytes)
└─ Filter: price > 500 (condition applied during scan)
Key Metrics: cost=0.00..25.50 means 0 startup cost and 25.50 total cost. rows=500 is estimated rows. width=100 is average row size in bytes. Sequential scan reads entire table.

🎯 Example 2: Index Scan

-- Create index first
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);

-- Now EXPLAIN the same query
EXPLAIN 
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 500;

-- Output:
-- Index Scan using idx_products_price on products  (cost=0.29..18.35 rows=500 width=100)
--   Index Cond: (price > 500::numeric)
Improvement: Cost decreased from 25.50 to 18.35! Index scan is faster because it only reads matching rows. Index Cond shows the condition used with the index.

🎯 Example 3: Join Operations

EXPLAIN
SELECT o.order_id, c.customer_name, o.total_amount
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01';

-- Output:
-- Hash Join  (cost=45.00..125.50 rows=1000 width=50)
--   Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.customer_id)
--   ->  Seq Scan on orders o  (cost=0.00..55.00 rows=1000 width=30)
--         Filter: (order_date >= '2024-01-01'::date)
--   ->  Hash  (cost=25.00..25.00 rows=1000 width=20)
--         ->  Seq Scan on customers c  (cost=0.00..25.00 rows=1000 width=20)
Join Plan Breakdown:
Hash Join (total cost: 125.50)
│
├─ Scan orders table (cost: 55.00)
│  └─ Filter by order_date
│
└─ Build hash table from customers (cost: 25.00)
   └─ Scan customers table
Join Method: Hash Join builds a hash table from the smaller table (customers) and probes it with rows from the larger table (orders). Good for large joins without indexes on join columns.

🎯 Example 4: Understanding Cost Components

-- Show detailed costs
EXPLAIN (COSTS TRUE, TIMING FALSE)
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) as product_count,
    AVG(price) as avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_price DESC;

-- Output:
-- Sort  (cost=45.50..46.00 rows=200 width=20)
--   Sort Key: (avg(price)) DESC
--   ->  HashAggregate  (cost=35.00..40.00 rows=200 width=20)
--         Group Key: category_id
--         Filter: (count(*) > 5)
--         ->  Seq Scan on products  (cost=0.00..25.00 rows=1000 width=12)
Plan Steps: 1) Sequential scan of products (25.00). 2) HashAggregate to group by category (40.00). 3) Filter groups with HAVING (40.00). 4) Sort results (46.00). Total cascades from bottom to top.

14.1.2 EXPLAIN ANALYZE

Real Execution Data: EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes the query and shows real timing, actual row counts, and buffer usage. This reveals if the planner's estimates were accurate. ⚠️ Warning: It runs the query including any modifications (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)!

🎯 Example 1: Comparing Estimates vs Actuals

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 500;

-- Output:
-- Index Scan using idx_products_price on products  
--   (cost=0.29..18.35 rows=500 width=100) 
--   (actual time=0.045..2.123 rows=487 loops=1)
--   Index Cond: (price > 500::numeric)
-- Planning Time: 0.234 ms
-- Execution Time: 2.567 ms
Estimate vs Actual Comparison:
Metric          | Estimated | Actual
----------------|-----------|--------
Rows            | 500       | 487     ✓ Close estimate
Total Cost      | 18.35     | N/A
Actual Time     | N/A       | 2.567ms
Planning Time   | N/A       | 0.234ms
Analysis: Estimated 500 rows, actually got 487 - good estimate! actual time=0.045..2.123 means 0.045ms to get first row, 2.123ms to get all rows. loops=1 means this node executed once.

🎯 Example 2: Detecting Bad Estimates

-- Query with possibly stale statistics
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM orders 
WHERE customer_id = 1 AND order_date > '2024-01-01';

-- Output with bad estimate:
-- Index Scan using idx_orders_customer on orders
--   (cost=0.29..8.31 rows=10 width=50)
--   (actual time=0.123..45.678 rows=5000 loops=1)  -- BIG MISMATCH!
--   Index Cond: (customer_id = 1)
--   Filter: (order_date > '2024-01-01'::date)
-- Planning Time: 0.456 ms
-- Execution Time: 67.890 ms
Problem Found: Estimated 10 rows but got 5000! This huge mismatch means planner made decisions based on wrong data. Solution: Run ANALYZE orders; to update statistics.

🎯 Example 3: Using BUFFERS Option

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT c.customer_name, COUNT(o.order_id)
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name;

-- Output:
-- HashAggregate  (cost=85.00..95.00 rows=1000 width=52)
--                (actual time=12.345..15.678 rows=1000 loops=1)
--   Group Key: c.customer_id, c.customer_name
--   Batches: 1  Memory Usage: 145kB
--   Buffers: shared hit=25 read=10
--   ->  Hash Right Join  (cost=35.00..75.00 rows=2000 width=44)
--                       (actual time=2.123..8.456 rows=2000 loops=1)
--         Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.customer_id)
--         Buffers: shared hit=20 read=8
--         ->  Seq Scan on orders o  (cost=0.00..30.00 rows=2000 width=4)
--                                   (actual time=0.012..3.456 rows=2000 loops=1)
--               Buffers: shared hit=15 read=5
--         ->  Hash  (cost=20.00..20.00 rows=1000 width=44)
--                   (actual time=1.234..1.234 rows=1000 loops=1)
--               Buckets: 1024  Batches: 1  Memory Usage: 72kB
--               Buffers: shared hit=5 read=3
--               ->  Seq Scan on customers c  (cost=0.00..20.00 rows=1000 width=44)
--                                            (actual time=0.008..0.789 rows=1000 loops=1)
--                     Buffers: shared hit=5 read=3
-- Planning Time: 0.345 ms
-- Execution Time: 16.123 ms
Buffer Statistics:
Buffers: shared hit=25 read=10
         │
         ├─ hit=25   (Pages found in cache - FAST!)
         └─ read=10  (Pages read from disk - SLOW)
Cache Analysis: shared hit = pages found in shared buffer cache (fast). read = pages read from disk (slow). 25 hits vs 10 reads = 71% cache hit rate. Higher is better! If mostly reads, consider increasing shared_buffers or running query again (warm cache).

🎯 Example 4: VERBOSE and FORMAT Options

-- JSON format (useful for tools)
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, FORMAT JSON)
SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Returns structured JSON
-- [
--   {
--     "Plan": {
--       "Node Type": "Index Scan",
--       "Relation Name": "products",
--       "Actual Total Time": 2.567,
--       "Actual Rows": 150,
--       ...
--     }
--   }
-- ]

-- VERBOSE shows output columns
EXPLAIN (VERBOSE)
SELECT product_id, name FROM products WHERE price > 100;

-- Output:
-- Seq Scan on public.products  (cost=0.00..25.00 rows=500 width=36)
--   Output: product_id, name
--   Filter: (products.price > 100::numeric)

🎯 Example 5: Understanding Loops

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT o.order_id, oi.product_id, oi.quantity
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.customer_id = 100;

-- Output:
-- Nested Loop  (cost=0.57..125.80 rows=50 width=12)
--              (actual time=0.123..5.678 rows=45 loops=1)
--   ->  Index Scan using idx_orders_customer on orders o
--         (cost=0.29..8.31 rows=5 width=4)
--         (actual time=0.045..0.234 rows=5 loops=1)
--         Index Cond: (customer_id = 100)
--   ->  Index Scan using idx_order_items_order on order_items oi
--         (cost=0.29..23.50 rows=10 width=12)
--         (actual time=0.023..1.012 rows=9 loops=5)  -- LOOPS=5!
