--         Index Cond: (order_id = o.order_id)
Loops Explained: Inner node (order_items scan) has loops=5 because it ran 5 times - once for each order found. The rows=9 is PER loop, so total rows = 9 × 5 = 45. Actual time is also per loop. High loops with high cost = performance problem!

14.1.3 Caveats

Important Warnings: EXPLAIN has important limitations and gotchas to understand. Using it incorrectly can lead to wrong conclusions or even data corruption.

⚠️ Caveat 1: EXPLAIN ANALYZE Executes the Query

-- DANGEROUS! This will actually DELETE data
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
DELETE FROM products WHERE category_id = 99;
-- Data is DELETED even though you're just "explaining"!

-- SAFE: Wrap in transaction and rollback
BEGIN;
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
DELETE FROM products WHERE category_id = 99;
ROLLBACK;  -- Undo the delete

-- Or use EXPLAIN without ANALYZE for DML
EXPLAIN 
DELETE FROM products WHERE category_id = 99;  -- Safe, doesn't execute
Critical Warning: EXPLAIN ANALYZE runs INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements for real! Always wrap in BEGIN/ROLLBACK or use plain EXPLAIN for DML queries.

⚠️ Caveat 2: Estimates Can Be Wrong

-- Outdated statistics lead to bad plans
CREATE TABLE test_data AS 
SELECT generate_series(1, 1000000) as id, random() as value;

-- Without statistics, terrible estimate
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM test_data WHERE value < 0.01;
-- Seq Scan on test_data  (cost=0.00..16925.00 rows=1000000 width=12)
--   Filter: (value < 0.01)
-- WRONG! Will actually return ~10,000 rows

-- After ANALYZE, accurate estimate
ANALYZE test_data;
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM test_data WHERE value < 0.01;
-- Seq Scan on test_data  (cost=0.00..16925.00 rows=10000 width=12)
--   Filter: (value < 0.01)
-- Correct estimate!

⚠️ Caveat 3: Cost Numbers Are Relative

-- Don't compare costs across different queries!
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM products;  -- cost=25.00
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM customers; -- cost=30.00

-- You CANNOT conclude products query is faster!
-- Cost units are arbitrary and only meaningful within same query
-- 25.00 vs 30.00 from different queries means nothing

-- DO compare different plans for SAME query:
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100;  -- cost=25.00
-- vs
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM products WHERE category_id = 1; -- cost=15.00
-- Now 15.00 < 25.00 is meaningful

⚠️ Caveat 4: Timing Variability

-- First run (cold cache)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM large_table;
-- Execution Time: 1250.567 ms

-- Second run (warm cache)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM large_table;
-- Execution Time: 45.123 ms

-- HUGE difference! Cache state matters
-- Always run multiple times for accurate measurement

⚠️ Caveat 5: EXPLAIN Overhead

-- EXPLAIN ANALYZE adds overhead
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products;  -- Fast query
-- Real runtime: ~1ms

EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products;
-- Execution Time: 1.2ms
-- 0.2ms overhead from EXPLAIN itself!

-- For very fast queries, overhead can be significant
-- For slow queries (>100ms), overhead is negligible

📋 EXPLAIN Best Practices Checklist

  • ✓ Always use BEGIN/ROLLBACK with ANALYZE on DML queries
  • ✓ Run ANALYZE regularly to keep statistics current
  • ✓ Compare costs only within the same query's different plans
  • ✓ Run queries multiple times to account for cache warming
  • ✓ Use BUFFERS to understand cache behavior
  • ✓ Look for big estimate vs actual discrepancies
  • ✓ Watch for nested loops with high loop counts
  • ✓ Consider FORMAT JSON for automated analysis

14.2 Statistics Used by the Planner

Query Planner Intelligence: PostgreSQL's query planner uses statistics about your data to choose optimal execution plans. Understanding these statistics helps you ensure accurate planning and identify when statistics need updating.

14.2.1 Single-Column Statistics

Per-Column Statistics: PostgreSQL collects statistics for each column: number of distinct values, null fraction, most common values, histogram of data distribution. The ANALYZE command samples rows to gather these statistics.

🎯 Example 1: Viewing Statistics

-- View statistics for a specific column
SELECT 
    attname AS column_name,
    n_distinct,  -- Number of distinct values (-1 = all unique, positive = actual count, negative = fraction)
    null_frac,   -- Fraction of NULL values
    avg_width,   -- Average width in bytes
    correlation  -- Physical vs logical order correlation (-1 to 1)
FROM pg_stats
WHERE tablename = 'products' 
  AND schemaname = 'public'
  AND attname = 'category_id';

-- Example output:
-- column_name | n_distinct | null_frac | avg_width | correlation
-- -----------+------------+-----------+-----------+-------------
-- category_id | 10         | 0          | 4         | 0.85

-- View most common values
SELECT 
    attname,
    most_common_vals,      -- Array of most frequent values
    most_common_freqs      -- Their frequencies
FROM pg_stats
WHERE tablename = 'products' AND attname = 'category_id';
Interpretation: n_distinct=10 means 10 unique categories. null_frac=0 means no NULLs. correlation=0.85 means data is mostly ordered by category_id (helps estimate index scan costs).

🎯 Example 2: Running ANALYZE

-- Analyze entire database
ANALYZE;

-- Analyze specific table
ANALYZE products;

-- Analyze specific columns only (faster)
ANALYZE products (category_id, price);

-- Verbose output
ANALYZE VERBOSE products;
-- INFO:  analyzing "public.products"
-- INFO:  "products": scanned 125 of 125 pages, containing 10000 live rows...

-- Check when last analyzed
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    last_analyze,
    last_autoanalyze,
    n_live_tup,
    n_dead_tup
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE tablename = 'products';

🎯 Example 3: Adjusting Statistics Target

-- Check current statistics target (default: 100)
SHOW default_statistics_target;
-- default_statistics_target
-- -------------------------
-- 100

-- Increase for better accuracy (more samples, slower ANALYZE)
SET default_statistics_target = 1000;

-- Set per-column (for important columns with many distinct values)
ALTER TABLE products 
ALTER COLUMN product_id SET STATISTICS 1000;

ALTER TABLE products 
ALTER COLUMN price SET STATISTICS 500;

-- Now ANALYZE collects more detailed statistics
ANALYZE products;

-- Check histogram bins
SELECT 
    attname,
    array_length(histogram_bounds, 1) AS num_bins
FROM pg_stats
WHERE tablename = 'products' AND attname = 'price';
-- More bins = better selectivity estimates

🎯 Example 4: Detecting Stale Statistics

-- Find tables that need ANALYZE
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    n_live_tup,
    n_dead_tup,
    n_mod_since_analyze,  -- Modifications since last ANALYZE
    last_analyze,
    last_autoanalyze,
    CASE 
        WHEN last_analyze IS NULL THEN 'NEVER ANALYZED'
        WHEN n_mod_since_analyze > n_live_tup * 0.1 THEN 'NEEDS ANALYZE'
        ELSE 'OK'
    END AS status
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY n_mod_since_analyze DESC;
Example Output:
 tablename | n_live_tup | n_mod_since_analyze | status
-----------+------------+--------------------+---------------
 products  | 10000      | 1500               | NEEDS ANALYZE
 orders    | 50000      | 2000               | OK
 customers | 5000       | 0                  | OK

14.2.2 Extended Statistics

Multi-Column Statistics: Extended statistics capture correlations between columns. Useful when query planner needs to understand how columns relate to each other (e.g., city and state, or product category and price range).

🎯 Example 1: Creating Extended Statistics

-- Problem: Planner assumes columns are independent
EXPLAIN 
SELECT * FROM orders 
WHERE customer_id = 100 
  AND order_date >= '2024-01-01';
-- Planner multiplies selectivity: 
-- (customers with id=100) * (orders after 2024-01-01)
-- But customer 100's orders might all be recent!

-- Create extended statistics to capture correlation
CREATE STATISTICS orders_customer_date_stats (dependencies)
ON customer_id, order_date 
FROM orders;

-- Run ANALYZE to collect the statistics
ANALYZE orders;

-- Now planner understands correlation
EXPLAIN 
SELECT * FROM orders 
WHERE customer_id = 100 
  AND order_date >= '2024-01-01';
-- Better row estimate!

🎯 Example 2: Multi-Column Distinct Statistics

-- Problem: Estimating distinct combinations
-- Planner assumes: distinct(a,b) ≈ distinct(a) * distinct(b)
-- Reality: Combinations might be limited

CREATE TABLE addresses (
    address_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    city VARCHAR(100),
    state CHAR(2),
    zip_code VARCHAR(10)
);

-- Create n-distinct statistics for city+state combination
CREATE STATISTICS addr_city_state_stats (ndistinct)
ON city, state 
FROM addresses;

ANALYZE addresses;

-- Query with both columns
EXPLAIN 
SELECT DISTINCT city, state FROM addresses;
-- Now has accurate estimate of unique city/state pairs

🎯 Example 3: Most Common Value (MCV) Lists

-- Problem: Correlated most common values
-- Products in category 1 tend to be priced $50-$100
-- Products in category 2 tend to be priced $500-$1000

-- Create MCV extended statistics
CREATE STATISTICS products_cat_price_mcv (mcv)
ON category_id, price 
FROM products;

ANALYZE products;

-- Query benefits from knowing common combinations
EXPLAIN 
SELECT * FROM products 
WHERE category_id = 1 AND price BETWEEN 50 AND 100;
-- Better estimate because it knows this combo is common

🎯 Example 4: Viewing Extended Statistics

-- List all extended statistics
SELECT 
    stxname AS stats_name,
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    stxkeys AS column_numbers,
    (SELECT string_agg(attname, ', ')
     FROM pg_attribute
     WHERE attrelid = stxrelid 
       AND attnum = ANY(stxkeys)) AS columns
FROM pg_statistic_ext_stats
JOIN pg_statistic_ext ON stxoid = stxoid
JOIN pg_class ON stxrelid = pg_class.oid
JOIN pg_namespace ON relnamespace = pg_namespace.oid
WHERE schemaname = 'public';

-- View dependencies
SELECT 
    stxname,
    stxdependencies
FROM pg_statistic_ext_stats
WHERE stxname = 'orders_customer_date_stats';

-- Drop extended statistics if needed
DROP STATISTICS orders_customer_date_stats;

📊 Statistics Types Summary

Type Purpose When to Use
dependencies Functional dependencies Columns with strong correlation
ndistinct Distinct value combinations GROUP BY on multiple columns
mcv Most common value lists WHERE with multiple columns

14.3 Controlling the Planner with Explicit JOIN Clauses

Join Order Control: The query planner usually chooses good join orders automatically, but you can influence its decisions using explicit JOIN syntax. This is useful when you know the data characteristics better than the planner, or when statistics are unreliable.

🎯 Example 1: Implicit vs Explicit Joins

-- Implicit join (planner chooses order)
SELECT *
FROM orders, customers, products, order_items
WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id
  AND order_items.order_id = orders.order_id
  AND order_items.product_id = products.product_id
  AND orders.order_date >= '2024-01-01';

-- Planner can join in ANY order: 4! = 24 possibilities
-- It will evaluate many combinations and pick best

-- Explicit join (suggests join order)
SELECT *
FROM orders
JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id
JOIN order_items ON order_items.order_id = orders.order_id
JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id
WHERE orders.order_date >= '2024-01-01';

-- Suggests: orders → customers → order_items → products
-- But planner can still reorder if it finds better plan!

🎯 Example 2: Forcing Join Order

-- Disable join reordering (use with caution!)
SET join_collapse_limit = 1;
SET from_collapse_limit = 1;

-- Now planner MUST follow your join order
SELECT *
FROM orders
JOIN order_items ON order_items.order_id = orders.order_id
JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id
WHERE orders.order_date >= '2024-01-01';

-- Reset to default (planner chooses)
RESET join_collapse_limit;
RESET from_collapse_limit;
Warning: Forcing join order disables optimizer intelligence. Only use when you're certain the planner is making poor choices, and test thoroughly!

🎯 Example 3: Strategic Join Ordering

-- BAD: Start with large table
SELECT *
FROM products  -- 1,000,000 rows
JOIN order_items ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id
JOIN orders ON order_items.order_id = orders.order_id
WHERE orders.customer_id = 100;  -- Filters to 10 orders

-- GOOD: Start with most selective filter
SELECT *
FROM orders  -- Start here: customer_id = 100 gives ~10 rows
JOIN order_items ON order_items.order_id = orders.order_id
JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id
WHERE orders.customer_id = 100;

-- Compare plans
EXPLAIN ANALYZE -- First query
-- vs
EXPLAIN ANALYZE -- Second query

🎯 Example 4: LEFT JOIN Ordering

-- LEFT JOIN order matters more
-- This works (customers on left)
SELECT c.customer_name, COUNT(o.order_id)
FROM customers c  -- Keep all customers
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name;

-- This is DIFFERENT (orders on left)
SELECT c.customer_name, COUNT(o.order_id)
FROM orders o  -- Keep all orders
LEFT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name;
-- Different semantics! Planner cannot reorder LEFT JOINs freely

🎯 Example 5: Subquery vs JOIN

-- Sometimes subquery is better
-- If filtering heavily, IN subquery might be faster
SELECT *
FROM products
WHERE product_id IN (
    SELECT product_id 
    FROM order_items 
    WHERE order_id IN (
        SELECT order_id 
        FROM orders 
        WHERE customer_id = 100
    )
);

-- Equivalent join (planner might choose same plan)
SELECT DISTINCT p.*
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.customer_id = 100;

-- Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to compare!
Best Practice: Start with explicit JOIN syntax for readability. Only force join order when profiling shows planner is consistently wrong. Always measure with EXPLAIN ANALYZE - don't guess!

14.4 Populating a Database

Bulk Loading Optimization: Loading large amounts of data requires different strategies than normal OLTP operations. This section covers techniques to maximize throughput during initial data loads or bulk imports.

⚡ Bulk Load Performance Checklist

Before Loading:
  • Drop indexes
  • Drop foreign keys
  • Disable autocommit
  • Increase maintenance_work_mem
  • Increase max_wal_size
After Loading:
  • Recreate indexes
  • Recreate foreign keys
  • Run ANALYZE
  • Reset configuration
  • VACUUM if needed

14.4.1 Disable Autocommit

Transaction Overhead: Each COMMIT forces a disk sync (fsync). Auto-committing every INSERT is extremely slow. Group thousands of INSERTs into one transaction for massive speedup.

🎯 Example: Autocommit Impact

-- SLOW: Autocommit (each INSERT is its own transaction)
-- In psql, autocommit is ON by default
INSERT INTO products VALUES (1, 'Product 1', 10.00, 1, 100);
INSERT INTO products VALUES (2, 'Product 2', 20.00, 1, 200);
-- ... 10,000 more
-- Each INSERT = 1 fsync = ~1ms
-- Total: 10,000ms = 10 seconds!

-- FAST: Wrap in single transaction
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO products VALUES (1, 'Product 1', 10.00, 1, 100);
INSERT INTO products VALUES (2, 'Product 2', 20.00, 1, 200);
-- ... 10,000 more
COMMIT;  -- Only 1 fsync
-- Total: ~100ms = 100x faster!

-- In psql: disable autocommit
\set AUTOCOMMIT off
-- Now must explicitly COMMIT

14.4.2 Use COPY

Fastest Bulk Load: COPY is PostgreSQL's bulk data loading command. It's 10-100x faster than INSERT for large datasets because it bypasses much of the SQL parsing and uses optimized binary format.

🎯 Example 1: COPY FROM File

-- Create CSV file: products.csv
-- 1,Laptop,999.00,1,50
-- 2,Mouse,25.00,2,200
-- 3,Keyboard,75.00,2,150

-- COPY from file (fast!)
COPY products (product_id, name, price, category_id, stock_quantity)
FROM '/path/to/products.csv'
WITH (FORMAT CSV, HEADER false);

-- With header row
COPY products (product_id, name, price, category_id, stock_quantity)
FROM '/path/to/products_with_header.csv'
WITH (FORMAT CSV, HEADER true);

-- Tab-delimited
COPY products
FROM '/path/to/products.tsv'
WITH (FORMAT TEXT, DELIMITER E'\t');

🎯 Example 2: COPY FROM Program

-- COPY from command output
COPY products
FROM PROGRAM 'curl https://example.com/products.csv'
WITH (FORMAT CSV);

-- COPY from compressed file
COPY products
FROM PROGRAM 'gunzip -c /data/products.csv.gz'
WITH (FORMAT CSV);

-- COPY from stdin (useful in scripts)
COPY products FROM STDIN WITH (FORMAT CSV);
1,Product 1,10.00,1,100
2,Product 2,20.00,1,200
\.  -- End with backslash-dot

🎯 Example 3: Performance Comparison

-- Method 1: Individual INSERTs (SLOWEST)
-- ~10,000 rows/second
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test VALUES (1, 'data');
INSERT INTO test VALUES (2, 'data');
-- ...
COMMIT;

-- Method 2: Multi-row INSERT (MEDIUM)
-- ~50,000 rows/second
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO test VALUES
    (1, 'data'),
    (2, 'data'),
    (3, 'data');
-- ... batch of 1000
COMMIT;

-- Method 3: COPY (FASTEST!)
-- ~200,000+ rows/second
COPY test FROM '/path/to/data.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);

14.4.3 Remove Indexes

Index Overhead: Every INSERT updates all indexes. Loading with indexes is slow. Drop indexes before bulk load, recreate after - usually faster even for moderate data volumes.

🎯 Example: Index Management

-- Before loading: Save index definitions
SELECT 
    indexdef 
FROM pg_indexes 
WHERE tablename = 'products';
-- Save this output to recreate later

-- Drop indexes
DROP INDEX idx_products_category;
DROP INDEX idx_products_price;
DROP INDEX idx_products_name;

-- Load data (fast without indexes!)
COPY products FROM '/data/products.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);

-- Recreate indexes (use CONCURRENTLY if table is in use)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name ON products(name);

-- Primary key is different - don't drop it!
-- Or if you must, use ALTER TABLE DROP/ADD CONSTRAINT

🎯 Example: Performance Impact

-- With 5 indexes: 10,000 rows/second
-- Without indexes: 100,000 rows/second
-- Recreate indexes after: ~30 seconds
-- 
-- Loading 1,000,000 rows:
-- With indexes: 100 seconds
-- Without + recreate: 10s + 30s = 40 seconds
-- Savings: 60% faster!

14.4.4 Remove Foreign Key Constraints

Constraint Checking Overhead: Foreign keys require lookups in referenced tables for every INSERT. Temporarily dropping them during bulk loads can provide significant speedup, especially for multi-table loads.

🎯 Example: Managing Foreign Keys

-- Save FK definitions
SELECT 
    conname AS constraint_name,
    pg_get_constraintdef(oid) AS definition
FROM pg_constraint
WHERE conrelid = 'order_items'::regclass
  AND contype = 'f';  -- Foreign key

-- Drop foreign keys
ALTER TABLE order_items DROP CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_order;
ALTER TABLE order_items DROP CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_product;

-- Load data (fast!)
COPY orders FROM '/data/orders.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);
COPY order_items FROM '/data/order_items.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);

-- Recreate foreign keys (use NOT VALID for faster creation)
ALTER TABLE order_items 
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_order 
FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES orders(order_id) NOT VALID;

ALTER TABLE order_items 
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_product 
FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES products(product_id) NOT VALID;

-- Validate in background (won't block)
ALTER TABLE order_items VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_order;
ALTER TABLE order_items VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_product;

14.4.5 Increase maintenance_work_mem

Memory for Maintenance: maintenance_work_mem controls memory for CREATE INDEX, VACUUM, and other maintenance operations. Higher values speed up index creation dramatically.

🎯 Example: Tuning Maintenance Memory

-- Check current setting
SHOW maintenance_work_mem;
-- maintenance_work_mem
-- --------------------
-- 64MB (default)

-- Increase for bulk operations (session only)
SET maintenance_work_mem = '2GB';

-- Create index (faster with more memory!)
CREATE INDEX idx_products_name ON products(name);

-- Reset to default
RESET maintenance_work_mem;

-- Or set globally in postgresql.conf
-- maintenance_work_mem = 2GB
-- Then reload config
Sizing Guidance: Set to 10-20% of RAM for bulk operations. Default 64MB is too low for large indexes. For a server with 16GB RAM, try 2-4GB for bulk loads.

14.4.6 Increase max_wal_size

WAL Configuration: Write-Ahead Log (WAL) stores changes before applying them. Larger max_wal_size delays checkpoints, reducing I/O during bulk loads.

🎯 Example: WAL Tuning

-- Check current WAL settings
SHOW max_wal_size;
-- max_wal_size
-- ------------
-- 1GB (default)

-- Increase for bulk load (requires postgresql.conf edit + reload)
-- In postgresql.conf:
-- max_wal_size = 10GB
-- Then: SELECT pg_reload_conf();

-- Monitor checkpoint frequency
SELECT 
    checkpoints_timed,
    checkpoints_req,
    checkpoint_write_time,
    checkpoint_sync_time
FROM pg_stat_bgwriter;

-- If checkpoints_req is high, increase max_wal_size more

14.4.7 Disable WAL Archival and Streaming Replication

Replication Overhead: WAL archival and streaming replication add overhead. For initial loads or rebuilds, temporarily disabling them provides faster loading.

🎯 Example: Disabling Replication

-- In postgresql.conf, before bulk load:
-- wal_level = minimal  (or replica if you need backups)
-- archive_mode = off
-- max_wal_senders = 0

-- Restart PostgreSQL
-- Then load data

-- After load, re-enable:
-- wal_level = replica
-- archive_mode = on
-- max_wal_senders = 10
-- Restart again

-- Note: This is only for initial loads or complete rebuilds
-- Don't do this on production systems with active replication!

14.4.8 Run ANALYZE Afterwards

Update Statistics: After bulk loading, table statistics are stale or missing. Always run ANALYZE to ensure the planner has accurate data for query optimization.

🎯 Example: Post-Load Analysis

-- After bulk load
ANALYZE products;
ANALYZE orders;
ANALYZE order_items;

-- Or analyze everything
ANALYZE;

-- Verbose output
ANALYZE VERBOSE products;
-- INFO:  analyzing "public.products"
-- INFO:  "products": scanned 1250 of 1250 pages...
-- INFO:  "products": 100000 rows sampled, 100000 estimated total rows

-- Check that statistics were updated
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    last_analyze,
    n_live_tup
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE tablename IN ('products', 'orders', 'order_items');

14.4.9 Some Notes about pg_dump

Optimized Dumps: pg_dump already follows many best practices - it outputs data in COPY format by default, and can optionally dump table data before indexes and constraints for faster restores.

🎯 Example: pg_dump Options

-- Standard dump (slow restore)
pg_dump mydb > mydb.sql

-- Optimized for fast restore
pg_dump --format=custom \
        --compress=9 \
        --file=mydb.dump \
        mydb

-- Restore with parallel jobs (fast!)
pg_restore --jobs=4 \
           --dbname=newdb \
           mydb.dump

-- Dump only data (COPY format)
pg_dump --data-only \
        --format=custom \
        --file=data.dump \
        mydb

-- Dump schema separately, data separately
pg_dump --schema-only > schema.sql
pg_dump --data-only --format=custom > data.dump

-- Restore order:
-- 1. Create schema (without indexes/FK)
-- 2. Load data (fast!)
-- 3. Create indexes
-- 4. Add foreign keys

✅ Complete Bulk Load Procedure

-- 1. Prepare database
BEGIN;
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_products_category;
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_products_price;
ALTER TABLE order_items DROP CONSTRAINT IF EXISTS fk_order_items_product;

-- 2. Configure for bulk load
SET maintenance_work_mem = '2GB';

-- 3. Load data
COPY products FROM '/data/products.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);
COPY orders FROM '/data/orders.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);
COPY order_items FROM '/data/order_items.csv' WITH (FORMAT CSV);

-- 4. Recreate structures
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);
ALTER TABLE order_items 
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_order_items_product 
FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES products(product_id);

-- 5. Update statistics
ANALYZE products;
ANALYZE orders;
ANALYZE order_items;

COMMIT;

-- 6. Reset configuration
RESET maintenance_work_mem;

14.5 Non-Durable Settings

⚠️ DANGER - Data Loss Risk: Non-durable settings maximize performance by sacrificing crash safety. Use ONLY for temporary/rebuildable data, testing, or initial loads where you can afford to lose data. NEVER use in production with important data!

🎯 Setting 1: fsync = off

-- EXTREME DANGER: Disables disk synchronization
-- In postgresql.conf:
-- fsync = off

-- Speedup: 2-10x faster writes
-- Risk: Data corruption if server crashes or loses power
-- Use case: Initial load of disposable test data only

-- Example speed difference:
-- With fsync=on:  10,000 commits/second
-- With fsync=off: 100,000 commits/second

-- ALWAYS re-enable after:
-- fsync = on
-- Then restart PostgreSQL

🎯 Setting 2: synchronous_commit = off

-- Less dangerous than fsync=off
-- Allows COMMIT to return before WAL is written to disk
SET synchronous_commit = off;

-- Speedup: 2-3x faster commits
-- Risk: Can lose last few committed transactions (not data corruption)
-- Recovery window: Usually < 1 second

-- Good for:
-- - Logging/audit data
-- - Analytics where occasional loss is acceptable
-- - High-throughput systems where slight data loss is OK

-- Session-level setting (safe)
BEGIN;
SET LOCAL synchronous_commit = off;
INSERT INTO logs VALUES (...);  -- Fast commits
COMMIT;

-- Next transaction uses default setting

🎯 Setting 3: full_page_writes = off

-- Disables full-page writes in WAL
-- In postgresql.conf:
-- full_page_writes = off

-- Speedup: 10-20% faster, less WAL volume
-- Risk: Torn pages possible if crash during write
-- Use case: Combined with filesystem that prevents torn pages,
--           or for disposable data

-- Don't use unless you have:
-- - ZFS or similar filesystem with atomic writes
-- - Battery-backed write cache
-- - Disposable/rebuildable data

🎯 Setting 4: Unlogged Tables

-- Create table without WAL logging
CREATE UNLOGGED TABLE temp_staging (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    data TEXT,
    processed BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
);

-- Advantages:
-- - Much faster writes (no WAL overhead)
-- - Good for temporary/staging data
-- - Survives normal shutdown

-- Disadvantages:
-- - Truncated on crash recovery
-- - Not replicated to standbys
-- - Cannot have foreign keys to/from logged tables

-- Example use case: ETL staging
CREATE UNLOGGED TABLE staging_products AS
SELECT * FROM external_source;

-- Process and copy to permanent table
INSERT INTO products 
SELECT * FROM staging_products WHERE is_valid = TRUE;

-- Drop staging
DROP TABLE staging_products;

-- Convert existing table to unlogged (DANGER!)
ALTER TABLE my_table SET UNLOGGED;

-- Convert back to logged
ALTER TABLE my_table SET LOGGED;

🎯 Setting 5: Increasing checkpoint_timeout and max_wal_size

-- Reduce checkpoint frequency (less I/O)
-- In postgresql.conf:

-- Default values
-- checkpoint_timeout = 5min
-- max_wal_size = 1GB

-- Aggressive for bulk loads
-- checkpoint_timeout = 30min
-- max_wal_size = 10GB

-- Trade-off:
-- + Faster bulk loads (fewer checkpoints)
-- - Longer recovery time if crash
-- - More disk space for WAL

-- Monitor checkpoint statistics
SELECT 
    checkpoints_timed,      -- Scheduled checkpoints
    checkpoints_req,        -- Forced checkpoints (bad if high)
    checkpoint_write_time,  -- Time writing dirty buffers
    checkpoint_sync_time,   -- Time syncing to disk
    buffers_checkpoint,     -- Buffers written during checkpoints
    buffers_backend         -- Buffers written by backends (bad if high)
FROM pg_stat_bgwriter;

-- If checkpoints_req / checkpoints_timed > 0.5, increase max_wal_size

🎯 Example: Complete Non-Durable Setup

-- For INITIAL LOAD ONLY (can afford complete loss)

-- In postgresql.conf:
-- fsync = off
-- synchronous_commit = off
-- full_page_writes = off
-- checkpoint_timeout = 30min
-- max_wal_size = 10GB
-- wal_level = minimal
-- archive_mode = off

-- Restart PostgreSQL, then load data
\copy products FROM 'products.csv' CSV

-- AFTER LOADING, restore safe settings:
-- fsync = on
-- synchronous_commit = on
-- full_page_writes = on
-- checkpoint_timeout = 5min
-- max_wal_size = 1GB
-- wal_level = replica

-- Restart PostgreSQL again

-- Run CHECKPOINT to ensure everything is flushed
CHECKPOINT;

-- Verify data integrity
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products;
ANALYZE products;

⚠️ Non-Durable Settings Safety Matrix

Setting Speedup Risk Level Safe For
synchronous_commit=off 2-3x Medium Logs, analytics, caches
Unlogged tables 3-5x Medium Staging, temp data
full_page_writes=off 10-20% High ZFS/special hardware
fsync=off 2-10x EXTREME Initial loads ONLY

✅ Chapter 14 Summary

This chapter covered essential PostgreSQL performance optimization techniques:

  • EXPLAIN: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to understand query execution and identify bottlenecks
  • Statistics: Keep statistics current with ANALYZE; use extended statistics for correlated columns
  • Join Planning: Use explicit JOINs for clarity; only force order when necessary
  • Bulk Loading: Disable autocommit, use COPY, drop indexes/FK temporarily, increase memory
  • Non-Durable: Use with extreme caution; only for disposable data or initial loads

Key Takeaway: Measure before optimizing! Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify actual bottlenecks. Different workloads need different optimizations - bulk loads need different settings than OLTP. Always test performance changes and understand the trade-offs.

⚡ Chapter 15. Parallel Query

PostgreSQL can devise query plans that leverage multiple CPUs to answer queries faster. This feature is called parallel query. This chapter explains how parallel query works, when it can be used, and best practices for optimal performance.

15.1. How Parallel Query Works

When the optimizer determines that parallel query is the fastest execution strategy for a particular query, it creates a query plan that includes a Gather or Gather Merge node. Here's how it works:

🔹 Parallel Query Architecture

1. Leader Process: The backend process that executes the query becomes the leader

2. Worker Processes: The leader recruits one or more background worker processes

3. Parallel Execution: Leader and workers cooperate to execute the plan

4. Gather Results: The Gather/Gather Merge node collects results from all workers

Example: Parallel Sequential Scan

-- Create a large table
CREATE TABLE big_table AS 
SELECT generate_series(1, 10000000) AS id,
       md5(random()::text) AS data;

-- Run parallel query
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM big_table WHERE data LIKE 'a%';

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Finalize Aggregate  (cost=... rows=1 ...)
--   ->  Gather  (cost=... rows=2 ...)
--         Workers Planned: 2
--         Workers Launched: 2
--         ->  Partial Aggregate  (cost=... rows=1 ...)
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on big_table  (cost=... rows=...)
--                     Filter: (data ~~ 'a%'::text)

✅ Key Concepts

Component Description
Gather Node Collects results from parallel workers; unordered output
Gather Merge Node Collects and merges pre-sorted results from workers; ordered output
Partial Aggregate Each worker computes partial aggregate; leader finalizes
Parallel Aware Plan nodes that can execute in parallel workers

Worker Process Lifecycle

-- Check worker configuration
SHOW max_worker_processes;        -- Maximum total background workers
SHOW max_parallel_workers;        -- Maximum parallel query workers
SHOW max_parallel_workers_per_gather;  -- Maximum per query

-- Example output:
-- max_worker_processes: 8
-- max_parallel_workers: 2
-- max_parallel_workers_per_gather: 2

⚠️ Important Notes

  • Leader Participation: The leader process itself always participates in executing the plan
  • Dynamic Workers: Number of workers launched may be less than planned if workers unavailable
  • Shared Memory: Workers share dynamic shared memory segment to coordinate
  • Transaction State: Workers inherit transaction snapshot and other state from leader
  • Termination: Workers exit when their portion of the plan completes

Parallel Query Diagram

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Leader Process                         │
│                                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │              Gather / Gather Merge Node              │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│           ↑              ↑              ↑                    │
│           │              │              │                    │
└───────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼────────────────────┘
            │              │              │
      ┌─────┴─────┐  ┌─────┴─────┐  ┌─────┴─────┐
      │  Worker 1 │  │  Worker 2 │  │  Worker N │
      │           │  │           │  │           │
      │ Parallel  │  │ Parallel  │  │ Parallel  │
      │   Scan    │  │   Scan    │  │   Scan    │
      │  Blocks   │  │  Blocks   │  │  Blocks   │
      │   1-100   │  │  101-200  │  │  201-300  │
      └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘

15.2. When Can Parallel Query Be Used?

Parallel query can be used only when certain conditions are met. Understanding these conditions helps you design queries that can benefit from parallelism.

✅ Requirements for Parallel Query

Requirement Description
Dynamic BGWorkers Available max_parallel_workers_per_gather > 0
Not in Transaction Block No explicit BEGIN unless READ ONLY
Read-Only Queries SELECT queries; no data modification
Sufficient Table Size Table larger than min_parallel_table_scan_size
Parallel-Safe Functions All functions marked PARALLEL SAFE or PARALLEL RESTRICTED

Configuration Parameters

-- Enable parallel query (default ON)
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 2;

-- Minimum table size for parallel scan (default 8MB)
SET min_parallel_table_scan_size = '8MB';

-- Minimum index size for parallel scan (default 512kB)
SET min_parallel_index_scan_size = '512kB';

-- Force parallel query (for testing)
SET force_parallel_mode = on;  -- or 'regress'

-- Disable parallel query
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0;

Example: When Parallel Query IS Used

-- Large table, simple read-only query
EXPLAIN SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table;
-- Finalize Aggregate
--   ->  Gather
--         Workers Planned: 2
--         ->  Partial Aggregate
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_table

-- Read-only transaction with sufficient data
BEGIN READ ONLY;
  EXPLAIN SELECT avg(value) FROM big_data WHERE category = 'A';
  -- Finalize Aggregate
  --   ->  Gather
  --         Workers Planned: 2
COMMIT;

Example: When Parallel Query is NOT Used

-- Too small table (< 8MB)
CREATE TABLE small_table AS SELECT generate_series(1, 1000) AS id;
EXPLAIN SELECT COUNT(*) FROM small_table;
-- Aggregate
--   ->  Seq Scan on small_table
-- (No parallel workers)

-- In a read-write transaction block
BEGIN;
  EXPLAIN SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table;
  -- Aggregate
  --   ->  Seq Scan on large_table
  -- (No parallel workers - transaction not READ ONLY)
COMMIT;

-- Function not marked parallel-safe
CREATE FUNCTION my_func(int) RETURNS int AS $$
BEGIN
  RETURN $1 * 2;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;  -- Default: PARALLEL UNSAFE

EXPLAIN SELECT my_func(id) FROM large_table;
-- Seq Scan on large_table
-- (No parallel workers - function unsafe)

-- Make it parallel-safe
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION my_func(int) RETURNS int AS $$
BEGIN
  RETURN $1 * 2;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql PARALLEL SAFE;

EXPLAIN SELECT my_func(id) FROM large_table;
-- Gather
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_table

⚠️ Common Scenarios That Prevent Parallelism

  • Data Modification: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, COPY cannot use parallel query
  • Cursors: Queries declared in cursors cannot be parallelized
  • Parallel-Unsafe Functions: Any PARALLEL UNSAFE function blocks parallelism
  • Temporary Tables: Queries on temp tables cannot be parallelized
  • CTEs: WITH queries (CTEs) generally cannot be parallelized (except in PostgreSQL 12+)
  • Serializable Isolation: Serializable isolation level may prevent parallelism
  • Foreign Tables: Foreign tables need parallel-capable FDW
  • InitPlans/SubPlans: Certain subplan types block parallelism

Checking Parallel Query Eligibility

-- View current parallel settings
SELECT name, setting, unit, short_desc 
FROM pg_settings 
WHERE name LIKE '%parallel%';

-- Check if a query uses parallel workers
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) 
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table;

-- Look for:
-- "Workers Planned: N" - planner decided to use N workers
-- "Workers Launched: N" - N workers were actually started
-- "Parallel Seq Scan" or "Parallel Index Scan" - parallel scan used

-- Check function parallel safety
SELECT proname, proparallel 
FROM pg_proc 
WHERE proname = 'my_func';

-- proparallel values:
-- 's' = PARALLEL SAFE
-- 'r' = PARALLEL RESTRICTED
-- 'u' = PARALLEL UNSAFE

💡 Best Practices

  • Mark Functions Correctly: Always specify parallel safety (SAFE/RESTRICTED/UNSAFE) for custom functions
  • Use READ ONLY: Explicitly declare read-only transactions to enable parallelism
  • Test with EXPLAIN: Always use EXPLAIN to verify parallel query is being used
  • Tune min_parallel_*: Adjust minimum sizes based on your workload and hardware
  • Monitor Workers: Check that planned workers are actually launched
  • Consider Costs: Parallel query has startup overhead; beneficial for large datasets only

15.3. Parallel Plans

The PostgreSQL query planner can generate various types of parallel plans depending on the query structure and data access patterns. This section covers different parallel plan types and their characteristics.

15.3.1. Parallel Scans

Parallel scans divide table or index data among multiple workers. Each worker processes a portion of the data independently.

Parallel Sequential Scan
-- Example: Parallel Seq Scan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM large_orders WHERE amount > 1000;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   Workers Launched: 2
--   ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders  (cost=... rows=...)
--         Filter: (amount > 1000)
--         Rows Removed by Filter: ...

How it works: Each worker scans different blocks of the table. The planner tries to divide work evenly based on block ranges.

Parallel Index Scan
-- Create index
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_date ON large_orders(order_date);

-- Example: Parallel Index Scan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM large_orders 
WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01' 
ORDER BY order_date;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather Merge  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   Workers Launched: 2
--   ->  Parallel Index Scan using idx_orders_date on large_orders
--         Index Cond: (order_date >= '2024-01-01'::date)

Note: Parallel index scans use Gather Merge when the order must be preserved.

Parallel Bitmap Heap Scan
-- Example: Parallel Bitmap Heap Scan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM large_orders 
WHERE status = 'pending' OR priority = 'high';

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   Workers Launched: 2
--   ->  Parallel Bitmap Heap Scan on large_orders
--         Recheck Cond: ((status = 'pending') OR (priority = 'high'))
--         ->  BitmapOr
--               ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_status
--               ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_priority

Use case: Efficient for queries using multiple indexes combined with OR conditions.

15.3.2. Parallel Joins

Joins can be parallelized in several ways. The query planner chooses the best parallel join strategy based on table sizes and join conditions.

Parallel Hash Join
-- Example: Parallel Hash Join
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT o.order_id, c.customer_name, o.amount
FROM large_orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01';

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   Workers Launched: 2
--   ->  Parallel Hash Join  (cost=... rows=...)
--         Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.customer_id)
--         ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders o
--               Filter: (order_date >= '2024-01-01'::date)
--         ->  Parallel Hash
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on customers c

How it works: Workers cooperatively build a shared hash table, then each probes it with their portion of the outer relation.

Parallel Nested Loop Join
-- Example: Parallel Nested Loop
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT o.order_id, p.product_name
FROM small_products p
JOIN large_orders o ON p.product_id = o.product_id;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   ->  Nested Loop
--         ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders o
--         ->  Index Scan using products_pkey on small_products p
--               Index Cond: (product_id = o.product_id)

Use case: Efficient when one table is small and the other is large with an index on the join key.

Parallel Merge Join
-- Example: Parallel Merge Join (both inputs sorted)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT o1.order_id, o2.order_id
FROM large_orders o1
JOIN large_orders o2 ON o1.customer_id = o2.customer_id
WHERE o1.order_id < o2.order_id;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   ->  Parallel Merge Join
--         Merge Cond: (o1.customer_id = o2.customer_id)
--         Join Filter: (o1.order_id < o2.order_id)
--         ->  Sort
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders o1
--         ->  Sort
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders o2

Requirement: Both inputs must be sorted on the join key. Can be very efficient for pre-sorted data.

15.3.3. Parallel Aggregation

Aggregation queries can benefit significantly from parallelism. PostgreSQL supports both simple and grouped parallel aggregation.

Simple Parallel Aggregation
-- Example: COUNT, SUM, AVG
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(amount), AVG(amount)
FROM large_orders;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Finalize Aggregate  (cost=... rows=1)
--   ->  Gather  (cost=... rows=2)
--         Workers Planned: 2
--         Workers Launched: 2
--         ->  Partial Aggregate  (cost=... rows=1)
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders

How it works: Each worker computes a partial aggregate. The leader finalizes by combining partial results.

Grouped Parallel Aggregation
-- Example: GROUP BY with parallel aggregation
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*), SUM(amount)
FROM large_orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Finalize GroupAggregate  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Group Key: customer_id
--   ->  Gather Merge  (cost=... rows=...)
--         Workers Planned: 2
--         Workers Launched: 2
--         ->  Partial GroupAggregate  (cost=... rows=...)
--               Group Key: customer_id
--               ->  Sort
--                     Sort Key: customer_id
--                     ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders

Note: Each worker groups its portion of data; Gather Merge combines and finalizes groups.

Parallel Hash Aggregation
-- Example: Hash-based aggregation
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT status, priority, COUNT(*), AVG(amount)
FROM large_orders
GROUP BY status, priority;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Finalize HashAggregate  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Group Key: status, priority
--   ->  Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--         Workers Planned: 2
--         Workers Launched: 2
--         ->  Partial HashAggregate  (cost=... rows=...)
--               Group Key: status, priority
--               ->  Parallel Seq Scan on large_orders

Use case: More efficient than sort-based aggregation when many groups exist.

✅ Supported Aggregate Functions
Function Parallel Support Notes
COUNT, SUM, AVG ✅ Full Fully parallelizable
MIN, MAX ✅ Full Combine partial results
ARRAY_AGG ⚠️ Partial Order may vary
STRING_AGG ⚠️ Partial Order may vary without ORDER BY
User-Defined ⚠️ Depends Must be marked PARALLEL SAFE

15.3.4. Parallel Append

Parallel Append is used when scanning multiple tables or partitions. Workers divide the tables/partitions among themselves.

Partitioned Table Example
-- Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE orders_partitioned (
    order_id SERIAL,
    order_date DATE,
    amount NUMERIC
) PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date);

CREATE TABLE orders_2023 PARTITION OF orders_partitioned
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2023-01-01') TO ('2024-01-01');
CREATE TABLE orders_2024 PARTITION OF orders_partitioned
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2025-01-01');

-- Query with Parallel Append
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(amount)
FROM orders_partitioned
WHERE order_date >= '2023-06-01';

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Finalize Aggregate  (cost=... rows=1)
--   ->  Gather  (cost=... rows=2)
--         Workers Planned: 2
--         Workers Launched: 2
--         ->  Partial Aggregate
--               ->  Parallel Append
--                     ->  Parallel Seq Scan on orders_2023
--                           Filter: (order_date >= '2023-06-01')
--                     ->  Parallel Seq Scan on orders_2024
--                           Filter: (order_date >= '2023-06-01')

How it works: Each worker takes a partition and scans it. Load balancing ensures even distribution.

UNION ALL Example
-- Parallel Append with UNION ALL
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT order_id, 'old' AS source FROM old_orders
UNION ALL
SELECT order_id, 'new' AS source FROM new_orders;

-- QUERY PLAN
-- Gather  (cost=... rows=...)
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   Workers Launched: 2
--   ->  Parallel Append
--         ->  Parallel Seq Scan on old_orders
--         ->  Parallel Seq Scan on new_orders

Note: UNION (without ALL) cannot be parallelized due to duplicate elimination requirement.

⚠️ Parallel Append Considerations
  • Load Balancing: Works best with similar-sized partitions
  • Partition Pruning: Fewer partitions = fewer append branches = better performance
  • Worker Distribution: Each worker can scan multiple partitions if needed
  • Subplans: Each append branch can itself be a parallel plan

15.3.5. Parallel Plan Tips

Best practices and tips for getting the most out of parallel query plans.

💡 Optimization Tips
  • Cost Model Tuning: Adjust parallel_tuple_cost and parallel_setup_cost to match your hardware
  • Worker Count: More workers aren't always better; test to find optimal count
  • Statistics: Keep table statistics up-to-date with ANALYZE for accurate cost estimates
  • Indexes: Parallel index scans can be faster than parallel seq scans for selective queries
  • Work Mem: Each worker gets work_mem; consider total memory usage
  • Connection Pooling: Parallel workers count against max_connections; plan accordingly
Configuration Tuning
-- Encourage more parallel plans (lower costs)
SET parallel_setup_cost = 100;    -- Default: 1000
SET parallel_tuple_cost = 0.01;   -- Default: 0.1

-- Discourage parallel plans (higher costs)
SET parallel_setup_cost = 10000;
SET parallel_tuple_cost = 1;

-- Adjust minimum sizes for specific queries
SET min_parallel_table_scan_size = '1MB';  -- Smaller threshold
SET min_parallel_index_scan_size = '64kB';

-- Force parallel for testing
SET force_parallel_mode = on;
SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4;
Monitoring Parallel Queries
-- Check active parallel queries
SELECT pid, query, state, backend_type
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_type = 'parallel worker';

-- View parallel query statistics
SELECT query, calls, total_exec_time, parallel_workers_to_launch
FROM pg_stat_statements
WHERE parallel_workers_to_launch > 0
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Check worker usage
SELECT count(*) AS active_parallel_workers
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_type = 'parallel worker';
❌ Common Pitfalls
  • Over-Parallelization: Too many workers can hurt performance due to coordination overhead
  • Small Data: Parallel query overhead exceeds benefits for small datasets
  • Memory Pressure: Each worker needs work_mem; can cause swapping
  • CPU Bottleneck: More workers than CPU cores reduces efficiency
  • Lock Contention: Parallel workers can create lock contention on shared resources
  • Unsafe Functions: Forgetting to mark functions PARALLEL SAFE blocks parallelism

15.4. Parallel Safety

For a query to use parallel query, all functions and expressions involved must be parallel-safe. Understanding parallel safety is critical for writing code that can leverage parallelism.

Parallel Safety Levels

Level Code Description Use Case
PARALLEL SAFE s Can run in parallel worker without restriction Pure functions with no side effects
PARALLEL RESTRICTED r Can run in parallel but only in leader process Functions accessing temp tables or session state
PARALLEL UNSAFE u Cannot be used in parallel query at all Functions with global side effects or transactions

15.4.1. Parallel Labeling for Functions and Aggregates

When creating functions and aggregates, you must explicitly specify their parallel safety level. The default is PARALLEL UNSAFE, which prevents parallelism.

Declaring Parallel-Safe Functions
-- PARALLEL SAFE: Pure function, no side effects
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION calculate_discount(price NUMERIC, rate NUMERIC)
RETURNS NUMERIC AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN price * (1 - rate);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql PARALLEL SAFE;

-- PARALLEL RESTRICTED: Accesses session variables
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_current_user_discount()
RETURNS NUMERIC AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN current_setting('app.user_discount')::NUMERIC;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql PARALLEL RESTRICTED;

-- PARALLEL UNSAFE: Has side effects (writes to table)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_query(query_text TEXT)
RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO query_log (query, logged_at) VALUES (query_text, NOW());
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql PARALLEL UNSAFE;

-- SQL function (automatically determined)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION double_value(x INT)
RETURNS INT AS $$
    SELECT x * 2;
$$ LANGUAGE sql PARALLEL SAFE;
Declaring Parallel-Safe Aggregates
-- Custom aggregate with parallel support
CREATE AGGREGATE custom_sum(NUMERIC) (
    SFUNC = numeric_add,
    STYPE = NUMERIC,
    INITCOND = '0',
    PARALLEL = SAFE,           -- Mark as parallel-safe
    COMBINEFUNC = numeric_add, -- Function to combine partial results
    FINALFUNC = my_final_func  -- Optional final transformation
);

-- Example usage
SELECT custom_sum(amount) FROM large_orders;
-- Can use parallel workers if other conditions met
Checking Parallel Safety
-- Check function parallel safety
SELECT 
    proname AS function_name,
    CASE proparallel
        WHEN 's' THEN 'PARALLEL SAFE'
        WHEN 'r' THEN 'PARALLEL RESTRICTED'
        WHEN 'u' THEN 'PARALLEL UNSAFE'
    END AS parallel_safety
FROM pg_proc
WHERE proname LIKE '%discount%';

-- Check all user-defined functions
SELECT 
    n.nspname AS schema,
    p.proname AS function,
    CASE p.proparallel
        WHEN 's' THEN '✅ SAFE'
        WHEN 'r' THEN '⚠️ RESTRICTED'
        WHEN 'u' THEN '❌ UNSAFE'
    END AS parallel_status
FROM pg_proc p
JOIN pg_namespace n ON p.pronamespace = n.oid
WHERE n.nspname NOT IN ('pg_catalog', 'information_schema')
ORDER BY p.proparallel, p.proname;
✅ Guidelines for PARALLEL SAFE

A function is PARALLEL SAFE if it:

  • No Side Effects: Does not modify database state (no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
  • Deterministic: Always returns the same output for same input
  • No Transaction Commands: Does not use BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK
  • No Sequence Access: Does not call nextval() or currval()
  • No Temp Tables: Does not access temporary tables
  • No Session State: Does not depend on session variables (except STABLE settings)
  • No File I/O: Does not read/write files
  • Safe Dependencies: All called functions are also PARALLEL SAFE
⚠️ Guidelines for PARALLEL RESTRICTED

A function is PARALLEL RESTRICTED if it:

  • Temp Tables: Accesses temporary tables (workers cannot see temp tables)
  • Session Variables: Uses session-specific settings or variables
  • Prepared Statements: Uses prepared statements that may vary by session
  • Can Run in Leader: Safe to execute in the leader process during parallel query
❌ Must be PARALLEL UNSAFE if:
  • Writes Data: Performs INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DDL
  • Transaction Control: Uses transaction commands
  • Sequences: Calls nextval() or setval() on sequences
  • File Operations: Reads or writes files
  • Network Calls: Makes external HTTP/network requests
  • Global State: Modifies global variables or shared memory
Changing Parallel Safety
-- Initially created without specifying (defaults to UNSAFE)
CREATE FUNCTION my_func(x INT) RETURNS INT AS $$
    SELECT x * 2;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Later, mark it as PARALLEL SAFE
ALTER FUNCTION my_func(INT) PARALLEL SAFE;

-- Verify the change
SELECT proname, proparallel FROM pg_proc WHERE proname = 'my_func';

-- Change aggregate parallel safety
ALTER AGGREGATE my_agg(NUMERIC) PARALLEL SAFE;
Real-World Example
-- Before: Function blocks parallelism
CREATE FUNCTION calculate_shipping(weight NUMERIC, distance NUMERIC)
RETURNS NUMERIC AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN weight * distance * 0.05;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;  -- Default: PARALLEL UNSAFE

-- Query cannot use parallel workers
EXPLAIN SELECT calculate_shipping(weight, distance) FROM orders;
-- Seq Scan on orders  (no parallel)

-- After: Mark as PARALLEL SAFE
ALTER FUNCTION calculate_shipping(NUMERIC, NUMERIC) PARALLEL SAFE;

-- Now query CAN use parallel workers
EXPLAIN SELECT calculate_shipping(weight, distance) FROM orders;
-- Gather
--   Workers Planned: 2
--   ->  Parallel Seq Scan on orders

-- Dramatic performance improvement for large tables!
💡 Best Practices
  • Always Specify: Explicitly declare parallel safety for all custom functions
  • Be Conservative: When in doubt, mark PARALLEL RESTRICTED or UNSAFE
  • Review Regularly: Audit functions periodically to mark safe ones appropriately
  • Document Decisions: Comment why a function is marked UNSAFE if not obvious
  • Test Thoroughly: Test parallel queries with force_parallel_mode = on
  • Safe by Default: Write functions without side effects when possible
  • Language Matters: SQL and IMMUTABLE functions are easier to mark SAFE

✅ Chapter 15 Summary

This chapter covered PostgreSQL's parallel query capabilities:

  • How It Works: Leader process recruits workers; Gather/Gather Merge nodes collect results
  • Requirements: Read-only queries, sufficient data size, parallel-safe functions, available workers
  • Parallel Plans: Scans (seq/index/bitmap), joins (hash/nested/merge), aggregation, append
  • Configuration: max_parallel_workers_per_gather, min_parallel_*_size, parallel costs
  • Parallel Safety: SAFE (no restrictions), RESTRICTED (leader only), UNSAFE (blocks parallelism)

Key Takeaway: Parallel query can dramatically improve performance for large data sets and complex aggregations. Always mark custom functions with appropriate parallel safety level. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify parallel execution and tune worker counts based on your hardware. Remember that more workers isn't always better - test to find the optimal configuration for your workload.

🔌 Chapter 36. Extending SQL

PostgreSQL is designed to be easily extensible. You can add your own data types, functions, operators, aggregates, index methods, and procedural languages. This chapter explores the powerful extensibility features that make PostgreSQL adaptable to virtually any application requirement.

36.1. How Extensibility Works

PostgreSQL's extensibility is built on several key principles that allow you to customize the database to fit your specific needs without modifying the core server code.

🔹 Core Extensibility Features

Feature Description Use Case
Data Types Define custom types for domain-specific data Geometric types, network types, custom formats
Functions Create custom logic in SQL, PL/pgSQL, C, etc. Business logic, data transformations
Operators Define custom operators for types Natural syntax for domain operations
Aggregates Custom aggregate functions Statistical functions, custom summaries
Index Methods New index access methods Specialized search capabilities
Procedural Languages Add support for new languages Python, Perl, JavaScript, etc.

Extensibility Architecture

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     PostgreSQL Core                        │
│                                                            │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │             System Catalog Tables                     │ │
│  │  pg_type │ pg_proc │ pg_operator │ pg_aggregate     │ │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                          ↑                                 │
│                          │                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │         Extension Registration System             │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                          ↑                                 │
└──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
       ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐
       │                   │                   │
┌──────┴──────┐    ┌──────┴──────┐    ┌──────┴──────┐
│   SQL/      │    │  Procedural │    │  C Language │
│  PL/pgSQL   │    │  Languages  │    │  Extensions │
│  Functions  │    │  (PL/Perl,  │    │  (Shared    │
│             │    │  PL/Python) │    │  Libraries) │
└─────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘

Simple Extension Example

-- Create a simple custom function
CREATE FUNCTION add_percent(value NUMERIC, percent NUMERIC)
RETURNS NUMERIC AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN value * (1 + percent / 100);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Use the function
SELECT add_percent(100, 15);  -- Returns 115.00

-- Create a custom operator
CREATE OPERATOR +% (
    LEFTARG = NUMERIC,
    RIGHTARG = NUMERIC,
    FUNCTION = add_percent
);

-- Use with natural syntax
SELECT 100 +% 15;  -- Returns 115.00

✅ Key Concepts

  • Catalog-Driven: All extensions are registered in system catalog tables
  • SQL Accessible: Extensions can be created and managed via SQL commands
  • Language Independent: Write extensions in SQL, PL/pgSQL, C, Python, Perl, etc.
  • Modular: Extensions can be packaged and distributed independently
  • Safe: PostgreSQL provides mechanisms to ensure extension safety and isolation
  • Versioned: Extensions support versioning and upgrades

Extension Packaging

-- Install a pre-packaged extension
CREATE EXTENSION postgis;

-- Check available extensions
SELECT * FROM pg_available_extensions ORDER BY name;

-- Check installed extensions
SELECT extname, extversion FROM pg_extension;

-- Update an extension
ALTER EXTENSION postgis UPDATE TO '3.3.3';

-- Remove an extension
DROP EXTENSION postgis CASCADE;

⚠️ Best Practices

  • Use CREATE EXTENSION: Package related objects together for easy management
  • Namespace Wisely: Use schemas to avoid naming conflicts
  • Document Everything: Provide clear documentation for your extensions
  • Version Control: Implement version numbers and upgrade paths
  • Test Thoroughly: Test extensions with different PostgreSQL versions
  • Consider Performance: Profile custom code to avoid bottlenecks

Real-World Extension Examples

-- PostGIS: Spatial database extension
CREATE EXTENSION postgis;
SELECT ST_Distance(
    ST_MakePoint(-73.9857, 40.7484),  -- New York
    ST_MakePoint(-118.2437, 34.0522)   -- Los Angeles
);

-- pg_trgm: Fuzzy string matching
CREATE EXTENSION pg_trgm;
SELECT similarity('PostgreSQL', 'Postgresql');  -- Returns 0.9

-- hstore: Key-value store
CREATE EXTENSION hstore;
SELECT 'name=>Alice, age=>30'::hstore -> 'name';  -- Returns 'Alice'

-- uuid-ossp: UUID generation
CREATE EXTENSION "uuid-ossp";
SELECT uuid_generate_v4();  -- Returns a UUID

36.2. The PostgreSQL Type System

PostgreSQL has a sophisticated type system that categorizes types into several classes. Understanding these categories is essential for effective extensibility.

36.2.1. Base Types

Base types are the fundamental data types that are not defined in terms of other types. They are implemented in C and include both built-in types and user-defined types.

Built-in Base Types
Category Examples
Numeric integer, numeric, real, double precision
String text, varchar, char
Date/Time date, time, timestamp, interval
Boolean boolean
Geometric point, line, polygon, circle
Network inet, cidr, macaddr
Creating a Custom Base Type (Simple Example)
-- Create a custom complex number type
CREATE TYPE complex AS (
    r DOUBLE PRECISION,  -- Real part
    i DOUBLE PRECISION   -- Imaginary part
);

-- Use the type
CREATE TABLE signals (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    frequency NUMERIC,
    amplitude complex
);

-- Insert data
INSERT INTO signals (frequency, amplitude) 
VALUES (440.0, ROW(1.5, 0.8)::complex);

-- Query data
SELECT frequency, (amplitude).r, (amplitude).i FROM signals;

36.2.2. Container Types

Container types hold collections of values of other types. PostgreSQL provides built-in container types for arrays and composite types.

Array Types
-- Any type can be made into an array
CREATE TABLE products (
    name TEXT,
    prices NUMERIC[],        -- Array of prices
    tags TEXT[]              -- Array of tags
);

INSERT INTO products VALUES 
    ('Widget', ARRAY[10.00, 12.50, 15.00], ARRAY['sale', 'new']);

-- Access array elements
SELECT name, prices[1], tags[2] FROM products;

-- Array functions
SELECT name, array_length(prices, 1) AS price_count FROM products;
Composite Types (Row Types)
-- Composite type example
CREATE TYPE address AS (
    street TEXT,
    city TEXT,
    state CHAR(2),
    zip TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE customers (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    billing_address address,
    shipping_address address
);

-- Insert using ROW constructor
INSERT INTO customers (name, billing_address) VALUES 
    ('John Doe', ROW('123 Main St', 'Anytown', 'CA', '12345'));

-- Access composite fields
SELECT name, (billing_address).city FROM customers;

36.2.3. Domains

Domains are user-defined types based on existing types with optional constraints. They're useful for enforcing business rules at the type level.

-- Create domain with constraint
CREATE DOMAIN email_address AS TEXT
CHECK (VALUE ~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}$');

CREATE DOMAIN us_postal_code AS TEXT
CHECK (VALUE ~ '^\d{5}(-\d{4})?$');

CREATE DOMAIN positive_numeric AS NUMERIC
CHECK (VALUE > 0);

-- Use domains in tables
CREATE TABLE contacts (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email email_address,      -- Automatically validated
    zipcode us_postal_code,
    salary positive_numeric
);

-- Valid insert
INSERT INTO contacts (email, zipcode, salary) 
VALUES ('user@example.com', '12345', 50000);

-- This would fail: CHECK constraint violation
-- INSERT INTO contacts (email) VALUES ('invalid-email');
💡 Domain Benefits
  • Centralized validation logic
  • Consistent constraints across tables
  • Self-documenting schema
  • Easy to modify constraints in one place

36.2.4. Pseudo-Types

Pseudo-types are special type declarations used in function signatures. They cannot be used for table columns.

Common Pseudo-Types
Type Description Use Case
record Unspecified row type Generic row return
void No return value Procedures, side effects
trigger Trigger function marker Trigger functions
event_trigger Event trigger marker DDL event triggers
internal Internal-only type C function communication 
-- Function returning void
CREATE FUNCTION log_message(msg TEXT) RETURNS void AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO log_table (message, logged_at) VALUES (msg, NOW());
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Trigger function (returns trigger pseudo-type)
CREATE FUNCTION audit_changes() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO audit_log (table_name, action, changed_at)
    VALUES (TG_TABLE_NAME, TG_OP, NOW());
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

36.2.5. Polymorphic Types

Polymorphic types allow functions to operate on multiple data types without rewriting the function for each type.

Polymorphic Type Keywords
Type Description
anyelement Any specific type; all occurrences must be same type
anyarray Any array type
anynonarray Any non-array type
anyenum Any enum type
anyrange Any range type 
-- Polymorphic function that works with any type
CREATE FUNCTION first_element(arr anyarray) RETURNS anyelement AS $$
    SELECT arr[1];
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Works with different array types
SELECT first_element(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]);           -- Returns 1 (integer)
SELECT first_element(ARRAY['a', 'b', 'c']);     -- Returns 'a' (text)
SELECT first_element(ARRAY[1.5, 2.5, 3.5]);     -- Returns 1.5 (numeric)

-- Polymorphic function with type consistency
CREATE FUNCTION make_array(anyelement, anyelement) 
RETURNS anyarray AS $$
    SELECT ARRAY[$1, $2];
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT make_array(1, 2);              -- Returns {1,2}
SELECT make_array('hello', 'world');  -- Returns {hello,world}
-- SELECT make_array(1, 'text');      -- ERROR: types must match
✅ Polymorphic Type Rules
  • All anyelement parameters must resolve to the same actual type
  • anyarray element type must match anyelement if both used
  • Return type is determined by actual parameter types
  • Enables generic programming in SQL functions

36.3. User-Defined Functions

User-defined functions are the most common form of extensibility. Functions can be written in SQL, PL/pgSQL, C, Python, Perl, and other languages.

Function Languages

Language Best For Performance
SQL Simple queries, data transformations ⚡⚡⚡ Fastest
PL/pgSQL Business logic, control flow ⚡⚡ Very Fast
C Performance-critical, low-level ⚡⚡⚡ Fastest
PL/Python Data science, external APIs ⚡ Moderate

Function Creation Syntax

-- Basic function structure
CREATE [OR REPLACE] FUNCTION function_name(param1 type1, param2 type2)
RETURNS return_type AS $$
    -- Function body
$$ LANGUAGE language_name
[IMMUTABLE | STABLE | VOLATILE]
[PARALLEL SAFE | PARALLEL RESTRICTED | PARALLEL UNSAFE];

-- Example: Calculate compound interest
CREATE FUNCTION compound_interest(
    principal NUMERIC,
    rate NUMERIC,
    years INT
) RETURNS NUMERIC AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN principal * power(1 + rate/100, years);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql IMMUTABLE PARALLEL SAFE;

SELECT compound_interest(1000, 5, 10);  -- Returns 1628.89

✅ Function Best Practices

  • Use Appropriate Volatility: IMMUTABLE for pure functions, STABLE for functions reading DB state, VOLATILE for modifying functions
  • Specify Parallel Safety: Mark as PARALLEL SAFE when possible
  • Name Parameters: Use named parameters for clarity
  • Handle NULL: Consider NULL input behavior (RETURNS NULL ON NULL INPUT)
  • Add COMMENT: Document your functions

36.4. User-Defined Procedures

Procedures are similar to functions but don't return a value and can control transactions. They're useful for administrative tasks and complex operations.

Function vs Procedure

Feature Function Procedure
Return Value ✅ Yes (required) ❌ No return
Invocation SELECT or expression CALL statement
Transaction Control ❌ No COMMIT/ROLLBACK ✅ Can use COMMIT/ROLLBACK
Use in SELECT ✅ Yes ❌ No

Creating Procedures

-- Simple procedure
CREATE PROCEDURE insert_log(message TEXT) AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO system_log (message, created_at) 
    VALUES (message, NOW());
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Call the procedure
CALL insert_log('System started');

-- Procedure with transaction control
CREATE PROCEDURE process_batch() AS $$
DECLARE
    batch_record RECORD;
BEGIN
    FOR batch_record IN SELECT * FROM pending_items LOOP
        BEGIN
            -- Process each item
            INSERT INTO processed_items SELECT * FROM pending_items 
            WHERE id = batch_record.id;
            
            DELETE FROM pending_items WHERE id = batch_record.id;
            
            -- Commit after each item (possible in procedures!)
            COMMIT;
        EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN
            -- Rollback failed item
            ROLLBACK;
            INSERT INTO error_log (item_id, error) 
            VALUES (batch_record.id, SQLERRM);
            COMMIT;
        END;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Procedure with OUT parameters
CREATE PROCEDURE calculate_stats(
    IN table_name TEXT,
    OUT row_count BIGINT,
    OUT avg_size NUMERIC
) AS $$
BEGIN
    EXECUTE format('SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(pg_column_size(*)) FROM %I', table_name)
    INTO row_count, avg_size;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Call with OUT parameters
CALL calculate_stats('customers', NULL, NULL);

⚠️ When to Use Procedures

  • Batch Processing: When you need to commit work incrementally
  • Administrative Tasks: Database maintenance, bulk operations
  • Complex Transactions: Multiple savepoints and transaction control
  • No Return Value: When you're performing actions, not returning data

36.5. Query Language (SQL) Functions

SQL functions are the simplest type of function to write. They consist of a list of SQL statements that are executed in order, with the result of the last statement returned.

36.5.1. Arguments for SQL Functions

SQL functions can reference arguments using $1, $2, etc., or by using named parameters.

-- Using positional parameters ($1, $2)
CREATE FUNCTION add_nums(integer, integer) RETURNS integer AS $$
    SELECT $1 + $2;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Using named parameters (recommended)
CREATE FUNCTION add_nums_named(a integer, b integer) RETURNS integer AS $$
    SELECT a + b;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Mixed: named parameters with default values
CREATE FUNCTION greet(name text, greeting text DEFAULT 'Hello') 
RETURNS text AS $$
    SELECT greeting || ', ' || name || '!';
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT greet('Alice');              -- Returns "Hello, Alice!"
SELECT greet('Bob', 'Hi');          -- Returns "Hi, Bob!"

36.5.2. SQL Functions on Base Types

-- Simple calculation function
CREATE FUNCTION calculate_tax(amount numeric, tax_rate numeric) 
RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT amount * tax_rate / 100;
$$ LANGUAGE sql IMMUTABLE;

-- String manipulation function
CREATE FUNCTION format_phone(phone text) RETURNS text AS $$
    SELECT '(' || substring(phone, 1, 3) || ') ' ||
           substring(phone, 4, 3) || '-' ||
           substring(phone, 7, 4);
$$ LANGUAGE sql IMMUTABLE STRICT;

SELECT format_phone('5551234567');  -- Returns "(555) 123-4567"

36.5.3. SQL Functions on Composite Types

-- Function accepting composite type
CREATE TYPE person AS (
    first_name text,
    last_name text,
    age integer
);

CREATE FUNCTION full_name(p person) RETURNS text AS $$
    SELECT p.first_name || ' ' || p.last_name;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Function returning composite type
CREATE FUNCTION make_person(fname text, lname text, person_age integer)
RETURNS person AS $$
    SELECT ROW(fname, lname, person_age)::person;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Usage
SELECT full_name(make_person('John', 'Doe', 30));  -- Returns "John Doe"

36.5.4. SQL Functions with Output Parameters

-- Function with OUT parameters (returns record implicitly)
CREATE FUNCTION sum_and_product(a integer, b integer, 
                                OUT sum integer, OUT product integer) AS $$
    SELECT a + b, a * b;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT * FROM sum_and_product(3, 4);  -- Returns (7, 12)

-- Multiple OUT parameters create a record type automatically
CREATE FUNCTION rectangle_metrics(width numeric, height numeric,
                                  OUT area numeric,
                                  OUT perimeter numeric,
                                  OUT diagonal numeric) AS $$
    SELECT width * height,
           2 * (width + height),
           sqrt(width^2 + height^2);
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT * FROM rectangle_metrics(3, 4);  
-- Returns area=12, perimeter=14, diagonal=5

36.5.5. SQL Procedures with Output Parameters

-- Procedure with INOUT parameter
CREATE PROCEDURE increment_counter(INOUT counter integer) AS $$
    UPDATE global_config SET value = value + 1 
    WHERE key = 'counter'
    RETURNING value;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Procedure with OUT parameters
CREATE PROCEDURE get_table_info(
    IN table_name text,
    OUT row_count bigint,
    OUT total_size text
) AS $$
    SELECT 
        (SELECT reltuples::bigint FROM pg_class WHERE relname = table_name),
        pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(table_name));
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

36.5.6. SQL Functions with Variable Numbers of Arguments

-- VARIADIC keyword for variable arguments
CREATE FUNCTION sum_all(VARIADIC numbers numeric[]) RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT sum(x) FROM unnest(numbers) AS x;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT sum_all(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);          -- Returns 15
SELECT sum_all(10, 20);                  -- Returns 30
SELECT sum_all(VARIADIC ARRAY[1,2,3]);  -- Explicit array

-- Variadic function with mandatory parameters
CREATE FUNCTION concat_with_separator(separator text, VARIADIC strings text[])
RETURNS text AS $$
    SELECT array_to_string(strings, separator);
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT concat_with_separator(' - ', 'one', 'two', 'three');
-- Returns "one - two - three"

36.5.7. SQL Functions with Default Values for Arguments

-- Function with default parameter values
CREATE FUNCTION calculate_price(
    base_price numeric,
    tax_rate numeric DEFAULT 10,
    discount numeric DEFAULT 0
) RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT base_price * (1 + tax_rate/100) * (1 - discount/100);
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Various ways to call it
SELECT calculate_price(100);                    -- Uses defaults: 110.00
SELECT calculate_price(100, 5);                 -- Custom tax: 105.00
SELECT calculate_price(100, 10, 20);            -- All params: 88.00
SELECT calculate_price(100, discount => 15);    -- Named params: 93.50

36.5.8. SQL Functions as Table Sources

-- Function returning table data
CREATE FUNCTION get_active_users() 
RETURNS TABLE(user_id integer, username text, last_login timestamp) AS $$
    SELECT id, username, last_login 
    FROM users 
    WHERE active = true
    ORDER BY last_login DESC;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Use in FROM clause
SELECT * FROM get_active_users();

-- Join with other tables
SELECT u.username, o.order_count
FROM get_active_users() u
LEFT JOIN order_summary o ON u.user_id = o.user_id;

36.5.9. SQL Functions Returning Sets

-- Function returning set of base type
CREATE FUNCTION get_even_numbers(max_val integer) 
RETURNS SETOF integer AS $$
    SELECT generate_series(2, max_val, 2);
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT * FROM get_even_numbers(10);  -- Returns 2,4,6,8,10

-- Function returning set of composite type
CREATE FUNCTION get_expensive_products(min_price numeric)
RETURNS SETOF products AS $$
    SELECT * FROM products WHERE price >= min_price;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT name, price FROM get_expensive_products(100);

36.5.10. SQL Functions Returning TABLE

-- RETURNS TABLE is clearer than SETOF record
CREATE FUNCTION sales_summary(year integer)
RETURNS TABLE(
    month integer,
    total_sales numeric,
    order_count bigint,
    avg_order_value numeric
) AS $$
    SELECT 
        EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date)::integer,
        SUM(amount),
        COUNT(*),
        AVG(amount)
    FROM orders
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = year
    GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date)
    ORDER BY month;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT * FROM sales_summary(2024);

-- Can also use column aliases in queries
SELECT month, total_sales AS revenue 
FROM sales_summary(2024)
WHERE total_sales > 10000;

36.5.11. Polymorphic SQL Functions

-- Generic function working with any type
CREATE FUNCTION is_greater(anyelement, anyelement) 
RETURNS boolean AS $$
    SELECT $1 > $2;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT is_greater(10, 5);           -- true
SELECT is_greater('b', 'a');        -- true
SELECT is_greater(3.14, 2.71);      -- true

-- Array manipulation function
CREATE FUNCTION array_reverse(anyarray) RETURNS anyarray AS $$
    SELECT array_agg(elem) 
    FROM (SELECT unnest($1) AS elem) t
    ORDER BY elem DESC;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

SELECT array_reverse(ARRAY[1,2,3,4,5]);      -- {5,4,3,2,1}
SELECT array_reverse(ARRAY['a','b','c']);    -- {c,b,a}

36.5.12. SQL Functions with Collations

-- Function with specific collation
CREATE FUNCTION case_insensitive_compare(text, text) 
RETURNS boolean AS $$
    SELECT $1 COLLATE "en_US" = $2 COLLATE "en_US";
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Collation in sorting function
CREATE FUNCTION get_sorted_names(country_code text)
RETURNS TABLE(name text) AS $$
    SELECT name 
    FROM customers 
    WHERE country = country_code
    ORDER BY name COLLATE "en_US.utf8";
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Using collation parameter
CREATE FUNCTION sort_array(arr text[], col text)
RETURNS text[] AS $$
    SELECT array_agg(elem ORDER BY elem COLLATE col)
    FROM unnest(arr) AS elem;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

36.6. Function Overloading

PostgreSQL allows multiple functions with the same name as long as they have different argument types or numbers. This is called function overloading.

-- Same function name, different argument types
CREATE FUNCTION calculate_area(radius numeric) RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT pi() * radius ^ 2;  -- Circle area
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

CREATE FUNCTION calculate_area(width numeric, height numeric) 
RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT width * height;  -- Rectangle area
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

CREATE FUNCTION calculate_area(base numeric, height numeric, shape text)
RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT CASE 
        WHEN shape = 'triangle' THEN base * height / 2
        WHEN shape = 'parallelogram' THEN base * height
        ELSE NULL
    END;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- PostgreSQL chooses the right function based on arguments
SELECT calculate_area(5);                    -- Circle: 78.54
SELECT calculate_area(4, 6);                 -- Rectangle: 24
SELECT calculate_area(4, 6, 'triangle');     -- Triangle: 12

✅ Overloading Rules

  • Functions with same name must differ in number or types of arguments
  • Return type alone is NOT sufficient to distinguish functions
  • PostgreSQL uses argument types to resolve which function to call
  • Default parameter values don't affect overloading resolution

36.7. Function Volatility Categories

Functions are classified by their volatility, which tells the optimizer how the function behaves and when it's safe to optimize calls.

Volatility Categories

Category Behavior Example
IMMUTABLE Always returns same result for same inputs; never modifies database Mathematical functions: sqrt, abs
STABLE Returns same result within single query; doesn't modify database current_timestamp, current_user
VOLATILE Can return different results on each call; may modify database random(), nextval(), INSERT/UPDATE 
-- IMMUTABLE: Pure mathematical function
CREATE FUNCTION celsius_to_fahrenheit(celsius numeric) 
RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT (celsius * 9/5) + 32;
$$ LANGUAGE sql IMMUTABLE;

-- STABLE: Depends on database state but not modifying
CREATE FUNCTION get_user_discount(user_id integer)
RETURNS numeric AS $$
    SELECT discount_rate FROM users WHERE id = user_id;
$$ LANGUAGE sql STABLE;

-- VOLATILE: Modifies database (default if not specified)
CREATE FUNCTION log_access(page text)
RETURNS void AS $$
    INSERT INTO access_log (page, accessed_at) VALUES (page, NOW());
$$ LANGUAGE sql VOLATILE;

❌ Common Mistakes

  • Marking VOLATILE as IMMUTABLE: Can cause wrong results if function behavior changes
  • Using NOW() in IMMUTABLE: NOW() is STABLE, makes entire function STABLE
  • Not specifying: Functions default to VOLATILE (most restrictive)

36.8. Procedural Language Functions

Procedural languages like PL/pgSQL, PL/Python, PL/Perl provide more programming constructs than SQL, including loops, conditionals, and exception handling.

PL/pgSQL Example

CREATE FUNCTION fibonacci(n integer) RETURNS integer AS $$
DECLARE
    a integer := 0;
    b integer := 1;
    temp integer;
    i integer;
BEGIN
    IF n <= 0 THEN
        RETURN 0;
    ELSIF n = 1 THEN
        RETURN 1;
    END IF;
    
    FOR i IN 2..n LOOP
        temp := a + b;
        a := b;
        b := temp;
    END LOOP;
