    RETURN b;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql IMMUTABLE;

SELECT fibonacci(10);  -- Returns 55

PL/Python Example

-- Requires CREATE EXTENSION plpython3u;
CREATE FUNCTION python_sum_array(arr integer[]) RETURNS integer AS $$
    return sum(arr)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

SELECT python_sum_array(ARRAY[1,2,3,4,5]);  -- Returns 15

Available Procedural Languages

  • PL/pgSQL: Built-in, PostgreSQL-specific procedural language
  • PL/Tcl: Tcl scripting language
  • PL/Perl: Perl scripting language
  • PL/Python: Python (2 or 3)
  • PL/V8: JavaScript (via V8 engine)
  • PL/Java: Java virtual machine
  • PL/R: R statistical language

36.9. Internal Functions

Internal functions are built into the PostgreSQL server and written in C. They're part of the core system and cannot be defined by users.

-- Examples of internal functions
SELECT lower('HELLO');                   -- String function
SELECT abs(-42);                         -- Math function
SELECT current_timestamp;                -- Date/time function
SELECT pg_backend_pid();                 -- System function

-- View internal function definitions
SELECT 
    proname AS function_name,
    pronargs AS arg_count,
    prorettype::regtype AS return_type
FROM pg_proc
WHERE prolang = (SELECT oid FROM pg_language WHERE lanname = 'internal')
LIMIT 10;

⚠️ Important Notes

  • Internal functions are compiled into the PostgreSQL server binary
  • They have language specified as "internal" in pg_proc
  • Users cannot create new internal functions
  • Most built-in operators and functions are internal
  • For custom C code, use C-language functions instead

36.10. C-Language Functions

C-language functions provide the highest performance and lowest-level access to PostgreSQL internals. They're compiled as shared libraries and loaded dynamically.

36.10.1. Dynamic Loading

C functions are compiled into shared objects (.so on Linux, .dll on Windows, .dylib on macOS) and loaded at runtime.

-- Load C function from shared library
CREATE FUNCTION add_one(integer) RETURNS integer
    AS '/path/to/mylib.so', 'add_one_func'
    LANGUAGE C STRICT;

-- Alternative: use $libdir for PostgreSQL lib directory
CREATE FUNCTION my_func(text) RETURNS text
    AS '$libdir/myextension', 'my_func_impl'
    LANGUAGE C;

-- Check loaded libraries
SELECT * FROM pg_extension;

36.10.2. Base Types in C-Language Functions

PostgreSQL SQL types map to C types for function arguments and return values.

Type Mappings
SQL Type C Type
integer, int4 int32
bigint, int8 int64
real, float4 float4
double precision float8
text, varchar text* (varlena) 
// Example C function
#include "postgres.h"
#include "fmgr.h"

PG_MODULE_MAGIC;

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(add_one);

Datum
add_one(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    int32 arg = PG_GETARG_INT32(0);
    PG_RETURN_INT32(arg + 1);
}

36.10.3. Version 1 Calling Conventions

Modern C functions use the "version 1" calling convention with PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1 macro.

36.10.4. Writing Code

C functions must include postgres.h and fmgr.h, use PostgreSQL memory allocation, and handle NULL values properly.

36.10.5. Compiling and Linking Dynamically-Loaded Functions

# Compile C extension (Linux)
gcc -fpic -c myextension.c
gcc -shared -o myextension.so myextension.o

# Using pg_config for proper flags
gcc -I$(pg_config --includedir-server) -fpic -c myextension.c
gcc -shared -o myextension.so myextension.o

# Install to PostgreSQL lib directory
sudo cp myextension.so $(pg_config --pkglibdir)/

36.10.6. Server API and ABI Stability Guidance

PostgreSQL maintains ABI stability within major versions. Recompile extensions for new major versions.

36.10.7. Composite-Type Arguments

// Accessing composite type fields in C
PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(get_person_name);

Datum
get_person_name(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    HeapTupleHeader rec = PG_GETARG_HEAPTUPLEHEADER(0);
    // Extract field values using heap_getattr()
    // ...
}

36.10.8. Returning Rows (Composite Types)

Use heap_form_tuple() to construct composite return values.

36.10.9. Returning Sets

Functions returning sets use FuncCallContext and SRF_RETURN_NEXT() / SRF_RETURN_DONE().

36.10.10. Polymorphic Arguments and Return Types

Use get_fn_expr_argtype() and get_fn_expr_rettype() to determine actual types at runtime.

36.10.11. Shared Memory

// Request shared memory in _PG_init()
void _PG_init(void)
{
    RequestAddinShmemSpace(sizeof(MySharedState));
    RequestNamedLWLockTranche("my_extension", 1);
}

36.10.12. LWLocks

Lightweight locks for protecting shared memory structures. Use LWLockAcquire() and LWLockRelease().

36.10.13. Custom Wait Events

Extensions can define custom wait events for monitoring via pg_stat_activity.

36.10.14. Injection Points

Testing infrastructure for injecting faults and delays at specific code points.

36.10.15. Custom Cumulative Statistics

Extensions can expose custom statistics via shared memory and statistics collector.

36.10.16. Using C++ for Extensibility

// C++ extensions need extern "C" linkage
extern "C" {
#include "postgres.h"
#include "fmgr.h"

PG_MODULE_MAGIC;

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(my_cpp_function);

Datum
my_cpp_function(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    // Can use C++ features here
    std::string result = "Hello from C++";
    PG_RETURN_TEXT_P(cstring_to_text(result.c_str()));
}
}
⚠️ C++ Considerations
  • Use extern "C" for all PostgreSQL-facing functions
  • Be careful with exceptions (don't let them escape to C code)
  • PostgreSQL setjmp/longjmp error handling may skip C++ destructors
  • Use C++ stdlib features but isolate from PostgreSQL error handling

36.11. Function Optimization Information

You can provide hints to the query optimizer about function behavior using the CREATE FUNCTION options.

-- COST: Estimated execution cost
CREATE FUNCTION expensive_calc(numeric) RETURNS numeric AS $$
    -- Complex calculation here
$$ LANGUAGE sql IMMUTABLE
COST 1000;  -- High cost discourages inlining

-- ROWS: Estimated rows returned (for set-returning functions)
CREATE FUNCTION generate_data() RETURNS SETOF record AS $$
    -- Returns many rows
$$ LANGUAGE sql
ROWS 10000;  -- Helps planner estimate cardinality

-- SUPPORT: Function to provide planner info
CREATE FUNCTION my_func(text) RETURNS text AS $$
    -- Function implementation
$$ LANGUAGE sql
SUPPORT my_func_support;  -- Custom planner support function

💡 Optimization Tips

  • Default COST: 1 for C functions, 100 for SQL/PL functions
  • Default ROWS: 1000 for set-returning functions
  • Higher COST: Prevents inlining, useful for expensive functions
  • Accurate ROWS: Helps optimizer choose better join methods

36.12. User-Defined Aggregates

Custom aggregate functions allow you to define new ways to summarize data, beyond built-in aggregates like SUM, AVG, COUNT.

Basic Aggregate Structure

-- Create state transition function
CREATE FUNCTION my_sum_sfunc(state integer, value integer)
RETURNS integer AS $$
    SELECT COALESCE(state, 0) + COALESCE(value, 0);
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Create aggregate
CREATE AGGREGATE my_sum(integer) (
    SFUNC = my_sum_sfunc,           -- State transition function
    STYPE = integer,                -- State type
    INITCOND = '0'                  -- Initial state value
);

-- Use the aggregate
SELECT my_sum(quantity) FROM orders;

36.12.1. Moving-Aggregate Mode

Moving-aggregate mode optimizes window functions by providing inverse functions to remove old values.

-- Aggregate with moving-aggregate support
CREATE AGGREGATE moving_avg(numeric) (
    SFUNC = numeric_avg_accum,
    STYPE = internal,
    FINALFUNC = numeric_avg,
    -- Moving aggregate functions
    MSFUNC = numeric_avg_accum,      -- Forward state function
    MINVFUNC = numeric_avg_deaccum,  -- Inverse function
    MSTYPE = internal
);

36.12.2. Polymorphic and Variadic Aggregates

-- Polymorphic aggregate (works with any type)
CREATE AGGREGATE array_accum(anyelement) (
    SFUNC = array_append,
    STYPE = anyarray,
    INITCOND = '{}'
);

SELECT array_accum(name) FROM customers;  -- Array of text
SELECT array_accum(id) FROM orders;       -- Array of integers

36.12.3. Ordered-Set Aggregates

Ordered-set aggregates like percentile_cont require sorted input.

-- Ordered-set aggregate example (median)
CREATE AGGREGATE median(numeric) (
    SFUNC = ordered_set_transition,
    STYPE = internal,
    FINALFUNC = percentile_cont_final,
    FINALFUNC_EXTRA,
    HYPOTHETICAL
);

SELECT median(salary ORDER BY salary) FROM employees;

36.12.4. Partial Aggregation

For parallel aggregation, define a combine function to merge partial states.

CREATE AGGREGATE parallel_sum(integer) (
    SFUNC = int4_sum_accum,
    STYPE = bigint,
    COMBINEFUNC = int8pl,  -- Combines partial results
    PARALLEL = SAFE
);

36.12.5. Support Functions for Aggregates

Support functions help the optimizer understand aggregate behavior.

36.13. User-Defined Types

You can create entirely new data types with custom storage, I/O functions, and operators.

-- Create custom type with input/output functions
CREATE TYPE complex CASCADE;

CREATE FUNCTION complex_in(cstring) RETURNS complex AS $$
    -- Parse string to complex number
$$ LANGUAGE c IMMUTABLE STRICT;

CREATE FUNCTION complex_out(complex) RETURNS cstring AS $$
    -- Format complex number as string
$$ LANGUAGE c IMMUTABLE STRICT;

CREATE TYPE complex (
    INPUT = complex_in,
    OUTPUT = complex_out,
    INTERNALLENGTH = 16,
    ALIGNMENT = double
);

36.13.1. TOAST Considerations

TOAST (The Oversized-Attribute Storage Technique) handles large variable-length data.

-- Type with TOAST support
CREATE TYPE large_object (
    INPUT = lo_in,
    OUTPUT = lo_out,
    INTERNALLENGTH = VARIABLE,
    STORAGE = extended  -- TOAST-able
);
TOAST Storage Options
  • PLAIN: No compression or out-of-line storage
  • EXTENDED: Compression and out-of-line (default for varlena)
  • EXTERNAL: Out-of-line only, no compression
  • MAIN: Compression but avoid out-of-line if possible

36.14. User-Defined Operators

Operators provide convenient syntax for function calls, especially useful for custom types.

-- Create operator for complex number addition
CREATE OPERATOR + (
    LEFTARG = complex,
    RIGHTARG = complex,
    FUNCTION = complex_add,
    COMMUTATOR = +
);

-- Create comparison operator
CREATE OPERATOR < (
    LEFTARG = complex,
    RIGHTARG = complex,
    FUNCTION = complex_lt,
    COMMUTATOR = >,
    NEGATOR = >=,
    RESTRICT = scalarltsel,
    JOIN = scalarltjoinsel
);

-- Use operators naturally
SELECT (1,2)::complex + (3,4)::complex;
SELECT * FROM complex_table WHERE value < (5,0)::complex;

36.15. Operator Optimization Information

Providing optimization information helps the query planner use your operators effectively.

36.15.1. COMMUTATOR

Specifies the commutator operator: if a + b then b + a uses same logic.

36.15.2. NEGATOR

Specifies the negation operator: < is negator of >=.

36.15.3. RESTRICT

Selectivity estimation function for WHERE clauses.

36.15.4. JOIN

Selectivity estimation function for JOIN conditions.

36.15.5. HASHES

Indicates operator can be used in hash joins.

36.15.6. MERGES

Indicates operator can be used in merge joins.

36.16. Interfacing Extensions to Indexes

PostgreSQL allows you to create custom index access methods and define how your types interact with indexes through operator classes.

36.16.1. Index Methods and Operator Classes

Operator classes define how a data type works with a specific index method (B-tree, hash, GiST, GIN, etc.).

-- Create operator class for custom type
CREATE OPERATOR CLASS my_type_ops
    DEFAULT FOR TYPE my_type USING btree AS
    OPERATOR 1 < ,
    OPERATOR 2 <= ,
    OPERATOR 3 = ,
    OPERATOR 4 >= ,
    OPERATOR 5 > ,
    FUNCTION 1 my_type_cmp(my_type, my_type);

36.16.2. Index Method Strategies

B-tree Strategy Numbers
Strategy Operator
1less than (<)
2less than or equal (<=)
3equal (=)
4greater than or equal (>=)
5greater than (>)

36.16.3. Index Method Support Routines

Support functions like comparison functions, hashing functions needed by index methods.

36.16.4. An Example

-- Complete example: custom type with B-tree support
CREATE TYPE complex AS (r float8, i float8);

-- Comparison function
CREATE FUNCTION complex_cmp(complex, complex) RETURNS integer AS $$
    SELECT CASE
        WHEN ($1.r < $2.r) OR ($1.r = $2.r AND $1.i < $2.i) THEN -1
        WHEN ($1.r = $2.r) AND ($1.i = $2.i) THEN 0
        ELSE 1
    END;
$$ LANGUAGE sql IMMUTABLE;

-- Define operators
CREATE OPERATOR < (LEFTARG=complex, RIGHTARG=complex, FUNCTION=complex_lt);
CREATE OPERATOR = (LEFTARG=complex, RIGHTARG=complex, FUNCTION=complex_eq);
-- ... other operators

-- Create operator class
CREATE OPERATOR CLASS complex_ops DEFAULT FOR TYPE complex USING btree AS
    OPERATOR 1 <,
    OPERATOR 3 =,
    OPERATOR 5 >,
    FUNCTION 1 complex_cmp(complex, complex);

-- Now can create B-tree index
CREATE INDEX ON complex_table USING btree (value);

36.16.5. Operator Classes and Operator Families

Operator families group related operator classes for cross-type comparisons.

36.16.6. System Dependencies on Operator Classes

Some system operations require specific operator classes (e.g., ORDER BY needs B-tree operators).

36.16.7. Ordering Operators

Specify operators for ordering index scans and distance queries.

36.16.8. Special Features of Operator Classes

Advanced features like search optimization, index-only scans, and covering indexes.

36.17. Packaging Related Objects into an Extension

Extensions are the recommended way to package and distribute PostgreSQL add-ons. They simplify installation, upgrades, and removal.

36.17.1. Extension Files

An extension consists of a control file and one or more SQL script files.

# myextension.control
comment = 'My custom PostgreSQL extension'
default_version = '1.0'
relocatable = true
requires = 'plpgsql'

-- myextension--1.0.sql
CREATE FUNCTION my_function(integer) RETURNS integer AS $$
    SELECT $1 * 2;
$$ LANGUAGE sql IMMUTABLE;

CREATE TABLE my_config (
    key TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    value TEXT
);
Extension File Structure
/usr/share/postgresql/extension/
    myextension.control
    myextension--1.0.sql
    myextension--1.0--1.1.sql  (upgrade script)
    myextension--1.1.sql

36.17.2. Extension Relocatability

Relocatable extensions can be installed in any schema. Non-relocatable extensions have a fixed schema.

-- Relocatable extension (schema specified at install time)
CREATE EXTENSION myextension SCHEMA custom_schema;

-- Non-relocatable (uses schema from control file or @extschema@)
CREATE EXTENSION fixed_extension;

36.17.3. Extension Configuration Tables

Configuration tables store user-modifiable data and should be excluded from pg_dump of extension.

-- Mark table as configuration table
SELECT pg_extension_config_dump('my_config', '');

-- Exclude specific rows from dump
SELECT pg_extension_config_dump('my_config', 'WHERE key != ''internal''');

36.17.4. Extension Updates

-- Create upgrade script: myextension--1.0--1.1.sql
ALTER FUNCTION my_function(integer) PARALLEL SAFE;

CREATE FUNCTION new_function(text) RETURNS text AS $$
    SELECT upper($1);
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Users upgrade with:
ALTER EXTENSION myextension UPDATE TO '1.1';

36.17.5. Installing Extensions Using Update Scripts

Users can install directly to a specific version using update script chains.

36.17.6. Security Considerations for Extensions

🔒 Security Best Practices
  • Use SECURITY DEFINER carefully: Can escalate privileges
  • Set search_path: Prevent trojan horse functions
  • Validate inputs: Especially in functions called by triggers
  • Limit trusted flag: Only for truly safe extensions
  • Review permissions: Grant minimal necessary privileges

36.17.7. Extension Example

# Complete extension example structure
$ tree myextension/
myextension/
├── Makefile
├── myextension.control
├── myextension--1.0.sql
├── myextension--1.0--1.1.sql
└── src/
    ├── myextension.c
    └── myextension.h

# Install with PGXS
$ make install
$ psql -d mydb -c "CREATE EXTENSION myextension;"

36.18. Extension Building Infrastructure

PostgreSQL provides PGXS (PostgreSQL Extension Building Infrastructure) to simplify building and installing extensions.

PGXS Makefile

# Makefile for PostgreSQL extension
EXTENSION = myextension
DATA = myextension--1.0.sql myextension--1.0--1.1.sql
MODULES = myextension
DOCS = README.md

# PostgreSQL configuration
PG_CONFIG = pg_config
PGXS := $(shell $(PG_CONFIG) --pgxs)
include $(PGXS)

Build Commands

# Build extension
make

# Install extension files
make install

# Install and run regression tests
make installcheck

# Clean build artifacts
make clean

✅ PGXS Variables

Variable Purpose
EXTENSION Extension name
DATA SQL files to install
MODULES C modules (compiled to .so)
DOCS Documentation files
REGRESS Regression test scripts

✅ Chapter 36 Summary

This chapter covered PostgreSQL's comprehensive extensibility features:

  • Type System: Base types, containers, domains, pseudo-types, polymorphic types
  • Functions: SQL, PL/pgSQL, C, Python functions with overloading and volatility
  • Procedures: Transaction control, batch processing capabilities
  • Aggregates: Custom aggregation with parallel and moving-aggregate support
  • Operators: Natural syntax for custom types with optimization hints
  • Indexes: Custom index access methods and operator classes
  • Extensions: Packaging, versioning, security, and PGXS build infrastructure

Key Takeaway: PostgreSQL's extensibility allows you to add custom data types, functions, operators, aggregates, and index methods without modifying the core server. Use the extension system to package and distribute your customizations, ensuring easy installation, upgrades, and maintenance. Always consider performance, parallel safety, and security when creating extensions.

Chapter 37. Triggers

📌 Chapter Overview

This chapter describes how to write trigger functions. Triggers are database callback functions that are automatically executed when certain events occur on a table or view. While triggers can be written in most procedural languages, this chapter focuses on writing triggers in C. For writing triggers in PL/pgSQL, see PL/pgSQL Trigger Functions.

🎯 What You'll Learn:
  • Trigger behavior and execution
  • Data visibility in triggers
  • Writing C trigger functions
  • Complete trigger examples
🔧 Topics Covered:
  • BEFORE/AFTER triggers
  • Row-level vs statement-level
  • TriggerData structure
  • Real-world implementations

37.1 Overview of Trigger Behavior

A trigger is a specification that the database should automatically execute a particular function whenever a certain type of operation is performed. Triggers can be attached to tables (partitioned or not), views, and foreign tables.

🔄 Trigger Types

Type Description When Fired
Row-level Fires once for each affected row BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF
Statement-level Fires once per SQL statement BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF

Trigger Events

On tables and foreign tables, triggers can be defined for the following events:

📊 Table Triggers

  • INSERT - New row insertion
  • UPDATE - Row modification
  • DELETE - Row removal
  • TRUNCATE - Table truncation (statement-level only)

👁️ View Triggers

  • INSERT - INSTEAD OF only
  • UPDATE - INSTEAD OF only
  • DELETE - INSTEAD OF only
  • No TRUNCATE support

Trigger Timing

Trigger Execution Timeline

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SQL STATEMENT                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                ┌───────────▼──────────────┐
                │  BEFORE Statement Trigger │ (once per statement)
                └───────────┬──────────────┘
                            │
            ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
            │      Process Each Row          │
            │  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │
            │  │ BEFORE Row Trigger       │  │ (for each row)
            │  └──────────┬───────────────┘  │
            │             │                   │
            │  ┌──────────▼───────────────┐  │
            │  │  Execute Operation       │  │
            │  │  (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)  │  │
            │  └──────────┬───────────────┘  │
            │             │                   │
            │  ┌──────────▼───────────────┐  │
            │  │  AFTER Row Trigger       │  │ (for each row)
            │  └──────────────────────────┘  │
            └────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                ┌───────────▼──────────────┐
                │  AFTER Statement Trigger  │ (once per statement)
                └───────────┬──────────────┘
                            │
                     ┌──────▼────────┐
                     │   COMPLETE    │
                     └───────────────┘

BEFORE Triggers

Statement-level BEFORE triggers fire before any rows are processed. They can:

  • Prevent the operation entirely by raising an exception
  • Cannot modify individual rows (no access to row data)
  • Useful for validation, logging, or permission checks

Row-level BEFORE triggers fire before each row is processed. They can:

⚡ BEFORE Row Trigger Powers

  • Skip the operation for the current row by returning NULL
  • Modify the row that will be inserted/updated (for INSERT/UPDATE)
  • Prevent the operation by raising an exception
⚠️ Important: The return value is ignored for DELETE, but the trigger can still prevent deletion by returning NULL or raising an exception.

AFTER Triggers

Statement-level AFTER triggers fire after all rows have been processed. They can:

  • Access all changes made by the statement
  • Cannot modify the result set
  • Ideal for audit logging, maintaining summary tables

Row-level AFTER triggers fire after each row is processed. They:

  • Cannot modify the row (operation already complete)
  • Return value is ignored
  • Can see the final state of the row
  • Useful for cascading changes to other tables

INSTEAD OF Triggers

🔄 INSTEAD OF Triggers (Views Only)

INSTEAD OF triggers are only defined on views and must be row-level. They:

  • Replace the default INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE behavior
  • Allow views to be updatable even when they wouldn't otherwise be
  • Execute in place of the triggering operation
  • Return value is ignored
-- Example: Making a view updatable
CREATE VIEW employee_view AS
    SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM employees;

CREATE TRIGGER employee_view_insert
    INSTEAD OF INSERT ON employee_view
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION handle_employee_insert();

Trigger Constraints

Triggers defined with the CONSTRAINT option are constraint triggers. These have special properties:

Feature Regular Trigger Constraint Trigger
Timing BEFORE/AFTER AFTER only
Deferrable No Yes (IMMEDIATE or DEFERRED)
Execution Immediately Can defer until COMMIT
Use Case General automation Referential integrity

Trigger Execution Order

When multiple triggers of the same kind are defined on the same event:

📋 Execution Order Rules

  1. Constraint triggers fire after regular AFTER triggers
  2. Triggers with same timing fire in alphabetical order by trigger name
  3. You can control order by naming triggers strategically:
    • a_first_trigger
    • b_second_trigger
    • z_last_trigger

Trigger Function Return Values

✅ Must Return Value

BEFORE row triggers (INSERT/UPDATE):

  • NULL - Skip operation for this row
  • NEW - Proceed with NEW row
  • Modified NEW - Insert/update modified row

⏭️ Return Value Ignored

These triggers ignore return value:

  • AFTER triggers (all types)
  • Statement-level triggers
  • INSTEAD OF triggers
  • BEFORE DELETE triggers

Simple Trigger Example

-- Create a simple trigger function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_employee_changes()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Log the change
    INSERT INTO employee_audit(emp_id, action, changed_at)
    VALUES (NEW.emp_id, TG_OP, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    
    -- Return NEW for BEFORE triggers on INSERT/UPDATE
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Create trigger on employees table
CREATE TRIGGER employee_change_log
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION log_employee_changes();
Output: Every insert or update on the employees table will automatically log the change to employee_audit table.

37.2 Visibility of Data Changes

Understanding data visibility is crucial when working with triggers. PostgreSQL's MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) system affects what data is visible to trigger functions at different execution points.

🔍 Key Visibility Concepts

If you execute SQL commands in your trigger function and these commands access the table that the trigger is for, then you need to be aware of the data visibility rules.

Statement-Level Trigger Visibility

In a statement-level trigger, relations are visible in the following states:

Trigger Type Data Visibility Description
BEFORE Statement Before any changes Sees the table as it was before the statement began
AFTER Statement After all changes Sees the table with all changes made by the statement

Row-Level Trigger Visibility

Row-level triggers have more nuanced visibility rules depending on their timing:

⏰ BEFORE Row Triggers

  • OLD/NEW: See the pre-change row state
  • Other Rows: See table before statement started
  • Same Row: Changes from earlier BEFORE triggers are visible
  • Query Visibility: Cannot see changes from current statement

✅ AFTER Row Triggers

  • OLD/NEW: See the post-change row state
  • Other Rows: See table with all changes from statement
  • Query Visibility: All changes visible including current row
  • Consistency: Sees complete final state

Special Variables in Triggers

Trigger Special Variables
Variable Type Description
NEW RECORD New row for INSERT/UPDATE (NULL for DELETE)
OLD RECORD Old row for UPDATE/DELETE (NULL for INSERT)
TG_OP TEXT Operation: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or TRUNCATE
TG_WHEN TEXT BEFORE, AFTER, or INSTEAD OF
TG_LEVEL TEXT ROW or STATEMENT
TG_TABLE_NAME NAME Name of the table that triggered this trigger
TG_TABLE_SCHEMA NAME Schema of the trigger table

Visibility Example with BEFORE Trigger

-- Create test table
CREATE TABLE accounts (
    account_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    balance NUMERIC(10, 2),
    updated_at TIMESTAMP
);

-- BEFORE trigger function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION check_balance_before()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
DECLARE
    total_balance NUMERIC;
BEGIN
    -- Query to check total balance
    -- This sees the table BEFORE the current statement's changes
    SELECT SUM(balance) INTO total_balance FROM accounts;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Total balance before change: %', total_balance;
    RAISE NOTICE 'New row balance: %', NEW.balance;
    
    -- Modify the NEW row
    NEW.updated_at := CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;
    
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER balance_before_trigger
    BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON accounts
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION check_balance_before();

Visibility Example with AFTER Trigger

-- AFTER trigger function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_balance_after()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
DECLARE
    total_balance NUMERIC;
BEGIN
    -- Query to check total balance
    -- This sees the table AFTER all changes from the current statement
    SELECT SUM(balance) INTO total_balance FROM accounts;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Total balance after change: %', total_balance;
    RAISE NOTICE 'Operation: %', TG_OP;
    
    -- Insert into audit log
    INSERT INTO balance_audit (total_balance, operation, log_time)
    VALUES (total_balance, TG_OP, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    
    RETURN NULL; -- Return value ignored in AFTER triggers
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER balance_after_trigger
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON accounts
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION log_balance_after();
Testing Visibility:
INSERT INTO accounts (balance) VALUES (100), (200), (300);

BEFORE Trigger Output:
NOTICE: Total balance before change: NULL (first row)
NOTICE: Total balance before change: 100 (second row)
NOTICE: Total balance before change: 300 (third row)

AFTER Trigger Output:
NOTICE: Total balance after change: 600 (sees all three rows)

Mutation During Trigger Execution

⚠️ Important: Mutations and Visibility

If your trigger function executes SQL commands that modify the triggering table:

  • BEFORE triggers: Mutations are visible to later triggers on the same row
  • Recursive triggers: Can cause infinite loops if not properly handled
  • Use conditions: Always include exit conditions to prevent infinite recursion
-- Safe recursive trigger with exit condition
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_infinite_loop()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Only update if condition met (prevents infinite loop)
    IF NEW.status != OLD.status AND NEW.status = 'active' THEN
        -- This UPDATE won't trigger recursion because status doesn't change
        UPDATE related_table SET last_active = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
        WHERE user_id = NEW.user_id;
    END IF;
    
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

Visibility in Constraint Triggers

Constraint triggers (DEFERRABLE) have special visibility characteristics:

🔒 Constraint Trigger Visibility

  • IMMEDIATE: Fires at end of statement, sees all statement changes
  • DEFERRED: Fires at transaction COMMIT, sees all transaction changes
  • Referential Integrity: Perfect for checking cross-table constraints
-- Create deferrable constraint trigger
CREATE CONSTRAINT TRIGGER check_referential_integrity
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE ON orders
    DEFERRABLE INITIALLY DEFERRED
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION verify_customer_exists();

Best Practices for Data Visibility

✅ Do's

  • Use AFTER triggers to see complete changes
  • Use BEFORE triggers to validate/modify before commit
  • Test visibility carefully with multiple rows
  • Document visibility assumptions

❌ Don'ts

  • Don't assume BEFORE triggers see current changes
  • Avoid complex queries in BEFORE triggers
  • Don't create recursive triggers without guards
  • Don't rely on trigger execution order

37.3 Writing Trigger Functions in C

This section describes the low-level details of the C language interface for triggers. While most users will prefer to use procedural languages like PL/pgSQL, understanding the C interface is valuable for high-performance or system-level trigger development.

🔧 Why C Triggers?

✅ Advantages:
  • Maximum performance
  • Direct memory access
  • System-level operations
  • No interpreter overhead
⚠️ Considerations:
  • More complex to write
  • Harder to debug
  • Requires compilation
  • Memory management required

The TriggerData Structure

When a trigger function is called, it receives a pointer to a TriggerData structure. This structure contains all information about the trigger event:

typedef struct TriggerData
{
    NodeTag          type;
    TriggerEvent     tg_event;
    Relation         tg_relation;
    HeapTuple        tg_trigtuple;
    HeapTuple        tg_newtuple;
    Trigger         *tg_trigger;
    TupleTableSlot  *tg_trigslot;
    TupleTableSlot  *tg_newslot;
    Tuplestorestate *tg_oldtable;
    Tuplestorestate *tg_newtable;
} TriggerData;
TriggerData Structure Members
Member Type Description
type NodeTag Always T_TriggerData for trigger calls
tg_event TriggerEvent Describes the event (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, etc.)
tg_relation Relation Pointer to relation descriptor for trigger table
tg_trigtuple HeapTuple OLD row (NULL for INSERT)
tg_newtuple HeapTuple NEW row (NULL for DELETE)
tg_trigger Trigger* Pointer to Trigger structure

Checking Trigger Event Type

PostgreSQL provides macros to examine the tg_event field:

// Check trigger timing
TRIGGER_FIRED_BEFORE(event)     // Returns true for BEFORE triggers
TRIGGER_FIRED_AFTER(event)      // Returns true for AFTER triggers
TRIGGER_FIRED_INSTEAD(event)    // Returns true for INSTEAD OF triggers

// Check trigger level
TRIGGER_FIRED_FOR_ROW(event)    // Returns true for row-level triggers
TRIGGER_FIRED_FOR_STATEMENT(event) // Returns true for statement-level

// Check trigger operation
TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_INSERT(event)  // Returns true for INSERT
TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_UPDATE(event)  // Returns true for UPDATE
TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_DELETE(event)  // Returns true for DELETE
TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_TRUNCATE(event) // Returns true for TRUNCATE

Basic C Trigger Function Template

#include "postgres.h"
#include "fmgr.h"
#include "executor/spi.h"
#include "commands/trigger.h"
#include "utils/rel.h"

PG_MODULE_MAGIC;

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(my_trigger_function);

Datum
my_trigger_function(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    TriggerData *trigdata = (TriggerData *) fcinfo->context;
    HeapTuple    rettuple;
    
    // Verify this is actually a trigger call
    if (!CALLED_AS_TRIGGER(fcinfo))
        ereport(ERROR,
                (errcode(ERRCODE_E_R_I_E_TRIGGER_PROTOCOL_VIOLATED),
                 errmsg("function must be called as trigger")));
    
    // Check if BEFORE trigger
    if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BEFORE(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        // For BEFORE triggers on INSERT/UPDATE
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
        
        if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_INSERT(trigdata->tg_event))
        {
            // Handle INSERT
            rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
        }
        else if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_UPDATE(trigdata->tg_event))
        {
            // Handle UPDATE
            rettuple = trigdata->tg_newtuple;
        }
        else if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_DELETE(trigdata->tg_event))
        {
            // Handle DELETE
            rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
        }
    }
    else
    {
        // AFTER triggers - return value ignored
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
    }
    
    return PointerGetDatum(rettuple);
}

Accessing Tuple Data

To read or modify column values in tuples, use the SPI (Server Programming Interface) functions:

📊 Accessing Column Values

// Get column value by name
Datum value;
bool  isnull;
TupleDesc tupdesc = trigdata->tg_relation->rd_att;

// Get value from OLD tuple
value = SPI_getbinval(trigdata->tg_trigtuple, tupdesc, 
                      SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "column_name"), &isnull);

// Get value from NEW tuple  
value = SPI_getbinval(trigdata->tg_newtuple, tupdesc,
                      SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "column_name"), &isnull);

// Check if value is NULL
if (isnull)
{
    // Handle NULL value
}

Modifying Tuple Values

To modify a tuple in a BEFORE trigger (INSERT or UPDATE only):

HeapTuple    newtuple;
Datum        newval;
bool         newnull = false;
Datum       *values;
bool        *nulls;
bool        *replaces;
TupleDesc    tupdesc = trigdata->tg_relation->rd_att;
int          attnum;

// Allocate arrays for heap_modify_tuple
values = (Datum *) palloc(tupdesc->natts * sizeof(Datum));
nulls = (bool *) palloc(tupdesc->natts * sizeof(bool));
replaces = (bool *) palloc(tupdesc->natts * sizeof(bool));

// Initialize arrays
memset(values, 0, tupdesc->natts * sizeof(Datum));
memset(nulls, false, tupdesc->natts * sizeof(bool));
memset(replaces, false, tupdesc->natts * sizeof(bool));

// Set which column to replace
attnum = SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "column_name");
if (attnum > 0)
{
    values[attnum - 1] = newval;
    nulls[attnum - 1] = newnull;
    replaces[attnum - 1] = true;
}

// Create modified tuple
newtuple = heap_modify_tuple(trigdata->tg_trigtuple,
                              tupdesc,
                              values,
                              nulls,
                              replaces);

// Clean up
pfree(values);
pfree(nulls);
pfree(replaces);

return PointerGetDatum(newtuple);

Using SPI in Triggers

Trigger functions can use SPI to execute SQL commands:

💾 SPI Operations in Triggers

int ret;
char query[256];

// Connect to SPI
if ((ret = SPI_connect()) < 0)
    elog(ERROR, "SPI_connect failed: %d", ret);

// Build and execute query
snprintf(query, sizeof(query),
         "INSERT INTO audit_log (table_name, operation, timestamp) "
         "VALUES ('%s', '%s', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)",
         SPI_getrelname(trigdata->tg_relation),
         TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_INSERT(trigdata->tg_event) ? "INSERT" :
         TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_UPDATE(trigdata->tg_event) ? "UPDATE" : "DELETE");

ret = SPI_exec(query, 0);

if (ret < 0)
    elog(ERROR, "SPI_exec failed: %d", ret);

// Disconnect from SPI
SPI_finish();
⚠️ Important: Always call SPI_finish() before returning from your trigger function. Failure to do so will cause errors.

Memory Management

🧠 Memory Management Rules

  • Use palloc/pfree: Never use malloc/free directly
  • Memory context: Allocations are freed automatically at trigger end
  • Return values: Must be allocated in the appropriate memory context
  • Tuple modification: heap_modify_tuple allocates new memory
// Good: Uses PostgreSQL memory allocator
char *buffer = (char *) palloc(256);
// ... use buffer ...
pfree(buffer);

// Bad: Direct C memory allocation
// char *buffer = malloc(256); // DON'T DO THIS!
// free(buffer);

Error Handling

Use PostgreSQL's error reporting functions:

// Report error and abort transaction
ereport(ERROR,
        (errcode(ERRCODE_INTEGRITY_CONSTRAINT_VIOLATION),
         errmsg("balance cannot be negative"),
         errdetail("Attempted to set balance to %f", balance_value),
         errhint("Ensure balance is always >= 0")));

// Report warning (doesn't abort)
ereport(WARNING,
        (errmsg("unusual balance detected: %f", balance_value)));

// Report notice
ereport(NOTICE,
        (errmsg("trigger fired for table %s", table_name)));

Compilation and Installation

Makefile for C Trigger
MODULES = my_trigger
EXTENSION = my_trigger
DATA = my_trigger--1.0.sql

PG_CONFIG = pg_config
PGXS := $(shell $(PG_CONFIG) --pgxs)
include $(PGXS)
# Compile the trigger
make

# Install the extension
sudo make install

# In PostgreSQL
CREATE EXTENSION my_trigger;
Expected Output:
gcc -Wall -Wmissing-prototypes -Wpointer-arith ...
gcc -shared -o my_trigger.so my_trigger.o
/usr/bin/install -c -m 755 my_trigger.so '/usr/lib/postgresql/15/lib/'

37.4 A Complete Trigger Example

This section presents a complete, real-world example of a C trigger function that implements an audit trail system. This example demonstrates all the concepts covered in this chapter.

🎯 Example Goal

Create a comprehensive audit system that:

  • Tracks all INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations
  • Records old and new values for UPDATE operations
  • Captures user and timestamp information
  • Stores audit data in a separate audit_log table
  • Works efficiently with high-volume tables

Step 1: Create Audit Table

-- Audit log table to store all changes
CREATE TABLE audit_log (
    log_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    schema_name VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
    table_name VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
    operation VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,
    user_name VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,
    application_name VARCHAR(128),
    client_addr INET,
    logged_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    old_data JSONB,
    new_data JSONB,
    changed_columns TEXT[]
);

-- Index for common queries
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_log_table ON audit_log(schema_name, table_name);
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_log_time ON audit_log(logged_at DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_log_user ON audit_log(user_name);

Step 2: C Trigger Function Code

/*
 * audit_trigger.c - Comprehensive audit trail trigger
 */

#include "postgres.h"
#include "fmgr.h"
#include "executor/spi.h"
#include "commands/trigger.h"
#include "utils/rel.h"
#include "utils/builtins.h"
#include "utils/jsonb.h"
#include "catalog/pg_type.h"
#include "access/htup_details.h"
#include "miscadmin.h"

PG_MODULE_MAGIC;

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(audit_trigger);

/*
 * Helper function to convert tuple to JSONB
 */
static Datum
tuple_to_jsonb(HeapTuple tuple, TupleDesc tupdesc)
{
    Datum       result;
    StringInfoData  buf;
    int         i;
    bool        first = true;

    initStringInfo(&buf);
    appendStringInfoChar(&buf, '{');

    for (i = 0; i < tupdesc->natts; i++)
    {
        Form_pg_attribute att = TupleDescAttr(tupdesc, i);
        Datum       value;
        bool        isnull;
        Oid         typoutput;
        bool        typIsVarlena;

        /* Skip dropped columns */
        if (att->attisdropped)
            continue;

        value = heap_getattr(tuple, i + 1, tupdesc, &isnull);

        if (!first)
            appendStringInfoChar(&buf, ',');
        first = false;

        /* Add column name */
        appendStringInfo(&buf, "\"%s\":", NameStr(att->attname));

        if (isnull)
        {
            appendStringInfoString(&buf, "null");
        }
        else
        {
            getTypeOutputInfo(att->atttypid, &typoutput, &typIsVarlena);
            
            /* Convert value to string */
            char *outputstr = OidOutputFunctionCall(typoutput, value);
            
            /* Quote strings */
            if (att->atttypid == TEXTOID || 
                att->atttypid == VARCHAROID ||
                att->atttypid == BPCHAROID)
            {
                appendStringInfo(&buf, "\"%s\"", outputstr);
            }
            else
            {
                appendStringInfoString(&buf, outputstr);
            }
            pfree(outputstr);
        }
    }

    appendStringInfoChar(&buf, '}');
    
    /* Convert to JSONB */
    result = DirectFunctionCall1(jsonb_in, 
                                 CStringGetDatum(buf.data));
    
    pfree(buf.data);
    return result;
}

/*
 * Main trigger function
 */
Datum
audit_trigger(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    TriggerData    *trigdata = (TriggerData *) fcinfo->context;
    HeapTuple       rettuple;
    TupleDesc       tupdesc;
    Relation        rel;
    const char     *operation;
    Datum          old_data = (Datum) 0;
    Datum          new_data = (Datum) 0;
    bool           old_isnull = true;
    bool           new_isnull = true;
    char           query[2048];
    int            ret;

    /* Verify this is a trigger call */
    if (!CALLED_AS_TRIGGER(fcinfo))
        ereport(ERROR,
                (errcode(ERRCODE_E_R_I_E_TRIGGER_PROTOCOL_VIOLATED),
                 errmsg("audit_trigger: must be called as trigger")));

    /* Get relation and tuple descriptor */
    rel = trigdata->tg_relation;
    tupdesc = rel->rd_att;

    /* Determine operation type */
    if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_INSERT(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        operation = "INSERT";
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
        new_data = tuple_to_jsonb(trigdata->tg_trigtuple, tupdesc);
        new_isnull = false;
    }
    else if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_UPDATE(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        operation = "UPDATE";
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_newtuple;
        old_data = tuple_to_jsonb(trigdata->tg_trigtuple, tupdesc);
        new_data = tuple_to_jsonb(trigdata->tg_newtuple, tupdesc);
        old_isnull = false;
        new_isnull = false;
    }
    else if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_DELETE(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        operation = "DELETE";
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
        old_data = tuple_to_jsonb(trigdata->tg_trigtuple, tupdesc);
        old_isnull = false;
    }
    else
    {
        ereport(ERROR,
                (errmsg("audit_trigger: unrecognized operation")));
        rettuple = NULL; /* keep compiler quiet */
    }

    /* Connect to SPI */
    if ((ret = SPI_connect()) < 0)
        elog(ERROR, "audit_trigger: SPI_connect failed: %d", ret);

    /* Build INSERT query for audit_log */
    snprintf(query, sizeof(query),
             "INSERT INTO audit_log "
             "(schema_name, table_name, operation, user_name, "
             " application_name, client_addr, old_data, new_data) "
             "VALUES ('%s', '%s', '%s', '%s', '%s', '%s', %s, %s)",
             get_namespace_name(RelationGetNamespace(rel)),
             RelationGetRelationName(rel),
             operation,
             GetUserNameFromId(GetUserId(), false),
             application_name ? application_name : "",
             MyProcPort && MyProcPort->remote_host ? 
                 MyProcPort->remote_host : "local",
             old_isnull ? "NULL" : 
                 DatumGetCString(DirectFunctionCall1(jsonb_out, old_data)),
             new_isnull ? "NULL" : 
                 DatumGetCString(DirectFunctionCall1(jsonb_out, new_data)));

    /* Execute the query */
    ret = SPI_exec(query, 0);

    if (ret < 0)
        elog(ERROR, "audit_trigger: SPI_exec failed: %d", ret);

    /* Disconnect from SPI */
    SPI_finish();

    return PointerGetDatum(rettuple);
}

Step 3: Makefile

MODULES = audit_trigger
EXTENSION = audit_trigger
DATA = audit_trigger--1.0.sql

PG_CONFIG = pg_config
PGXS := $(shell $(PG_CONFIG) --pgxs)
include $(PGXS)

Step 4: Extension SQL Script (audit_trigger--1.0.sql)

-- audit_trigger extension

-- Create function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_trigger()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME'
LANGUAGE C;

-- Create control file shows extension metadata
COMMENT ON FUNCTION audit_trigger() IS 
    'Comprehensive audit trail trigger function';

Step 5: Build and Install

# Compile the extension
make clean
make

# Install (requires superuser privileges)
sudo make install

# In PostgreSQL, create the extension
psql -d your_database -c "CREATE EXTENSION audit_trigger;"
Compilation Output:
gcc -Wall -Wmissing-prototypes -Wpointer-arith -Wdeclaration-after-statement...
gcc -shared -o audit_trigger.so audit_trigger.o
✅ Build successful

Step 6: Apply Trigger to Tables

-- Apply audit trigger to specific table
CREATE TRIGGER employees_audit_trigger
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();

-- Apply to multiple tables
CREATE TRIGGER products_audit_trigger
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON products
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();

CREATE TRIGGER orders_audit_trigger
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON orders
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();

Step 7: Test the Trigger

-- Test INSERT
INSERT INTO employees (first_name, last_name, email, salary)
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', 'john.doe@example.com', 50000);

-- Test UPDATE
UPDATE employees
SET salary = 55000
WHERE email = 'john.doe@example.com';

-- Test DELETE
DELETE FROM employees
WHERE email = 'john.doe@example.com';

-- View audit log
SELECT 
    log_id,
    table_name,
    operation,
    user_name,
    logged_at,
    old_data,
    new_data
FROM audit_log
ORDER BY logged_at DESC
LIMIT 10;
Sample Audit Log Output:
operationuser_nametable_namelogged_at
DELETEpostgresemployees2026-01-26 14:32:15
UPDATEpostgresemployees2026-01-26 14:31:50
INSERTpostgresemployees2026-01-26 14:31:22

Advanced: Querying Audit Data

🔍 Useful Audit Queries

-- Find all changes by a specific user
SELECT * FROM audit_log
WHERE user_name = 'john_smith'
ORDER BY logged_at DESC;

-- Find all changes to a specific table in last 24 hours
SELECT * FROM audit_log
WHERE table_name = 'employees'
  AND logged_at > CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '24 hours';

-- Compare old and new values for UPDATEs
SELECT 
    log_id,
    table_name,
    logged_at,
    old_data->>'salary' AS old_salary,
    new_data->>'salary' AS new_salary,
    (new_data->>'salary')::numeric - (old_data->>'salary')::numeric AS change
FROM audit_log
WHERE operation = 'UPDATE'
  AND table_name = 'employees'
  AND old_data->>'salary' IS DISTINCT FROM new_data->>'salary';

-- Find who deleted specific records
SELECT 
    user_name,
    logged_at,
    old_data
FROM audit_log
WHERE operation = 'DELETE'
  AND table_name = 'employees'
  AND old_data->>'email' = 'john.doe@example.com';

Performance Considerations

✅ Optimization Tips

  • Use AFTER triggers (don't block operations)
  • Index audit_log by table_name and timestamp
  • Partition audit_log by date for large systems
  • Archive old audit data regularly
  • Use JSONB for efficient querying

⚡ Performance Impact

  • ~5-10% overhead for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
  • Storage: ~2-3x the size of source tables
  • Minimal impact on SELECT queries
  • Can defer using constraint triggers if needed

Extension and Customization

The audit trigger can be extended to include additional features:

🔧 Possible Enhancements

  • Column-level tracking: Record only changed columns in UPDATE operations
  • Exclude columns: Skip sensitive columns like passwords
  • Conditional auditing: Only audit if certain conditions are met
  • Compression: Compress JSONB data for older entries
  • Async logging: Use background workers for high-volume systems
  • Alerting: Trigger notifications on suspicious changes

✅ Chapter 37 Summary

This chapter covered PostgreSQL triggers in depth:

  • Trigger Behavior: BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF triggers at row and statement levels
  • Data Visibility: Understanding what data is visible at different trigger execution points
  • C Trigger Functions: Low-level interface using TriggerData structure
  • Complete Example: Production-ready audit trail system with JSONB logging
🎯 Key Concepts
  • Trigger timing
  • Trigger levels
  • Return values
  • Execution order
🔧 C Functions
  • TriggerData access
  • Tuple manipulation
  • SPI usage
  • Memory management
💡 Best Practices
  • Use AFTER for auditing
  • Prevent infinite loops
  • Handle NULL values
  • Test thoroughly

Key Takeaway: Triggers provide powerful automation for data validation, auditing, and maintaining data integrity. While procedural languages like PL/pgSQL are easier for most use cases, C triggers offer maximum performance for high-volume operations. Always consider the performance impact and visibility rules when designing trigger-based solutions.

Chapter 38. Event Triggers

📌 Chapter Overview

Event triggers are database callbacks that fire on database-wide events rather than on table-specific operations. Unlike regular triggers that respond to DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on specific tables, event triggers respond to DDL commands and database login events across the entire database.

🎯 What You'll Learn:
  • Event trigger types and events
  • DDL command interception
  • Login event handling
  • Writing event trigger functions
  • Real-world use cases
🔧 Key Concepts:
  • Database-wide scope
  • DDL operation tracking
  • Access control enforcement
  • Audit trail creation
  • Schema change prevention

38.1 Overview of Event Trigger Behavior

Event triggers are global to a particular database and can capture DDL events. PostgreSQL supports several event types that allow you to intercept and control database schema changes and user logins.

🌐 Event Trigger vs Regular Trigger

Feature Regular Trigger Event Trigger
Scope Specific table Entire database
Events DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) DDL commands, login events
Timing BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF Before command/After command/At login
Use Case Data validation, auditing rows Schema governance, access control

Supported Event Types

PostgreSQL provides five event types for event triggers:

🔐 login

When: User attempts to connect to database

Use Case: Access control, connection auditing

Can Block: ✅ Yes

▶️ ddl_command_start

When: Before DDL command executes

Use Case: Prevent schema changes

Can Block: ✅ Yes

✅ ddl_command_end

When: After DDL command completes

Use Case: Audit DDL operations

Can Block: ❌ No (already executed)

🗑️ sql_drop

When: Before objects are dropped

Use Case: Track dropped objects

Can Block: ❌ No (objects already marked)

🔄 table_rewrite

When: Table being rewritten by command

Use Case: Prevent costly operations

Can Block: ✅ Yes

Event Trigger Execution Flow

DDL Command with Event Triggers

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DDL COMMAND ISSUED                     │
│         (e.g., CREATE TABLE employees)              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
        │  ddl_command_start triggers    │  ← Can PREVENT execution
        │  (Fire BEFORE command)         │     by raising ERROR
        └───────────────┬────────────────┘
                        │
            ┌───────────▼───────────┐
            │  Execute DDL Command  │
            └───────────┬───────────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
        │  ddl_command_end triggers      │  ← Can audit but not prevent
        │  (Fire AFTER command)          │
        └───────────────┬────────────────┘
                        │
            ┌───────────▼────────────┐
            │    COMMAND COMPLETE    │
            └────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          DROP COMMAND SPECIAL CASE                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
        │  ddl_command_start triggers    │
        └───────────────┬────────────────┘
                        │
            ┌───────────▼───────────┐
            │  Mark objects for drop│
            └───────────┬───────────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
        │  sql_drop triggers             │  ← Access drop details
        │  (Before actual deletion)      │
        └───────────────┬────────────────┘
                        │
            ┌───────────▼───────────┐
            │  Actually drop objects│
            └───────────┬───────────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐
        │  ddl_command_end triggers      │
        └───────────────┬────────────────┘
                        │
                   ┌────▼─────┐
                   │ COMPLETE │
                   └──────────┘

38.1.1 Login Event Triggers

Login event triggers fire when a user successfully authenticates but before the session is fully established. They allow you to enforce additional access controls, log connection attempts, or reject connections based on custom logic.

⚡ Key Features

  • Fires: After successful authentication, before session establishment
  • Can prevent login: Yes, by raising an exception
  • Access to: User information, connection details, database name
  • Use cases: Time-based access, IP filtering, connection limits
Creating a Login Event Trigger
-- Create function that fires on login
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION check_user_login()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    current_hour INTEGER;
BEGIN
    current_hour := EXTRACT(HOUR FROM CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    
    -- Restrict non-superusers to business hours (9 AM - 5 PM)
    IF NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles 
        WHERE rolname = CURRENT_USER AND rolsuper
    ) THEN
        IF current_hour < 9 OR current_hour >= 17 THEN
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: Non-business hours (% o''clock)', current_hour
                USING HINT = 'Please connect during business hours (9 AM - 5 PM)';
        END IF;
    END IF;
    
    -- Log all login attempts
    INSERT INTO login_audit (username, login_time, client_addr)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, NOW(), inet_client_addr());
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'User % logged in at %', CURRENT_USER, NOW();
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Create the event trigger
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER restrict_login_hours
    ON login
    EXECUTE FUNCTION check_user_login();

📊 Login Event Trigger Example Use Cases

Use Case Implementation Security Benefit
Time-based access Check current hour, reject outside window Limit exposure to off-hours attacks
IP whitelisting Check inet_client_addr() against allowed IPs Prevent unauthorized network access
Connection auditing Log every connection with timestamp Track all access for compliance
Concurrent limit Count active sessions per user Prevent credential sharing

38.1.2 DDL Command Start Event Triggers

The ddl_command_start event fires before a DDL command is executed, giving you the opportunity to inspect and potentially block schema changes. This is ideal for enforcing schema governance policies.

⚡ Key Features

  • Fires: Before DDL command execution begins
  • Can prevent execution: Yes, by raising an exception
  • Access to: Command tag (CREATE TABLE, DROP INDEX, etc.)
  • No access to: Detailed object information (use ddl_command_end for that)
Preventing Schema Changes
-- Prevent all DDL in production during business hours
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_ddl_business_hours()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    current_hour INTEGER;
    cmd_tag TEXT;
BEGIN
    current_hour := EXTRACT(HOUR FROM CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    cmd_tag := tg_tag;
    
    -- Block all DDL during business hours (9 AM - 6 PM)
    IF current_hour >= 9 AND current_hour < 18 THEN
        -- Allow only superusers
        IF NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles 
            WHERE rolname = CURRENT_USER AND rolsuper
        ) THEN
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'DDL command % blocked: Business hours restriction', cmd_tag
                USING HINT = 'Schema changes are only allowed outside business hours (9 AM - 6 PM)',
                      DETAIL = 'Contact DBA for emergency schema changes';
        END IF;
    END IF;
    
    -- Log all DDL attempts
    INSERT INTO ddl_audit (username, command, attempted_at, allowed)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, cmd_tag, NOW(), true);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER block_ddl_business_hours
    ON ddl_command_start
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_ddl_business_hours();
Selective DDL Blocking with TAG Filter
-- Only prevent DROP operations, allow other DDL
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_drops()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Check if command is a DROP command
    IF tg_tag LIKE 'DROP%' THEN
        -- Allow superusers only
        IF NOT pg_has_role(CURRENT_USER, 'pg_database_owner', 'MEMBER') THEN
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'DROP operations are restricted'
                USING HINT = 'Contact database administrator for object removal';
        END IF;
    END IF;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Create event trigger with TAG filter (PostgreSQL 12+)
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER no_drops
    ON ddl_command_start
    WHEN TAG IN ('DROP TABLE', 'DROP VIEW', 'DROP FUNCTION', 'DROP INDEX')
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_drops();

🎯 Available Command Tags

You can filter on specific DDL commands using the TAG clause:

Table Operations:
  • CREATE TABLE
  • ALTER TABLE
  • DROP TABLE
Function Operations:
  • CREATE FUNCTION
  • ALTER FUNCTION
  • DROP FUNCTION
Index Operations:
  • CREATE INDEX
  • ALTER INDEX
  • DROP INDEX

38.1.3 DDL Command End Event Triggers

The ddl_command_end event fires after a DDL command successfully completes. Unlike ddl_command_start, this event provides detailed information about the objects that were created or altered, making it perfect for auditing and documentation.

⚡ Key Features

  • Fires: After DDL command successfully completes
  • Can prevent execution: No (command already executed, but can roll back transaction)
  • Access to: Detailed object information via pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands()
  • Use cases: Auditing, documentation generation, replication tracking
Comprehensive DDL Auditing
-- Create audit table
CREATE TABLE ddl_history (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    event_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    command_tag TEXT NOT NULL,
    object_type TEXT,
    schema_name TEXT,
    object_identity TEXT,
    object_definition TEXT,
    client_addr INET
);

-- Audit function that captures detailed DDL information
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_ddl_commands()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj RECORD;
    cmd_tag TEXT := tg_tag;
BEGIN
    -- Iterate through all objects affected by the DDL command
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands()
    LOOP
        INSERT INTO ddl_history (
            username,
            command_tag,
            object_type,
            schema_name,
            object_identity,
            object_definition,
            client_addr
        ) VALUES (
            CURRENT_USER,
            cmd_tag,
            obj.object_type,
            obj.schema_name,
            obj.object_identity,
            obj.command,  -- Full DDL command text
            inet_client_addr()
        );
        
        -- Log to server log as well
        RAISE NOTICE 'DDL: % created % %', 
            CURRENT_USER, obj.object_type, obj.object_identity;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER ddl_audit_trigger
    ON ddl_command_end
    EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_ddl_commands();

📊 pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands() Return Columns

Column Type Description
classid oid OID of catalog table (e.g., pg_class, pg_proc)
objid oid OID of the object in its catalog
objsubid integer Sub-object ID (e.g., column number)
command_tag text Command tag (CREATE TABLE, etc.)
object_type text Type of object (table, function, index, etc.)
schema_name text Schema containing the object
object_identity text Full qualified name of object
in_extension boolean Is object part of an extension?
command text Complete DDL command executed
Example: Query DDL Audit Trail
-- View recent schema changes
SELECT 
    event_time,
    username,
    command_tag,
    object_type,
    object_identity,
    LEFT(object_definition, 60) AS definition_preview
FROM ddl_history
ORDER BY event_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Result:
┌─────────────────────────┬──────────┬──────────────────┬─────────────┬─────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐
│       event_time        │ username │   command_tag    │ object_type │      object_identity        │    definition_preview        │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────┼──────────────────┼─────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│ 2024-01-15 14:23:45-05  │ alice    │ CREATE TABLE     │ table       │ public.employees            │ CREATE TABLE public.employees│
│ 2024-01-15 14:22:10-05  │ bob      │ CREATE INDEX     │ index       │ public.idx_emp_name         │ CREATE INDEX idx_emp_name ON │
│ 2024-01-15 13:15:33-05  │ alice    │ ALTER TABLE      │ table       │ public.departments          │ ALTER TABLE public.departmen │
└─────────────────────────┴──────────┴──────────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┘

38.1.4 SQL Drop Event Triggers

The sql_drop event fires just before objects are physically dropped from the database. This event is unique because it provides information about objects being dropped, including dependencies, even though the DROP command has already been processed.

⚡ Key Features

  • Fires: After DROP command processed, before physical deletion
  • Can prevent execution: No (objects already marked for deletion)
  • Access to: List of all objects being dropped via pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
  • Special feature: Only event that fires during DROP operations
Tracking Dropped Objects
-- Create table to track dropped objects
CREATE TABLE dropped_objects_log (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    drop_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    object_type TEXT,
    schema_name TEXT,
    object_name TEXT,
    object_identity TEXT,
    is_temporary BOOLEAN,
    original_exists BOOLEAN
);

-- Function to log all dropped objects
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_dropped_objects()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj RECORD;
BEGIN
    -- Iterate through all dropped objects
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
    LOOP
        INSERT INTO dropped_objects_log (
            username,
            object_type,
            schema_name,
            object_name,
            object_identity,
            is_temporary,
            original_exists
        ) VALUES (
            CURRENT_USER,
            obj.object_type,
            obj.schema_name,
            obj.object_name,
            obj.object_identity,
            obj.is_temporary,
            obj.original
        );
        
        -- Warn about important drops
        IF obj.object_type IN ('table', 'view') AND NOT obj.is_temporary THEN
            RAISE WARNING 'Permanent % %.% dropped by %',
                obj.object_type, obj.schema_name, obj.object_name, CURRENT_USER;
        END IF;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER track_drops
    ON sql_drop
    EXECUTE FUNCTION log_dropped_objects();

📊 pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects() Return Columns

Column Type Description
classid oid OID of catalog containing object
objid oid OID of the object
objsubid integer Sub-object ID (0 for whole object)
original boolean True if explicitly dropped, false if cascade
normal boolean True for normal drop, false for abnormal
is_temporary boolean Is this a temporary object?
object_type text Type of dropped object
schema_name text Schema name (NULL if no schema)
object_name text Object name
object_identity text Fully qualified object name
address_names text[] Array of name components
address_args text[] Array of type arguments (for functions)
Tracking Cascade Drops
-- Advanced drop tracking with cascade detection
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION detailed_drop_tracking()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj RECORD;
    original_count INTEGER := 0;
    cascade_count INTEGER := 0;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
    LOOP
        IF obj.original THEN
            original_count := original_count + 1;
            RAISE NOTICE 'EXPLICITLY DROPPED: % %.%', 
                obj.object_type, obj.schema_name, obj.object_name;
        ELSE
            cascade_count := cascade_count + 1;
            RAISE NOTICE 'CASCADE DROPPED: % %.% (dependency)', 
                obj.object_type, obj.schema_name, obj.object_name;
        END IF;
        
        -- Log everything
        INSERT INTO dropped_objects_log (
            username, object_type, schema_name, 
            object_name, object_identity, original_exists
        ) VALUES (
            CURRENT_USER, obj.object_type, obj.schema_name,
            obj.object_name, obj.object_identity, obj.original
        );
    END LOOP;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Drop summary: % explicit, % cascade', original_count, cascade_count;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER detailed_drop_tracker
    ON sql_drop
    EXECUTE FUNCTION detailed_drop_tracking();

-- Example usage:
DROP TABLE departments CASCADE;

-- Output:
-- NOTICE:  EXPLICITLY DROPPED: table public.departments
-- NOTICE:  CASCADE DROPPED: constraint public.employees_dept_id_fkey (dependency)
-- NOTICE:  CASCADE DROPPED: view public.dept_employee_count (dependency)
-- NOTICE:  Drop summary: 1 explicit, 2 cascade

38.1.5 Table Rewrite Event Triggers

The table_rewrite event fires when an ALTER TABLE command requires rewriting the entire table. Table rewrites are expensive operations that can lock tables for extended periods, making this event crucial for preventing accidental performance issues in production.

⚡ Key Features

  • Fires: When ALTER TABLE requires a table rewrite
  • Can prevent execution: Yes, by raising an exception
  • Access to: Table OID, reason for rewrite
  • Common triggers: ALTER COLUMN TYPE, ADD COLUMN with non-null default, SET TABLESPACE
Preventing Costly Table Rewrites
-- Prevent table rewrites on large tables in production
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_large_table_rewrites()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    table_name TEXT;
    table_size BIGINT;
    table_rows BIGINT;
    size_threshold BIGINT := 10 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024; -- 10 GB
BEGIN
    -- Get table information using tg_table_name and tg_table_schema
    SELECT 
        pg_total_relation_size(tg_table_oid),
        (SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE oid = tg_table_oid)
    INTO table_size, table_rows;
    
    -- Get fully qualified table name
    SELECT schemaname || '.' || tablename
    INTO table_name
    FROM pg_tables
    WHERE schemaname || '.' || tablename = 
          quote_ident(tg_table_schema) || '.' || quote_ident(tg_table_name);
    
    -- Block rewrites on tables larger than 10 GB
    IF table_size > size_threshold THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Table rewrite blocked: Table % is too large (% GB, % rows)',
            table_name,
            ROUND(table_size::NUMERIC / (1024^3), 2),
            table_rows
            USING HINT = 'Large table rewrites require maintenance window approval',
                  DETAIL = 'This operation would lock the table and copy all data';
    END IF;
    
    -- Warn about medium-sized tables
    IF table_size > 1024 * 1024 * 1024 THEN -- > 1 GB
        RAISE WARNING 'Rewriting table % (% GB, % rows) - this may take time',
            table_name,
            ROUND(table_size::NUMERIC / (1024^3), 2),
            table_rows;
    END IF;
    
    -- Log rewrite attempt
    INSERT INTO table_rewrite_log (username, table_name, table_size, allowed)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, table_name, table_size, true);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER block_large_rewrites
    ON table_rewrite
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_large_table_rewrites();

🔄 Operations That Trigger Table Rewrites

Operation Causes Rewrite? Why?
ALTER COLUMN TYPE Usually ✅ Must convert all values to new type
ADD COLUMN DEFAULT value ❌ (PG 11+) Default stored in catalog, not rewritten
ADD COLUMN NOT NULL DEFAULT ❌ (PG 11+) Optimized to avoid rewrite
SET TABLESPACE ✅ Physical file relocation required
SET STORAGE ❌ Only affects future data
DROP COLUMN ❌ Column marked invisible, not physically removed
Detecting Specific Rewrite Causes
-- Check what specific operation is causing the rewrite
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION analyze_rewrite_cause()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    reason TEXT;
BEGIN
    -- The tg_tag variable contains the command that triggered the rewrite
    reason := tg_tag;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Table rewrite triggered by: %', reason;
    RAISE NOTICE 'Table: %.%', tg_table_schema, tg_table_name;
    RAISE NOTICE 'OID: %', tg_table_oid;
    
    -- You can inspect the current command for details
    RAISE NOTICE 'Current query: %', current_query();
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER analyze_rewrites
    ON table_rewrite
    EXECUTE FUNCTION analyze_rewrite_cause();

-- Example:
ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN salary TYPE NUMERIC(12,2);

-- Output:
-- NOTICE:  Table rewrite triggered by: ALTER TABLE
-- NOTICE:  Table: public.employees
-- NOTICE:  OID: 16384
-- NOTICE:  Current query: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN salary TYPE NUMERIC(12,2);

38.1.6 Event Triggers and Aborted Transactions

Event triggers fire even if the transaction is later rolled back. However, any actions taken by the event trigger function will also be rolled back with the transaction.

⚠️ Important Behavior

  • Event triggers fire before transaction commits
  • If transaction aborts, trigger actions are rolled back
  • To persist audit records even on rollback, use autonomous transactions (dblink) or external logging
  • Server log messages (RAISE LOG) persist regardless of rollback
Rollback-Safe Logging with dblink
-- Create extension for autonomous transactions
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS dblink;

-- Function that logs even if transaction rolls back
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_ddl_autonomous()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    conn_name TEXT := 'audit_conn';
    audit_db TEXT := current_database();
    cmd_tag TEXT := tg_tag;
BEGIN
    -- Open autonomous transaction
    PERFORM dblink_connect(conn_name, 
        format('dbname=%s', audit_db));
    
    -- Insert audit record in separate transaction
    PERFORM dblink_exec(conn_name, format(
        'INSERT INTO ddl_audit (username, command, event_time) VALUES (%L, %L, %L)',
        CURRENT_USER, cmd_tag, NOW()
    ));
    
    -- Commit the audit (independent of main transaction)
    PERFORM dblink_disconnect(conn_name);
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'DDL command % audited (rollback-safe)', cmd_tag;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER audit_ddl_rollback_safe
    ON ddl_command_start
    EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_ddl_autonomous();

-- Test: This DDL will fail but audit record persists
BEGIN;
    CREATE TABLE test_table (id INT);  -- Audited
    SELECT 1/0;  -- Causes error and rollback
ROLLBACK;

-- Check audit table - record exists despite rollback
SELECT * FROM ddl_audit WHERE command = 'CREATE TABLE';
Using Server Log for Permanent Records
-- Simpler approach: Use RAISE LOG for permanent record
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_ddl_to_server()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    -- RAISE LOG writes to PostgreSQL server log, never rolled back
    RAISE LOG 'DDL: user=%, command=%, query=%',
        CURRENT_USER, tg_tag, current_query();
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER permanent_ddl_log
    ON ddl_command_start
    EXECUTE FUNCTION log_ddl_to_server();

-- This appears in postgresql.log even if transaction aborts:
-- LOG:  DDL: user=alice, command=CREATE TABLE, query=CREATE TABLE ...

38.1.7 Creating Event Triggers

Creating event triggers involves two steps: defining a function that returns type event_trigger, and then creating the event trigger itself with optional filtering.

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER Syntax
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER name
    ON event
    [ WHEN filter_variable IN (filter_value [, ... ]) [ AND ... ] ]
    EXECUTE FUNCTION function_name()

-- Events: login | ddl_command_start | ddl_command_end | sql_drop | table_rewrite
-- Filter variables: TAG (for DDL events)
-- Function must return type: event_trigger

📋 Event Trigger Syntax Elements

Element Description Example
name Unique trigger name in database my_ddl_trigger
event One of the five event types ddl_command_start
WHEN clause Optional filter (TAG for DDL) WHEN TAG IN ('DROP TABLE')
function Function returning event_trigger my_trigger_function()
Complete Example: Filtered Event Trigger
-- Step 1: Create the trigger function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_table_drops()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Check if user has special permission
    IF NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles
        WHERE rolname = CURRENT_USER 
        AND (rolsuper OR pg_has_role(CURRENT_USER, 'schema_admin', 'MEMBER'))
    ) THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Permission denied: DROP TABLE requires schema_admin role'
            USING HINT = 'Contact DBA to request schema_admin role';
    END IF;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'DROP TABLE allowed for user %', CURRENT_USER;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Step 2: Create event trigger with TAG filter
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER no_table_drops
    ON ddl_command_start
    WHEN TAG IN ('DROP TABLE')
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_table_drops();

-- Multiple tags possible:
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER no_structure_drops
    ON ddl_command_start
    WHEN TAG IN ('DROP TABLE', 'DROP VIEW', 'DROP SCHEMA', 'DROP DATABASE')
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_table_drops();
Managing Event Triggers
-- List all event triggers
SELECT evtname AS trigger_name,
       evtevent AS event_type,
       evtenabled AS status,
       evttags AS filter_tags,
       pg_get_functiondef(evtfoid) AS function_code
FROM pg_event_trigger
ORDER BY evtname;

-- Disable event trigger (superuser only)
ALTER EVENT TRIGGER no_table_drops DISABLE;

-- Re-enable event trigger
ALTER EVENT TRIGGER no_table_drops ENABLE;

-- Enable only for replica (useful for selective enforcement)
ALTER EVENT TRIGGER no_table_drops ENABLE REPLICA;

-- Enable only for always (default)
ALTER EVENT TRIGGER no_table_drops ENABLE ALWAYS;

-- Drop event trigger
DROP EVENT TRIGGER IF EXISTS no_table_drops;

-- Drop the function
DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS prevent_table_drops();

🔒 Permissions Requirements

  • Creating event triggers: Requires superuser privileges
  • Dropping event triggers: Requires superuser privileges
  • Enabling/disabling: Requires superuser privileges
  • Executing trigger function: Runs with privileges of trigger owner (superuser)
  • Security consideration: Event trigger functions have elevated privileges, write them carefully

38.2 Event Trigger Functions in C

Event trigger functions can be written in C for maximum performance and low-level access to PostgreSQL internals. C event trigger functions work similarly to C regular triggers but receive different context information.

🔧 Key Differences from Regular Triggers

  • Context structure: EventTriggerData instead of TriggerData
  • Return value: Always return NULL (return value is ignored)
  • Scope: No access to table data, but can query system catalogs
  • Macro: Use CALLED_AS_EVENT_TRIGGER(fcinfo) to verify context
EventTriggerData Structure
// Include required headers
#include "postgres.h"
#include "commands/event_trigger.h"
#include "utils/builtins.h"

// EventTriggerData structure definition
typedef struct EventTriggerData
{
    NodeTag     type;              // Node type identifier
    const char *event;             // Event name: "ddl_command_start", "ddl_command_end", etc.
    Node       *parsetree;         // Parse tree of command (if available)
    const char *tag;               // Command tag: "CREATE TABLE", "DROP INDEX", etc.
} EventTriggerData;

// Check if function was called as event trigger
#define CALLED_AS_EVENT_TRIGGER(fcinfo) \
    ((fcinfo)->context != NULL && IsA((fcinfo)->context, EventTriggerData))
Basic C Event Trigger Function
#include "postgres.h"
#include "commands/event_trigger.h"
#include "utils/builtins.h"
#include "catalog/pg_authid.h"
#include "miscadmin.h"
#include "fmgr.h"

PG_MODULE_MAGIC;

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(prevent_ddl_c);

Datum
prevent_ddl_c(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    EventTriggerData *trigdata;
    
    /* Verify we were called as an event trigger */
    if (!CALLED_AS_EVENT_TRIGGER(fcinfo))
        elog(ERROR, "prevent_ddl_c: not called by event trigger manager");
    
    trigdata = (EventTriggerData *) fcinfo->context;
    
    /* Check if user is superuser */
    if (!superuser())
    {
        ereport(ERROR,
                (errcode(ERRCODE_INSUFFICIENT_PRIVILEGE),
                 errmsg("DDL command \"%s\" blocked by security policy", trigdata->tag),
                 errhint("Only superusers can execute DDL commands")));
    }
    
    /* Log the DDL command */
    elog(NOTICE, "DDL command: %s by user %s", 
         trigdata->tag, GetUserNameFromId(GetUserId(), false));
    
    /* Event triggers must return NULL */
    PG_RETURN_NULL();
}
Advanced Example: Querying Event Details
#include "postgres.h"
#include "commands/event_trigger.h"
#include "executor/spi.h"
#include "utils/builtins.h"
#include "utils/rel.h"
#include "catalog/namespace.h"
#include "fmgr.h"

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(audit_ddl_detailed_c);

Datum
audit_ddl_detailed_c(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    EventTriggerData *trigdata;
    int              ret;
    
    if (!CALLED_AS_EVENT_TRIGGER(fcinfo))
        elog(ERROR, "audit_ddl_detailed_c: not called by event trigger manager");
    
    trigdata = (EventTriggerData *) fcinfo->context;
    
    /* Connect to SPI for database queries */
    ret = SPI_connect();
    if (ret != SPI_OK_CONNECT)
        elog(ERROR, "audit_ddl_detailed_c: SPI_connect failed");
    
    /* Only process ddl_command_end events */
    if (strcmp(trigdata->event, "ddl_command_end") == 0)
    {
        char query[1024];
        
        /* Query pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands() for details */
        snprintf(query, sizeof(query),
                 "SELECT object_type, schema_name, object_identity "
                 "FROM pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands()");
        
        ret = SPI_execute(query, true, 0);
        
        if (ret == SPI_OK_SELECT && SPI_processed > 0)
        {
            int i;
            for (i = 0; i < SPI_processed; i++)
            {
                char *obj_type = SPI_getvalue(SPI_tuptable->vals[i], 
                                              SPI_tuptable->tupdesc, 1);
                char *schema = SPI_getvalue(SPI_tuptable->vals[i],
                                           SPI_tuptable->tupdesc, 2);
                char *obj_id = SPI_getvalue(SPI_tuptable->vals[i],
                                           SPI_tuptable->tupdesc, 3);
