                elog(NOTICE, "Created %s: %s.%s", obj_type, schema, obj_id);
            }
        }
    }
    
    SPI_finish();
    PG_RETURN_NULL();
}
Makefile and Installation
# Makefile for event trigger C functions
MODULE_big = event_trigger_funcs
OBJS = event_trigger_funcs.o

EXTENSION = event_trigger_funcs
DATA = event_trigger_funcs--1.0.sql

PG_CONFIG = pg_config
PGXS := $(shell $(PG_CONFIG) --pgxs)
include $(PGXS)
Installation Commands
# Compile and install the C module
make
sudo make install

# In PostgreSQL, register the functions
CREATE FUNCTION prevent_ddl_c() RETURNS event_trigger
AS 'event_trigger_funcs', 'prevent_ddl_c'
LANGUAGE C;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER prevent_ddl_trigger
    ON ddl_command_start
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_ddl_c();

38.3 A Complete Event Trigger Example

This comprehensive example demonstrates a production-ready event trigger system that provides schema governance, auditing, and access control. It combines multiple event types to create a complete solution.

🎯 System Features

  • Access control: Time-based login restrictions
  • Schema protection: Prevent accidental DROP operations
  • DDL auditing: Track all schema changes
  • Drop tracking: Log all dropped objects including cascades
  • Rewrite protection: Block expensive table rewrites
Step 1: Create Audit Tables
-- Login audit table
CREATE TABLE login_audit (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    login_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    client_addr INET,
    application_name TEXT,
    success BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- DDL audit table
CREATE TABLE ddl_audit (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    event_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    command_tag TEXT NOT NULL,
    object_type TEXT,
    schema_name TEXT,
    object_identity TEXT,
    object_definition TEXT,
    client_addr INET,
    application_name TEXT
);

-- Drop audit table
CREATE TABLE drop_audit (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    drop_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    object_type TEXT,
    schema_name TEXT,
    object_name TEXT,
    object_identity TEXT,
    was_original BOOLEAN,
    was_temporary BOOLEAN
);

-- Table rewrite log
CREATE TABLE rewrite_audit (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    rewrite_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    table_name TEXT,
    table_size_bytes BIGINT,
    table_rows BIGINT,
    allowed BOOLEAN
);

-- Create indexes for efficient querying
CREATE INDEX idx_login_audit_time ON login_audit(login_time);
CREATE INDEX idx_login_audit_user ON login_audit(username);
CREATE INDEX idx_ddl_audit_time ON ddl_audit(event_time);
CREATE INDEX idx_ddl_audit_user ON ddl_audit(username);
CREATE INDEX idx_drop_audit_time ON drop_audit(drop_time);
Step 2: Login Event Trigger
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION secure_login_check()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    current_hour INTEGER;
    user_is_admin BOOLEAN;
    client_ip INET;
    app_name TEXT;
BEGIN
    current_hour := EXTRACT(HOUR FROM CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    client_ip := inet_client_addr();
    app_name := current_setting('application_name', true);
    
    -- Check if user has admin privileges
    user_is_admin := EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles
        WHERE rolname = CURRENT_USER 
        AND (rolsuper OR pg_has_role(CURRENT_USER, 'db_admin', 'MEMBER'))
    );
    
    -- Enforce business hours for non-admin users
    IF NOT user_is_admin THEN
        IF current_hour < 7 OR current_hour >= 19 THEN
            -- Log failed attempt
            INSERT INTO login_audit (username, client_addr, application_name, success)
            VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, app_name, false);
            
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: Non-business hours (current: % o''clock)', current_hour
                USING HINT = 'Business hours: 7 AM - 7 PM. Contact DBA for after-hours access.',
                      ERRCODE = 'insufficient_privilege';
        END IF;
    END IF;
    
    -- Log successful login
    INSERT INTO login_audit (username, client_addr, application_name, success)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, app_name, true);
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'User % logged in from % at %', CURRENT_USER, client_ip, NOW();
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER enforce_login_policy
    ON login
    EXECUTE FUNCTION secure_login_check();
Step 3: DDL Protection and Auditing
-- Prevent DROP operations on critical schemas
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION protect_critical_objects()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    cmd TEXT := tg_tag;
    protected_schemas TEXT[] := ARRAY['audit', 'security', 'core'];
    query_text TEXT := current_query();
BEGIN
    -- Block DROP operations on protected schemas
    IF cmd LIKE 'DROP%' THEN
        -- Check if any protected schema is mentioned
        IF EXISTS (
            SELECT 1 FROM unnest(protected_schemas) AS schema
            WHERE query_text ~* ('\\m' || schema || '\\M')
        ) THEN
            IF NOT pg_has_role(CURRENT_USER, 'schema_owner', 'MEMBER') THEN
                RAISE EXCEPTION 'DROP operation blocked: Protected schema'
                    USING HINT = 'Protected schemas: %. Contact DBA.', 
                          array_to_string(protected_schemas, ', ');
            END IF;
        END IF;
    END IF;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'DDL command % initiated by %', cmd, CURRENT_USER;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER protect_schemas
    ON ddl_command_start
    WHEN TAG IN ('DROP SCHEMA', 'DROP TABLE', 'DROP VIEW', 
                 'DROP FUNCTION', 'DROP SEQUENCE')
    EXECUTE FUNCTION protect_critical_objects();

-- Comprehensive DDL auditing (runs AFTER command succeeds)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_ddl_complete()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj RECORD;
    app_name TEXT := current_setting('application_name', true);
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands()
    LOOP
        INSERT INTO ddl_audit (
            username,
            command_tag,
            object_type,
            schema_name,
            object_identity,
            object_definition,
            client_addr,
            application_name
        ) VALUES (
            CURRENT_USER,
            tg_tag,
            obj.object_type,
            obj.schema_name,
            obj.object_identity,
            obj.command,
            inet_client_addr(),
            app_name
        );
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER audit_all_ddl
    ON ddl_command_end
    EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_ddl_complete();
Step 4: Track Dropped Objects
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION track_all_drops()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj RECORD;
    explicit_count INTEGER := 0;
    cascade_count INTEGER := 0;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
    LOOP
        INSERT INTO drop_audit (
            username,
            object_type,
            schema_name,
            object_name,
            object_identity,
            was_original,
            was_temporary
        ) VALUES (
            CURRENT_USER,
            obj.object_type,
            obj.schema_name,
            obj.object_name,
            obj.object_identity,
            obj.original,
            obj.is_temporary
        );
        
        IF obj.original THEN
            explicit_count := explicit_count + 1;
            RAISE NOTICE 'DROPPED: % %', obj.object_type, obj.object_identity;
        ELSE
            cascade_count := cascade_count + 1;
            RAISE NOTICE 'CASCADE: % %', obj.object_type, obj.object_identity;
        END IF;
    END LOOP;
    
    IF cascade_count > 0 THEN
        RAISE WARNING 'Drop operation affected % explicit + % cascaded objects',
            explicit_count, cascade_count;
    END IF;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER track_drops
    ON sql_drop
    EXECUTE FUNCTION track_all_drops();
Step 5: Prevent Expensive Rewrites
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_large_rewrites()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    tbl_size BIGINT;
    tbl_rows BIGINT;
    tbl_name TEXT;
    size_limit BIGINT := 5 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024; -- 5 GB
BEGIN
    -- Get table information
    SELECT pg_total_relation_size(tg_table_oid),
           (SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE oid = tg_table_oid)::BIGINT,
           quote_ident(tg_table_schema) || '.' || quote_ident(tg_table_name)
    INTO tbl_size, tbl_rows, tbl_name;
    
    -- Log the rewrite attempt
    INSERT INTO rewrite_audit (username, table_name, table_size_bytes, 
                               table_rows, allowed)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, tbl_name, tbl_size, tbl_rows, tbl_size <= size_limit);
    
    -- Block large rewrites
    IF tbl_size > size_limit THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Table rewrite blocked: Table % is too large', tbl_name
            USING DETAIL = format('Size: %s GB, Rows: %s', 
                                 ROUND(tbl_size::NUMERIC/(1024^3), 2), tbl_rows),
                  HINT = 'Large table alterations require a maintenance window';
    END IF;
    
    -- Warn about medium tables
    IF tbl_size > 1024 * 1024 * 1024 THEN
        RAISE WARNING 'Rewriting table % (% GB) - this will take time',
            tbl_name, ROUND(tbl_size::NUMERIC/(1024^3), 2);
    END IF;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER block_large_rewrites
    ON table_rewrite
    EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_large_rewrites();
Step 6: Monitoring and Reporting Views
-- View: Recent DDL activity
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW recent_ddl AS
SELECT 
    event_time,
    username,
    command_tag,
    object_type,
    schema_name,
    object_identity,
    LEFT(object_definition, 80) AS definition_preview
FROM ddl_audit
WHERE event_time > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days'
ORDER BY event_time DESC;

-- View: Recent drops with cascade analysis
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW recent_drops AS
SELECT 
    drop_time,
    username,
    object_type,
    schema_name,
    object_name,
    CASE WHEN was_original THEN 'EXPLICIT' ELSE 'CASCADE' END AS drop_type
FROM drop_audit
WHERE drop_time > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days'
ORDER BY drop_time DESC;

-- View: Login patterns
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW login_patterns AS
SELECT 
    username,
    DATE(login_time) AS login_date,
    COUNT(*) AS login_count,
    MIN(login_time) AS first_login,
    MAX(login_time) AS last_login,
    COUNT(DISTINCT client_addr) AS distinct_ips
FROM login_audit
WHERE success = TRUE
GROUP BY username, DATE(login_time)
ORDER BY login_date DESC, login_count DESC;

-- View: Schema change frequency
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW schema_change_frequency AS
SELECT 
    schema_name,
    object_type,
    COUNT(*) AS change_count,
    MAX(event_time) AS last_change,
    array_agg(DISTINCT username) AS changed_by
FROM ddl_audit
WHERE event_time > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY schema_name, object_type
ORDER BY change_count DESC;
Testing the Complete System
-- Test 1: Login enforcement (will fail outside business hours for non-admins)
\c mydb regular_user
-- RESULT: ERROR or SUCCESS depending on time

-- Test 2: Create table (should be audited)
CREATE TABLE test_table (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT NOT NULL
);
-- Check audit:
SELECT * FROM ddl_audit WHERE object_identity LIKE '%test_table%';

-- Test 3: Try to drop (may be blocked if in protected schema)
DROP TABLE test_table;
-- Check drop audit:
SELECT * FROM drop_audit WHERE object_name = 'test_table';

-- Test 4: Attempt table rewrite on large table
CREATE TABLE large_table AS SELECT * FROM generate_series(1, 1000000);
ALTER TABLE large_table ALTER COLUMN generate_series TYPE BIGINT;
-- RESULT: May be blocked if table exceeds size threshold

-- Test 5: Drop with cascade
CREATE VIEW test_view AS SELECT * FROM test_table;
DROP TABLE test_table CASCADE;
-- Check for cascade tracking:
SELECT * FROM recent_drops WHERE drop_time > NOW() - INTERVAL '1 minute';

-- Test 6: View all recent activity
SELECT * FROM recent_ddl LIMIT 10;
SELECT * FROM login_patterns WHERE username = CURRENT_USER;
SELECT * FROM schema_change_frequency;

✅ Benefits of This System

Security:
  • Time-based access control
  • Protected schema enforcement
  • Comprehensive audit trail
Operations:
  • Prevent accidental data loss
  • Block expensive operations
  • Track all schema changes
Compliance:
  • Complete activity logs
  • User action tracking
  • Dependency analysis
Maintenance:
  • Historical change tracking
  • Pattern analysis views
  • Easy troubleshooting

38.4 A Table Rewrite Event Trigger Example

This focused example demonstrates advanced table rewrite event trigger usage, including size checking, operation analysis, and integration with a maintenance window system.

Maintenance Window Table
-- Create maintenance window tracking
CREATE TABLE maintenance_windows (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    start_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    end_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    created_by TEXT NOT NULL,
    reason TEXT,
    active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    CONSTRAINT valid_window CHECK (end_time > start_time)
);

-- Function to check if we're in a maintenance window
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION in_maintenance_window()
RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM maintenance_windows
        WHERE active = TRUE
        AND NOW() BETWEEN start_time AND end_time
    );
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql STABLE;

-- Function to schedule maintenance window
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION schedule_maintenance(
    p_start TIMESTAMPTZ,
    p_end TIMESTAMPTZ,
    p_reason TEXT
) RETURNS INTEGER AS $$
DECLARE
    window_id INTEGER;
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO maintenance_windows (start_time, end_time, created_by, reason)
    VALUES (p_start, p_end, CURRENT_USER, p_reason)
    RETURNING id INTO window_id;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Maintenance window % scheduled: % to %', 
        window_id, p_start, p_end;
    
    RETURN window_id;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
Advanced Table Rewrite Protection
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION advanced_rewrite_protection()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    tbl_oid OID := tg_table_oid;
    tbl_schema TEXT := tg_table_schema;
    tbl_name TEXT := tg_table_name;
    tbl_qualified TEXT := quote_ident(tbl_schema) || '.' || quote_ident(tbl_name);
    
    -- Table metrics
    tbl_size BIGINT;
    tbl_rows BIGINT;
    tbl_size_mb NUMERIC;
    
    -- Thresholds
    size_limit_mb NUMERIC := 1024;  -- 1 GB
    row_limit BIGINT := 10000000;   -- 10 million rows
    
    -- Operation details
    operation TEXT := current_query();
    rewrite_reason TEXT;
    
    -- Status flags
    in_maintenance BOOLEAN;
    user_is_admin BOOLEAN;
    allow_rewrite BOOLEAN := FALSE;
BEGIN
    -- Get table metrics
    SELECT 
        pg_total_relation_size(tbl_oid),
        COALESCE((SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE oid = tbl_oid)::BIGINT, 0)
    INTO tbl_size, tbl_rows;
    
    tbl_size_mb := ROUND(tbl_size::NUMERIC / (1024 * 1024), 2);
    
    -- Check maintenance window
    in_maintenance := in_maintenance_window();
    
    -- Check if user is admin
    user_is_admin := pg_has_role(CURRENT_USER, 'pg_database_owner', 'MEMBER') 
                     OR EXISTS (
                         SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles 
                         WHERE rolname = CURRENT_USER AND rolsuper
                     );
    
    -- Determine rewrite reason
    CASE
        WHEN operation ~* 'ALTER\s+COLUMN.*TYPE' THEN
            rewrite_reason := 'Column type change';
        WHEN operation ~* 'SET\s+TABLESPACE' THEN
            rewrite_reason := 'Tablespace change';
        WHEN operation ~* 'ADD\s+COLUMN.*DEFAULT.*NOT\s+NULL' THEN
            rewrite_reason := 'Add NOT NULL column with default';
        ELSE
            rewrite_reason := 'Unknown ALTER TABLE operation';
    END CASE;
    
    -- Decision logic
    IF user_is_admin AND in_maintenance THEN
        -- Admins in maintenance window: allow all
        allow_rewrite := TRUE;
        RAISE NOTICE 'Rewrite allowed: Admin user in maintenance window';
    ELSIF in_maintenance THEN
        -- Non-admin in maintenance window: allow small tables only
        IF tbl_size_mb < size_limit_mb AND tbl_rows < row_limit THEN
            allow_rewrite := TRUE;
            RAISE NOTICE 'Rewrite allowed: Small table in maintenance window';
        ELSE
            allow_rewrite := FALSE;
        END IF;
    ELSIF user_is_admin THEN
        -- Admin outside maintenance: allow small tables only
        IF tbl_size_mb < size_limit_mb / 10 THEN  -- 100 MB for emergency changes
            allow_rewrite := TRUE;
            RAISE WARNING 'Emergency rewrite by admin outside maintenance window';
        ELSE
            allow_rewrite := FALSE;
        END IF;
    ELSE
        -- Regular user outside maintenance: block all
        allow_rewrite := FALSE;
    END IF;
    
    -- Log the attempt
    INSERT INTO rewrite_audit (
        username, table_name, table_size_bytes, 
        table_rows, allowed
    ) VALUES (
        CURRENT_USER, tbl_qualified, tbl_size, 
        tbl_rows, allow_rewrite
    );
    
    -- Block if not allowed
    IF NOT allow_rewrite THEN
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Table rewrite blocked: %', rewrite_reason
            USING DETAIL = format(
                      'Table: %s, Size: %s MB, Rows: %s',
                      tbl_qualified, tbl_size_mb, tbl_rows
                  ),
                  HINT = format(
                      'Schedule a maintenance window or contact DBA. %s',
                      CASE 
                          WHEN NOT in_maintenance THEN 'No active maintenance window.'
                          WHEN NOT user_is_admin THEN 'Requires admin privileges for large tables.'
                          ELSE ''
                      END
                  );
    END IF;
    
    -- Provide warnings even when allowed
    IF tbl_size_mb > 100 THEN
        RAISE WARNING 'Rewriting large table % (% MB, % rows) - estimated time: % minutes',
            tbl_qualified, tbl_size_mb, tbl_rows,
            GREATEST(1, ROUND(tbl_size_mb / 100));  -- Rough estimate: 100 MB/min
    END IF;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER enforce_rewrite_policy
    ON table_rewrite
    EXECUTE FUNCTION advanced_rewrite_protection();
Usage Examples
-- Example 1: Schedule maintenance window
SELECT schedule_maintenance(
    NOW() + INTERVAL '1 day',
    NOW() + INTERVAL '1 day 4 hours',
    'Monthly schema maintenance'
);
-- RESULT: Maintenance window 1 scheduled: ...

-- Example 2: Try rewrite outside maintenance (will fail for large tables)
CREATE TABLE large_employees AS 
SELECT generate_series(1, 15000000) AS id, 
       md5(random()::TEXT) AS name;

ALTER TABLE large_employees ALTER COLUMN id TYPE BIGINT;
-- ERROR: Table rewrite blocked: Column type change
-- DETAIL: Table: public.large_employees, Size: 1234 MB, Rows: 15000000
-- HINT: Schedule a maintenance window or contact DBA. No active maintenance window.

-- Example 3: Small table rewrite (allowed)
CREATE TABLE small_test (id INT);
INSERT INTO small_test SELECT generate_series(1, 1000);

ALTER TABLE small_test ALTER COLUMN id TYPE BIGINT;
-- NOTICE: Rewrite allowed: Small table in maintenance window
-- ALTER TABLE

-- Example 4: During maintenance window
-- (First, create active maintenance window)
INSERT INTO maintenance_windows (start_time, end_time, created_by, reason, active)
VALUES (NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour', NOW() + INTERVAL '3 hours', 'admin', 'Testing', true);

ALTER TABLE large_employees ALTER COLUMN id TYPE BIGINT;
-- WARNING: Rewriting large table public.large_employees (1234 MB, 15000000 rows) - estimated time: 13 minutes
-- ALTER TABLE

-- Example 5: View rewrite attempts
SELECT 
    rewrite_time,
    username,
    table_name,
    ROUND(table_size_bytes::NUMERIC / (1024*1024), 2) AS size_mb,
    table_rows,
    allowed
FROM rewrite_audit
ORDER BY rewrite_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Example 6: Close maintenance window
UPDATE maintenance_windows SET active = FALSE WHERE id = 1;

🎯 Rewrite Protection Matrix

User Type Maintenance Window Table Size Allowed?
Admin ✅ Active Any size ✅ YES
Admin ❌ None < 100 MB ⚠️ YES (warning)
Admin ❌ None > 100 MB ❌ NO
Regular User ✅ Active < 1 GB & < 10M rows ✅ YES
Regular User ✅ Active > 1 GB or > 10M rows ❌ NO
Regular User ❌ None Any size ❌ NO

38.5 A Login Event Trigger Example

This comprehensive login event trigger example demonstrates advanced access control patterns including IP whitelisting, concurrent session limits, and rate limiting to prevent brute force attacks.

Access Control Tables
-- IP whitelist/blacklist
CREATE TABLE ip_access_control (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    ip_address CIDR NOT NULL,
    access_type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (access_type IN ('whitelist', 'blacklist')),
    reason TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    created_by TEXT NOT NULL,
    active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- User session limits
CREATE TABLE user_session_limits (
    username TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    max_concurrent_sessions INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
    notes TEXT,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Login attempt tracking (for rate limiting)
CREATE TABLE login_attempts (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    client_addr INET,
    attempt_time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    success BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    failure_reason TEXT
);

-- Create indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_ip_access_control_ip ON ip_access_control USING GIST (ip_address inet_ops);
CREATE INDEX idx_login_attempts_user_time ON login_attempts(username, attempt_time);
CREATE INDEX idx_login_attempts_ip_time ON login_attempts(client_addr, attempt_time);

-- Default session limits
INSERT INTO user_session_limits (username, max_concurrent_sessions, notes)
VALUES 
    ('postgres', 10, 'Superuser account'),
    ('admin', 5, 'Admin account')
ON CONFLICT (username) DO NOTHING;
Advanced Login Security Function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION comprehensive_login_security()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    client_ip INET := inet_client_addr();
    client_host TEXT := inet_client_host();
    app_name TEXT := current_setting('application_name', true);
    
    -- Rate limiting
    recent_failures INTEGER;
    lockout_minutes INTEGER := 15;
    max_failures INTEGER := 5;
    
    -- Session limiting
    current_sessions INTEGER;
    max_allowed_sessions INTEGER;
    
    -- IP access control
    ip_status TEXT;
    
    -- Time-based access
    current_hour INTEGER := EXTRACT(HOUR FROM CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
    current_dow INTEGER := EXTRACT(DOW FROM CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);  -- 0=Sunday
    
    -- Flags
    is_weekend BOOLEAN := (current_dow = 0 OR current_dow = 6);
    user_is_privileged BOOLEAN;
BEGIN
    -- Check if user is privileged
    user_is_privileged := EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles
        WHERE rolname = CURRENT_USER 
        AND (rolsuper OR pg_has_role(CURRENT_USER, 'privileged_users', 'MEMBER'))
    );
    
    -- === IP ACCESS CONTROL ===
    -- Check blacklist first
    SELECT access_type INTO ip_status
    FROM ip_access_control
    WHERE client_ip <<= ip_address
    AND active = TRUE
    ORDER BY CASE access_type WHEN 'blacklist' THEN 1 ELSE 2 END
    LIMIT 1;
    
    IF ip_status = 'blacklist' THEN
        INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
        VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, 'IP blacklisted');
        
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: IP address % is blacklisted', client_ip
            USING ERRCODE = 'insufficient_privilege',
                  HINT = 'Contact security team if you believe this is an error';
    END IF;
    
    -- Check whitelist for non-privileged users
    IF NOT user_is_privileged THEN
        IF ip_status IS NULL OR ip_status != 'whitelist' THEN
            INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
            VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, 'IP not whitelisted');
            
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: IP address % not in whitelist', client_ip
                USING ERRCODE = 'insufficient_privilege',
                      HINT = 'Contact administrator to add your IP to whitelist';
        END IF;
    END IF;
    
    -- === RATE LIMITING ===
    -- Count recent failed attempts
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO recent_failures
    FROM login_attempts
    WHERE username = CURRENT_USER
    AND success = FALSE
    AND attempt_time > NOW() - (lockout_minutes || ' minutes')::INTERVAL;
    
    IF recent_failures >= max_failures THEN
        INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
        VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, 'Rate limit exceeded');
        
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: Too many failed login attempts'
            USING ERRCODE = 'insufficient_privilege',
                  DETAIL = format('% failed attempts in last % minutes', 
                                 recent_failures, lockout_minutes),
                  HINT = format('Account locked for % minutes. Contact administrator.', 
                               lockout_minutes);
    END IF;
    
    -- === SESSION LIMITING ===
    -- Get max allowed sessions for user
    SELECT COALESCE(max_concurrent_sessions, 1) INTO max_allowed_sessions
    FROM user_session_limits
    WHERE username = CURRENT_USER;
    
    -- Count current active sessions for this user
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO current_sessions
    FROM pg_stat_activity
    WHERE usename = CURRENT_USER
    AND pid != pg_backend_pid()  -- Exclude current session
    AND state != 'idle';
    
    IF current_sessions >= max_allowed_sessions THEN
        INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
        VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, 'Session limit exceeded');
        
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: Maximum concurrent sessions reached'
            USING DETAIL = format('Current: %, Max: %', 
                                 current_sessions, max_allowed_sessions),
                  HINT = 'Close existing sessions or contact administrator to increase limit';
    END IF;
    
    -- === TIME-BASED ACCESS ===
    IF NOT user_is_privileged THEN
        -- Weekend restriction
        IF is_weekend THEN
            INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
            VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, 'Weekend access denied');
            
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: Weekend access restricted'
                USING HINT = 'Regular users cannot connect on weekends';
        END IF;
        
        -- Business hours restriction (7 AM - 7 PM)
        IF current_hour < 7 OR current_hour >= 19 THEN
            INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
            VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, 'Outside business hours');
            
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'Access denied: Outside business hours (% o''clock)', current_hour
                USING HINT = 'Business hours: 7 AM - 7 PM, Monday-Friday';
        END IF;
    END IF;
    
    -- === LOG SUCCESSFUL LOGIN ===
    INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, TRUE);
    
    INSERT INTO login_audit (username, client_addr, application_name, success)
    VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, app_name, TRUE);
    
    -- Provide welcome notice
    RAISE NOTICE 'Welcome %! Connected from % (%) at %',
        CURRENT_USER, client_ip, COALESCE(client_host, 'unknown'), NOW();
    
    -- Warn if recent failures exist
    IF recent_failures > 0 THEN
        RAISE WARNING 'Note: % failed login attempts in last % minutes',
            recent_failures, lockout_minutes;
    END IF;
    
EXCEPTION
    WHEN insufficient_privilege THEN
        -- Re-raise security exceptions
        RAISE;
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        -- Log unexpected errors
        INSERT INTO login_attempts (username, client_addr, success, failure_reason)
        VALUES (CURRENT_USER, client_ip, FALSE, SQLERRM);
        RAISE;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER enforce_login_security
    ON login
    EXECUTE FUNCTION comprehensive_login_security();
Management Functions
-- Add IP to whitelist
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION whitelist_ip(
    p_ip CIDR,
    p_reason TEXT
) RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO ip_access_control (ip_address, access_type, reason, created_by)
    VALUES (p_ip, 'whitelist', p_reason, CURRENT_USER);
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'IP % added to whitelist', p_ip;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Add IP to blacklist
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION blacklist_ip(
    p_ip CIDR,
    p_reason TEXT
) RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO ip_access_control (ip_address, access_type, reason, created_by)
    VALUES (p_ip, 'blacklist', p_reason, CURRENT_USER);
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'IP % added to blacklist', p_ip;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Set user session limit
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION set_session_limit(
    p_username TEXT,
    p_limit INTEGER,
    p_notes TEXT DEFAULT NULL
) RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO user_session_limits (username, max_concurrent_sessions, notes)
    VALUES (p_username, p_limit, p_notes)
    ON CONFLICT (username) 
    DO UPDATE SET 
        max_concurrent_sessions = p_limit,
        notes = COALESCE(p_notes, user_session_limits.notes),
        updated_at = NOW();
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Session limit for % set to %', p_username, p_limit;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Clear failed login attempts (unlock user)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION unlock_user(p_username TEXT)
RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    DELETE FROM login_attempts
    WHERE username = p_username
    AND success = FALSE;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'User % unlocked', p_username;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- View recent login activity
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW login_security_summary AS
SELECT 
    username,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE success = TRUE) AS successful_logins,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE success = FALSE) AS failed_logins,
    MAX(attempt_time) FILTER (WHERE success = TRUE) AS last_successful_login,
    MAX(attempt_time) FILTER (WHERE success = FALSE) AS last_failed_login,
    array_agg(DISTINCT client_addr::TEXT) AS client_ips
FROM login_attempts
WHERE attempt_time > NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours'
GROUP BY username
ORDER BY MAX(attempt_time) DESC;
Testing and Examples
-- Setup: Whitelist an IP range
SELECT whitelist_ip('192.168.1.0/24', 'Office network');
SELECT whitelist_ip('10.0.0.0/8', 'Internal network');

-- Setup: Blacklist a problematic IP
SELECT blacklist_ip('203.0.113.45/32', 'Repeated attack attempts');

-- Setup: Set session limits
SELECT set_session_limit('app_user', 3, 'Application service account');
SELECT set_session_limit('analyst', 2, 'Data analyst account');

-- Test 1: Normal user login from whitelisted IP during business hours
-- (Should succeed if all conditions met)

-- Test 2: View login activity
SELECT * FROM login_security_summary;

-- Test 3: Check failed attempts for specific user
SELECT 
    username,
    client_addr,
    attempt_time,
    failure_reason
FROM login_attempts
WHERE username = 'app_user'
AND success = FALSE
ORDER BY attempt_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Test 4: Unlock user after investigation
SELECT unlock_user('app_user');

-- Test 5: View IP access control list
SELECT 
    ip_address,
    access_type,
    reason,
    created_by,
    created_at
FROM ip_access_control
WHERE active = TRUE
ORDER BY access_type, created_at DESC;

-- Test 6: Check current sessions for user
SELECT 
    usename,
    application_name,
    client_addr,
    backend_start,
    state
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE usename = 'app_user';

-- Test 7: Disable login security temporarily (superuser only)
ALTER EVENT TRIGGER enforce_login_security DISABLE;
-- ... perform maintenance ...
ALTER EVENT TRIGGER enforce_login_security ENABLE;

🔒 Security Features Summary

Network Security
  • IP whitelist/blacklist with CIDR support
  • Automatic DNS resolution logging
  • Geographic restrictions (if combined with GeoIP)
Rate Limiting
  • Brute force protection
  • Automatic lockout after N failures
  • Time-based lockout expiration
Session Control
  • Per-user concurrent session limits
  • Prevents credential sharing
  • Real-time session monitoring
Temporal Access
  • Business hours enforcement
  • Weekend access control
  • Holiday restrictions (extensible)

📚 Chapter 38 Summary

✅ Key Concepts Covered

  • Event Types: login, ddl_command_start, ddl_command_end, sql_drop, table_rewrite
  • Event Scope: Database-wide vs table-specific triggers
  • Timing: BEFORE vs AFTER command execution
  • Prevention: Which events can block operations
  • Special Functions: pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands(), pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()

🎯 Common Use Cases

  • Security: Access control, login restrictions, IP filtering
  • Governance: Schema change policies, DROP prevention
  • Auditing: DDL tracking, drop logging, change history
  • Performance: Block expensive rewrites, enforce maintenance windows
  • Compliance: Complete activity logs for regulatory requirements

⚠️ Important Considerations

  • Privileges: Creating event triggers requires superuser
  • Security: Trigger functions run with elevated privileges
  • Rollback: Trigger actions roll back with transaction (use dblink for autonomous transactions)
  • Performance: Event triggers add overhead to DDL operations
  • Filtering: Use TAG filters to target specific commands

🔧 Best Practices

  • Modular Design: Separate functions for different concerns
  • Comprehensive Logging: Always log trigger actions for audit trail
  • Graceful Errors: Provide helpful hints in exception messages
  • Testing: Test triggers thoroughly before production deployment
  • Disabling: Plan for temporarily disabling during maintenance

🔄 Event Trigger vs Regular Trigger Quick Comparison

Aspect Regular Trigger Event Trigger
Attached to Specific table Entire database
Fires on DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, TRUNCATE) DDL commands and login events
Access to OLD/NEW row data Command metadata, no row data
Can modify NEW row (BEFORE triggers) Cannot modify command
Return value trigger (OLD/NEW/NULL/SKIP) event_trigger (always NULL)
Creation privilege Table owner or TRIGGER privilege Superuser only
Quick Reference: Event Trigger Functions
-- Get DDL command details (ddl_command_end only)
SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands();

-- Get dropped object details (sql_drop only)
SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects();

-- Available trigger variables:
-- tg_event     - Event name ('ddl_command_start', etc.)
-- tg_tag       - Command tag ('CREATE TABLE', etc.)

-- For table_rewrite event only:
-- tg_table_name   - Name of table being rewritten
-- tg_table_schema - Schema of table being rewritten
-- tg_table_oid    - OID of table being rewritten

-- Syntax to create event trigger:
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER name
    ON event
    [ WHEN TAG IN (tags...) ]
    EXECUTE FUNCTION function_name();

Key Takeaway: Event triggers provide powerful database-wide automation for security, governance, and auditing. They complement regular triggers by operating at the DDL and session level rather than the data manipulation level. When combined with regular triggers, they create a comprehensive database automation and security framework. Always test event triggers carefully in non-production environments, as they run with elevated privileges and can block critical database operations.

Chapter 39. The Rule System

📌 Chapter Overview

The rule system is PostgreSQL's query rewrite mechanism that transforms queries before they are executed. While most users interact with it through views, the rule system is a powerful low-level facility that enables query transformation, view implementation, and conditional command execution.

🎯 What You'll Learn:
  • How PostgreSQL rewrites queries
  • View implementation mechanics
  • Materialized vs regular views
  • Rules vs triggers comparison
  • Updatable views
🔧 Key Concepts:
  • Query tree transformation
  • Rule execution model
  • View materialization
  • Privilege considerations
  • Command status handling

⚠️ Important Note

The rule system is a legacy feature primarily used for view implementation. For most data manipulation tasks, triggers (Chapter 37) are the preferred mechanism. Rules have complex semantics and can produce unexpected results. Use triggers unless you specifically need query rewriting behavior.

39.1 The Query Tree

To understand the rule system, you must first understand how PostgreSQL processes queries internally. When a SQL statement is submitted, PostgreSQL performs several transformation steps before execution.

🔄 Query Processing Pipeline

Query Transformation Stages

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   1. SQL TEXT INPUT                             │
│            SELECT name, salary FROM employees;                  │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼────────────────┐
        │      2. PARSER                │
        │   - Lexical analysis          │
        │   - Syntax validation         │
        │   - Build parse tree          │
        └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼────────────────┐
        │   3. ANALYZER/REWRITER        │
        │   - Semantic analysis         │
        │   - Name resolution           │
        │   - Type checking             │
        └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼────────────────┐
        │   4. RULE SYSTEM  ⭐          │
        │   - Apply rewrite rules       │
        │   - Expand views              │
        │   - Transform query tree      │
        └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼────────────────┐
        │   5. PLANNER/OPTIMIZER        │
        │   - Generate execution plans  │
        │   - Cost estimation           │
        │   - Plan selection            │
        └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼────────────────┐
        │   6. EXECUTOR                 │
        │   - Execute plan              │
        │   - Return results            │
        └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                       │
        ┌──────────────▼────────────────┐
        │     RESULT SET                │
        └───────────────────────────────┘

The Query Tree Structure

A query tree is an internal representation of a SQL statement. It's a structured tree where each node represents an operation, table reference, expression, or condition. The rule system operates on this tree representation.

Example: Simple SELECT Query Tree
-- Original SQL
SELECT e.name, e.salary 
FROM employees e 
WHERE e.department_id = 10;

-- Conceptual Query Tree Structure
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Query (SELECT)                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Target List:                            │
│   - e.name (Var: employees.name)        │
│   - e.salary (Var: employees.salary)    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ From Clause:                            │
│   - RangeVar: employees (alias: e)      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Where Clause:                           │
│   - OpExpr (=)                          │
│     - Left: Var (e.department_id)       │
│     - Right: Const (10)                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

How Rules Transform Query Trees

When a rule is defined on a table or view, the rule system modifies the query tree before execution. This transformation happens transparently and allows PostgreSQL to implement views, perform query optimization, and enable conditional query rewriting.

📊 Query Tree Transformation Example

View Definition
-- Create a view
CREATE VIEW high_earners AS
SELECT name, salary, department_id
FROM employees
WHERE salary > 100000;

-- This creates a rewrite rule internally:
-- CREATE RULE "_RETURN" AS
--   ON SELECT TO high_earners
--   DO INSTEAD
--     SELECT name, salary, department_id
--     FROM employees
--     WHERE salary > 100000;
Query Rewriting in Action
-- User writes:
SELECT name FROM high_earners WHERE department_id = 10;

-- Rule system rewrites to:
SELECT name 
FROM employees
WHERE salary > 100000      -- From view definition
  AND department_id = 10;  -- From user query

-- Process:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Original Query Tree                       │
│  SELECT name FROM high_earners WHERE ...   │
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
               │
    ┌──────────▼──────────┐
    │  RULE SYSTEM        │
    │  Finds _RETURN rule │
    │  on high_earners    │
    └──────────┬──────────┘
               │
┌──────────────▼─────────────────────────────┐
│  Rewritten Query Tree                      │
│  SELECT name FROM employees WHERE ...      │
│  (View definition merged with query)       │
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
               │
    ┌──────────▼──────────┐
    │  PLANNER            │
    │  Optimizes merged   │
    │  query              │
    └─────────────────────┘

✅ Key Properties of Query Trees

Property Description Impact
Immutable Original tree is copied before modification Rules don't affect original query
Recursive Rules can trigger other rules Views can be built on views
Transparent Rewriting happens automatically Users don't see transformation
Optimizable Planner sees final rewritten tree Can optimize across view boundaries
Viewing Query Rewriting (DEBUG)
-- Enable debug output to see rewriting (for educational purposes)
SET debug_print_rewritten = on;

SELECT name FROM high_earners WHERE department_id = 10;

-- PostgreSQL logs show the rewritten query tree
-- (actual output is complex internal structure)

-- To see the actual execution plan:
EXPLAIN (VERBOSE) 
SELECT name FROM high_earners WHERE department_id = 10;

-- Output shows view expansion:
                          QUERY PLAN
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 Seq Scan on public.employees
   Output: employees.name
   Filter: ((employees.salary > 100000) AND 
            (employees.department_id = 10))
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

🎓 Understanding Rule Application

The rule system applies rules in a specific order:

  1. Parse: SQL text converted to parse tree
  2. Analyze: Parse tree converted to query tree with semantic information
  3. Rewrite: Rules applied to query tree (views expanded, etc.)
  4. Plan: Rewritten tree optimized and planned
  5. Execute: Plan executed against actual tables

Important: Rules are applied once per query tree node. This is different from triggers which fire once per affected row.

39.2 Views and the Rule System

Views are the most common use of the rule system. When you create a view, PostgreSQL automatically creates a rewrite rule that transforms queries against the view into queries against the underlying tables.

🔍 What is a View?

A view is a virtual table defined by a SELECT statement. It doesn't store data itself; instead, it provides a named query that can be referenced like a table. Views offer several benefits:

✅ Advantages
  • Simplify complex queries
  • Enforce security (hide columns/rows)
  • Provide abstraction layer
  • Backward compatibility
⚠️ Considerations
  • No data storage (computed each time)
  • Complex views may be slow
  • Update restrictions apply
  • Rule system complexity

39.2.1 How SELECT Rules Work

When you create a view, PostgreSQL automatically creates an ON SELECT rule named _RETURN. This rule tells the system how to rewrite queries that reference the view.

Creating a View and Its Underlying Rule
-- Create a simple view
CREATE VIEW active_employees AS
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    name,
    email,
    hire_date,
    salary
FROM employees
WHERE status = 'active'
ORDER BY hire_date DESC;

-- PostgreSQL internally creates this rule:
-- CREATE RULE "_RETURN" AS
--   ON SELECT TO active_employees
--   DO INSTEAD
--     SELECT employee_id, name, email, hire_date, salary
--     FROM employees
--     WHERE status = 'active'
--     ORDER BY hire_date DESC;

-- View the rule definition
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    rulename,
    definition
FROM pg_rules
WHERE tablename = 'active_employees';

┌────────────┬───────────────────┬──────────┬──────────────────────────┐
│ schemaname │    tablename      │ rulename │       definition         │
├────────────┼───────────────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────┤
│ public     │ active_employees  │ _RETURN  │ CREATE RULE _RETURN AS...│
└────────────┴───────────────────┴──────────┴──────────────────────────┘

🔄 Query Rewriting Process

Step-by-Step Query Transformation
-- User Query
SELECT name, salary 
FROM active_employees 
WHERE salary > 80000;

-- Step 1: Parse and analyze
-- Query tree created with active_employees as target

-- Step 2: Rule system finds _RETURN rule
-- Rule conditions: ON SELECT TO active_employees

-- Step 3: DO INSTEAD clause replaces the range table entry
-- Original: active_employees
-- Replaced with: employees WHERE status = 'active'

-- Step 4: Merge WHERE clauses
-- View condition: status = 'active'
-- User condition: salary > 80000
-- Combined: status = 'active' AND salary > 80000

-- Final Rewritten Query (what actually executes)
SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE status = 'active'       -- From view definition
  AND salary > 80000;         -- From user query

-- Step 5: Planner optimizes this merged query
EXPLAIN SELECT name, salary 
FROM active_employees 
WHERE salary > 80000;

                          QUERY PLAN
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 Seq Scan on employees
   Filter: ((status = 'active') AND (salary > 80000))
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Complex View Example with Joins
-- Create view with JOIN
CREATE VIEW employee_department_info AS
SELECT 
    e.employee_id,
    e.name AS employee_name,
    e.salary,
    d.department_id,
    d.name AS department_name,
    d.location
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE e.status = 'active';

-- Query the view
SELECT employee_name, department_name, salary
FROM employee_department_info
WHERE location = 'New York'
  AND salary > 75000;

-- Rewritten to:
SELECT 
    e.name AS employee_name,
    d.name AS department_name,
    e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE e.status = 'active'      -- From view
  AND d.location = 'New York'  -- From query
  AND e.salary > 75000;        -- From query

-- The planner can now optimize across the entire query
EXPLAIN (VERBOSE)
SELECT employee_name, department_name, salary
FROM employee_department_info
WHERE location = 'New York' AND salary > 75000;

                          QUERY PLAN
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 Hash Join
   Hash Cond: (e.department_id = d.department_id)
   ->  Seq Scan on employees e
         Filter: ((status = 'active') AND (salary > 75000))
   ->  Hash
         ->  Seq Scan on departments d
               Filter: (location = 'New York')
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

✨ Benefits of Rule-Based Views

  • Transparent: View expansion happens automatically, users don't need to know about underlying tables
  • Optimizable: Planner sees the full merged query and can optimize it as a whole
  • No Performance Penalty: Views are not materialized; they're just query rewrites
  • Flexible: Can add indexes on base tables to speed up view queries
  • Secure: Users can be granted access to views without access to base tables

39.2.2 View Rules in Non-SELECT Statements

By default, views are read-only. However, you can make views updatable by creating additional rules for INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations. This allows users to modify data through the view.

⚠️ Automatically Updatable Views

PostgreSQL can automatically make simple views updatable without explicit rules if they meet these criteria:

  • View query has exactly one table in FROM clause
  • View query must not contain GROUP BY, HAVING, LIMIT, OFFSET, DISTINCT, WITH, UNION, INTERSECT, or EXCEPT
  • View's SELECT list must not contain window functions, set-returning functions, or aggregates
  • Columns in SELECT list must be simple column references (not expressions)

For complex views, you must create explicit rules.

Simple Automatically Updatable View
-- Create simple view (automatically updatable)
CREATE VIEW active_employees_simple AS
SELECT employee_id, name, email, salary, department_id
FROM employees
WHERE status = 'active';

-- INSERT works automatically
INSERT INTO active_employees_simple (name, email, salary, department_id)
VALUES ('John Doe', 'john@example.com', 65000, 3);

-- This is rewritten to:
INSERT INTO employees (name, email, salary, department_id, status)
VALUES ('John Doe', 'john@example.com', 65000, 3, 'active');

-- UPDATE works automatically
UPDATE active_employees_simple
SET salary = 70000
WHERE employee_id = 123;

-- Rewritten to:
UPDATE employees
SET salary = 70000
WHERE employee_id = 123
  AND status = 'active';  -- View's WHERE clause ensures we only update active employees

-- DELETE works automatically
DELETE FROM active_employees_simple
WHERE employee_id = 123;

-- Rewritten to:
DELETE FROM employees
WHERE employee_id = 123
  AND status = 'active';
Manual Rules for Complex Views
-- Create a complex view (JOIN - not automatically updatable)
CREATE VIEW emp_dept_summary AS
SELECT 
    e.employee_id,
    e.name AS emp_name,
    e.salary,
    d.name AS dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

-- Attempting UPDATE without a rule fails
UPDATE emp_dept_summary SET salary = 80000 WHERE employee_id = 10;
-- ERROR: cannot update view "emp_dept_summary"
-- DETAIL: Views that do not select from a single table or view are not automatically updatable.

-- Create an UPDATE rule
CREATE RULE emp_dept_summary_update AS
ON UPDATE TO emp_dept_summary
DO INSTEAD
    UPDATE employees
    SET salary = NEW.salary
    WHERE employee_id = OLD.employee_id;

-- Now UPDATE works
UPDATE emp_dept_summary SET salary = 80000 WHERE employee_id = 10;
-- Successfully updates employees table

-- Create INSERT rule
CREATE RULE emp_dept_summary_insert AS
ON INSERT TO emp_dept_summary
DO INSTEAD
    INSERT INTO employees (name, salary, department_id)
    VALUES (
        NEW.emp_name,
        NEW.salary,
        (SELECT department_id FROM departments WHERE name = NEW.dept_name)
    );

-- Create DELETE rule
CREATE RULE emp_dept_summary_delete AS
ON DELETE TO emp_dept_summary
DO INSTEAD
    DELETE FROM employees
    WHERE employee_id = OLD.employee_id;

⚡ NEW and OLD in Rules

When writing rules for INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE:

Operation NEW Available? OLD Available? Description
INSERT ✅ Yes ❌ No NEW contains values to insert
UPDATE ✅ Yes ✅ Yes NEW has new values, OLD has current values
DELETE ❌ No ✅ Yes OLD contains values being deleted

Note: Unlike triggers, rules work on the query tree, not on individual rows. NEW and OLD represent the target list and qualification of the query, not actual row values.

39.2.3 The Power of Views in PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL's rule-based view system offers unique capabilities that set it apart from other database systems. Views can simplify complex queries, enforce security policies, and provide abstraction layers for application development.

💪 Advanced View Capabilities

🔒 Security & Abstraction
  • Hide sensitive columns
  • Filter rows by security level
  • Limit data exposure per user
  • Provide simplified interfaces
⚡ Performance & Optimization
  • No materialization overhead
  • Full query optimization
  • Index usage on base tables
  • Predicate pushdown
Example: Row-Level Security with Views
-- Scenario: Different users see different data

-- Create base table with all employee data
CREATE TABLE employees_full (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    email VARCHAR(100),
    salary NUMERIC(10,2),
    ssn VARCHAR(11),           -- Sensitive
    performance_review TEXT,   -- Sensitive
    department_id INTEGER,
    manager_id INTEGER
);

-- Public view: Basic info only (all users)
CREATE VIEW employees_public AS
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    name,
    email,
    department_id
FROM employees_full;

-- Manager view: Includes salary and performance for their team
CREATE VIEW employees_manager AS
SELECT 
    e.employee_id,
    e.name,
    e.email,
    e.salary,
    e.performance_review,
    e.department_id,
    e.manager_id
FROM employees_full e
WHERE e.manager_id = (
    SELECT employee_id 
    FROM employees_full 
    WHERE email = current_user || '@company.com'
);

-- HR view: Full access including sensitive data
CREATE VIEW employees_hr AS
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    name,
    email,
    salary,
    ssn,
    performance_review,
    department_id,
    manager_id
FROM employees_full;

-- Grant appropriate access
GRANT SELECT ON employees_public TO public;
GRANT SELECT ON employees_manager TO manager_role;
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON employees_hr TO hr_role;

-- Users automatically see only authorized data
-- Regular user:
SELECT * FROM employees_public;  -- ✅ Works, sees basic info

-- Manager:
SELECT * FROM employees_manager; -- ✅ Works, sees only their reports

-- HR:
SELECT * FROM employees_hr;      -- ✅ Works, sees everything
Example: Simplifying Complex Queries
-- Complex reporting query
CREATE VIEW quarterly_sales_summary AS
SELECT 
    d.name AS department,
    e.name AS employee,
    DATE_TRUNC('quarter', o.order_date) AS quarter,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS unique_customers,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS avg_order_value,
    RANK() OVER (
        PARTITION BY DATE_TRUNC('quarter', o.order_date) 
        ORDER BY SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) DESC
    ) AS revenue_rank
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
JOIN orders o ON o.employee_id = e.employee_id
JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY d.name, e.name, DATE_TRUNC('quarter', o.order_date);

-- Now users can query simply:
SELECT 
    department,
    employee,
    quarter,
    total_revenue,
    revenue_rank
FROM quarterly_sales_summary
WHERE quarter = DATE_TRUNC('quarter', CURRENT_DATE)
  AND revenue_rank <= 10
ORDER BY revenue_rank;

-- Much simpler than writing the entire complex query each time!
Example: Computed Columns and Transformations
-- View with computed columns
CREATE VIEW product_analytics AS
SELECT 
    p.product_id,
    p.name,
    p.category,
    p.unit_price,
    p.cost,
    -- Computed columns
    (p.unit_price - p.cost) AS profit_per_unit,
    ROUND(((p.unit_price - p.cost) / p.unit_price * 100), 2) AS profit_margin_pct,
    -- Aggregated data from related tables
    COALESCE(inv.quantity_on_hand, 0) AS inventory_qty,
    COALESCE(sales.units_sold_30d, 0) AS units_sold_last_30_days,
    COALESCE(sales.revenue_30d, 0) AS revenue_last_30_days,
    -- Computed metrics
    CASE 
        WHEN COALESCE(sales.units_sold_30d, 0) = 0 THEN NULL
        WHEN COALESCE(inv.quantity_on_hand, 0) / sales.units_sold_30d < 1 THEN 'LOW_STOCK'
        WHEN COALESCE(inv.quantity_on_hand, 0) / sales.units_sold_30d < 2 THEN 'MODERATE_STOCK'
        ELSE 'ADEQUATE_STOCK'
    END AS stock_status
FROM products p
LEFT JOIN inventory inv ON p.product_id = inv.product_id
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        SUM(quantity) AS units_sold_30d,
        SUM(quantity * unit_price) AS revenue_30d
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
    WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
    GROUP BY product_id
) sales ON p.product_id = sales.product_id;

-- Simple queries on computed data
SELECT 
    name,
    profit_margin_pct,
    stock_status,
    inventory_qty,
    units_sold_last_30_days
FROM product_analytics
WHERE stock_status = 'LOW_STOCK'
  AND profit_margin_pct > 20
ORDER BY units_sold_last_30_days DESC;

🎯 View Best Practices

  • Naming: Use descriptive names that indicate the view's purpose (e.g., v_active_customers, rpt_monthly_sales)
  • Documentation: Add comments to explain view purpose and complex logic
  • Performance: Be cautious with views that join many tables or include expensive computations
  • Security: Grant permissions on views, not base tables, to control data access
  • Maintenance: Document view dependencies; changes to base tables may break views
  • Testing: Verify that view queries perform well with expected data volumes

39.2.4 Updating a View

Making views updatable requires careful consideration. PostgreSQL provides both automatic updateability for simple views and manual rule creation for complex scenarios.

🔄 Updatable View Decision Tree


Is your view automatically updatable?

START
  │
  ├─ Does view SELECT from single table/view? ──────────[NO]──→ Need manual rules
  │                                             │
  │                                           [YES]
  ├─ Contains GROUP BY/HAVING/DISTINCT? ────────────────[YES]──→ Need manual rules
  │                                             │
  │                                            [NO]
  ├─ Contains LIMIT/OFFSET? ────────────────────────────[YES]──→ Need manual rules
  │                                             │
  │                                            [NO]
  ├─ Contains set operations (UNION/etc.)? ─────────────[YES]──→ Need manual rules
  │                                             │
  │                                            [NO]
  ├─ SELECT list has expressions/aggregates? ───────────[YES]──→ Need manual rules
  │                                             │
  │                                            [NO]
  ├─ All columns are simple references? ────────────────[YES]──→ ✅ AUTOMATICALLY UPDATABLE!
  │                                             │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Automatically Updatable View Example
-- Simple view - automatically updatable
CREATE VIEW california_customers AS
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    name,
    email,
    phone,
    address,
    city,
    state,
    zip_code
FROM customers
WHERE state = 'CA';

-- Check if view is updatable
SELECT 
    table_name,
    is_insertable_into
FROM information_schema.tables
WHERE table_name = 'california_customers';

┌───────────────────────┬────────────────────┐
│      table_name       │ is_insertable_into │
├───────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ california_customers  │ YES                │
└───────────────────────┴────────────────────┘

-- INSERT works automatically
INSERT INTO california_customers (name, email, phone, city, state, zip_code)
VALUES ('Alice Johnson', 'alice@example.com', '555-0100', 'Los Angeles', 'CA', '90001');
-- ✅ Inserts into customers table with state = 'CA'

-- UPDATE works automatically
UPDATE california_customers
SET phone = '555-0200'
WHERE customer_id = 100;
-- ✅ Updates customers table WHERE customer_id = 100 AND state = 'CA'

-- DELETE works automatically
DELETE FROM california_customers
WHERE customer_id = 100;
-- ✅ Deletes from customers table WHERE customer_id = 100 AND state = 'CA'

-- ⚠️ Potential issue: What if we UPDATE state to non-CA?
UPDATE california_customers SET state = 'NY' WHERE customer_id = 100;
-- ✅ Works, but row disappears from view!
-- Solution: Use WITH CHECK OPTION
WITH CHECK OPTION - Preventing Invalid Updates
-- Recreate view with CHECK OPTION
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW california_customers AS
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    name,
    email,
    phone,
    address,
    city,
    state,
    zip_code
FROM customers
WHERE state = 'CA'
WITH CHECK OPTION;

-- Now try to INSERT non-CA customer
INSERT INTO california_customers (name, email, state)
VALUES ('Bob Smith', 'bob@example.com', 'NY');
-- ❌ ERROR: new row violates check option for view "california_customers"

-- Try to UPDATE state to non-CA
UPDATE california_customers SET state = 'NY' WHERE customer_id = 100;
-- ❌ ERROR: new row violates check option for view "california_customers"

-- Valid operations still work
INSERT INTO california_customers (name, email, state)
VALUES ('Carol Davis', 'carol@example.com', 'CA');
-- ✅ Works!

UPDATE california_customers SET phone = '555-0300' WHERE customer_id = 100;
-- ✅ Works!

-- WITH LOCAL CHECK OPTION vs WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION
-- LOCAL: Only check this view's WHERE clause
-- CASCADED: Check this view AND all underlying views' WHERE clauses (default)
Complex View with INSTEAD OF Triggers
-- Complex view with JOIN - not automatically updatable
CREATE VIEW customer_orders_summary AS
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.name AS customer_name,
    c.email,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS total_orders,
    COALESCE(SUM(o.total_amount), 0) AS total_spent
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.name, c.email;

-- Attempt to update fails
UPDATE customer_orders_summary SET email = 'new@example.com' WHERE customer_id = 1;
-- ❌ ERROR: cannot update view "customer_orders_summary"
-- DETAIL: Views that return aggregate functions are not automatically updatable.

-- Solution 1: Use rules (older method)
CREATE RULE customer_orders_summary_update AS
ON UPDATE TO customer_orders_summary
DO INSTEAD
    UPDATE customers
    SET email = NEW.email,
        name = NEW.customer_name
    WHERE customer_id = OLD.customer_id;

-- Solution 2: Use INSTEAD OF triggers (modern, recommended)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION customer_orders_summary_update_trigger()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    UPDATE customers
    SET email = NEW.email,
        name = NEW.customer_name
    WHERE customer_id = NEW.customer_id;
    
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER customer_orders_summary_update_trigger
INSTEAD OF UPDATE ON customer_orders_summary
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION customer_orders_summary_update_trigger();

-- Now UPDATE works
UPDATE customer_orders_summary 
SET email = 'newemail@example.com' 
WHERE customer_id = 1;
-- ✅ Works! Triggers update customers table

💡 Updatable Views: Rules vs INSTEAD OF Triggers

Aspect Rules INSTEAD OF Triggers
Processing Query tree rewriting Row-by-row execution
Complexity Complex semantics Intuitive, procedural
Recommendation ⚠️ Legacy, avoid for new code ✅ Modern, recommended
Use Case View implementation Complex update logic

Recommendation: For updatable views on complex queries, use INSTEAD OF triggers rather than rules. Triggers are easier to understand, debug, and maintain.

39.3 Materialized Views

Unlike regular views which are virtual and computed on-demand, materialized views physically store the query results. They trade storage space and update complexity for dramatically faster query performance.

🗄️ Regular View vs Materialized View

Aspect Regular View Materialized View
Storage No data stored (virtual) ✅ Data physically stored
Query Speed Depends on base tables ✅ Very fast (pre-computed)
Data Freshness ✅ Always current Stale until refreshed
Indexable No (indexes on base tables) ✅ Yes, can have own indexes
Maintenance None needed Must refresh periodically
Use Case Simple queries, always fresh data Complex aggregations, reporting
Creating and Using Materialized Views
-- Create a materialized view
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales_summary AS
SELECT 
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month,
    d.name AS department,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customer_count,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS avg_order_value,
    SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN employees e ON o.employee_id = e.employee_id
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date), d.name;

-- Query the materialized view (very fast!)
SELECT 
    month,
    department,
    total_revenue,
    customer_count
FROM monthly_sales_summary
WHERE month >= '2024-01-01'
ORDER BY month DESC, total_revenue DESC;

-- Check materialized view definition
\d+ monthly_sales_summary

                    Materialized view "public.monthly_sales_summary"
     Column      |            Type             | Storage  | Description 
─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────
 month           | timestamp with time zone    | plain    | 
 department      | character varying(100)      | extended | 
 order_count     | bigint                      | plain    | 
 customer_count  | bigint                      | plain    | 
 total_revenue   | numeric                     | main     | 
 avg_order_value | numeric                     | main     | 
 units_sold      | numeric                     | main     | 
View definition:
 SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month,
    d.name AS department,
    ...
Refreshing Materialized Views
-- Complete refresh (rebuilds entire materialized view)
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales_summary;
-- ⚠️ Blocks reads during refresh

-- Concurrent refresh (allows reads during refresh)
-- Requires a UNIQUE index
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON monthly_sales_summary (month, department);

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY monthly_sales_summary;
-- ✅ Allows SELECT queries during refresh

-- Check last refresh time
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    matviewname,
    hasindexes,
    ispopulated,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||matviewname)) AS size
FROM pg_matviews
WHERE matviewname = 'monthly_sales_summary';

┌────────────┬────────────────────────┬────────────┬─────────────┬────────┐
│ schemaname │     matviewname        │ hasindexes │ ispopulated │  size  │
├────────────┼────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────┼────────┤
│ public     │ monthly_sales_summary  │ t          │ t           │ 128 kB │
└────────────┴────────────────────────┴────────────┴─────────────┴────────┘

-- Drop data without dropping the materialized view structure
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales_summary WITH NO DATA;
-- Now ispopulated = false, queries will fail

-- Repopulate
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales_summary WITH DATA;
Indexing Materialized Views for Performance
-- Create indexes on commonly queried columns
CREATE INDEX idx_monthly_sales_month ON monthly_sales_summary(month);
CREATE INDEX idx_monthly_sales_dept ON monthly_sales_summary(department);
CREATE INDEX idx_monthly_sales_revenue ON monthly_sales_summary(total_revenue DESC);

-- Composite index for common query patterns
CREATE INDEX idx_monthly_sales_month_dept 
ON monthly_sales_summary(month DESC, department);

-- Query using indexes
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT department, SUM(total_revenue) AS total
FROM monthly_sales_summary
WHERE month >= '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY total DESC;

                          QUERY PLAN
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 GroupAggregate
   Group Key: department
   ->  Index Scan using idx_monthly_sales_month on monthly_sales_summary
         Index Cond: (month >= '2024-01-01')
 Planning Time: 0.123 ms
 Execution Time: 0.456 ms    -- ⚡ Very fast!
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

🔄 Automatic Refresh Strategies

Strategy 1: Scheduled Refresh with pg_cron
-- Install pg_cron extension (requires superuser)
CREATE EXTENSION pg_cron;

-- Schedule refresh every night at 2 AM
SELECT cron.schedule(
    'refresh-monthly-sales',                    -- job name
    '0 2 * * *',                                -- cron schedule
    'REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY monthly_sales_summary'
);

-- Schedule refresh every hour
SELECT cron.schedule(
    'refresh-hourly-stats',
    '0 * * * *',
    'REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY hourly_stats'
);

-- View scheduled jobs
SELECT * FROM cron.job;

-- Remove a schedule
SELECT cron.unschedule('refresh-monthly-sales');
Strategy 2: Trigger-Based Refresh
-- Create function to refresh materialized view
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION refresh_monthly_sales()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    -- Only refresh if significant changes
    IF (TG_OP = 'INSERT' AND NEW.status = 'completed') OR
       (TG_OP = 'UPDATE' AND OLD.status <> 'completed' AND NEW.status = 'completed') THEN
        
        -- Use pg_background or dblink for async refresh
        PERFORM dblink_exec('dbname=' || current_database(),
            'REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY monthly_sales_summary'
        );
    END IF;
    
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Create trigger on orders table
CREATE TRIGGER refresh_sales_matview
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE ON orders
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION refresh_monthly_sales();

-- ⚠️ Warning: This can cause performance issues with high-volume tables
-- Consider rate limiting or batch refreshes instead
Strategy 3: Application-Controlled Refresh
-- Create tracking table
CREATE TABLE matview_refresh_log (
    matview_name VARCHAR(100) PRIMARY KEY,
    last_refresh TIMESTAMPTZ,
    refresh_duration INTERVAL,
    row_count BIGINT
);

-- Function to safely refresh with logging
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION safe_refresh_matview(view_name TEXT)
RETURNS VOID AS $$
DECLARE
    start_time TIMESTAMPTZ;
    row_cnt BIGINT;
BEGIN
    start_time := clock_timestamp();
    
    -- Refresh the view
    EXECUTE format('REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY %I', view_name);
    
    -- Count rows
    EXECUTE format('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM %I', view_name) INTO row_cnt;
    
    -- Log the refresh
    INSERT INTO matview_refresh_log (matview_name, last_refresh, refresh_duration, row_count)
    VALUES (view_name, start_time, clock_timestamp() - start_time, row_cnt)
    ON CONFLICT (matview_name) 
    DO UPDATE SET 
        last_refresh = EXCLUDED.last_refresh,
        refresh_duration = EXCLUDED.refresh_duration,
        row_count = EXCLUDED.row_count;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Refreshed % in % (% rows)', 
        view_name, clock_timestamp() - start_time, row_cnt;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Usage
SELECT safe_refresh_matview('monthly_sales_summary');

-- Check refresh history
SELECT 
    matview_name,
    last_refresh,
    refresh_duration,
    row_count,
    AGE(NOW(), last_refresh) AS staleness
FROM matview_refresh_log
ORDER BY last_refresh DESC;

💡 Materialized View Best Practices

  • When to Use:
    • Complex aggregations or joins that are slow
    • Reports that don't need real-time data
    • Data that changes infrequently
    • Dashboard queries executed frequently
  • When NOT to Use:
    • Data must be real-time
    • Base tables change constantly
    • Query is already fast
    • Storage space is limited
  • Performance Tips:
    • Create indexes on materialized views
    • Use CONCURRENTLY to allow reads during refresh
    • Schedule refreshes during low-traffic periods
    • Monitor refresh duration and optimize base query
    • Consider partitioning for very large materialized views
Dropping and Modifying Materialized Views
-- Drop materialized view
DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW IF EXISTS monthly_sales_summary;

-- Recreate with different definition
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales_summary AS
SELECT 
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month,
    d.name AS department,
    -- Added new columns
    p.category,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
JOIN employees e ON o.employee_id = e.employee_id
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date), d.name, p.category;

-- ⚠️ Note: Can't ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW to change definition
-- Must DROP and CREATE to change columns or query logic

-- Can rename
ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales_summary 
RENAME TO mv_monthly_sales;

-- Can change owner
ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_monthly_sales 
OWNER TO reporting_user;

-- Can change tablespace
ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_monthly_sales 
SET TABLESPACE reporting_tablespace;

39.4 Rules on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE

While rules are most commonly used for views, they can also be defined directly on tables to rewrite DML operations. However, triggers are usually the better choice for this purpose due to their more predictable behavior.

⚠️ Important Caveat

Rules on DML operations have non-intuitive semantics and can produce unexpected results. The PostgreSQL documentation strongly recommends using triggers instead of rules for data manipulation tasks. Rules are mainly retained for backward compatibility and view implementation.

39.4.1 How Update Rules Work

When a rule is defined on a table for INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE, the rule system rewrites the original query tree. The rule can either supplement (DO ALSO) or replace (DO INSTEAD) the original operation.

DO INSTEAD Rules
-- Example: Audit table with rule
CREATE TABLE employees_current (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    salary NUMERIC(10,2),
    department_id INTEGER,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE employees_history (
    history_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    employee_id INTEGER,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    salary NUMERIC(10,2),
    department_id INTEGER,
    operation VARCHAR(10),
    changed_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Rule: On DELETE, move to history instead of actually deleting
CREATE RULE employee_delete_to_history AS
ON DELETE TO employees_current
DO INSTEAD
    INSERT INTO employees_history (employee_id, name, salary, department_id, operation)
    VALUES (OLD.employee_id, OLD.name, OLD.salary, OLD.department_id, 'DELETE');

-- User executes DELETE
DELETE FROM employees_current WHERE employee_id = 10;

-- Rule rewrites this to INSERT into history
-- Original row in employees_current remains!
-- This is confusing - trigger would be clearer
DO ALSO Rules
-- DO ALSO: Execute original operation AND additional actions
CREATE RULE employee_update_audit AS
ON UPDATE TO employees_current
DO ALSO
    INSERT INTO employees_history (employee_id, name, salary, department_id, operation)
    VALUES (NEW.employee_id, NEW.name, NEW.salary, NEW.department_id, 'UPDATE');

-- User executes UPDATE
UPDATE employees_current SET salary = 80000 WHERE employee_id = 10;

-- Rule system generates TWO operations:
-- 1. UPDATE employees_current SET salary = 80000 WHERE employee_id = 10;
-- 2. INSERT INTO employees_history ... (audit record)

-- ⚠️ Problem: What if UPDATE affects multiple rows?
UPDATE employees_current SET salary = salary * 1.1 WHERE department_id = 3;
-- Multiple INSERT statements generated, but they all use the SAME NEW values!
-- This causes unexpected behavior - use triggers instead!

🐛 Common Rule Problems

Problem Why It Happens Trigger Solution
Multi-row operations Rules operate on query tree, not per row Triggers fire once per row
NEW/OLD confusion NEW/OLD are query references, not row values NEW/OLD are actual row data
Side effects Query rewriting can duplicate operations Procedural, predictable execution
Debugging difficulty Hidden query transformation Explicit, traceable logic

39.4.2 Cooperation with Views

Rules on views work more predictably than rules on tables because view operations are always single-query operations. This is where rules shine.

View with Multiple Rule Actions
-- Create a view that combines data from multiple tables
CREATE VIEW employee_contact_info AS
SELECT 
    e.employee_id,
    e.name,
    e.email AS work_email,
    c.phone AS personal_phone,
    c.address
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employee_contacts c ON e.employee_id = c.employee_id;

-- Make view updatable with rules
-- Rule 1: UPDATE employee name and email
CREATE RULE employee_contact_update AS
ON UPDATE TO employee_contact_info
DO INSTEAD (
    UPDATE employees
    SET name = NEW.name,
        email = NEW.work_email
    WHERE employee_id = OLD.employee_id;
    
    UPDATE employee_contacts
    SET phone = NEW.personal_phone,
        address = NEW.address
    WHERE employee_id = OLD.employee_id;
);

-- Rule 2: INSERT into both tables
CREATE RULE employee_contact_insert AS
ON INSERT TO employee_contact_info
DO INSTEAD (
    INSERT INTO employees (name, email)
    VALUES (NEW.name, NEW.work_email)
    RETURNING employee_id;
    
    INSERT INTO employee_contacts (employee_id, phone, address)
    VALUES (currval('employees_employee_id_seq'), NEW.personal_phone, NEW.address);
);

-- Now view operations work on multiple tables
UPDATE employee_contact_info
SET work_email = 'newemail@company.com',
    personal_phone = '555-0199'
WHERE employee_id = 10;
-- Updates both employees and employee_contacts tables

39.5 Rules and Privileges

Rules execute with the privileges of the rule owner, not the user executing the query. This is an important security consideration and differs from trigger behavior.

🔐 Privilege Execution Model

Mechanism Executes As Security Impact
Rules Rule owner Can bypass user permissions
Triggers (default) Calling user Respects user permissions
Triggers (SECURITY DEFINER) Function owner Can elevate privileges
Example: Security Implications
-- As superuser, create a view
CREATE VIEW public_employees AS
SELECT employee_id, name, email, department_id
FROM employees;

-- Grant SELECT to all users
GRANT SELECT ON public_employees TO PUBLIC;

-- Users can query the view
-- But underlying SELECT is rewritten to:
-- SELECT employee_id, name, email, department_id FROM employees
-- This executes with the PRIVILEGES of the view owner (superuser)
-- So it works even if users have no direct access to employees table

-- This is intentional for views (provides abstraction)
-- But be careful with DO ALSO rules that could expose data

39.6 Rules and Command Status

The PostgreSQL command status (e.g., "INSERT 0 1", "UPDATE 5") can be affected by rules in non-obvious ways. DO INSTEAD rules can cause the command status to report zero affected rows even when operations succeeded.

Command Status with Rules
-- Without rules
INSERT INTO employees (name, email) VALUES ('John Doe', 'john@example.com');
-- Returns: INSERT 0 1 (1 row inserted)

UPDATE employees SET salary = 75000 WHERE employee_id = 10;
-- Returns: UPDATE 1 (1 row updated)

DELETE FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 10;
-- Returns: DELETE 1 (1 row deleted)

-- With DO INSTEAD rule that redirects to another table
CREATE RULE employee_insert_redirect AS
ON INSERT TO employees
DO INSTEAD
    INSERT INTO employees_staging (name, email)
    VALUES (NEW.name, NEW.email);

INSERT INTO employees (name, email) VALUES ('Jane Smith', 'jane@example.com');
-- Returns: INSERT 0 0 (looks like nothing happened!)
-- But row was actually inserted into employees_staging

-- This can confuse applications that check affected row counts
-- Triggers return more predictable command status

39.7 Rules Versus Triggers

The choice between rules and triggers is crucial. While they can achieve similar goals, their execution models and behavior are fundamentally different.

⚖️ Comprehensive Comparison

Aspect Rules Triggers
Execution Model Query tree rewriting (once per query) Procedural execution (once per row)
When Executes During query planning During query execution
Multi-row Operations ❌ Confusing (operates on query, not rows) ✅ Intuitive (fires per row)
NEW/OLD Values Query references Actual row data
Conditional Logic Limited (WHERE clause) ✅ Full procedural control
Debugging ❌ Difficult (hidden rewriting) ✅ Easier (explicit code)
Performance Can be faster (query optimization) Overhead per row
Use Case ✅ View implementation ✅ Data validation, auditing, cascades
Recommendation Only for views ✅ For data manipulation logic
Same Goal, Different Approaches
