            VALUES ($1, $2, $3::jsonb, $4::jsonb, current_user)
        """, ["text", "text", "text", "text"]),
        [TD["table_name"], operation, old_json, new_json]
    )
    
    # For BEFORE triggers, return the row to continue
    if TD["when"] == "BEFORE":
        if operation == "DELETE":
            return "OK"  # DELETE doesn't use return value
        else:
            return new_data or "OK"
    
    return "OK"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

CREATE TRIGGER employees_audit
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();

-- Test the trigger
INSERT INTO employees (name, salary) VALUES ('Alice', 75000);
UPDATE employees SET salary = 80000 WHERE name = 'Alice';
DELETE FROM employees WHERE name = 'Alice';

SELECT * FROM audit_log;

-- Validation trigger (BEFORE)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION validate_employee() RETURNS trigger AS $$
    new_row = TD["new"]
    
    # Validate salary
    if new_row["salary"] is not None:
        if new_row["salary"] < 0:
            plpy.error("Salary cannot be negative")
        elif new_row["salary"] > 1000000:
            plpy.warning(f"High salary detected: {new_row['salary']}")
    
    # Normalize name
    if new_row["name"]:
        new_row["name"] = new_row["name"].strip().title()
    
    # Auto-update timestamp
    from datetime import datetime
    new_row["updated_at"] = datetime.now()
    
    return new_row
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

CREATE TRIGGER validate_employee_trigger
    BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION validate_employee();

-- Conditional trigger with arguments
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION conditional_trigger() RETURNS trigger AS $$
    args = TD["args"]
    threshold = int(args[0]) if args else 50000
    
    if TD["event"] == "UPDATE":
        old_salary = TD["old"]["salary"]
        new_salary = TD["new"]["salary"]
        
        if new_salary > threshold and old_salary <= threshold:
            plpy.notice(f"Employee {TD['new']['name']} crossed threshold!")
            
            # Notify management
            plpy.execute("""
                INSERT INTO notifications (message, created_at)
                VALUES ($1, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
            """, [f"Salary increase for {TD['new']['name']} exceeds ${threshold}"])
    
    return "OK"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

CREATE TRIGGER salary_threshold_trigger
    AFTER UPDATE ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW
    EXECUTE FUNCTION conditional_trigger('60000');

-- Statement-level trigger
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION statement_trigger() RETURNS trigger AS $$
    operation = TD["event"]
    table_name = TD["table_name"]
    
    plpy.info(f"{operation} operation on {table_name}")
    plpy.info(f"Trigger level: {TD['level']}")
    
    # Count affected rows
    if operation == "INSERT":
        count = plpy.execute(f"SELECT count(*) FROM {table_name}")[0]["count"]
        plpy.info(f"Table now has {count} rows")
    
    return "OK"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

CREATE TRIGGER employees_statement_trigger
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON employees
    FOR EACH STATEMENT EXECUTE FUNCTION statement_trigger();

💡 Trigger Return Values

  • BEFORE ROW triggers: Return modified row dict to change data, or "OK" to proceed unchanged
  • AFTER triggers: Return value is ignored, use "OK" by convention
  • DELETE triggers: Return "OK" (no row to modify)
  • Returning None: Cancels the operation (BEFORE triggers only)

BEFORE Triggers

  • Can modify row data
  • Can prevent operation (return None)
  • Validation and normalization
  • Auto-generate values

AFTER Triggers

  • Cannot modify row data
  • Logging and auditing
  • Notifications
  • Cascading operations

🗄️ 44.6. Database Access

The plpy module provides functions for executing SQL queries from Python code.

44.6.1. Database Access Functions

Function Description Returns
plpy.execute(query [, limit]) Execute SQL query Result object
plpy.prepare(query, types) Prepare parameterized query Plan object
plpy.execute(plan, values [, limit]) Execute prepared plan Result object
plpy.cursor(query) Create cursor for large results Cursor object

Basic Query Execution

-- Simple query
CREATE FUNCTION count_employees() RETURNS integer AS $$
    rv = plpy.execute("SELECT count(*) AS cnt FROM employees")
    return rv[0]["cnt"]
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Query with results
CREATE FUNCTION get_high_earners(min_salary numeric) RETURNS SETOF employees AS $$
    query = f"SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary >= {min_salary} ORDER BY salary DESC"
    rv = plpy.execute(query)
    
    for row in rv:
        yield row
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Accessing result properties
CREATE FUNCTION query_info() RETURNS text AS $$
    rv = plpy.execute("SELECT * FROM employees LIMIT 5")
    
    info = []
    info.append(f"Rows returned: {rv.nrows()}")
    info.append(f"Status: {rv.status()}")
    info.append(f"Column names: {rv.colnames()}")
    info.append(f"Column types: {rv.coltypes()}")
    
    return "\n".join(info)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

Prepared Statements

-- Using plpy.prepare() for parameterized queries
CREATE FUNCTION insert_employee(emp_name text, emp_salary numeric) RETURNS integer AS $$
    # Prepare the statement (prevents SQL injection)
    plan = plpy.prepare("""
        INSERT INTO employees (name, salary)
        VALUES ($1, $2)
        RETURNING id
    """, ["text", "numeric"])
    
    # Execute with parameters
    rv = plpy.execute(plan, [emp_name, emp_salary])
    
    return rv[0]["id"]
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Reusing prepared statements with caching
CREATE FUNCTION update_salary_cached(emp_id integer, new_salary numeric) RETURNS void AS $$
    # Cache prepared statement
    if "update_plan" not in SD:
        SD["update_plan"] = plpy.prepare("""
            UPDATE employees
            SET salary = $2, updated_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
            WHERE id = $1
        """, ["integer", "numeric"])
    
    plpy.execute(SD["update_plan"], [emp_id, new_salary])
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Complex prepared query
CREATE FUNCTION search_employees(
    search_name text,
    min_salary numeric,
    max_salary numeric
) RETURNS SETOF employees AS $$
    plan = plpy.prepare("""
        SELECT * FROM employees
        WHERE name ILIKE '%' || $1 || '%'
          AND salary BETWEEN $2 AND $3
        ORDER BY salary DESC
    """, ["text", "numeric", "numeric"])
    
    rv = plpy.execute(plan, [search_name, min_salary, max_salary])
    
    for row in rv:
        yield row
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

Cursors for Large Result Sets

-- Using cursor for memory efficiency
CREATE FUNCTION process_large_table() RETURNS text AS $$
    # Create cursor
    cursor = plpy.cursor("SELECT * FROM large_table ORDER BY id")
    
    total_processed = 0
    batch_size = 1000
    
    while True:
        # Fetch batch
        rows = cursor.fetch(batch_size)
        
        if not rows:
            break
        
        # Process batch
        for row in rows:
            # Do something with each row
            total_processed += 1
    
    cursor.close()
    return f"Processed {total_processed} rows"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Cursor with prepared statement
CREATE FUNCTION export_employees_csv(min_salary numeric) RETURNS SETOF text AS $$
    plan = plpy.prepare("""
        SELECT name, salary, hire_date
        FROM employees
        WHERE salary >= $1
        ORDER BY salary DESC
    """, ["numeric"])
    
    cursor = plpy.cursor(plan, [min_salary])
    
    # Yield CSV header
    yield "Name,Salary,Hire Date"
    
    # Process in batches
    while True:
        rows = cursor.fetch(100)
        if not rows:
            break
        
        for row in rows:
            yield f"{row['name']},{row['salary']},{row['hire_date']}"
    
    cursor.close()
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

✅ Best Practices

  • Use plpy.prepare() for queries with user input to prevent SQL injection
  • Cache prepared statements in SD for frequently executed queries
  • Use cursors (plpy.cursor()) when processing large result sets
  • Set appropriate limit parameter to prevent memory issues

44.6.2. Trapping Errors

Use Python's try/except to handle database errors. PL/Python raises plpy.SPIError for SQL errors.

-- Basic error handling
CREATE FUNCTION safe_insert(emp_name text, emp_salary numeric) RETURNS text AS $$
    try:
        plan = plpy.prepare("""
            INSERT INTO employees (name, salary)
            VALUES ($1, $2)
            RETURNING id
        """, ["text", "numeric"])
        
        rv = plpy.execute(plan, [emp_name, emp_salary])
        return f"Success: Inserted employee with ID {rv[0]['id']}"
        
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        return f"Error: {e}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Handling specific error codes
CREATE FUNCTION update_with_retry(emp_id integer, new_salary numeric) RETURNS text AS $$
    import time
    
    max_retries = 3
    retry_count = 0
    
    while retry_count < max_retries:
        try:
            plan = plpy.prepare("""
                UPDATE employees
                SET salary = $2
                WHERE id = $1
            """, ["integer", "numeric"])
            
            plpy.execute(plan, [emp_id, new_salary])
            return "Update successful"
            
        except plpy.SPIError as e:
            # Check for deadlock error (40P01)
            if e.sqlstate == "40P01":
                retry_count += 1
                if retry_count < max_retries:
                    plpy.warning(f"Deadlock detected, retry {retry_count}/{max_retries}")
                    time.sleep(0.1 * retry_count)  # Exponential backoff
                else:
                    return f"Failed after {max_retries} retries: {e}"
            else:
                # Other errors, don't retry
                return f"Error: {e} (SQLSTATE: {e.sqlstate})"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Error information access
CREATE FUNCTION error_details_demo() RETURNS text AS $$
    try:
        # This will fail (table doesn't exist)
        plpy.execute("SELECT * FROM nonexistent_table")
        
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        details = []
        details.append(f"Message: {e}")
        details.append(f"SQLSTATE: {e.sqlstate}")
        
        # Additional error attributes (if available)
        if hasattr(e, 'spidata'):
            for key, value in e.spidata.items():
                details.append(f"{key}: {value}")
        
        return "\n".join(details)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Combining subtransactions with error handling
CREATE FUNCTION batch_insert_with_errors(names text[], salaries numeric[]) RETURNS text AS $$
    success_count = 0
    error_count = 0
    errors = []
    
    plan = plpy.prepare("""
        INSERT INTO employees (name, salary)
        VALUES ($1, $2)
    """, ["text", "numeric"])
    
    for i, (name, salary) in enumerate(zip(names, salaries)):
        try:
            with plpy.subtransaction():
                plpy.execute(plan, [name, salary])
                success_count += 1
        except plpy.SPIError as e:
            error_count += 1
            errors.append(f"Row {i+1} ({name}): {e}")
    
    result = [f"Success: {success_count}, Errors: {error_count}"]
    if errors:
        result.append("\nErrors:")
        result.extend(errors[:5])  # Show first 5 errors
        if len(errors) > 5:
            result.append(f"... and {len(errors)-5} more errors")
    
    return "\n".join(result)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Validation with custom errors
CREATE FUNCTION validate_and_insert(emp_name text, emp_salary numeric) RETURNS integer AS $$
    # Validation
    if not emp_name or emp_name.strip() == "":
        plpy.error("Employee name cannot be empty")
    
    if emp_salary < 0:
        plpy.error("Salary cannot be negative")
    
    if emp_salary > 1000000:
        plpy.error("Salary exceeds maximum allowed value")
    
    # Check for duplicates
    try:
        rv = plpy.execute(
            plpy.prepare("SELECT id FROM employees WHERE name = $1", ["text"]),
            [emp_name]
        )
        
        if rv.nrows() > 0:
            plpy.error(f"Employee '{emp_name}' already exists")
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        plpy.error(f"Database error during validation: {e}")
    
    # Insert
    try:
        plan = plpy.prepare("""
            INSERT INTO employees (name, salary)
            VALUES ($1, $2)
            RETURNING id
        """, ["text", "numeric"])
        
        rv = plpy.execute(plan, [emp_name, emp_salary])
        return rv[0]["id"]
        
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        plpy.error(f"Failed to insert employee: {e}")
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;
Error Function Severity Behavior
plpy.debug(msg) DEBUG Detailed debug information
plpy.info(msg) INFO Informational messages
plpy.notice(msg) NOTICE Important notifications
plpy.warning(msg) WARNING Warning conditions
plpy.error(msg) ERROR Abort transaction, raise error
plpy.fatal(msg) FATAL Abort session

🔄 44.7. Explicit Subtransactions

PL/Python allows explicit subtransaction control, enabling you to recover from errors in part of a function without aborting the entire transaction.

-- Basic subtransaction
CREATE FUNCTION subtransaction_demo() RETURNS text AS $$
    results = []
    
    # This will succeed
    plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table (id, data) VALUES (1, 'First')")
    results.append("First insert: SUCCESS")
    
    # Try a failing operation in subtransaction
    try:
        with plpy.subtransaction():
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table (id, data) VALUES (1, 'Duplicate')")
            # This will fail due to duplicate key
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        results.append(f"Duplicate insert: FAILED ({e.sqlstate})")
    
    # This will still succeed because we caught the error
    plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table (id, data) VALUES (2, 'Second')")
    results.append("Second insert: SUCCESS")
    
    return "\n".join(results)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Batch processing with subtransactions
CREATE FUNCTION batch_process_with_subtrans(
    ids integer[],
    data_values text[]
) RETURNS text AS $$
    success_count = 0
    error_count = 0
    errors = []
    
    for i, (id_val, data_val) in enumerate(zip(ids, data_values), 1):
        try:
            with plpy.subtransaction():
                plan = plpy.prepare("""
                    INSERT INTO test_table (id, data)
                    VALUES ($1, $2)
                """, ["integer", "text"])
                
                plpy.execute(plan, [id_val, data_val])
                success_count += 1
                
        except plpy.SPIError as e:
            error_count += 1
            errors.append(f"Row {i} (id={id_val}): {e.sqlstate}")
    
    result = [
        f"Processed {len(ids)} items",
        f"Success: {success_count}",
        f"Errors: {error_count}"
    ]
    
    if errors:
        result.append("\nErrors:")
        result.extend(errors[:10])  # Show first 10
        if len(errors) > 10:
            result.append(f"... and {len(errors)-10} more")
    
    return "\n".join(result)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Nested subtransactions
CREATE FUNCTION nested_subtransactions_demo() RETURNS text AS $$
    results = []
    
    try:
        with plpy.subtransaction():
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (10, 'Outer')")
            results.append("Outer insert: SUCCESS")
            
            try:
                with plpy.subtransaction():
                    plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (20, 'Inner')")
                    results.append("Inner insert: SUCCESS")
                    
                    # This will fail
                    plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (20, 'Duplicate')")
                    
            except plpy.SPIError:
                results.append("Inner subtransaction: ROLLED BACK")
            
            # Outer subtransaction continues
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (30, 'After inner')")
            results.append("After inner insert: SUCCESS")
            
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        results.append(f"Outer subtransaction: FAILED ({e})")
    
    return "\n".join(results)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

44.7.1. Subtransaction Context Managers

The plpy.subtransaction() returns a context manager that handles enter/exit automatically.

-- Advanced context manager usage
CREATE FUNCTION context_manager_example() RETURNS text AS $$
    results = []
    
    # Explicit context manager
    subtxn = plpy.subtransaction()
    subtxn.enter()
    
    try:
        plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (100, 'Manual context')")
        results.append("Manual context: SUCCESS")
        subtxn.exit(None, None, None)  # Commit
        
    except Exception as e:
        subtxn.exit(type(e), e, e.__traceback__)  # Rollback
        results.append(f"Manual context: FAILED ({e})")
    
    # Using 'with' statement (recommended)
    try:
        with plpy.subtransaction():
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (200, 'With statement')")
            results.append("With statement: SUCCESS")
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        results.append(f"With statement: FAILED ({e})")
    
    return "\n".join(results)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Real-world example: Data validation and cleanup
CREATE FUNCTION import_and_validate_data(raw_data jsonb) RETURNS text AS $$
    import json
    
    data = json.loads(raw_data)
    imported = 0
    skipped = 0
    errors = []
    
    for record in data:
        try:
            with plpy.subtransaction():
                # Validate required fields
                if not record.get('id'):
                    raise ValueError("Missing id field")
                
                if not record.get('name'):
                    raise ValueError("Missing name field")
                
                # Validate data types and ranges
                emp_id = int(record['id'])
                if emp_id <= 0:
                    raise ValueError("ID must be positive")
                
                salary = float(record.get('salary', 0))
                if salary < 0:
                    raise ValueError("Salary cannot be negative")
                
                # Insert validated data
                plan = plpy.prepare("""
                    INSERT INTO employees (id, name, salary)
                    VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
                    ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE
                    SET name = EXCLUDED.name,
                        salary = EXCLUDED.salary
                """, ["integer", "text", "numeric"])
                
                plpy.execute(plan, [emp_id, record['name'], salary])
                imported += 1
                
        except (ValueError, KeyError) as e:
            skipped += 1
            errors.append(f"Record {record.get('id', 'unknown')}: {e}")
            
        except plpy.SPIError as e:
            skipped += 1
            errors.append(f"Record {record.get('id', 'unknown')}: DB error {e.sqlstate}")
    
    result = [
        f"Import complete",
        f"Imported: {imported}",
        f"Skipped: {skipped}"
    ]
    
    if errors:
        result.append("\nErrors (first 5):")
        result.extend(errors[:5])
    
    return "\n".join(result)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Complex transaction with savepoints
CREATE FUNCTION complex_transaction_processing() RETURNS text AS $$
    results = []
    
    # Main transaction body
    plpy.execute("INSERT INTO audit_log (message) VALUES ('Process started')")
    
    # Phase 1: Critical operations (must succeed)
    try:
        with plpy.subtransaction():
            plpy.execute("UPDATE accounts SET status = 'processing'")
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO audit_log (message) VALUES ('Phase 1 complete')")
            results.append("Phase 1: SUCCESS")
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        plpy.error(f"Critical phase 1 failed: {e}")
    
    # Phase 2: Optional operations (can fail)
    phase2_success = False
    try:
        with plpy.subtransaction():
            plpy.execute("UPDATE optional_table SET flag = true")
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO audit_log (message) VALUES ('Phase 2 complete')")
            phase2_success = True
            results.append("Phase 2: SUCCESS")
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        results.append(f"Phase 2: SKIPPED ({e.sqlstate})")
    
    # Phase 3: Finalization (depends on phase 2)
    try:
        with plpy.subtransaction():
            if phase2_success:
                plpy.execute("UPDATE accounts SET status = 'completed'")
            else:
                plpy.execute("UPDATE accounts SET status = 'partial'")
            
            plpy.execute("INSERT INTO audit_log (message) VALUES ('Process finished')")
            results.append("Phase 3: SUCCESS")
    except plpy.SPIError as e:
        plpy.error(f"Critical phase 3 failed: {e}")
    
    return "\n".join(results)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

✅ Use Subtransactions For

  • Batch operations where some items may fail
  • Optimistic locking with retry logic
  • Data validation with partial rollback
  • Error recovery in complex workflows

⚠️ Performance Considerations

  • Subtransactions have overhead
  • Avoid excessive nesting (>5 levels)
  • Consider batch size vs. transaction cost
  • Test performance with realistic data volumes

💡 Subtransaction Behavior

When a subtransaction is exited normally, its changes are committed to the parent transaction. If an exception occurs within the with plpy.subtransaction() block, the subtransaction is rolled back, but the parent transaction continues unless the exception is re-raised.

⚙️ 44.8. Transaction Management

PL/Python procedures (not functions) can control transactions using plpy.commit() and plpy.rollback().

⚠️ Important Distinction

Functions: Cannot control transactions, run in atomic transaction context
Procedures: Can commit/rollback, useful for long-running batch operations 

-- Create procedure (not function) for transaction control
CREATE PROCEDURE batch_update_with_commits(batch_size integer) AS $$
    processed = 0
    
    while True:
        # Process a batch
        rv = plpy.execute(f"""
            UPDATE large_table
            SET processed = true
            WHERE id IN (
                SELECT id FROM large_table
                WHERE NOT processed
                LIMIT {batch_size}
            )
        """)
        
        rows_updated = rv.nrows()
        if rows_updated == 0:
            break
        
        processed += rows_updated
        
        # Commit after each batch to avoid long-running transaction
        plpy.commit()
        
        plpy.info(f"Processed {processed} rows so far...")
    
    plpy.info(f"Total processed: {processed} rows")
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Call the procedure
CALL batch_update_with_commits(1000);

-- Procedure with conditional rollback
CREATE PROCEDURE conditional_data_migration() AS $$
    # Phase 1: Copy data
    plpy.execute("""
        INSERT INTO new_table (id, data, migrated_at)
        SELECT id, data, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
        FROM old_table
        WHERE NOT migrated
    """)
    
    migrated_count = plpy.execute("SELECT count(*) AS cnt FROM new_table")[0]['cnt']
    plpy.info(f"Migrated {migrated_count} rows")
    
    # Commit phase 1
    plpy.commit()
    
    # Phase 2: Validate migrated data
    validation_query = """
        SELECT count(*) AS invalid_count
        FROM new_table nt
        LEFT JOIN old_table ot ON nt.id = ot.id
        WHERE nt.data != ot.data
    """
    
    invalid_count = plpy.execute(validation_query)[0]['invalid_count']
    
    if invalid_count > 0:
        plpy.warning(f"Validation failed: {invalid_count} mismatches found")
        plpy.rollback()  # Rollback phase 2
        plpy.error("Migration validation failed, rolled back")
    else:
        # Mark as migrated in old table
        plpy.execute("UPDATE old_table SET migrated = true")
        plpy.commit()
        plpy.info("Migration completed successfully")
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Long-running data processing with progress commits
CREATE PROCEDURE process_data_with_progress() AS $$
    import time
    
    # Get total count
    total = plpy.execute("SELECT count(*) AS cnt FROM processing_queue")[0]['cnt']
    plpy.info(f"Starting processing of {total} items")
    
    batch_size = 100
    processed = 0
    start_time = time.time()
    
    while processed < total:
        # Process batch
        rv = plpy.execute(f"""
            WITH batch AS (
                SELECT id FROM processing_queue
                WHERE status = 'pending'
                LIMIT {batch_size}
                FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
            )
            UPDATE processing_queue
            SET status = 'completed',
                completed_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
            FROM batch
            WHERE processing_queue.id = batch.id
        """)
        
        batch_count = rv.nrows()
        if batch_count == 0:
            break
        
        processed += batch_count
        
        # Commit to release locks and persist progress
        plpy.commit()
        
        # Progress reporting
        elapsed = time.time() - start_time
        rate = processed / elapsed if elapsed > 0 else 0
        remaining = total - processed
        eta = remaining / rate if rate > 0 else 0
        
        plpy.info(f"Progress: {processed}/{total} ({processed*100/total:.1f}%) - "
                  f"Rate: {rate:.1f} items/sec - ETA: {eta:.0f}s")
        
        # Small delay to avoid overwhelming the system
        time.sleep(0.1)
    
    total_time = time.time() - start_time
    plpy.info(f"Processing complete: {processed} items in {total_time:.1f}s")
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Procedure with error handling and transaction control
CREATE PROCEDURE safe_batch_insert(
    data_json jsonb
) AS $$
    import json
    
    data = json.loads(data_json)
    total_items = len(data)
    success_count = 0
    error_count = 0
    
    plan = plpy.prepare("""
        INSERT INTO target_table (id, name, value)
        VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
    """, ["integer", "text", "numeric"])
    
    batch_count = 0
    batch_size = 50
    
    for item in data:
        try:
            plpy.execute(plan, [
                item['id'],
                item['name'],
                item.get('value', 0)
            ])
            success_count += 1
            batch_count += 1
            
            # Commit every batch_size items
            if batch_count >= batch_size:
                plpy.commit()
                plpy.info(f"Committed batch: {success_count} successful so far")
                batch_count = 0
                
        except (KeyError, ValueError) as e:
            error_count += 1
            plpy.warning(f"Invalid data for item: {e}")
            plpy.rollback()  # Rollback this item
            batch_count = 0
            
        except plpy.SPIError as e:
            error_count += 1
            plpy.warning(f"Database error for item: {e}")
            plpy.rollback()  # Rollback this item
            batch_count = 0
    
    # Final commit for remaining items
    if batch_count > 0:
        plpy.commit()
    
    plpy.info(f"Batch insert complete: {success_count} success, {error_count} errors")
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;
Function Description Usage
plpy.commit() Commit current transaction, start new one Procedures only
plpy.rollback() Roll back current transaction, start new one Procedures only

🛠️ 44.9. Utility Functions

PL/Python provides several utility functions for logging, quoting, and other operations.

Logging Functions

-- Logging at different levels
CREATE FUNCTION logging_example() RETURNS void AS $$
    plpy.debug("Debug message - very detailed")
    plpy.log("Log message - detailed info")
    plpy.info("Info message - general information")
    plpy.notice("Notice message - important info")
    plpy.warning("Warning message - potential issue")
    
    # plpy.error() would abort the function
    # plpy.fatal() would terminate the session
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Using logging in real function
CREATE FUNCTION complex_calculation(x integer, y integer) RETURNS integer AS $$
    plpy.debug(f"Starting calculation with x={x}, y={y}")
    
    if y == 0:
        plpy.warning("Division by zero avoided, using y=1")
        y = 1
    
    result = (x * x + y * y) // y
    
    plpy.info(f"Calculation result: {result}")
    return result
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

Quoting Functions

-- SQL quoting utilities
CREATE FUNCTION quoting_example(user_input text) RETURNS text AS $$
    # Quote string literal
    quoted_literal = plpy.quote_literal(user_input)
    plpy.info(f"Literal: {quoted_literal}")
    
    # Quote identifier (table/column name)
    table_name = user_input
    quoted_ident = plpy.quote_ident(table_name)
    plpy.info(f"Identifier: {quoted_ident}")
    
    # Quote nullable (NULL becomes NULL, not 'NULL')
    nullable_value = None
    quoted_nullable = plpy.quote_nullable(nullable_value)
    plpy.info(f"Nullable: {quoted_nullable}")
    
    return "Check messages for quoted values"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

SELECT quoting_example('O''Reilly');

-- Dynamic query with safe quoting
CREATE FUNCTION dynamic_query_safe(
    tbl_name text,
    col_name text,
    search_value text
) RETURNS SETOF record AS $$
    # Safely quote identifiers
    safe_table = plpy.quote_ident(tbl_name)
    safe_column = plpy.quote_ident(col_name)
    
    # Safely quote literal
    safe_value = plpy.quote_literal(search_value)
    
    # Build dynamic query
    query = f"""
        SELECT * FROM {safe_table}
        WHERE {safe_column} = {safe_value}
    """
    
    plpy.info(f"Executing: {query}")
    
    return plpy.execute(query)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

Other Utility Functions

-- Getting PostgreSQL version info
CREATE FUNCTION get_pg_version_info() RETURNS text AS $$
    # Execute version query
    rv = plpy.execute("SELECT version()")
    version_string = rv[0]["version"]
    
    # Can also get specific version components
    rv = plpy.execute("""
        SELECT current_setting('server_version') AS version,
               current_setting('server_version_num') AS version_num
    """)
    
    info = [
        f"Full version: {version_string}",
        f"Version: {rv[0]['version']}",
        f"Version number: {rv[0]['version_num']}"
    ]
    
    return "\n".join(info)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Checking for NULL values
CREATE FUNCTION null_handling_utils(val anyelement) RETURNS text AS $$
    if val is None:
        return "Value is NULL"
    else:
        return f"Value is: {val}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Working with result status
CREATE FUNCTION result_status_example() RETURNS text AS $$
    # Different query types return different statuses
    
    rv1 = plpy.execute("SELECT 1")
    status1 = rv1.status()  # SPI_OK_SELECT
    
    rv2 = plpy.execute("INSERT INTO test_table VALUES (1, 'test') ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING")
    status2 = rv2.status()  # SPI_OK_INSERT or similar
    nrows2 = rv2.nrows()
    
    rv3 = plpy.execute("UPDATE test_table SET data = 'updated' WHERE id = 1")
    status3 = rv3.status()
    nrows3 = rv3.nrows()
    
    return f"Statuses: {status1}, {status2} ({nrows2} rows), {status3} ({nrows3} rows)"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;
Function Purpose Example
plpy.quote_literal(str) Quote string for SQL 'O''Reilly' → 'O''Reilly'
plpy.quote_ident(str) Quote identifier user name → "user name"
plpy.quote_nullable(str) Quote or return NULL None → NULL
result.nrows() Number of rows affected Returns integer count
result.status() Query execution status SPI status code
result.colnames() Column names list ['id', 'name', 'salary']
result.coltypes() Column type OIDs [23, 25, 1700]

✅ Security Best Practices

  • Always use plpy.prepare() with parameters for user input
  • Use plpy.quote_ident() when dynamically building table/column names
  • Use plpy.quote_literal() only when prepared statements aren't possible
  • Never concatenate user input directly into SQL strings

🔄 44.10. Python 2 vs. Python 3

PostgreSQL 17 primarily supports Python 3 through the plpython3u language. The legacy plpythonu language uses Python 2, which has reached end-of-life.

⚠️ Python 2 Deprecation

Python 2 reached end-of-life on January 1, 2020. The plpythonu language is deprecated and should not be used for new development. Always use plpython3u for new functions.

Key Differences

Feature Python 2 (plpythonu) Python 3 (plpython3u)
String Type str (bytes), unicode str (unicode), bytes
Print Statement print "hello" print("hello")
Division 5/2 = 2 (integer) 5/2 = 2.5 (float)
Integer Division 5/2 5//2
Iterators range() returns list range() returns iterator
Dict Methods dict.keys() returns list dict.keys() returns view
Exception Syntax except Error, e: except Error as e:
Long Type long exists Only int (arbitrary precision)

Migration Examples

-- Python 2 (OLD - Don't use)
CREATE FUNCTION py2_example(x integer) RETURNS text AS $$
    # Python 2 syntax
    result = []
    
    # Print statement (no parentheses)
    print "Processing:", x
    
    # Integer division
    half = x / 2  # Integer division in Python 2
    
    # String handling
    text = u"Unicode string"
    byte_string = str(text)
    
    # Dictionary iteration
    d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
    for key in d.keys():  # Returns list in Python 2
        result.append(key)
    
    # Exception handling (old syntax)
    try:
        value = 10 / 0
    except ZeroDivisionError, e:
        result.append("Error caught")
    
    return ", ".join(result)
$$ LANGUAGE plpythonu;  -- Deprecated!

-- Python 3 (NEW - Use this)
CREATE FUNCTION py3_example(x integer) RETURNS text AS $$
    # Python 3 syntax
    result = []
    
    # Print function (with parentheses)
    print(f"Processing: {x}")
    
    # Integer division
    half = x // 2  # Explicit integer division in Python 3
    float_div = x / 2  # Always returns float
    
    # String handling (all strings are unicode)
    text = "Unicode string by default"
    byte_string = text.encode('utf-8')
    
    # Dictionary iteration
    d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
    for key in d.keys():  # Returns view in Python 3
        result.append(key)
    
    # Exception handling (new syntax)
    try:
        value = 10 / 0
    except ZeroDivisionError as e:
        result.append("Error caught")
    
    return ", ".join(result)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;  -- Use this!

-- String handling differences
CREATE FUNCTION string_handling_py3() RETURNS text AS $$
    # In Python 3, PostgreSQL text becomes str (unicode)
    pg_text = "Hello, 世界"
    
    # Encode to bytes if needed
    utf8_bytes = pg_text.encode('utf-8')
    
    # Decode bytes back to str
    decoded = utf8_bytes.decode('utf-8')
    
    return f"Original: {pg_text}, Bytes length: {len(utf8_bytes)}, Decoded: {decoded}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Division behavior
CREATE FUNCTION division_differences() RETURNS text AS $$
    # Python 3 division always returns float
    result1 = 5 / 2      # 2.5 (float division)
    result2 = 5 // 2     # 2 (floor division)
    result3 = 5 % 2      # 1 (modulo)
    result4 = 5 / 2.0    # 2.5 (explicit float)
    
    return f"/ operator: {result1}, // operator: {result2}, % operator: {result3}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

✅ Python 3 Advantages

  • Better Unicode support (default)
  • More consistent semantics
  • Active development and security updates
  • Modern language features (f-strings, etc.)
  • Better async support
  • Type hints available

📋 Migration Checklist

  • Change plpythonu to plpython3u
  • Add parentheses to print statements
  • Update exception syntax (as instead of comma)
  • Use // for integer division
  • Remove u prefix from strings
  • Update dictionary iteration if needed

Compatibility Tips

-- Using future imports for compatibility (if needed)
CREATE FUNCTION py3_compatible() RETURNS text AS $$
    # These imports make Python 2 code more Python 3-like
    from __future__ import print_function
    from __future__ import division
    from __future__ import unicode_literals
    
    # Now print is a function
    print("This works in both Python 2.7+ and Python 3")
    
    # Division behaves like Python 3
    result = 5 / 2  # Returns 2.5 in both versions
    
    return f"Result: {result}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

🌍 44.11. Environment Variables

Several environment variables affect PL/Python behavior. These are typically set at the system or PostgreSQL configuration level.

Variable Description Example
PYTHONPATH Additional directories for Python module search /usr/local/lib/python
PYTHONHOME Python installation location /usr/local/python3
PYTHONOPTIMIZE Enable Python optimizations 1 or 2
PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE Prevent .pyc file creation 1

Accessing Environment Variables

-- Reading environment variables
CREATE FUNCTION show_python_env() RETURNS text AS $$
    import os
    import sys
    
    info = []
    
    # Python version
    info.append(f"Python version: {sys.version}")
    info.append(f"Python executable: {sys.executable}")
    
    # Module search paths
    info.append(f"\nPython path has {len(sys.path)} entries")
    info.append("First 3 paths:")
    for path in sys.path[:3]:
        info.append(f"  - {path}")
    
    # Environment variables
    info.append("\nEnvironment variables:")
    
    python_vars = ['PYTHONPATH', 'PYTHONHOME', 'PYTHONOPTIMIZE', 'PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE']
    for var in python_vars:
        value = os.environ.get(var, 'Not set')
        info.append(f"  {var}: {value}")
    
    return "\n".join(info)
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

SELECT show_python_env();

-- Working with custom Python modules
CREATE FUNCTION use_custom_module() RETURNS text AS $$
    import sys
    import os
    
    # Add custom module directory
    custom_path = '/path/to/custom/modules'
    if custom_path not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, custom_path)
    
    # Now can import from custom location
    # import my_custom_module
    
    return f"Custom path added: {custom_path}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Checking Python configuration
CREATE FUNCTION python_config_info() RETURNS TABLE(
    config_name text,
    config_value text
) AS $$
    import sys
    import os
    
    configs = []
    
    # System configuration
    configs.append(('Platform', sys.platform))
    configs.append(('Version Info', str(sys.version_info)))
    configs.append(('API Version', str(sys.api_version)))
    configs.append(('Max Unicode', str(sys.maxunicode)))
    
    # File system encoding
    configs.append(('FS Encoding', sys.getfilesystemencoding()))
    configs.append(('Default Encoding', sys.getdefaultencoding()))
    
    # Module search path count
    configs.append(('Path Count', str(len(sys.path))))
    
    # Prefix and exec_prefix
    configs.append(('Prefix', sys.prefix))
    configs.append(('Exec Prefix', sys.exec_prefix))
    
    return configs
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

Setting Variables in PostgreSQL

-- In postgresql.conf or via ALTER SYSTEM
ALTER SYSTEM SET plpython3u.python_path TO '/usr/local/lib/python3/site-packages';

-- Reload configuration
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

-- Per-database setting
ALTER DATABASE mydb SET plpython3u.python_path TO '/custom/path';

-- Per-role setting
ALTER ROLE myuser SET plpython3u.python_path TO '/user/specific/path';

-- Session-level setting
SET plpython3u.python_path TO '/tmp/test_modules';

Module Management

-- Check if module is available
CREATE FUNCTION check_module(module_name text) RETURNS text AS $$
    import importlib.util
    
    spec = importlib.util.find_spec(module_name)
    
    if spec is None:
        return f"Module '{module_name}' not found"
    else:
        return f"Module '{module_name}' found at: {spec.origin}"
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

SELECT check_module('json');     -- Should be found
SELECT check_module('requests'); -- May not be found
SELECT check_module('numpy');    -- Depends on installation

-- List installed packages (requires pip)
CREATE FUNCTION list_installed_packages() RETURNS SETOF text AS $$
    import pkg_resources
    
    installed_packages = []
    for package in pkg_resources.working_set:
        installed_packages.append(f"{package.key} {package.version}")
    
    # Sort and return
    installed_packages.sort()
    return installed_packages
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

-- Check Python library versions
CREATE FUNCTION check_library_versions() RETURNS TABLE(
    library text,
    version text,
    location text
) AS $$
    import sys
    import importlib
    
    libraries = ['sys', 'os', 'json', 'datetime', 'decimal', 'collections']
    results = []
    
    for lib_name in libraries:
        try:
            module = importlib.import_module(lib_name)
            version = getattr(module, '__version__', 'Built-in')
            location = getattr(module, '__file__', 'Built-in')
            results.append((lib_name, version, location))
        except ImportError:
            results.append((lib_name, 'Not available', 'N/A'))
    
    return results
$$ LANGUAGE plpython3u;

⚠️ Security Considerations

  • Be cautious when adding paths to sys.path - only use trusted directories
  • External Python modules may have security vulnerabilities
  • Use plpython3u (trusted) unless you specifically need untrusted operations
  • Regularly update Python and installed packages for security patches

✅ Best Practices

  • Set PYTHONPATH at server startup
  • Use virtual environments for package isolation
  • Document required Python packages
  • Test with the exact Python version in production

📦 Common Packages

  • json - JSON parsing (built-in)
  • datetime - Date/time handling (built-in)
  • decimal - Precision arithmetic (built-in)
  • re - Regular expressions (built-in)
  • requests - HTTP requests (external)
  • numpy - Numerical computing (external)

🎓 Chapter 44 Summary: PL/Python Language

PL/Python brings the power of Python to PostgreSQL, enabling complex data processing, scientific computing, and integration with external Python libraries. Key features include:

  • Seamless Type Conversion: Automatic mapping between PostgreSQL and Python types
  • Database Access: Execute SQL queries using plpy module with prepared statements and cursors
  • Triggers: Create trigger functions with access to OLD/NEW row data via TD dictionary
  • Error Handling: Python-style exception handling with plpy.SPIError
  • Transaction Control: Procedures can commit/rollback with plpy.commit() and plpy.rollback()
  • Subtransactions: Fine-grained error recovery with context managers
  • Python 3: Modern Python features with plpython3u (Python 2 deprecated)
  • Data Sharing: Session-wide (GD) and function-specific (SD) dictionaries

Use PL/Python when you need Python's rich ecosystem of libraries, complex data transformations, or when your team has Python expertise. For performance-critical operations, consider PL/pgSQL.

🔌

Chapter 45: SPI - Server Programming Interface

PostgreSQL Version 17

The Server Programming Interface (SPI) allows C functions to access PostgreSQL's internal database functionality. It provides a standardized interface for executing SQL commands, managing memory, and handling transactions from within procedural language functions and C extensions.

📌 Key Concepts

  • Connection Management: Connect/disconnect from SPI manager
  • Command Execution: Execute SQL commands and prepared statements
  • Cursor Operations: Work with cursors for large result sets
  • Memory Management: Allocate and free memory properly
  • Transaction Control: Manage commits and rollbacks

⚙️ 45.1. Interface Functions

The SPI provides a comprehensive set of functions for interacting with the database. Functions are organized into categories based on their primary purpose.

Connection Management

Function Description
SPI_connect() Connect a C function to the SPI manager. Must be called before any other SPI function.
SPI_finish() Disconnect from the SPI manager. Must be called before returning from the function. 
/* Basic SPI connection example */
int ret;

/* Connect to SPI */
ret = SPI_connect();
if (ret != SPI_OK_CONNECT)
    elog(ERROR, "SPI_connect failed: error code %d", ret);

/* ... perform SPI operations ... */

/* Disconnect from SPI */
SPI_finish();

Command Execution Functions

Function Purpose Use Case
SPI_execute() Execute a command string Simple queries without parameters
SPI_exec() Execute in read/write mode DML operations
SPI_execute_extended() Execute with out-of-line parameters Advanced parameter handling
SPI_execute_with_args() Execute with typed arguments Parameterized queries 
/* Execute a simple query */
int ret;
SPITupleTable *tuptable;
uint64 proc;

ret = SPI_execute("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000", 
                  true,  /* read_only = true */
                  10);   /* max rows to return */

if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
{
    tuptable = SPI_tuptable;
    proc = SPI_processed;
    
    /* Process results */
    for (uint64 i = 0; i < proc; i++)
    {
        HeapTuple tuple = tuptable->vals[i];
        /* ... work with tuple ... */
    }
}

/* Execute with parameters */
Oid argtypes[] = {INT4OID};
Datum values[] = {Int32GetDatum(50000)};
char nulls[] = {' '};

ret = SPI_execute_with_args(
    "SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > $1",
    1,          /* number of parameters */
    argtypes,   /* parameter types */
    values,     /* parameter values */
    nulls,      /* null indicators */
    true,       /* read_only */
    10          /* max rows */
);

Prepared Statement Functions

Function Description
SPI_prepare() Prepare a statement without executing it
SPI_prepare_cursor() Prepare a statement for cursor use
SPI_prepare_extended() Prepare with extended options
SPI_prepare_params() Prepare with parameter information
SPI_getargcount() Get number of arguments for prepared statement
SPI_getargtypeid() Get data type OID for an argument
SPI_is_cursor_plan() Check if plan can be used with cursors
SPI_execute_plan() Execute a prepared statement
SPI_execute_plan_extended() Execute with extended options
SPI_execute_plan_with_paramlist() Execute with parameter list
SPI_execp() Execute in read/write mode
SPI_keepplan() Save a prepared statement
SPI_saveplan() Save a plan for later use 
/* Prepare and execute a statement */
SPIPlanPtr plan;
Oid argtypes[] = {INT4OID};
Datum values[] = {Int32GetDatum(50000)};
char nulls[] = {' '};
int ret;

/* Prepare the statement */
plan = SPI_prepare("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > $1", 1, argtypes);

if (plan == NULL)
    elog(ERROR, "SPI_prepare failed");

/* Save plan across function calls */
plan = SPI_saveplan(plan);

/* Execute the prepared statement */
ret = SPI_execute_plan(plan, values, nulls, true, 10);

if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
{
    /* Process results */
}

/* Check plan properties */
int argcount = SPI_getargcount(plan);
Oid argtype = SPI_getargtypeid(plan, 0);
bool is_cursor = SPI_is_cursor_plan(plan);

Cursor Functions

Function Description
SPI_cursor_open() Open a cursor using a prepared statement
SPI_cursor_open_with_args() Open a cursor with query and parameters
SPI_cursor_open_with_paramlist() Open cursor with parameter list
SPI_cursor_parse_open() Parse and open cursor in one step
SPI_cursor_find() Find an existing cursor by name
SPI_cursor_fetch() Fetch rows from cursor (forward only)
SPI_cursor_move() Move cursor position (forward only)
SPI_scroll_cursor_fetch() Fetch rows with scrolling support
SPI_scroll_cursor_move() Move cursor with scrolling support
SPI_cursor_close() Close an open cursor 
/* Working with cursors */
Portal portal;
SPIPlanPtr plan;
Oid argtypes[] = {INT4OID};
Datum values[] = {Int32GetDatum(50000)};
char nulls[] = {' '};

/* Prepare statement */
plan = SPI_prepare("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > $1", 1, argtypes);

/* Open cursor */
portal = SPI_cursor_open("employee_cursor", plan, values, nulls, true);

/* Fetch rows in batches */
while (true)
{
    SPI_cursor_fetch(portal, true, 100);  /* Fetch 100 rows */
    
    if (SPI_processed == 0)
        break;  /* No more rows */
    
    /* Process fetched rows */
    for (uint64 i = 0; i < SPI_processed; i++)
    {
        HeapTuple tuple = SPI_tuptable->vals[i];
        /* ... process tuple ... */
    }
}

/* Close cursor */
SPI_cursor_close(portal);

/* Alternative: Open cursor with scrolling */
portal = SPI_cursor_open_with_args(
    "scroll_cursor",
    "SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > $1",
    1,          /* number of args */
    argtypes,
    values,
    nulls,
    true,       /* read_only */
    CURSOR_OPT_SCROLL  /* allow scrolling */
);

/* Scroll backward */
SPI_scroll_cursor_fetch(portal, FETCH_BACKWARD, 10);

/* Move without fetching */
SPI_scroll_cursor_move(portal, FETCH_FORWARD, 50);

Registry Functions

Function Description
SPI_register_relation() Make an ephemeral named relation available in SPI queries
SPI_unregister_relation() Remove an ephemeral named relation from the registry
SPI_register_trigger_data() Make ephemeral trigger data available in SPI queries

💡 Return Codes

All SPI functions return an integer code indicating success or failure:

  • SPI_OK_CONNECT - Successful connection
  • SPI_OK_SELECT - SELECT executed successfully
  • SPI_OK_INSERT, SPI_OK_DELETE, SPI_OK_UPDATE - DML operations
  • SPI_ERROR_XXX - Various error conditions

🔧 45.2. Interface Support Functions

Support functions provide utilities for working with query results, extracting column information, and obtaining metadata about tuples and relations.

Column Information Functions

Function Purpose Returns
SPI_fname() Get column name by number char *
SPI_fnumber() Get column number by name int
SPI_gettype() Get data type name char *
SPI_gettypeid() Get data type OID Oid

Value Extraction Functions

Function Purpose Returns
SPI_getvalue() Get column value as string char *
SPI_getbinval() Get column value as binary Datum Datum

Relation Metadata Functions

Function Purpose Returns
SPI_getrelname() Get relation name char *
SPI_getnspname() Get namespace (schema) name char *

Utility Functions

Function Purpose Returns
SPI_result_code_string() Convert error code to string const char * 
/* Example: Processing query results */
int ret;
SPITupleTable *tuptable;
TupleDesc tupdesc;
uint64 proc;

ret = SPI_execute("SELECT id, name, salary FROM employees", true, 0);

if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
{
    tuptable = SPI_tuptable;
    tupdesc = tuptable->tupdesc;
    proc = SPI_processed;
    
    /* Get column information */
    int id_colnum = SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "id");
    int name_colnum = SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "name");
    int salary_colnum = SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "salary");
    
    /* Get column names */
    char *col1_name = SPI_fname(tupdesc, 1);
    char *col2_name = SPI_fname(tupdesc, 2);
    
    /* Get type information */
    char *id_typename = SPI_gettype(tupdesc, id_colnum);
    Oid salary_typeid = SPI_gettypeid(tupdesc, salary_colnum);
    
    elog(INFO, "Column 1: %s (type: %s)", col1_name, id_typename);
    
    /* Process each row */
    for (uint64 i = 0; i < proc; i++)
    {
        HeapTuple tuple = tuptable->vals[i];
        bool isnull;
        
        /* Extract values as strings */
        char *id_str = SPI_getvalue(tuple, tupdesc, id_colnum);
        char *name_str = SPI_getvalue(tuple, tupdesc, name_colnum);
        char *salary_str = SPI_getvalue(tuple, tupdesc, salary_colnum);
        
        elog(INFO, "Row %lu: ID=%s, Name=%s, Salary=%s", 
             i, id_str, name_str, salary_str);
        
        /* Extract values as binary Datums */
        Datum id_datum = SPI_getbinval(tuple, tupdesc, id_colnum, &isnull);
        if (!isnull)
        {
            int32 id = DatumGetInt32(id_datum);
            elog(INFO, "ID as integer: %d", id);
        }
        
        Datum salary_datum = SPI_getbinval(tuple, tupdesc, salary_colnum, &isnull);
        if (!isnull)
        {
            Numeric salary = DatumGetNumeric(salary_datum);
            /* ... work with numeric value ... */
        }
    }
    
    /* Get relation information */
    Relation rel = tupdesc->attrs[0]->attrelid;
    char *relname = SPI_getrelname(rel);
    char *nspname = SPI_getnspname(rel);
    
    elog(INFO, "Data from relation: %s.%s", nspname, relname);
}

/* Error handling with string conversion */
if (ret < 0)
{
    const char *errstr = SPI_result_code_string(ret);
    elog(ERROR, "SPI query failed: %s", errstr);
}

📋 SPI_getvalue() vs SPI_getbinval()

SPI_getvalue(): Returns string representation, convenient for logging and display

SPI_getbinval(): Returns binary Datum, efficient for processing and calculations

💡 Column Numbering

Columns are numbered starting from 1 (not 0). Use SPI_fnumber() to convert column names to numbers, avoiding hardcoded indexes.

✅ Best Practices

  • Always use SPI_fnumber() to get column positions by name instead of hardcoding
  • Check for NULL values using the isnull pointer with SPI_getbinval()
  • Use SPI_getbinval() for numeric operations to avoid string conversion overhead
  • Use SPI_getvalue() for logging, debugging, and text operations
  • Free string values returned by SPI_getvalue() using pfree() when done

💾 45.3. Memory Management

SPI provides memory management functions that allocate memory in the upper executor context, ensuring proper lifetime management for data that must persist beyond individual SPI calls.

⚠️ Memory Context Awareness

Memory allocated during SPI operations is automatically freed when SPI_finish() is called. Use SPI_palloc() and related functions to allocate memory that persists in the upper context.

Memory Allocation Functions

Function Purpose Signature
SPI_palloc() Allocate memory in upper context void * SPI_palloc(Size size)
SPI_repalloc() Reallocate memory void * SPI_repalloc(void *ptr, Size size)
SPI_pfree() Free allocated memory void SPI_pfree(void *ptr)

Tuple Management Functions

Function Purpose
SPI_copytuple() Make a copy of a tuple in upper context
SPI_returntuple() Prepare a tuple to return as a Datum
SPI_modifytuple() Create a new tuple by modifying selected fields
SPI_freetuple() Free a tuple allocated in upper context
SPI_freetuptable() Free a tuple table created by SPI_execute

Plan Management

Function Purpose
SPI_freeplan() Free a previously saved prepared statement 
/* Example: Basic memory allocation */
char *result;
size_t size = 1024;

/* Allocate memory in upper context */
result = (char *) SPI_palloc(size);
if (result == NULL)
    elog(ERROR, "out of memory");

/* Use the memory */
snprintf(result, size, "Processed %d rows", SPI_processed);

/* Memory persists after SPI_finish() */
/* Free when done */
SPI_pfree(result);

/* Reallocate if needed */
size = 2048;
result = (char *) SPI_repalloc(result, size);
/* Example: Working with tuples */
HeapTuple tuple, newtuple, copytuple;
TupleDesc tupdesc;
Datum *values;
bool *nulls;
int ret;

/* Execute query and get result */
ret = SPI_execute("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = 1", true, 1);

if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
{
    tuple = SPI_tuptable->vals[0];
    tupdesc = SPI_tuptable->tupdesc;
    
    /* Copy tuple to upper context (persists after SPI_finish) */
    copytuple = SPI_copytuple(tuple);
    
    /* Modify specific fields */
    int ncolumns = tupdesc->natts;
    values = (Datum *) palloc(ncolumns * sizeof(Datum));
    nulls = (bool *) palloc(ncolumns * sizeof(bool));
    
    /* Get current values */
    heap_deform_tuple(tuple, tupdesc, values, nulls);
    
    /* Modify salary field (column 3) */
    int salary_colnum = SPI_fnumber(tupdesc, "salary");
    values[salary_colnum - 1] = Float8GetDatum(75000.0);
    nulls[salary_colnum - 1] = false;
    
    /* Create modified tuple */
    newtuple = SPI_modifytuple(
        SPI_tuptable->vals[0],  /* original tuple */
        tupdesc,
        &salary_colnum,         /* column number to modify */
        &values[salary_colnum - 1],  /* new value */
        &nulls[salary_colnum - 1]    /* null indicator */
    );
    
    /* Use newtuple ... */
    
    /* Clean up */
    SPI_freetuple(copytuple);
    SPI_freetuple(newtuple);
    pfree(values);
    pfree(nulls);
}
/* Example: Returning tuples from a function */
Datum my_function(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    HeapTuple tuple;
    TupleDesc tupdesc;
    Datum values[3];
    bool nulls[3] = {false, false, false};
    int ret;
    
    /* Connect to SPI */
    SPI_connect();
    
    /* Execute query */
    ret = SPI_execute("SELECT id, name, salary FROM employees WHERE id = 1", 
                      true, 1);
    
    if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
    {
        tuple = SPI_tuptable->vals[0];
        tupdesc = SPI_tuptable->tupdesc;
        
        /* Extract values */
        bool isnull;
        values[0] = SPI_getbinval(tuple, tupdesc, 1, &isnull);
        values[1] = SPI_getbinval(tuple, tupdesc, 2, &isnull);
        values[2] = SPI_getbinval(tuple, tupdesc, 3, &isnull);
        
        /* Prepare tuple for return */
        tuple = heap_form_tuple(tupdesc, values, nulls);
        
        /* Convert to Datum for return */
        Datum result = SPI_returntuple(tuple, tupdesc);
        
        SPI_finish();
        PG_RETURN_DATUM(result);
    }
    
    SPI_finish();
    PG_RETURN_NULL();
}

/* Example: Freeing tuple tables */
int ret;

ret = SPI_execute("SELECT * FROM large_table", true, 0);

if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
{
    /* Process results... */
    
    /* Free the tuple table when done */
    SPI_freetuptable(SPI_tuptable);
}

/* Example: Managing prepared plans */
SPIPlanPtr plan, saved_plan;

/* Prepare statement */
plan = SPI_prepare("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept = $1", 1, (Oid[]){TEXTOID});

/* Save plan for reuse */
saved_plan = SPI_saveplan(plan);

/* Use the plan multiple times... */

/* Free when no longer needed */
SPI_freeplan(saved_plan);

✅ Upper Context Benefits

  • Memory persists after SPI_finish()
  • Safe to return from function
  • Automatically freed when function completes
  • No manual context switching needed

⚠️ Memory Pitfalls

  • Don't use regular palloc() for persistent data
  • Don't access SPI_tuptable after SPI_finish()
  • Copy tuples with SPI_copytuple() if needed later
  • Free large allocations explicitly to avoid bloat

💡 Memory Lifetime Summary

Allocation Method Lifetime Use For
palloc() Current memory context Temporary data within SPI session
SPI_palloc() Until function returns Data to return from function
SPI_copytuple() Until explicitly freed Tuples needed after SPI_finish()

🔄 45.4. Transaction Management

SPI provides functions for transaction control within procedures. These functions allow procedures to commit or rollback transactions explicitly.

⚠️ Procedures Only

Transaction control functions can only be called from procedures (created with CREATE PROCEDURE), not from regular functions. Functions run in an atomic transaction context that cannot be broken.

Function Description Signature
SPI_commit() Commit the current transaction and start a new one void SPI_commit(void)
SPI_rollback() Roll back the current transaction and start a new one void SPI_rollback(void)
SPI_start_transaction() Obsolete function (no longer needed) Deprecated 
/* Example: Procedure with transaction control */
CREATE PROCEDURE process_batch_data()
LANGUAGE C
AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME', 'process_batch_data';

/* C implementation */
PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(process_batch_data);

Datum
process_batch_data(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    int ret;
    int batch_size = 1000;
    int total_processed = 0;
    
    /* Connect to SPI */
    SPI_connect();
    
    while (true)
    {
        /* Process a batch */
        ret = SPI_execute(
            "UPDATE large_table SET processed = true "
            "WHERE id IN ("
            "  SELECT id FROM large_table "
            "  WHERE NOT processed "
            "  LIMIT 1000"
            ")",
            false,  /* read_only = false */
            0       /* no row limit */
        );
        
        if (SPI_processed == 0)
            break;  /* No more rows to process */
        
        total_processed += SPI_processed;
        
        /* Commit after each batch to avoid long transactions */
        SPI_commit();
        
        elog(INFO, "Processed %d rows so far", total_processed);
    }
    
    elog(INFO, "Total processed: %d rows", total_processed);
    
    SPI_finish();
    PG_RETURN_VOID();
}
/* Example: Procedure with conditional rollback */
CREATE PROCEDURE migrate_data_with_validation()
LANGUAGE C
AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME', 'migrate_data';

Datum
migrate_data(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    int ret;
    int64 migrated_count = 0;
    int64 validation_errors = 0;
    
    SPI_connect();
    
    /* Phase 1: Copy data */
    ret = SPI_execute(
        "INSERT INTO new_table (id, data, migrated_at) "
        "SELECT id, data, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP "
        "FROM old_table WHERE NOT migrated",
        false, 0
    );
    
    migrated_count = SPI_processed;
    elog(INFO, "Migrated %ld rows", migrated_count);
    
    /* Commit phase 1 */
    SPI_commit();
    
    /* Phase 2: Validate */
    ret = SPI_execute(
        "SELECT count(*) AS invalid_count "
        "FROM new_table nt "
        "LEFT JOIN old_table ot ON nt.id = ot.id "
        "WHERE nt.data != ot.data",
        true, 0
    );
    
    if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
    {
        bool isnull;
        Datum count_datum = SPI_getbinval(
            SPI_tuptable->vals[0],
            SPI_tuptable->tupdesc,
            1,
            &isnull
        );
        
        validation_errors = DatumGetInt64(count_datum);
    }
    
    if (validation_errors > 0)
    {
        elog(WARNING, "Validation failed: %ld mismatches", validation_errors);
        
        /* Rollback validation phase */
        SPI_rollback();
        
        /* Clean up migrated data */
        SPI_execute("DELETE FROM new_table WHERE migrated_at = CURRENT_DATE", 
                    false, 0);
        
        SPI_commit();
        
        elog(ERROR, "Migration validation failed");
    }
    else
    {
        /* Mark as migrated in old table */
        SPI_execute("UPDATE old_table SET migrated = true", false, 0);
        SPI_commit();
        
        elog(INFO, "Migration completed successfully");
    }
    
    SPI_finish();
    PG_RETURN_VOID();
}

💡 Transaction Control Behavior

  • SPI_commit() commits the current transaction and automatically starts a new one
  • SPI_rollback() rolls back and starts a new transaction
  • After commit/rollback, you must call SPI_execute() again (not reuse old plans immediately)
  • Cursors and prepared plans become invalid after commit/rollback
  • Use commits in procedures to break up long-running operations

👁️ 45.5. Visibility of Data Changes

Understanding data visibility is crucial when using SPI, as it affects what data your function can see based on transaction isolation levels and command execution order.

📊 Read-Only Queries

Read-only queries (SELECT) see a snapshot of the database as it existed at the start of the query. Changes made by your function are not visible to subsequent read-only SPI calls within the same command.

✏️ Read-Write Queries

Read-write queries (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) can see changes made by earlier SPI calls in the same function. Each SPI call with read_only = false gets an updated snapshot.

Visibility Rules

Scenario Visibility Reason
SELECT after INSERT (same function) ❌ Not visible (read-only) Uses original snapshot
SELECT after UPDATE (read-write mode) ✅ Visible New snapshot created
UPDATE after INSERT (same function) ✅ Visible Read-write sees changes
Changes from other transactions ❌ Not visible Transaction isolation
After SPI_commit() in procedure ✅ Visible New transaction starts 
/* Example: Visibility demonstration */
Datum visibility_demo(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    int ret;
    int64 count1, count2, count3;
    
    SPI_connect();
    
    /* Initial count */
    ret = SPI_execute("SELECT count(*) FROM test_table", true, 0);
    count1 = get_count_from_result();
    elog(INFO, "Initial count: %ld", count1);
    
    /* Insert new row */
    SPI_execute("INSERT INTO test_table (id, data) VALUES (999, 'test')", 
                false, 0);
    
    /* Read-only SELECT doesn't see the insert */
    ret = SPI_execute("SELECT count(*) FROM test_table", true, 0);
    count2 = get_count_from_result();
    elog(INFO, "Count after INSERT (read-only): %ld", count2);
    /* count2 == count1 (change not visible) */
    
    /* Read-write query sees the change */
    ret = SPI_execute("SELECT count(*) FROM test_table", false, 0);
    count3 = get_count_from_result();
    elog(INFO, "Count after INSERT (read-write): %ld", count3);
    /* count3 == count1 + 1 (change visible) */
    
    SPI_finish();
    PG_RETURN_VOID();
}

/* Working around visibility limitations */
Datum workaround_example(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    SPI_connect();
    
    /* Insert data */
    SPI_execute("INSERT INTO employees (name, salary) VALUES ('Alice', 50000)",
                false, 0);
    
    /* To see inserted data in subsequent queries, use read-write mode */
    SPI_execute("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE name = 'Alice'", 
                false,  /* read_only = false to see changes */
                0);
    
    /* Or use RETURNING clause */
    SPI_execute("INSERT INTO employees (name, salary) VALUES ('Bob', 60000) "
                "RETURNING id, name, salary",
                false, 0);
    /* Result includes the inserted row immediately */
    
    SPI_finish();
    PG_RETURN_VOID();
}

⚠️ Common Visibility Pitfalls

  • Expecting to see your own INSERT/UPDATE in a subsequent SELECT (use read-write mode or RETURNING)
  • Assuming other sessions' changes are visible (they're not, due to isolation)
  • Not accounting for snapshot timing in read-only queries
  • Forgetting that cursors use the snapshot from when they were opened

✅ Best Practices for Visibility

  • Use RETURNING clause to immediately get inserted/updated data
  • Set read_only = false when you need to see your own changes
  • Be aware that read-only mode is more efficient but has visibility limitations
  • In procedures, use SPI_commit() to make changes visible to new snapshots
  • Document visibility assumptions in your function comments

📚 45.6. Examples

Complete examples demonstrating common SPI usage patterns.

Example 1: Simple Query Function

/* Count employees with salary above threshold */
CREATE FUNCTION count_high_earners(threshold numeric)
RETURNS integer
LANGUAGE C STRICT
AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME', 'count_high_earners';

/* C implementation */
PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(count_high_earners);

Datum
count_high_earners(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    float8 threshold = PG_GETARG_FLOAT8(0);
    char query[256];
    int ret;
    int64 count = 0;
    
    /* Connect to SPI */
    if (SPI_connect() != SPI_OK_CONNECT)
        elog(ERROR, "SPI_connect failed");
    
    /* Build and execute query */
    snprintf(query, sizeof(query),
             "SELECT count(*) FROM employees WHERE salary > %f",
             threshold);
    
    ret = SPI_execute(query, true, 0);
    
    if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL && SPI_processed > 0)
    {
        bool isnull;
        Datum count_datum = SPI_getbinval(
            SPI_tuptable->vals[0],
            SPI_tuptable->tupdesc,
            1,
            &isnull
        );
        
        if (!isnull)
            count = DatumGetInt64(count_datum);
    }
    
    SPI_finish();
    
    PG_RETURN_INT32((int32) count);
}

Example 2: Function Returning Set of Rows

/* Get employees in department */
CREATE FUNCTION get_dept_employees(dept_name text)
RETURNS SETOF employees
LANGUAGE C STRICT
AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME', 'get_dept_employees';

/* C implementation */
PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(get_dept_employees);

Datum
get_dept_employees(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    FuncCallContext *funcctx;
    int call_cntr;
    int max_calls;
    TupleDesc tupdesc;
    AttInMetadata *attinmeta;
    
    if (SRF_IS_FIRSTCALL())
    {
        MemoryContext oldcontext;
        text *dept = PG_GETARG_TEXT_P(0);
        char *dept_str = text_to_cstring(dept);
        int ret;
        
        funcctx = SRF_FIRSTCALL_INIT();
        oldcontext = MemoryContextSwitchTo(funcctx->multi_call_memory_ctx);
        
        /* Connect to SPI */
        SPI_connect();
        
        /* Execute query */
        char query[512];
        snprintf(query, sizeof(query),
                 "SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = '%s'",
                 dept_str);
        
        ret = SPI_execute(query, true, 0);
        
        if (ret > 0 && SPI_tuptable != NULL)
        {
            funcctx->max_calls = SPI_processed;
            funcctx->user_fctx = SPI_tuptable;
            tupdesc = SPI_tuptable->tupdesc;
            
            attinmeta = TupleDescGetAttInMetadata(tupdesc);
            funcctx->attinmeta = attinmeta;
        }
        
        MemoryContextSwitchTo(oldcontext);
    }
    
    funcctx = SRF_PERCALL_SETUP();
    call_cntr = funcctx->call_cntr;
    max_calls = funcctx->max_calls;
    
    if (call_cntr < max_calls)
    {
        SPITupleTable *tuptable = (SPITupleTable *) funcctx->user_fctx;
        HeapTuple tuple = tuptable->vals[call_cntr];
        
        SRF_RETURN_NEXT(funcctx, HeapTupleGetDatum(tuple));
    }
    else
    {
        SPI_finish();
        SRF_RETURN_DONE(funcctx);
    }
}

Example 3: Trigger Function

/* Audit trigger logging changes */
CREATE FUNCTION audit_trigger()
RETURNS trigger
LANGUAGE C
AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME', 'audit_trigger';

/* C implementation */
PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(audit_trigger);

Datum
audit_trigger(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    TriggerData *trigdata = (TriggerData *) fcinfo->context;
    HeapTuple rettuple;
    char *operation;
    int ret;
    
    /* Make sure it's called as a trigger */
    if (!CALLED_AS_TRIGGER(fcinfo))
        elog(ERROR, "audit_trigger: not called by trigger manager");
    
    SPI_connect();
    
    /* Determine operation type */
    if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_INSERT(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        operation = "INSERT";
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
    }
    else if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_UPDATE(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        operation = "UPDATE";
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_newtuple;
    }
    else if (TRIGGER_FIRED_BY_DELETE(trigdata->tg_event))
    {
        operation = "DELETE";
        rettuple = trigdata->tg_trigtuple;
    }
    else
    {
        elog(ERROR, "Unknown trigger operation");
        rettuple = NULL;  /* keep compiler quiet */
    }
    
    /* Log to audit table */
    char query[1024];
    snprintf(query, sizeof(query),
             "INSERT INTO audit_log (table_name, operation, username, timestamp) "
             "VALUES ('%s', '%s', current_user, current_timestamp)",
             SPI_getrelname(trigdata->tg_relation),
             operation);
    
    ret = SPI_execute(query, false, 0);
    
    if (ret < 0)
        elog(ERROR, "audit_trigger: SPI_execute failed");
    
    SPI_finish();
    
    return PointerGetDatum(rettuple);
}

Example 4: Using Prepared Statements

/* Update salary using prepared statement */
CREATE FUNCTION update_salary(emp_id integer, new_salary numeric)
RETURNS void
LANGUAGE C STRICT
AS 'MODULE_PATHNAME', 'update_salary';

/* C implementation with static plan caching */
static SPIPlanPtr cached_plan = NULL;

PG_FUNCTION_INFO_V1(update_salary);

Datum
update_salary(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
{
    int32 emp_id = PG_GETARG_INT32(0);
    Numeric new_salary = PG_GETARG_NUMERIC(1);
    Datum values[2];
    char nulls[2] = {' ', ' '};
    int ret;
    
    SPI_connect();
    
    /* Prepare plan on first call */
    if (cached_plan == NULL)
    {
        Oid argtypes[] = {INT4OID, NUMERICOID};
        
        cached_plan = SPI_prepare(
            "UPDATE employees SET salary = $2, updated_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP "
            "WHERE id = $1",
            2,
            argtypes
        );
        
        if (cached_plan == NULL)
            elog(ERROR, "SPI_prepare failed");
        
        /* Save plan so it survives SPI_finish */
        cached_plan = SPI_saveplan(cached_plan);
    }
    
    /* Execute with parameters */
    values[0] = Int32GetDatum(emp_id);
    values[1] = NumericGetDatum(new_salary);
    
    ret = SPI_execute_plan(cached_plan, values, nulls, false, 0);
    
    if (ret < 0)
        elog(ERROR, "SPI_execute_plan failed");
    
    elog(INFO, "Updated %lu rows", SPI_processed);
    
    SPI_finish();
    
    PG_RETURN_VOID();
}

🎓 Chapter 45 Summary: Server Programming Interface

The Server Programming Interface (SPI) is PostgreSQL's gateway for C functions to execute SQL commands and access database functionality. It provides a comprehensive API for building high-performance extensions and procedural languages.

  • Connection Management: SPI_connect() and SPI_finish() establish SPI session boundaries
  • Query Execution: Execute SQL with SPI_execute() or use prepared statements for efficiency
  • Cursors: Handle large result sets efficiently with cursor-based iteration
  • Memory Management: Use SPI_palloc() for persistent allocations and SPI_copytuple() for tuple preservation
  • Transaction Control: Procedures can use SPI_commit() and SPI_rollback() for explicit control
  • Data Visibility: Understand snapshot isolation—read-only queries don't see uncommitted changes
  • Support Functions: Extract and inspect query results with metadata functions

SPI is fundamental to PostgreSQL extensibility, enabling procedural languages (PL/pgSQL, PL/Python, etc.) and custom C extensions to leverage the full power of the database engine.
